definition: A group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if (i) its members can interbreed with one another, (ii) they produce fertile offspring, and (iii) they are reproductively isolated from other such groups.
conditions: members can interbreed
produce fertile offspring
reproductively isolated from others
counterexample: Ring species, such as the Larus gulls encircling the Arctic, present a case where adjacent populations can interbreed and produce fertile offspring, but the terminal populations at the ends of the ring cannot. The conditions are satisfied locally but fail globally.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that reproductive compatibility is a transitive relation, which ring species demonstrate it is not.
passage: The biological species concept holds that a group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if its members can interbreed with one another, they produce fertile offspring, and they are reproductively isolated from other such groups. This definition serves well in many cases, but it encounters a significant difficulty with ring species. Consider the Larus gulls, whose populations form a ring around the Arctic. Each population can interbreed with its immediate neighbours and produce fertile offspring. But the populations at the two ends of the ring, where it closes in Europe, are reproductively isolated from one another: they do not interbreed. The conditions of the definition are satisfied locally — each adjacent pair meets the requirements — but fail globally, for the terminal populations are as distinct as any two species. The definition implicitly assumes that reproductive compatibility is a transitive relation: if A can breed with B, and B with C, then A can breed with C. Ring species demonstrate that this assumption is false, and that the biological species concept, for all its utility, admits of cases it cannot cleanly resolve.

definition: An entity is alive if and only if (i) it metabolises, (ii) it reproduces, and (iii) it responds to stimuli from its environment.
conditions: metabolises
reproduces
responds to stimuli
counterexample: A virus does not metabolise on its own and does not respond to stimuli in the usual sense, yet it reproduces (using host machinery) and exhibits many features we associate with life. Conversely, a mule is indisputably alive but cannot reproduce. The conditions are neither jointly sufficient nor individually necessary.
missing_condition: The definition must accommodate borderline cases and may need to treat life as a cluster concept rather than one defined by necessary and sufficient conditions.
passage: A familiar definition holds that an entity is alive if and only if it metabolises, reproduces, and responds to stimuli from its environment. This definition captures a wide range of living things, from bacteria to elephants. Yet it encounters difficulty at the boundaries. Consider a virus. Outside a host cell, it is inert: it does not metabolise, it does not respond to stimuli, it does nothing at all. Inside a host cell, however, it commandeers the cell's machinery to reproduce itself, often with remarkable efficiency. The virus satisfies one of the three conditions (reproduction, after a fashion) but fails the other two. Yet it possesses a genome, it evolves by natural selection, and its behaviour is intimately bound up with the living world. To exclude it entirely from the category of life seems arbitrary. Now consider a mule, the offspring of a horse and a donkey. A mule metabolises, responds to stimuli, and is by every ordinary measure alive. But it cannot reproduce. The second condition fails, yet no one would deny that a mule is alive. The definition, it appears, identifies conditions that are characteristic of life but neither jointly sufficient to capture all cases nor individually necessary for every living thing.

definition: A celestial body is a planet if and only if (i) it orbits a star, (ii) it has sufficient mass for its gravity to give it a roughly spherical shape, and (iii) it is the dominant body in its orbital zone.
conditions: orbits a star
sufficient mass for spherical shape
dominant in orbital zone
counterexample: A body ejected from its solar system by gravitational interaction may have sufficient mass, be spherical, and have once been the dominant body in its orbital zone, but it no longer orbits a star. The first condition fails, yet the body is in every other respect a planet.
missing_condition: The definition is tied to a current orbital relationship that may be disrupted by dynamical processes, yet the intrinsic planetary nature of the body seems unchanged.
passage: The current astronomical definition holds that a celestial body is a planet if and only if it orbits a star, it has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid body forces and assume a roughly spherical shape, and it has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit, being the dominant body in its orbital zone. This definition, adopted to resolve the status of certain distant bodies in our solar system, proves vulnerable to the following case. Suppose a body of planetary mass and spherical form, which has for millions of years been the dominant body in its orbital zone, is ejected from its solar system by a close gravitational encounter with another massive body. It now drifts through interstellar space, orbiting no star. The first condition fails: the body does not orbit a star. Yet in its composition, its mass, its shape, and its geological complexity, it is indistinguishable from a body we would unhesitatingly call a planet. The definition has made planethood contingent upon an external relationship — the orbital connection to a star — rather than upon the intrinsic properties of the body itself.

definition: A condition is a disease if and only if (i) it involves a departure from normal biological functioning, (ii) it has an identifiable cause, and (iii) it causes suffering or impairment in the affected individual.
conditions: departure from normal functioning
identifiable cause
causes suffering or impairment
counterexample: An individual carries a bacterial infection that causes a departure from normal functioning and has an identifiable cause, but is entirely asymptomatic — he suffers no impairment whatever. The third condition fails, yet he has a disease in the medical sense and may transmit it to others.
missing_condition: A condition may constitute a disease even in the absence of suffering or impairment, provided it involves a pathological process that characteristically causes harm.
passage: A natural account of disease holds that a condition is a disease if and only if it involves a departure from normal biological functioning, it has an identifiable cause, and it causes suffering or impairment in the affected individual. This definition captures the paradigm cases — pneumonia, diabetes, malaria — in which all three conditions are plainly met. But consider an individual who carries the tubercle bacillus in his lungs. There is a departure from normal functioning: the bacillus has established itself in the tissue. There is an identifiable cause: the bacterium itself. Yet the individual is entirely asymptomatic. He suffers no impairment, no discomfort, no limitation of activity. The third condition fails. Yet the physician would not hesitate to say that the man has tuberculosis, for the pathological process is present and may at any time become active. Moreover, the man is capable of transmitting the disease to others. The definition has erred in requiring actual suffering; a disease may be present even when its characteristic harms have not yet manifested.

definition: A substance is a chemical element if and only if (i) it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) it consists of atoms of a single kind, and (iii) it occupies a definite place in the periodic table.
conditions: cannot be decomposed by chemical means
atoms of single kind
occupies place in periodic table
counterexample: Isotopes of a single element (e.g. carbon-12 and carbon-14) consist of atoms that differ in their number of neutrons and hence are not strictly of a single kind, yet they are the same element. The second condition, taken literally, is too restrictive.
missing_condition: Atoms of the same element share the same atomic number (number of protons), but may differ in mass (isotopes); 'single kind' must be understood as 'same atomic number.'
passage: A traditional definition holds that a substance is a chemical element if and only if it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, it consists of atoms of a single kind, and it occupies a definite place in the periodic table. The second condition, however, proves problematic when examined closely. Consider the element carbon. Natural carbon consists of atoms of at least two kinds: carbon-12, with six neutrons, and carbon-14, with eight neutrons. These atoms differ in mass and in their nuclear properties — carbon-14 is radioactive, carbon-12 is not. If 'atoms of a single kind' means atoms that are identical in all respects, then carbon fails the second condition. Yet carbon is indisputably an element. The definition has used the phrase 'atoms of a single kind' without sufficient precision. What makes carbon a single element is that all its atoms share the same number of protons — six — and hence the same atomic number. The concept of 'kind' must be understood in terms of atomic number, not in terms of identity in all nuclear properties.

definition: An object is a fossil if and only if (i) it is the preserved remains or trace of a once-living organism, (ii) it is embedded in sedimentary rock, and (iii) it is of considerable geological age.
conditions: preserved remains or trace of once-living organism
embedded in sedimentary rock
of considerable geological age
counterexample: Insects preserved in amber are universally regarded as fossils, yet they are not embedded in sedimentary rock. The amber — fossilised tree resin — is an organic medium, not a sediment. The second condition fails, yet the specimens are paradigmatic fossils.
missing_condition: The definition ties fossilisation to a particular medium of preservation; what is essential is that the remains have undergone long-term natural preservation, regardless of the enclosing material.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an object is a fossil if and only if it is the preserved remains or trace of a once-living organism, it is embedded in sedimentary rock, and it is of considerable geological age. This definition captures the familiar cases: the trilobite in limestone, the fern impression in shale, the dinosaur bone in sandstone. Yet it proves too narrow upon examination. Consider the insects preserved in Baltic amber, many of which are tens of millions of years old. These specimens display exquisite anatomical detail — the veins of the wings, the segments of the antennae, even the hairs upon the legs — and they are universally regarded as fossils by palaeontologists and laymen alike. But they are not embedded in sedimentary rock. The amber in which they are entombed is fossilised tree resin, an organic material quite unlike limestone or shale. The second condition fails. The definition, it appears, has mistaken a common circumstance of preservation — embedment in sedimentary rock — for an essential feature of fossilisation. What makes something a fossil is not the nature of its enclosing matrix but the fact of its long-term natural preservation, by whatever means that preservation was achieved.

definition: A substance is a mineral if and only if (i) it is naturally occurring, (ii) it is inorganic, and (iii) it has a definite crystalline structure.
conditions: naturally occurring
inorganic
has definite crystalline structure
counterexample: Mercury as found in cinnabar deposits is naturally occurring and inorganic, yet in its native liquid state at ordinary temperatures it possesses no crystalline structure whatever. It is nonetheless classified among the minerals. The third condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition requires crystallinity, but certain naturally occurring inorganic substances such as native mercury and opal (an amorphous mineraloid) are recognised as minerals or near-minerals despite lacking a crystal lattice.
passage: One might propose that a substance is a mineral if and only if it is naturally occurring, it is inorganic, and it has a definite crystalline structure. This definition captures a very wide range of minerals — quartz, feldspar, calcite, mica, and the great majority of specimens encountered in geological fieldwork. Yet it proves too restrictive in certain cases. Consider native mercury, which occurs naturally in cinnabar deposits and has been known since antiquity. Mercury is naturally occurring and is unquestionably inorganic. But at ordinary temperatures it is a liquid, and a liquid, by definition, lacks the long-range atomic order that constitutes a crystalline structure. The third condition fails. Yet mineralogists have traditionally included native mercury among the minerals, and it appears in standard mineralogical catalogues. A similar difficulty arises with opal, which is amorphous — it has no regular crystal lattice — yet is widely treated as a mineral or at the very least as a mineraloid of great importance. The definition has identified crystallinity as essential, when in practice the concept of a mineral is somewhat more accommodating than the strict requirement of crystal structure would allow.

definition: An interaction constitutes a force if and only if (i) it acts upon a body, (ii) it produces or tends to produce a change in the body's motion, and (iii) it has a determinate magnitude and direction.
conditions: acts upon a body
produces or tends to produce change in motion
has determinate magnitude and direction
counterexample: Consider the centrifugal force experienced by a passenger in a turning motorcar. It acts upon the passenger's body, it has a determinate magnitude and direction, and it produces a change in the passenger's apparent motion relative to the car. Yet the centrifugal force is not a force in the Newtonian sense; it is a fictitious force arising from the non-inertial reference frame. The three conditions are met, yet the interaction is not a genuine force.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish between genuine forces and pseudo-forces that appear only in accelerating reference frames; a further condition is required specifying that the force be observable in an inertial frame.
passage: A standard account holds that an interaction constitutes a force if and only if it acts upon a body, it produces or tends to produce a change in the body's motion, and it has a determinate magnitude and direction. This analysis captures the paradigm cases admirably: gravity pulls the apple downward, the spring pushes the block, the wind deflects the sail. Yet it admits of a troublesome case. Consider a passenger seated in a motorcar as the driver negotiates a sharp bend. The passenger feels himself pressed outward against the door. Something acts upon his body, it produces a change in his motion relative to the interior of the car, and it has a determinate magnitude and direction — radially outward from the centre of the curve. The three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the centrifugal force is not a genuine force in the Newtonian sense. It arises solely from the fact that the car constitutes an accelerating reference frame; an observer standing on the roadside would describe the passenger's experience without any outward force, attributing the motion entirely to the passenger's inertia. The definition has failed to require that the force be present in an inertial reference frame, and accordingly admits fictitious forces alongside genuine ones.

definition: A system possesses energy if and only if (i) it is capable of performing work, (ii) its energy can be quantified in determinate units, and (iii) the energy is conserved when the system is isolated.
conditions: capable of performing work
quantifiable in determinate units
conserved when system is isolated
counterexample: A body at rest in a gravitational field possesses gravitational potential energy, which can be quantified and is conserved. Yet the assignment of a definite value to this energy depends upon the choice of a reference point: the body has one quantity of potential energy relative to the floor and quite another relative to sea level. The energy is not an intrinsic property of the system but depends on a convention. The second condition is met only relative to an arbitrary datum.
missing_condition: The definition treats energy as an absolute quantity of the system, when in fact potential energy is defined only relative to a chosen reference configuration.
passage: It is commonly held that a system possesses energy if and only if it is capable of performing work, its energy can be quantified in determinate units, and the energy is conserved when the system is isolated. This account serves well for kinetic energy — the energy of a moving body is determinately half the product of its mass and the square of its velocity, and is conserved in the appropriate circumstances. But consider the gravitational potential energy of a book resting upon a table. The book is capable of performing work — if it falls, it may drive a mechanism or compress a spring. Its potential energy can be expressed in joules. And energy is conserved in the system as a whole. The three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the value of the book's potential energy depends entirely upon the choice of a reference point. Relative to the table-top, its potential energy is zero. Relative to the floor beneath, it is positive. Relative to the centre of the earth, it is vastly larger still. The energy is not an intrinsic and determinate property of the book-and-table system; it is defined only in relation to an arbitrary datum. The definition has presented energy as an absolute quantity, but potential energy, at any rate, is irreducibly relational.

definition: An entity is an individual organism if and only if (i) it is a spatially bounded living thing, (ii) it is genetically homogeneous, and (iii) it maintains itself through its own metabolic processes.
conditions: spatially bounded living thing
genetically homogeneous
maintains itself through own metabolic processes
counterexample: A large grove of quaking aspen trees connected by a shared root system constitutes a single genetic individual — a clone — that is genetically homogeneous and maintains itself metabolically. Yet it is not spatially bounded in the way a single animal is; it occupies many hectares and appears to be a forest of separate trees. The first condition fails if we take 'bounded' in the usual sense, yet the grove is a single organism.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that organisms are compact, discrete entities, but clonal organisms may extend over vast areas as physiologically connected but spatially diffuse individuals.
passage: One might define an individual organism as an entity that is a spatially bounded living thing, is genetically homogeneous, and maintains itself through its own metabolic processes. This definition captures the paradigm cases: a horse, a fish, a single oak tree. Each is spatially bounded, genetically uniform (setting aside somatic mutation), and metabolically self-sustaining. Yet the definition encounters a significant difficulty with clonal organisms. Consider the quaking aspen of western North America. A grove of what appear to be thousands of separate trees may in fact constitute a single genetic individual, connected beneath the soil by an extensive root system. The grove is genetically homogeneous — every trunk shares the same genome — and maintains itself through its own photosynthetic and metabolic processes. But it is not spatially bounded in the manner of a horse or a fish; it extends across many hectares, and its aerial parts appear quite separate from one another. If we insist upon spatial boundedness in the ordinary sense, we must deny that the aspen grove is a single organism, which seems to conflict with the genetic and physiological evidence. The definition has imported from the animal kingdom an assumption of compactness that does not hold for all living things.

definition: A region has a particular climate if and only if (i) the average temperature and precipitation are stable over a long period, (ii) the region can be assigned to a recognised climatic zone, and (iii) the climatic conditions are determined by the region's latitude and altitude.
conditions: average temperature and precipitation stable over long period
assignable to recognised climatic zone
conditions determined by latitude and altitude
counterexample: The climate of western Norway is far warmer and wetter than its latitude and altitude alone would predict, owing to the influence of the Gulf Stream. The third condition fails — the climatic conditions are not determined solely by latitude and altitude — yet the region plainly has a climate, and a distinctive one.
missing_condition: Climate is shaped by ocean currents, prevailing winds, and continental position as well as by latitude and altitude; the definition has oversimplified the determining factors.
passage: A natural account of climate holds that a region has a particular climate if and only if the average temperature and precipitation are stable over a long period, the region can be assigned to a recognised climatic zone, and the climatic conditions are determined by the region's latitude and altitude. This definition serves tolerably well for many regions of the world: the tropics are warm and wet because they lie near the equator, and mountain peaks are cold because of their elevation. Yet the definition encounters a decisive difficulty with regions whose climate is anomalous for their latitude and altitude. Consider western Norway. Bergen lies at approximately the same latitude as the southern tip of Greenland, yet its winters are incomparably milder. The explanation lies not in latitude or altitude but in the Gulf Stream, which carries warm Atlantic water to the Norwegian coast and raises the temperature far above what one would expect. The third condition fails: the region's climate is not determined by its latitude and altitude but by the influence of an ocean current. Yet western Norway plainly has a climate, and a well-characterised one at that. The definition has oversimplified the causal structure of climate by reducing it to two geographical variables, when in reality the factors that determine a region's climate include ocean currents, prevailing winds, and proximity to large bodies of water.

definition: A species is extinct if and only if (i) no living individuals of that species remain, (ii) the last individual has died, and (iii) the species cannot be revived by natural means.
conditions: no living individuals remain
last individual has died
cannot be revived by natural means
counterexample: The coelacanth was believed to have been extinct for sixty-five million years: no living individuals were known, the last known individual appeared to have died in the Cretaceous, and revival by natural means seemed impossible. All three conditions were apparently met. Yet in 1938 a living coelacanth was discovered off the coast of South Africa. The species was not extinct; the conditions were merely believed to be satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition conflates our knowledge of a species' status with its actual status; the conditions may appear satisfied when they are not, which reveals that extinction is an objective fact about the world, not a function of human observation.
passage: One naturally supposes that a species is extinct if and only if no living individuals of that species remain, the last individual has died, and the species cannot be revived by natural means. This definition captures the paradigm case of extinction: the dodo, the passenger pigeon, the thylacine. In each case, the last individual perished, no others survived, and no natural process could restore the species. Yet the definition proves vulnerable to a peculiar class of counterexamples. Consider the coelacanth, a lobe-finned fish known from the fossil record. By the early twentieth century, palaeontologists were confident that the coelacanth had been extinct for some sixty-five million years. No living individuals were known; the last individual appeared to have died in the Cretaceous period; and revival by natural means seemed a sheer impossibility. The three conditions were, so far as anyone could determine, satisfied in full. Yet in 1938, a fisherman's trawl off the coast of South Africa brought up a living coelacanth. The species had persisted, undetected, for millions of years. The conditions were not in fact satisfied; they merely appeared to be. The definition, though correct in its requirements, highlights a deeper difficulty: extinction is an objective fact about the world, not a fact about our observations, and we may be mistaken about whether its conditions obtain.

definition: A biological relationship is symbiotic if and only if (i) it involves two organisms of different species living in close physical association, (ii) both organisms derive benefit from the relationship, and (iii) the association is long-term or permanent.
conditions: two organisms of different species in close physical association
both derive benefit
association is long-term or permanent
counterexample: Parasitism, such as the relationship between a tapeworm and its host, involves two organisms of different species living in close, long-term physical association. But only the parasite benefits; the host is harmed. The second condition fails, yet parasitism is universally classified as a form of symbiosis in the biological literature.
missing_condition: The definition restricts symbiosis to mutualism, when in fact the term encompasses any sustained interspecific association, including parasitism and commensalism, where the benefit is not necessarily reciprocal.
passage: A common account holds that a biological relationship is symbiotic if and only if it involves two organisms of different species living in close physical association, both organisms derive benefit from the relationship, and the association is long-term or permanent. This definition captures the familiar mutualistic cases admirably: the clown fish and the anemone, the lichen composed of fungus and alga, the mycorrhizal association between fungus and plant root. In each case, both partners benefit. Yet the definition proves too narrow. Consider the relationship between a tapeworm and its human host. The tapeworm lives in intimate, long-term physical association with the host. It derives considerable benefit, absorbing nutrients from the host's intestine. But the host derives no benefit whatever; on the contrary, the host is harmed, sometimes severely. The second condition fails. Yet parasitism is universally classified as a form of symbiosis in the biological literature; indeed, it is one of the three principal types, alongside mutualism and commensalism. The definition has restricted symbiosis to mutualism alone, when in truth the concept is broader: it encompasses any sustained interspecific association, whether the benefit is mutual, one-sided, or accompanied by harm.

definition: A trait is an adaptation if and only if (i) it currently serves a function that enhances the organism's fitness, (ii) it arose through natural selection for that function, and (iii) it is heritable.
conditions: currently serves function enhancing fitness
arose through natural selection for that function
heritable
counterexample: The human capacity to read is heritable in the sense that the underlying neural architecture is genetically transmitted, and it currently enhances fitness in literate societies. Yet the capacity to read did not arise through natural selection for reading; writing has existed for only a few thousand years. The second condition fails, yet literacy plausibly builds upon traits that are genuine adaptations.
missing_condition: The definition does not accommodate exaptations — traits that evolved for one function and were later co-opted for another — nor traits whose current utility is a by-product of selection for a different purpose.
passage: Biologists frequently say that a trait is an adaptation if and only if it currently serves a function that enhances the organism's fitness, it arose through natural selection for that function, and it is heritable. This definition captures the textbook cases: the eye arose through selection for vision, the wing through selection for flight, the thick fur of the Arctic fox through selection for insulation. Each trait currently serves the function for which it was selected, and each is heritable. Yet the definition encounters difficulty with a class of traits that serve functions for which they were not originally selected. Consider the human capacity for reading. The underlying neural architecture is genetically transmitted and hence heritable. The ability to read manifestly enhances fitness in societies where literacy is essential for economic participation. Yet reading is a cultural invention scarcely five thousand years old; no natural selection for reading as such has had time to operate. The neural circuits employed in reading were selected for other purposes — pattern recognition, perhaps, or the processing of visual symbols — and have been co-opted for a function their evolutionary history did not anticipate. The definition fails to accommodate such exaptations, in which a trait's current function diverges from the function for which it was selected.

definition: A place is the habitat of a species if and only if (i) the species naturally occurs there, (ii) the place provides the resources necessary for the species' survival, and (iii) the species reproduces there.
conditions: species naturally occurs there
provides resources for survival
species reproduces there
counterexample: Many migratory birds, such as the Arctic tern, spend half the year in the Arctic and the other half in the Antarctic. They reproduce only in the Arctic, and the Antarctic provides resources only for survival, not for breeding. The third condition fails for the Antarctic habitat, yet we should not deny that the Antarctic is part of the tern's habitat.
missing_condition: The definition requires that all three conditions be met in a single location, but for migratory species the habitat is distributed across multiple locations, each of which may satisfy different subsets of the conditions.
passage: A plausible account holds that a place is the habitat of a species if and only if the species naturally occurs there, the place provides the resources necessary for the species' survival, and the species reproduces there. This definition serves well for sedentary organisms: the coral polyp's habitat is the reef, where it occurs, feeds, and reproduces. But it encounters a difficulty with migratory species. Consider the Arctic tern, which breeds in the Arctic during the northern summer and then migrates to the Antarctic for the southern summer, traversing some forty thousand miles each year. The tern naturally occurs in both the Arctic and the Antarctic. Both regions provide resources necessary for its survival. Yet the tern reproduces only in the Arctic; it does not breed in the Antarctic at all. The third condition fails for the Antarctic portion of its range. Yet it would be perverse to deny that the Antarctic is part of the Arctic tern's habitat; the bird spends half its life there and depends upon the resources of the southern oceans for its sustenance. The definition has assumed that all essential activities occur in a single place, when for migratory species the habitat is a distributed system spanning different regions that serve different biological functions.

definition: A biological unit is a cell if and only if (i) it is bounded by a membrane, (ii) it contains genetic material, and (iii) it is capable of independent metabolic activity.
conditions: bounded by membrane
contains genetic material
capable of independent metabolic activity
counterexample: A mature mammalian red blood cell is bounded by a membrane and carries on metabolic activity (glycolysis), yet it has ejected its nucleus during development and contains no genetic material. The second condition fails, yet it is indisputably a cell.
missing_condition: The definition requires the current presence of genetic material, but certain specialised cells lose their nuclei during maturation while remaining genuine cells by every other criterion.
passage: The classical account holds that a biological unit is a cell if and only if it is bounded by a membrane, it contains genetic material, and it is capable of independent metabolic activity. This definition captures the vast majority of cells admirably: a bacterium has a membrane, a chromosome, and metabolic machinery; a plant cell has a membrane, a nucleus, and photosynthetic apparatus. Yet the definition encounters an instructive difficulty with a very common type of cell. The mature mammalian red blood cell — the erythrocyte — is bounded by a cell membrane and carries on certain metabolic processes, notably glycolysis. But during its maturation in the bone marrow, the erythrocyte ejects its nucleus, along with its mitochondria and other organelles. The mature cell, released into the bloodstream, contains no genetic material whatever. The second condition fails. Yet no biologist would deny that the erythrocyte is a cell; it is, indeed, the most abundant cell type in the human body. The definition has treated the presence of genetic material as essential to cellhood, when in fact it is a feature that characterises most but not all cells. Some cells shed their genetic cargo as part of their normal developmental programme, without thereby ceasing to be cells.

definition: A population is evolving if and only if (i) heritable variation exists among its members, (ii) some variants are better adapted to the environment than others, and (iii) the better-adapted variants leave more offspring.
conditions: heritable variation exists
some variants better adapted
better-adapted variants leave more offspring
counterexample: Genetic drift in a small population can produce evolutionary change — a shift in allele frequencies across generations — even when no variant is better adapted than any other. All variants may be equally fit, yet random sampling effects cause some alleles to increase and others to vanish. The second and third conditions fail, yet the population is evolving.
missing_condition: The definition identifies evolution with natural selection alone, omitting genetic drift, mutation pressure, and gene flow as mechanisms of evolutionary change that operate independently of differential adaptation.
passage: A familiar account holds that a population is evolving if and only if heritable variation exists among its members, some variants are better adapted to the environment than others, and the better-adapted variants leave more offspring. This is, in effect, a statement of natural selection, and it captures one important mechanism of evolutionary change. Yet it is too narrow as a definition of evolution itself. Consider a small, isolated population of beetles in which all individuals are equally well adapted to their environment: no variant enjoys any advantage over any other. Heritable variation exists — some beetles are slightly darker, some lighter — but the variation is selectively neutral. Now suppose that, by chance, a storm kills a large fraction of the population, and the survivors happen to be predominantly dark. In the next generation, the population has shifted in allele frequency: dark alleles are more common, light alleles rarer. The population has evolved — its genetic composition has changed across generations — yet neither the second nor the third condition was satisfied. The change was wrought not by differential adaptation but by random sampling, the process known as genetic drift. The definition has mistaken one mechanism of evolution for the whole of it, excluding drift, mutation pressure, and migration from the account.

definition: An organism belongs to a given biological species if and only if (i) it can produce fertile offspring with other members of the group, (ii) it shares a common morphological body plan with those members, and (iii) it inhabits the same geographical range as other members of the group.
conditions: it can produce fertile offspring with other members of the group
it shares a common morphological body plan with those members
it inhabits the same geographical range as other members of the group
counterexample: Consider the case of the herring gull and the lesser black-backed gull in northern Europe. These two populations are recognised as distinct species and do not ordinarily interbreed where their ranges overlap in Britain. Yet they are connected by a chain of interbreeding populations encircling the Arctic, forming what is known as a ring species. Some members of the chain can produce fertile offspring with adjacent populations yet share neither the morphology nor the range of the terminal populations. The third condition fails for many members, and the first condition holds only locally, not across the species as a whole.
missing_condition: The definition requires co-occurrence in the same geographical range, but species membership is not contingent upon present-day geography. Populations of the same species may be geographically disjunct yet remain conspecific, and a proper characterisation must rely upon reproductive compatibility and shared ancestry rather than upon spatial overlap.
passage: One might propose that an organism belongs to a given biological species if and only if it can produce fertile offspring with other members of the group, it shares a common morphological body plan with those members, and it inhabits the same geographical range. This definition appears to capture the intuitive notion of a species admirably. Dogs of various breeds, for instance, can interbreed, share the general canid body plan, and occupy overlapping ranges across the globe. The conditions seem jointly sufficient. Yet one must consider the phenomenon of ring species, of which the herring gull complex provides a celebrated illustration. Around the Arctic, a chain of gull populations extends from western Europe through Siberia and back. Adjacent populations interbreed freely and produce fertile offspring. But the terminal populations — the herring gull and the lesser black-backed gull — overlap in Britain and do not normally interbreed. Intermediate populations separated by vast distances share reproductive compatibility with their neighbours but not with populations at the far end of the ring, and they do not inhabit the same range as those distant relatives. The geographical condition fails: populations of what is, by continuity of gene flow, a single species complex may be separated by thousands of miles. Moreover, the morphological condition is strained, for the body plan shifts gradually along the ring, and the terminal forms differ markedly in plumage and size. The proposed definition has mistaken a frequent accompaniment of species membership — geographical co-occurrence — for an essential feature, when in truth what matters is the possibility of gene flow through intermediate populations and shared evolutionary lineage.

definition: An entity is alive if and only if (i) it is composed of one or more cells, (ii) it carries out metabolism by consuming energy from its environment, and (iii) it is capable of independent reproduction.
conditions: it is composed of one or more cells
it carries out metabolism by consuming energy from its environment
it is capable of independent reproduction
counterexample: Consider the worker honeybee. It is composed of cells, it metabolises sugars and other nutrients, and it is by every ordinary criterion a living organism. Yet the worker bee is sterile; it is incapable of independent reproduction. Only the queen reproduces, and the workers contribute to the propagation of the colony's genes indirectly. The third condition fails, yet no biologist would deny that the worker bee is alive.
missing_condition: The definition demands independent reproductive capacity, but many living organisms — sterile hybrids, worker castes in social insects, post-reproductive individuals — are alive without being capable of reproduction. A satisfactory characterisation of life must accommodate organisms whose contribution to reproduction is indirect or absent altogether.
passage: Suppose one defines an entity as alive if and only if it is composed of one or more cells, it carries out metabolism by consuming energy from its environment, and it is capable of independent reproduction. The definition appears to capture the central features of living things. A bacterium is cellular, metabolises nutrients, and reproduces by binary fission. A flowering plant is composed of cells, photosynthesises, and produces seeds. The conditions seem to delineate the boundary between the living and the non-living with tolerable precision. Yet one must consider the worker honeybee. This insect is unquestionably composed of cells — trillions of them, organised into tissues and organs of considerable complexity. It metabolises sugars derived from nectar and honey, powering flight muscles and nervous activity. By every ordinary biological criterion, the worker bee is alive. But the worker bee is sterile. Its reproductive organs are vestigial, suppressed by pheromones from the queen. It cannot reproduce independently; indeed, it never reproduces at all. The propagation of its genes depends entirely upon the queen's fecundity. The third condition of the definition fails outright. One might attempt to rescue the definition by speaking of the species' capacity for reproduction rather than the individual's, but this manoeuvre changes the subject: the definition purported to characterise individual living entities, not species. The difficulty reveals that reproduction, whilst a characteristic of life at the population level, is not a necessary feature of every individual living organism. Mules, sterile hybrid plants, and elderly post-reproductive animals are all alive, and none can reproduce. A sound characterisation of life must acknowledge this.

definition: A celestial body is a planet if and only if (i) it orbits a star, (ii) it is spherical in shape owing to its own gravitational force, and (iii) it possesses at least one natural satellite.
conditions: it orbits a star
it is spherical in shape owing to its own gravitational force
it possesses at least one natural satellite
counterexample: Consider the planet Venus. It orbits the Sun in a nearly circular path, and its mass is sufficient for gravitational forces to have rendered it essentially spherical. By every standard astronomical criterion, Venus is a planet. Yet Venus possesses no natural satellite whatsoever. No moon orbits it, nor has any been discovered despite careful searches. The third condition fails entirely, yet Venus remains indisputably a planet.
missing_condition: The definition requires possession of a natural satellite, but planetary status is not contingent upon having moons. Venus and Mercury both lack natural satellites yet are planets by every accepted criterion. A proper characterisation must concern itself with the body's orbital and gravitational properties rather than with the accidental presence or absence of satellites.
passage: One might propose that a celestial body is a planet if and only if it orbits a star, it is spherical owing to its own gravitational force, and it possesses at least one natural satellite. The definition appears to fit the most familiar cases rather well. The Earth orbits the Sun, is spherical, and possesses the Moon. Jupiter orbits the Sun, is spherical, and possesses dozens of moons. Saturn, Mars, Uranus, and Neptune all satisfy the three conditions. One might suppose that the possession of satellites is a natural mark of planetary status, reflecting the gravitational dominance of a large body in its neighbourhood. Yet one must consider Venus. This body orbits the Sun at a mean distance of roughly one hundred and eight million kilometres. Its mass is approximately eighty-two per cent that of the Earth, more than sufficient for self-gravity to have produced a spherical shape. Venus is, by every standard criterion employed in astronomy, a planet. But Venus has no natural satellite. Repeated searches, including radar observations and surveys by visiting spacecraft, have found nothing. No moon orbits Venus, nor is there any evidence that one ever did. The third condition of the definition fails. Mercury, too, orbits the Sun, is spherical, and lacks any moon. The proposed definition would exclude both bodies from the category of planets, a result that no astronomer would accept. The difficulty is that the possession of natural satellites, whilst common among the planets, is a contingent feature rather than a defining one. A satisfactory definition must rest upon the body's own properties and its relationship to its parent star, not upon the accidental presence of orbiting companions.

definition: A substance is a chemical element if and only if (i) it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, (ii) it is composed of atoms all having the same number of protons in the nucleus, and (iii) it exists as a solid under standard conditions of temperature and pressure.
conditions: it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means
it is composed of atoms all having the same number of protons in the nucleus
it exists as a solid under standard conditions of temperature and pressure
counterexample: Consider the element oxygen. It cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical reactions, and every atom of oxygen contains precisely eight protons in its nucleus. Oxygen is, by every accepted chemical criterion, an element. Yet oxygen does not exist as a solid under standard conditions of temperature and pressure; at room temperature and one atmosphere it is a colourless gas. The third condition fails, yet oxygen is indisputably an element.
missing_condition: The definition requires existence as a solid under standard conditions, but the phase of a substance at standard temperature and pressure is a contingent physical property unrelated to elemental status. Eleven elements are gases and two are liquids under standard conditions. A correct characterisation of elements must concern atomic number alone and not phase.
passage: Suppose one defines a substance as a chemical element if and only if it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, it is composed of atoms all having the same number of protons, and it exists as a solid under standard conditions of temperature and pressure. The definition captures many familiar cases: iron, copper, gold, carbon, and sulphur are all non-decomposable, uniform in atomic number, and solid at room temperature. One might suppose that the solid state is characteristic of elements, since so many of them — indeed, the great majority — are solid metals or metalloids under ordinary conditions. Yet one must consider oxygen. Oxygen cannot be broken down into simpler substances by any chemical reaction; it is a fundamental constituent of matter. Every atom of oxygen contains eight protons, satisfying the second condition precisely. Oxygen is as indisputably an element as iron or gold. But at standard temperature and pressure — twenty-five degrees Celsius and one atmosphere — oxygen is a gas. It is not solid, not liquid, but a colourless, odourless gas composing roughly twenty-one per cent of the atmosphere. The third condition fails entirely. Nor is oxygen the sole difficulty: nitrogen, hydrogen, helium, neon, argon, fluorine, chlorine, and several other elements are likewise gaseous under standard conditions, and both bromine and mercury are liquid. The proposed definition has mistaken a statistical regularity — most elements happen to be solid at room temperature — for a defining feature. The essence of an element lies in the uniformity of its atomic number, not in its phase behaviour, and a proper characterisation must accommodate elements in any state of matter.

definition: A condition is a disease if and only if (i) it is caused by a pathogenic microorganism, (ii) it produces observable symptoms in the affected individual, and (iii) it can be transmitted from one individual to another.
conditions: it is caused by a pathogenic microorganism
it produces observable symptoms in the affected individual
it can be transmitted from one individual to another
counterexample: Consider scurvy. It produces observable symptoms — bleeding gums, fatigue, skin lesions, and if untreated, death. It is universally classified as a disease. Yet scurvy is not caused by any pathogenic microorganism; it results from a prolonged deficiency of ascorbic acid in the diet. Nor is it transmissible from one person to another. Both the first and the third conditions fail, yet scurvy is unquestionably a disease.
missing_condition: The definition requires a microbial cause and transmissibility, but many diseases — nutritional deficiencies, genetic disorders, autoimmune conditions, cancers — have no infectious aetiology and are not communicable. A proper characterisation of disease must encompass any pathological deviation from normal function, regardless of its cause.
passage: One might propose that a condition is a disease if and only if it is caused by a pathogenic microorganism, it produces observable symptoms, and it can be transmitted from one individual to another. This definition fits the paradigmatic cases of infectious illness quite well. Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, produces cough and wasting, and is transmitted through the air. Cholera is caused by Vibrio cholerae, produces violent diarrhoea, and spreads through contaminated water. The conditions appear to capture what is essential to the concept of disease. Yet one must consider scurvy. This condition was for centuries the scourge of long sea voyages, afflicting sailors deprived of fresh fruit and vegetables. Its symptoms are unmistakable: bleeding gums, loosened teeth, haemorrhaging beneath the skin, profound fatigue, and, if the deficiency persists, death. No physician would hesitate to classify scurvy as a disease. But scurvy is not caused by any microorganism. It arises from a simple nutritional deficiency — the absence of sufficient ascorbic acid in the diet. There is no bacterium, no virus, no fungus, no parasite. The first condition fails entirely. Moreover, scurvy is not transmissible; one cannot catch it from a fellow sailor, for it is not an infection but a metabolic consequence of dietary inadequacy. The third condition fails as well. The proposed definition, by restricting disease to infectious and communicable conditions, excludes an entire class of ailments that are universally recognised as diseases: not only scurvy, but rickets, pellagra, diabetes mellitus, haemophilia, and many cancers. The essence of disease lies not in microbial causation but in the disruption of normal biological function, and a sound definition must reflect this.

definition: A region constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) it contains both living organisms and non-living physical components, (ii) it receives energy primarily from sunlight through photosynthesis, and (iii) it is bounded by clearly identifiable geographical features.
conditions: it contains both living organisms and non-living physical components
it receives energy primarily from sunlight through photosynthesis
it is bounded by clearly identifiable geographical features
counterexample: Consider a deep-sea hydrothermal vent community on the ocean floor. It contains living organisms — chemosynthetic bacteria, giant tube worms, vent crabs — together with non-living components such as mineral-rich water and basalt rock. It is by every ecological criterion an ecosystem. Yet it receives essentially no energy from sunlight; photosynthesis is impossible at depths of two thousand metres or more. The primary producers are chemosynthetic bacteria that derive energy from hydrogen sulphide. The second condition fails, yet the vent community is undeniably an ecosystem.
missing_condition: The definition requires that energy be derived primarily from photosynthesis, but ecosystems may be powered by chemosynthesis or by the import of organic matter from elsewhere. A correct characterisation must specify only that energy flows through the system via trophic interactions, not that it originates from sunlight.
passage: Suppose one defines a region as constituting an ecosystem if and only if it contains both living organisms and non-living physical components, it receives energy primarily from sunlight through photosynthesis, and it is bounded by clearly identifiable geographical features. The definition seems to capture the familiar cases well enough. A temperate forest contains trees, animals, fungi, and soil; it receives energy from the sun via photosynthesis; and it may be bounded by rivers, mountain ridges, or changes in vegetation. A coral reef contains living corals, fish, and plankton alongside water and rock; it is powered by photosynthetic algae; and the reef itself forms a recognisable geographical structure. Yet one must consider the communities that flourish around deep-sea hydrothermal vents. These vents, discovered along mid-ocean ridges at depths exceeding two thousand metres, support thriving assemblages of organisms: chemosynthetic bacteria, giant tube worms reaching two metres in length, blind shrimp, and specialised crabs. The non-living components include superheated water laden with hydrogen sulphide, dissolved minerals, and volcanic rock. By any ecological standard, these vent communities are ecosystems — they exhibit trophic structure, nutrient cycling, and energy flow. But no sunlight reaches these depths. Photosynthesis is impossible. The primary producers are bacteria that oxidise hydrogen sulphide and other inorganic compounds, a process termed chemosynthesis. The second condition of the definition fails entirely. Furthermore, the boundaries of a vent community are diffuse and patchy, defined by the plume of heated water rather than by any crisp geographical feature. The third condition is at best questionable. The proposed definition has taken the most visible form of primary production — photosynthesis — and elevated it to a necessary condition, when in truth what matters is that energy enters and flows through the system, regardless of its ultimate source.

definition: A gene is a segment of DNA if and only if (i) it encodes a single polypeptide chain, (ii) it occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome, and (iii) it is inherited as an indivisible unit from parent to offspring.
conditions: it encodes a single polypeptide chain
it occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome
it is inherited as an indivisible unit from parent to offspring
counterexample: Consider the genes encoding ribosomal RNA. These segments of DNA are transcribed into RNA molecules that form essential structural and catalytic components of the ribosome. They occupy fixed chromosomal loci and are inherited from parent to offspring. By every accepted criterion, they are genes. Yet they do not encode any polypeptide chain whatsoever; their product is RNA, not protein. The first condition fails, yet ribosomal RNA genes are among the most conserved and functionally important genes in all of biology.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a gene encode a polypeptide, but many genes produce functional RNA molecules — ribosomal RNA, transfer RNA, and other non-coding RNAs — that never serve as templates for protein synthesis. A correct characterisation must allow that the product of a gene may be either a polypeptide or a functional RNA.
passage: One might propose that a gene is a segment of DNA if and only if it encodes a single polypeptide chain, it occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome, and it is inherited as an indivisible unit from parent to offspring. This definition appears to reflect the central dogma of molecular biology: DNA makes RNA makes protein. The gene for haemoglobin encodes the polypeptide chains of that protein, occupies a known locus, and is inherited in Mendelian fashion. The gene for insulin likewise encodes a polypeptide, sits at a fixed chromosomal position, and passes from parent to child. The conditions seem to capture the essence of what a gene is. Yet one must consider the genes that encode ribosomal RNA. In every known organism, substantial stretches of DNA are devoted to encoding the RNA molecules that form the structural and catalytic core of the ribosome. These ribosomal RNA genes occupy fixed loci — in humans, they are clustered on the short arms of chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21, and 22. They are inherited from parent to offspring. They are among the most highly conserved sequences in the whole of biology, preserved across billions of years of evolution. By any biological standard, they are genes. But they do not encode polypeptide chains. The product of a ribosomal RNA gene is an RNA molecule, not a protein. The RNA itself is the functional end product. The first condition of the proposed definition fails. The same difficulty arises for transfer RNA genes, small nuclear RNA genes, and a growing catalogue of non-coding RNA genes whose products carry out regulatory, structural, and catalytic roles without ever being translated into protein. The definition has taken one function of genes — protein coding — and made it the sole defining criterion, when in truth the concept of the gene must accommodate functional RNA products as well.

definition: A physical interaction constitutes a force if and only if (i) it involves direct contact between two material bodies, (ii) it produces a change in the velocity of at least one of those bodies, and (iii) it is proportional to the mass of the bodies involved.
conditions: it involves direct contact between two material bodies
it produces a change in the velocity of at least one of those bodies
it is proportional to the mass of the bodies involved
counterexample: Consider gravitational attraction between the Earth and the Moon. The Moon's velocity is continually changed — it is deflected from a straight-line path into an orbit — and the force is indeed proportional to the masses of both bodies. By every criterion of physics, gravitation is a force. Yet there is no direct contact between the Earth and the Moon. They are separated by roughly three hundred and eighty thousand kilometres of vacuum. The first condition fails, yet gravitational attraction is the paradigmatic example of a force in classical mechanics.
missing_condition: The definition requires direct contact, but forces may act at a distance — gravitational, electromagnetic, and magnetic forces all operate across empty space without material contact. A proper characterisation of force must define it in terms of its dynamical effects, not in terms of the mechanism of transmission.
passage: Suppose one defines a physical interaction as constituting a force if and only if it involves direct contact between two material bodies, it produces a change in the velocity of at least one of those bodies, and it is proportional to the mass of the bodies involved. The definition captures certain everyday instances of force tolerably well. When one pushes a box across a floor, one's hands are in contact with the box, the box accelerates, and greater mass requires a greater push. When a hammer strikes a nail, the contact is evident, the nail accelerates into the wood, and the effect depends upon the mass of the hammer. Yet one must consider gravitation. The Earth exerts a gravitational force upon the Moon, continually deflecting it from the straight-line path it would otherwise follow and holding it in an approximately elliptical orbit. This force is proportional to the product of the Earth's mass and the Moon's mass, satisfying the third condition. The Moon's velocity is constantly changing in direction, satisfying the second condition. But there is no contact between the Earth and the Moon. They are separated by some three hundred and eighty thousand kilometres of empty space. No material medium connects them; the gravitational interaction operates across a vacuum. The first condition fails entirely. Newton himself recognised this as a profound puzzle, writing that the notion of one body acting upon another at a distance through a vacuum was to him an absurdity. Yet the gravitational force is real, measurable, and precisely described by the inverse-square law. The proposed definition, by insisting upon contact, excludes gravitational attraction and, by the same reasoning, electrostatic attraction and repulsion, magnetic forces, and every other interaction that operates across empty space. The concept of force must be defined by its dynamical consequences — the production of acceleration — and not by the parochial requirement of physical contact.

definition: Two organisms belong to the same species if and only if (i) they are morphologically indistinguishable to a trained observer, (ii) they occupy the same ecological niche, and (iii) they produce viable offspring when crossed.
conditions: they are morphologically indistinguishable to a trained observer
they occupy the same ecological niche
they produce viable offspring when crossed
counterexample: Consider the phenomenon of sexual dimorphism in the bird of paradise. The male is adorned with elaborate, brightly coloured plumage, elongated tail feathers, and ornamental structures, whilst the female is plainly coloured, smaller, and altogether different in appearance. A trained observer, confronted with a male and a female without prior knowledge, might well judge them morphologically quite distinguishable. Yet they are conspecific, mating readily and producing viable, fertile offspring. The first condition fails, yet they belong to the same species.
missing_condition: The definition requires morphological indistinguishability, but members of the same species may differ dramatically in appearance owing to sexual dimorphism, developmental stage, or polymorphism. Species membership must be grounded in reproductive compatibility and shared lineage, not in superficial resemblance.
passage: One might propose that two organisms belong to the same species if and only if they are morphologically indistinguishable to a trained observer, they occupy the same ecological niche, and they produce viable offspring when crossed. This definition fits many simple cases. Two robins of the same sex, for instance, look alike, occupy the same niche, and can interbreed. Two oak trees of the same species share leaf shape, bark texture, and habitat, and they cross-pollinate readily. The conditions appear to capture species identity. Yet one must consider the birds of paradise of New Guinea. In many species of this family, the male and the female differ so profoundly in plumage, body size, and ornamentation that early naturalists sometimes classified them as separate species. The male Raggiana bird of paradise displays cascading orange flank plumes and performs elaborate courtship dances, whilst the female is a modest brown bird of quite different proportions. A trained observer, if presented with the two sexes side by side without prior instruction, might reasonably judge them morphologically distinguishable. Indeed, historically, such errors were common. Yet the male and the female are unquestionably conspecific. They mate, and the female lays fertile eggs from which healthy chicks of both sexes emerge. The first condition of the definition fails. Sexual dimorphism — the phenomenon of marked morphological difference between the sexes — is widespread in the animal kingdom, occurring in insects, fish, birds, and mammals. Any definition of species that demands morphological indistinguishability will be confounded by it. The essence of species membership must be sought not in outward resemblance but in the capacity for gene flow and shared evolutionary descent.

definition: An entity is a living organism if and only if (i) it grows by increasing in size over the course of its existence, (ii) it responds to external stimuli, and (iii) it is composed of organic carbon-based molecules.
conditions: it grows by increasing in size over the course of its existence
it responds to external stimuli
it is composed of organic carbon-based molecules
counterexample: Consider a crystal of copper sulphate placed in a saturated solution. The crystal grows by accreting additional molecules from the solution, increasing in size over time. It responds to external stimuli in a rudimentary sense — changes in temperature or concentration alter its rate of growth and may cause dissolution. And copper sulphate, whilst inorganic, might be set aside; but consider instead a crystal of urea, which is organic and carbon-based. Such a crystal grows, responds to its chemical environment, and is composed of organic molecules. Yet no one would call it alive. All three conditions are met, yet the entity is not a living organism.
missing_condition: The definition provides conditions that are not jointly sufficient for life, since non-living systems such as crystals can satisfy all three. A correct characterisation must include self-replication with heritable variation, or some analogous criterion that distinguishes living from merely growing and responsive chemical systems.
passage: Suppose one defines an entity as a living organism if and only if it grows by increasing in size, it responds to external stimuli, and it is composed of organic carbon-based molecules. The definition appears to capture familiar features of life. A seedling grows into a tree; an animal flinches from pain; all known organisms are built from carbon-based organic chemistry. The three conditions seem to delineate the living from the non-living. Yet one must consider the behaviour of certain crystals. When a seed crystal of urea — an organic, carbon-based molecule of the formula CO(NH₂)₂ — is placed in a supersaturated solution of urea in water, the crystal grows. Additional urea molecules are incorporated into the crystal lattice, and the crystal increases in size in a regular, structured manner. It responds to changes in its chemical environment: an increase in temperature may dissolve it, an increase in concentration hastens its growth, and mechanical disturbance may cause it to fracture and nucleate new crystals. Urea is an organic, carbon-based molecule; the third condition is satisfied. Here, then, is an entity that satisfies all three conditions of the proposed definition yet is manifestly not alive. It has no metabolism in the biological sense, no capacity for self-replication with heritable variation, no genetic material, no cell structure, no evolutionary lineage. The definition has identified properties that are necessary for life but has failed to include conditions sufficient to distinguish living organisms from organised but non-living chemical systems. What is wanted is a criterion that captures the distinctive capacity of living things to reproduce with variation and to undergo natural selection — a capacity that no crystal, however orderly its growth, possesses.

definition: A celestial body is a planet if and only if (i) it orbits the Sun, (ii) it has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to maintain a roughly spherical shape, and (iii) it possesses a substantial gaseous atmosphere.
conditions: it orbits the Sun
it has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to maintain a roughly spherical shape
it possesses a substantial gaseous atmosphere
counterexample: Consider the planet Mercury. It orbits the Sun in an elliptical path and has sufficient mass for self-gravity to render it approximately spherical. Mercury is one of the classical planets, known since antiquity. Yet Mercury possesses essentially no atmosphere; its exosphere is so tenuous — a trillionth the density of Earth's atmosphere — that it scarcely merits the name. The third condition fails, yet Mercury is indisputably a planet.
missing_condition: The definition requires a substantial atmosphere, but atmospheric retention is a contingent property depending on mass, temperature, and proximity to the Sun. Mercury and, to a lesser extent, Mars demonstrate that a body can be a planet without possessing a thick atmosphere. A sound definition of planet must omit atmospheric requirements.
passage: One might propose that a celestial body is a planet if and only if it orbits the Sun, it has sufficient mass for self-gravity to maintain a roughly spherical shape, and it possesses a substantial gaseous atmosphere. Earth, Venus, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune all satisfy these conditions: each orbits the Sun, each is spherical, and each has a significant atmosphere, ranging from Earth's nitrogen-oxygen envelope to Jupiter's immense hydrogen-helium mantle. The definition seems to capture what is essential to planetary status. Yet one must consider Mercury. This body has been recognised as a planet since antiquity, observed by the Babylonians and recorded in the astronomical traditions of every civilisation that studied the sky. It orbits the Sun, completing a revolution in approximately eighty-eight days. Its mass, though modest by planetary standards, is more than sufficient for self-gravity to have produced a spherical shape. Mercury is, by every accepted astronomical criterion, a planet. But Mercury has virtually no atmosphere. Its surface is exposed almost directly to the vacuum of space; what little gas surrounds it — atoms of sodium, potassium, and oxygen sputtered from the surface by the solar wind — is so sparse that it is properly termed an exosphere rather than an atmosphere. The density of this exosphere is a trillionth that of Earth's atmosphere at sea level. The third condition fails. The proposed definition has taken a property common to most planets — atmospheric retention — and elevated it to a necessary condition, when in truth it is a consequence of mass and temperature rather than a criterion of planetary status. Any adequate definition must accommodate airless bodies that satisfy the gravitational and orbital requirements.

definition: A substance is a chemical element if and only if (i) it consists of atoms all having the same number of protons, (ii) it is stable and does not undergo spontaneous radioactive decay, and (iii) it occurs naturally on the Earth.
conditions: it consists of atoms all having the same number of protons
it is stable and does not undergo spontaneous radioactive decay
it occurs naturally on the Earth
counterexample: Consider uranium. It consists of atoms all having ninety-two protons in the nucleus, satisfying the first condition. It occurs naturally on Earth in various mineral deposits. Yet uranium is radioactive; all of its isotopes undergo spontaneous decay, with half-lives ranging from microseconds to billions of years. The second condition fails, yet uranium is unquestionably a chemical element, with atomic number 92, a well-defined chemistry, and a place in the periodic table that no one disputes.
missing_condition: The definition requires stability against radioactive decay, but many elements — uranium, thorium, radium, radon, and all elements beyond bismuth — are inherently radioactive. Elemental status is determined by atomic number alone, not by nuclear stability.
passage: Suppose one defines a substance as a chemical element if and only if it consists of atoms all having the same number of protons, it is stable and does not undergo spontaneous radioactive decay, and it occurs naturally on Earth. The definition fits a great many elements admirably. Carbon has six protons, is stable, and is found abundantly in nature. Iron has twenty-six protons, is stable, and is the principal component of the Earth's core. Gold, silver, oxygen, and nitrogen all satisfy the three conditions. One might suppose that stability and natural occurrence are marks of genuine elemental status, distinguishing real elements from fleeting laboratory curiosities. Yet one must consider uranium. This substance consists of atoms all having ninety-two protons in the nucleus. It occurs naturally in the Earth's crust, concentrated in minerals such as uraninite and carnotite, and it was identified as an element by Martin Klaproth in 1789. No chemist would deny that uranium is an element. But uranium is radioactive. Every isotope of uranium undergoes spontaneous nuclear decay. Uranium-238, the most abundant isotope, has a half-life of approximately four and a half billion years, while uranium-235, the fissile isotope, has a half-life of roughly seven hundred million years. The second condition fails for uranium and fails equally for thorium, radium, radon, polonium, and every element with an atomic number above eighty-three. The proposed definition would exclude more than two dozen naturally occurring elements from the periodic table. The difficulty is that nuclear stability is a property of individual isotopes, not of elements as such, and many perfectly good elements have no stable isotopes at all. Elemental identity is a matter of proton number and nothing more.

definition: A condition constitutes a disease if and only if (i) it causes a measurable deviation from normal physiological function, (ii) it is accompanied by subjective suffering or discomfort, and (iii) it reduces the expected lifespan of the affected individual.
conditions: it causes a measurable deviation from normal physiological function
it is accompanied by subjective suffering or discomfort
it reduces the expected lifespan of the affected individual
counterexample: Consider colour blindness — specifically, red-green colour vision deficiency. It is a measurable deviation from normal physiological function: the affected individual's retinae lack certain cone photoreceptors or possess altered versions of them, and standard clinical tests readily detect the anomaly. It is classified as a disease or disorder in medical literature. Yet most individuals with red-green colour blindness experience no subjective suffering whatsoever; they are unaware of their condition until tested. Moreover, colour blindness does not reduce expected lifespan by any measurable amount. The second and third conditions both fail.
missing_condition: The definition requires subjective suffering and reduced lifespan, but many recognised diseases and disorders cause neither. Colour blindness, mild astigmatism, and many genetic conditions involve measurable physiological deviation without suffering or shortened life. A correct characterisation of disease must rest on deviation from normal function alone, without requiring symptomatic distress or mortality.
passage: One might propose that a condition constitutes a disease if and only if it causes a measurable deviation from normal physiological function, it is accompanied by subjective suffering or discomfort, and it reduces the expected lifespan of the affected individual. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases of serious illness. Tuberculosis causes measurable lung damage, produces suffering in the form of coughing and wasting, and if untreated substantially reduces lifespan. Heart disease causes measurable cardiac dysfunction, produces chest pain and breathlessness, and is a leading cause of premature death. The conditions seem to delineate the concept of disease with some precision. Yet one must consider red-green colour vision deficiency, commonly known as colour blindness. This condition is caused by the absence or alteration of specific cone photoreceptors in the retina, and it is readily detected by standardised clinical tests such as the Ishihara plates. The deviation from normal colour vision is measurable and well-characterised; the first condition is satisfied. Colour blindness is classified as a disorder in ophthalmological literature and is coded in international disease classifications. Yet the great majority of individuals with red-green colour blindness experience no subjective suffering. They may be entirely unaware of their condition until it is detected by screening, and most live full, unrestricted lives. The second condition fails. Furthermore, colour blindness has no effect whatsoever on expected lifespan; epidemiological studies reveal no increase in mortality among colour-blind individuals. The third condition fails as well. The proposed definition, by insisting upon suffering and reduced longevity, excludes a recognised medical condition from the category of disease. A sound characterisation must acknowledge that disease is fundamentally a matter of deviation from normal function, whether or not it is accompanied by distress or shortened life.

definition: A biological community and its physical environment constitute an ecosystem if and only if (i) the community contains representatives of at least three trophic levels, (ii) the system exhibits nutrient cycling between living and non-living components, and (iii) the system has persisted in a stable state for at least one century.
conditions: the community contains representatives of at least three trophic levels
the system exhibits nutrient cycling between living and non-living components
the system has persisted in a stable state for at least one century
counterexample: Consider a newly formed volcanic island, such as Surtsey off the coast of Iceland, which emerged from the sea in 1963. Within a few years, bacteria, algae, mosses, and insects colonised the bare lava. Seabirds arrived, bringing nutrients in their guano, and rudimentary nutrient cycling began. The colonising community included primary producers, herbivores, and decomposers — three trophic levels. Yet the system had persisted for only a few years, not a century. The third condition fails, yet ecologists universally regard such pioneer communities as ecosystems.
missing_condition: The definition requires persistence for a century, but ecosystems may be newly formed, transient, or frequently disturbed and still constitute genuine ecosystems. Persistence is a contingent historical feature, not a necessary criterion. An ecosystem is defined by its current ecological processes, not by its age.
passage: Suppose one defines a biological community and its physical environment as constituting an ecosystem if and only if the community contains representatives of at least three trophic levels, the system exhibits nutrient cycling between living and non-living components, and the system has persisted in a stable state for at least one century. The definition fits many well-known ecosystems. An old-growth temperate forest contains primary producers, herbivores, and predators; nutrients cycle through soil, water, and organisms; and such forests have endured for centuries or millennia. A coral reef likewise displays multiple trophic levels, nutrient cycling, and, in many cases, centuries of stability.
