One might suppose that persistence is a mark of genuine ecosystem status, distinguishing real ecosystems from ephemeral assemblages. Yet one must consider newly formed volcanic islands. When Surtsey emerged from the North Atlantic in 1963, it was a barren mass of lava and tephra. Within years, colonisation began: bacteria and cyanobacteria established themselves on the rock, mosses and lichens followed, insects arrived, and seabirds began nesting, depositing guano that enriched the soil. By the early 1970s, a rudimentary community with primary producers, herbivores, and decomposers was functioning, and nutrients were cycling between the living colonists and the volcanic substrate. Ecologists studying Surtsey regarded it as an ecosystem from an early stage — a young, simple, and rapidly changing one, but an ecosystem nonetheless. The system had persisted for less than a decade, not a century. The third condition of the definition fails entirely. The difficulty is that the definition has elevated stability and longevity to necessary conditions, when in truth ecosystems are defined by their current structure and processes: energy flow, nutrient cycling, and biotic interactions. A newly born ecosystem is no less an ecosystem for its youth, just as a newly hatched chick is no less a bird.

definition: A gene is a unit of heredity if and only if (i) it consists of a continuous, uninterrupted sequence of nucleotides on a single strand of DNA, (ii) it is transmitted from parent to offspring according to Mendelian ratios, and (iii) it specifies a single observable trait in the organism.
conditions: it consists of a continuous, uninterrupted sequence of nucleotides on a single strand of DNA
it is transmitted from parent to offspring according to Mendelian ratios
it specifies a single observable trait in the organism
counterexample: Consider the genes of higher eukaryotes, which are typically interrupted by introns — long stretches of non-coding DNA interspersed among the coding sequences (exons). The gene for human beta-globin, for instance, contains two introns that split the coding sequence into three separate exons. The gene is not a continuous, uninterrupted sequence; it is a mosaic of coding and non-coding segments. The first condition fails, yet the beta-globin gene is one of the best-characterised genes in all of molecular biology.
missing_condition: The definition requires continuity of nucleotide sequence, but eukaryotic genes are characteristically interrupted by introns. The gene as a functional unit includes both exons and introns, and continuity of coding sequence is achieved only after transcription and splicing. A proper characterisation must accommodate split genes.
passage: One might propose that a gene is a unit of heredity if and only if it consists of a continuous, uninterrupted sequence of nucleotides on a single strand of DNA, it is transmitted according to Mendelian ratios, and it specifies a single observable trait. The definition reflects the classical picture of the gene as a bead on a string — a discrete, contiguous unit carrying instructions for one character. Mendel's factors for seed colour and seed shape in peas fit this model: each occupies a definite position on a chromosome, segregates according to Mendelian ratios, and determines a single observable trait. The conditions appear to capture the concept admirably. Yet one must consider the structure of genes in higher eukaryotic organisms. When the gene for human beta-globin was sequenced, it was discovered that the coding sequence is not continuous. It is interrupted by two introns — stretches of DNA, hundreds of nucleotides in length, that do not encode any part of the beta-globin protein. The coding information is split into three separate exons, separated by these non-coding insertions. After transcription, the cell's splicing machinery removes the introns and joins the exons together to produce a continuous messenger RNA. The gene as it exists in the DNA is therefore not a continuous, uninterrupted sequence; it is a mosaic. The first condition of the definition fails. This is not an anomaly: the vast majority of genes in animals and plants are interrupted by introns, often many introns, and the total length of intronic DNA typically exceeds the coding sequence by a considerable factor. Furthermore, many genes influence multiple traits (pleiotropy), and many traits are influenced by multiple genes (polygeny), straining the third condition as well. The proposed definition reflects a prokaryotic simplicity that does not obtain in the eukaryotic world, and a proper characterisation of the gene must accommodate the complex, interrupted architecture of eukaryotic hereditary material.

definition: A force is a physical quantity if and only if (i) it acts along the line connecting the centres of two interacting bodies, (ii) it obeys Newton's third law such that action and reaction are equal and opposite, and (iii) it diminishes with the square of the distance between the bodies.
conditions: it acts along the line connecting the centres of two interacting bodies
it obeys Newton's third law such that action and reaction are equal and opposite
it diminishes with the square of the distance between the bodies
counterexample: Consider the magnetic force experienced by a charged particle moving through a magnetic field. This force, described by the Lorentz force law, acts perpendicularly to both the velocity of the particle and the direction of the magnetic field. It does not act along the line connecting any two centres of mass; it acts at right angles to the particle's motion. Moreover, the magnetic force on a moving charge does not obey a simple inverse-square law with respect to distance from the source. The first and third conditions both fail, yet the magnetic force is indisputably a force, measurable and consequential.
missing_condition: The definition restricts forces to central, inverse-square interactions, but many forces — magnetic forces, friction, tension, the strong nuclear force — do not act along the line of centres, do not obey an inverse-square law, or both. A proper characterisation must define force by its effect on motion, not by the geometry or distance-dependence of any particular interaction.
passage: Suppose one defines a force as a physical quantity if and only if it acts along the line connecting the centres of two interacting bodies, it obeys Newton's third law, and it diminishes with the square of the distance between the bodies. This definition captures gravitational and electrostatic forces rather well. The gravitational force between two point masses acts along the line joining them, the two bodies exert equal and opposite forces on each other, and the force diminishes as the inverse square of the distance. The electrostatic force between two point charges behaves identically in these respects. One might suppose that these three properties are essential to the concept of force itself. Yet one must consider magnetic forces. When a charged particle moves through a magnetic field, it experiences a force given by the Lorentz law: the force is proportional to the charge, to the speed of the particle, and to the strength of the magnetic field, and it is directed perpendicularly to both the velocity and the field. This force does not act along the line connecting the particle to the source of the magnetic field; it acts at right angles to the particle's motion. The first condition fails. Furthermore, the dependence of the magnetic force on distance is not a simple inverse-square relation; it depends on the geometry of the current distribution or magnet producing the field, and the force on a moving charge depends on the particle's velocity, a factor entirely absent from the proposed definition. The third condition fails as well. No physicist would deny that magnetic forces are genuine forces — they deflect particles in bubble chambers, confine plasmas in fusion devices, and align compass needles. The proposed definition has taken the special properties of gravitational and electrostatic forces and generalised them to all forces, when in truth the concept of force is far broader, encompassing any interaction that produces acceleration, regardless of its geometric character or distance dependence.

definition: An organism is a member of a particular species if and only if (i) its DNA sequence is at least 97 per cent identical to other members of that species, (ii) it can produce fertile offspring with other members, and (iii) it develops through the same sequence of embryonic stages as other members.
conditions: its DNA sequence is at least 97 per cent identical to other members
it can produce fertile offspring with other members
it develops through the same sequence of embryonic stages as other members
counterexample: Consider humans and chimpanzees. Their DNA sequences are approximately 98.7 per cent identical, exceeding the 97 per cent threshold. Yet humans and chimpanzees are universally recognised as distinct species; they cannot interbreed, their developmental sequences diverge significantly after early embryogenesis, and they differ profoundly in morphology, cognition, and behaviour. The first condition is satisfied between species that are manifestly not conspecific.
missing_condition: The definition uses a fixed percentage of DNA identity as a necessary condition, but closely related yet distinct species may exceed any arbitrary threshold of sequence similarity. Species boundaries are determined by reproductive isolation and evolutionary independence, not by a percentage cutoff in sequence identity.
passage: One might propose that an organism is a member of a particular species if and only if its DNA sequence is at least 97 per cent identical to other members of that species, it can produce fertile offspring with them, and it develops through the same embryonic stages. The definition attempts to combine molecular, reproductive, and developmental criteria into a comprehensive characterisation. For many cases, it works passably: two dogs of different breeds share well over 97 per cent of their DNA, interbreed freely, and develop through the same embryonic stages. Two oak trees of the same species satisfy all three conditions. Yet one must consider the relationship between humans and chimpanzees. Comparative genomic studies have revealed that the DNA sequences of Homo sapiens and Pan troglodytes are approximately 98.7 per cent identical — well above the 97 per cent threshold stipulated by the definition. The first condition is thus satisfied between humans and chimpanzees. But no biologist classifies humans and chimpanzees as members of the same species. They cannot produce fertile offspring; indeed, no viable human-chimpanzee hybrid has ever been documented. Their embryonic development, whilst similar in the earliest stages as von Baer's observations would predict, diverges significantly in later stages, producing organisms of profoundly different morphology and neurological complexity. The second and third conditions fail, but the critical lesson is that the first condition — the DNA similarity threshold — succeeds across a species boundary. Any fixed percentage of sequence identity, whether 97 or 99 per cent, will either exclude genuinely conspecific populations that happen to be genetically divergent or include closely related but reproductively isolated species. The difficulty is not merely one of calibration; it is conceptual. Species identity is not a matter of sequence arithmetic but of reproductive cohesion and evolutionary fate.

definition: An entity is alive if and only if (i) it maintains homeostasis by regulating its internal chemical environment, (ii) it possesses a genome composed of DNA, and (iii) it obtains energy through cellular respiration.
conditions: it maintains homeostasis by regulating its internal chemical environment
it possesses a genome composed of DNA
it obtains energy through cellular respiration
counterexample: Consider an obligately fermentative anaerobic bacterium such as Clostridium tetani. It is alive by every accepted biological criterion: it grows, reproduces, maintains homeostasis, and possesses a DNA genome. Yet it does not obtain energy through cellular respiration in the standard sense — it lacks an electron transport chain and obtains energy exclusively through fermentation. The third condition fails, yet the organism is indisputably alive.
missing_condition: The definition requires cellular respiration as an energy-obtaining mechanism, but many organisms — obligate fermenters, certain archaea, and photosynthetic organisms in the dark — obtain energy through alternative metabolic pathways. A correct characterisation of life must allow for any metabolic strategy that sustains the organism.
passage: Suppose one defines an entity as alive if and only if it maintains homeostasis, it possesses a genome composed of DNA, and it obtains energy through cellular respiration. The definition captures the familiar cases of animal and plant life admirably. A mammalian cell regulates its internal pH, ion concentrations, and temperature; it possesses a DNA genome in its nucleus; and it generates ATP through oxidative phosphorylation in its mitochondria. A green plant cell likewise maintains homeostasis, has a DNA genome, and carries out cellular respiration in addition to photosynthesis. The conditions seem to delineate the living from the non-living with reasonable precision. Yet one must consider the obligately anaerobic bacterium Clostridium tetani, the causative agent of tetanus. This organism is manifestly alive: it grows in anaerobic environments, reproduces by binary fission, maintains the chemical composition of its cytoplasm within narrow limits, and possesses a DNA genome of some 2.8 million base pairs. It is a textbook example of a living organism. But Clostridium tetani does not carry out cellular respiration. It lacks the cytochrome enzymes and the electron transport chain that are the hallmarks of respiratory metabolism. Instead, it obtains all of its energy through fermentation — the anaerobic breakdown of organic substrates without the use of an external electron acceptor. The third condition of the definition fails. Nor is this an isolated case: many anaerobic bacteria and archaea, thriving in environments devoid of oxygen, obtain energy through fermentation, methanogenesis, or other non-respiratory pathways. The proposed definition has taken one metabolic strategy — aerobic respiration — and elevated it to a criterion of life itself, when in truth life has evolved a remarkable diversity of energy-obtaining mechanisms, and any adequate definition must accommodate them all.

definition: A celestial body is a planet if and only if (i) it orbits a star, (ii) it has a nearly circular orbit with an eccentricity of less than 0.1, and (iii) its orbital plane is aligned within five degrees of the star's equatorial plane.
conditions: it orbits a star
it has a nearly circular orbit with an eccentricity of less than 0.1
its orbital plane is aligned within five degrees of the star's equatorial plane
counterexample: Consider the planet Mercury. It orbits the Sun and is one of the eight recognised planets of the solar system. Yet Mercury's orbital eccentricity is approximately 0.206 — more than double the threshold of 0.1 stipulated by the definition. Furthermore, Mercury's orbital inclination to the Sun's equatorial plane is roughly 3.4 degrees, which satisfies the third condition, but the failure of the second condition alone is sufficient to exclude Mercury from planetary status under this definition. The second condition fails, yet Mercury is undeniably a planet.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an arbitrary eccentricity threshold, but orbital eccentricity is a contingent dynamical property that varies over time and bears no necessary relation to planetary status. Mercury, Mars, and many confirmed exoplanets have eccentricities exceeding 0.1. A proper definition must not restrict orbital shape.
passage: One might propose that a celestial body is a planet if and only if it orbits a star, its orbit is nearly circular with an eccentricity below 0.1, and its orbital plane lies within five degrees of the star's equatorial plane. This definition captures several solar system planets rather well. Venus has an orbital eccentricity of 0.007 and a low inclination. Earth's eccentricity is 0.017. Neptune's is 0.009. These planets move in nearly circular, well-aligned orbits, and the definition seems to capture an orderly, planetary character. Yet one must consider Mercury, the innermost planet of the solar system. Mercury has been recognised as a planet since antiquity; its transits across the disc of the Sun were predicted and observed with great precision, and its anomalous perihelion precession provided one of the early confirmations of general relativity. No astronomer questions its planetary status. But Mercury's orbit is markedly eccentric. Its eccentricity is approximately 0.206, meaning its distance from the Sun varies from about 46 million kilometres at perihelion to 70 million kilometres at aphelion. This value exceeds the proposed threshold of 0.1 by a factor of two. The second condition fails decisively. Mars, too, has an eccentricity of approximately 0.093, perilously close to the threshold and at times exceeding it owing to gravitational perturbations. Among confirmed exoplanets, highly eccentric orbits are commonplace; many gas giants orbit their host stars on paths with eccentricities of 0.3, 0.5, or even higher. The proposed definition would exclude a substantial fraction of known planets from the category. The difficulty is that orbital eccentricity is a dynamical property shaped by gravitational interactions, not an intrinsic quality of the body itself, and any sound definition of planet must rest upon the body's own characteristics rather than upon the contingent shape of its orbit.

definition: A substance is a chemical element if and only if (i) all of its atoms have the same atomic number, (ii) it has a fixed, definite melting point, and (iii) it can form compounds by combining with other elements through chemical bonds.
conditions: all of its atoms have the same atomic number
it has a fixed, definite melting point
it can form compounds by combining with other elements through chemical bonds
counterexample: Consider helium. All helium atoms have atomic number 2, satisfying the first condition. Helium is unquestionably a chemical element. Yet helium does not form compounds with other elements under ordinary conditions; it is a noble gas whose closed electron shell renders it chemically inert. No stable helium compound has ever been prepared under standard conditions. The third condition fails, yet helium occupies its rightful place in the periodic table as element number 2.
missing_condition: The definition requires the capacity to form chemical compounds, but several noble gases — helium, neon, and to a considerable extent argon — are chemically inert and form no stable compounds under ordinary conditions. Elemental status depends on atomic number alone, not on chemical reactivity.
passage: Suppose one defines a substance as a chemical element if and only if all of its atoms have the same atomic number, it has a fixed melting point, and it can form compounds by combining with other elements through chemical bonds. The definition captures the familiar elements well. Carbon has atomic number 6, a definite melting point of approximately 3550 degrees Celsius, and forms an enormous variety of compounds. Iron has atomic number 26, melts at 1538 degrees Celsius, and combines readily with oxygen and other elements. The conditions appear to characterise the concept of a chemical element with tolerable precision. Yet one must consider helium. This substance consists of atoms all having atomic number 2 — two protons in the nucleus. It is listed as the second element in the periodic table, discovered spectroscopically in the Sun before it was isolated on Earth. No chemist questions its elemental status. But helium does not form compounds with other elements under any ordinary conditions. Its two electrons fill the 1s orbital completely, and the resulting closed-shell configuration is so stable that the energy required to form a chemical bond exceeds any stabilisation the bond could provide. No stable helium compound has been prepared at standard temperature and pressure. Neon, with atomic number 10, presents the same difficulty: it is a chemical element but forms no known compounds whatsoever. The third condition of the definition fails for these elements. Furthermore, the requirement of a fixed melting point is problematic for elements that exist in amorphous forms — amorphous selenium, for instance, softens gradually over a range of temperatures rather than melting sharply at a single point. The proposed definition has conflated chemical reactivity with elemental identity, when in truth an element is defined solely by its atomic number, regardless of whether it participates in chemical bonding.

definition: A condition is a disease if and only if (i) it has a single identifiable cause, (ii) it follows a predictable clinical course with defined stages, and (iii) it responds to a specific treatment or therapy.
conditions: it has a single identifiable cause
it follows a predictable clinical course with defined stages
it responds to a specific treatment or therapy
counterexample: Consider essential hypertension — chronically elevated blood pressure without an identifiable secondary cause. It is classified as a disease by every major medical authority and is one of the most prevalent diseases in the world. Yet essential hypertension has no single identifiable cause; it arises from the interaction of multiple genetic and environmental factors. Its clinical course is highly variable among individuals, and whilst treatments exist, there is no single specific therapy — management typically involves combinations of lifestyle changes and medications from several drug classes. All three conditions are at best dubious, yet essential hypertension is indisputably a disease.
missing_condition: The definition assumes a monocausal, deterministic model of disease, but most diseases — hypertension, diabetes, cancer, depression — are multifactorial, variable in course, and managed rather than cured by any single therapy. A sound definition must accommodate multifactorial aetiology and clinical heterogeneity.
passage: One might propose that a condition is a disease if and only if it has a single identifiable cause, it follows a predictable clinical course with defined stages, and it responds to a specific treatment. This definition captures certain infectious diseases tolerably well. Malaria has a single cause — infection by Plasmodium parasites — follows a recognisable clinical course of febrile paroxysms, and responds to specific antimalarial drugs. Tetanus is caused by the toxin of Clostridium tetani, follows a predictable course of muscular rigidity and spasm, and responds to antitoxin and supportive care. The conditions seem to capture the medical concept of disease. Yet one must consider essential hypertension, the condition of chronically elevated arterial blood pressure in the absence of any identifiable secondary cause. Essential hypertension affects approximately one billion people worldwide and is a leading risk factor for stroke, heart failure, and kidney disease. Every major medical organisation classifies it as a disease. But it has no single identifiable cause. Genetic studies have implicated hundreds of genes, each contributing a small effect, and environmental factors — dietary salt intake, physical inactivity, chronic stress, obesity — interact with genetic predisposition in ways that are still incompletely understood. The first condition fails. The clinical course of essential hypertension is highly variable: some individuals progress rapidly to end-organ damage, whilst others maintain elevated pressures for decades without apparent harm. There are no sharply defined stages. The second condition fails, or at best holds only loosely. Treatment involves not a single specific therapy but a combination of lifestyle modifications and medications chosen from multiple drug classes — diuretics, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, ACE inhibitors — tailored to the individual patient. The third condition fails as well. The proposed definition reflects an idealised model of disease that obtains for some infectious illnesses but not for the chronic, multifactorial conditions that constitute the majority of the global disease burden.

definition: A natural system constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) it occupies a terrestrial environment, (ii) it contains primary producers that are vascular plants, and (iii) energy flows through the system via a food web with at least three trophic levels.
conditions: it occupies a terrestrial environment
it contains primary producers that are vascular plants
energy flows through the system via a food web with at least three trophic levels
counterexample: Consider a coral reef. It is one of the most biodiverse and productive ecosystems on Earth, containing hundreds of species interacting through complex food webs with numerous trophic levels. Yet a coral reef does not occupy a terrestrial environment — it is entirely marine. Furthermore, its primary producers are not vascular plants but zooxanthellae (symbiotic dinoflagellate algae) and other photosynthetic microorganisms. The first and second conditions both fail, yet a coral reef is a paradigmatic ecosystem.
missing_condition: The definition restricts ecosystems to terrestrial systems with vascular-plant primary producers, but aquatic, marine, and freshwater ecosystems are no less genuine. Primary production may be carried out by algae, cyanobacteria, or chemosynthetic organisms. A correct characterisation must be agnostic as to environment type and primary producer identity.
passage: Suppose one defines a natural system as constituting an ecosystem if and only if it occupies a terrestrial environment, it contains primary producers that are vascular plants, and energy flows through a food web with at least three trophic levels. The definition captures many familiar ecosystems well. A temperate deciduous forest occupies land, its primary producers are trees and herbaceous plants — all vascular — and energy flows through herbivores, predators, and decomposers. A tropical grassland is terrestrial, grasses are its vascular primary producers, and multiple trophic levels sustain the community. The conditions seem to characterise ecosystems with reasonable accuracy. Yet one must consider the coral reef. Coral reefs are among the most productive and species-rich systems in the biosphere, sometimes called the rainforests of the sea. They support intricate food webs: photosynthetic algae are consumed by herbivorous fish, which are consumed by predatory fish, which are consumed by sharks. Energy flows through multiple trophic levels with a complexity rivalling any terrestrial system. Ecologists universally regard coral reefs as ecosystems. But the coral reef does not occupy a terrestrial environment. It is submerged beneath the ocean, built upon the calcareous skeletons of coral animals in shallow tropical seas. The first condition fails. Furthermore, the primary producers of a coral reef are not vascular plants. They are zooxanthellae — tiny photosynthetic dinoflagellates living symbiotically within the coral tissue — together with filamentous algae, coralline algae, and phytoplankton. None of these is a vascular plant. The second condition fails as well. The proposed definition has been constructed from terrestrial examples and generalised without warrant, excluding the vast aquatic realm from the concept of ecosystem. Any adequate definition must recognise that ecosystems exist wherever communities of organisms interact with their physical environment, on land or in water, with any type of primary producer.

definition: A gene is a hereditary unit if and only if (i) it produces a single, invariant protein product in all cells of the organism, (ii) it is located on a nuclear chromosome, and (iii) its expression is constant and does not vary with environmental conditions.
conditions: it produces a single, invariant protein product in all cells of the organism
it is located on a nuclear chromosome
its expression is constant and does not vary with environmental conditions
counterexample: Consider the genes encoded in mitochondrial DNA. Human mitochondria contain a circular DNA molecule of approximately 16,500 base pairs encoding 13 protein-coding genes, 22 transfer RNA genes, and 2 ribosomal RNA genes. These genes are inherited maternally and are essential for oxidative phosphorylation. They are indisputably genes. Yet they are not located on any nuclear chromosome; they reside in the mitochondrial matrix, outside the nucleus entirely. The second condition fails, yet mitochondrial genes are among the most extensively studied in all of genetics.
missing_condition: The definition requires a nuclear chromosomal location, but genes may reside in mitochondria, chloroplasts, or plasmids. Furthermore, the requirement of invariant expression and a single protein product ignores alternative splicing and regulated gene expression. A proper characterisation must accommodate extranuclear genes and variable expression.
passage: One might propose that a gene is a hereditary unit if and only if it produces a single, invariant protein product in all cells, it is located on a nuclear chromosome, and its expression is constant regardless of environmental conditions. The definition reflects a simplified picture of gene function: one gene, one protein, one location, one level of activity. For certain housekeeping genes — those encoding ribosomal proteins or glycolytic enzymes, for instance — this picture may be approximately correct. The gene is on a chromosome, it encodes a single protein, and it is expressed in most cells at a roughly constant level. Yet one must consider the genes of the mitochondrial genome. Every human cell contains hundreds of mitochondria, each harbouring multiple copies of a circular DNA molecule some 16,500 base pairs in length. This genome encodes 13 proteins essential for the electron transport chain, along with the RNA molecules needed for their translation. These mitochondrial genes are inherited maternally, they are expressed in response to the cell's energetic demands, and they are among the most intensively studied genes in human genetics. Disease-causing mutations in mitochondrial genes produce well-characterised clinical syndromes. No geneticist would deny that these are genuine genes. But they are not located on any nuclear chromosome. They reside in the mitochondrial matrix, physically separate from the nucleus and its chromosomes. The second condition of the definition fails. Moreover, the first condition — invariant protein product — is violated by the widespread phenomenon of alternative splicing, whereby a single nuclear gene can produce multiple different protein variants depending on the cell type. The Drosophila Dscam gene, for example, can generate over 38,000 distinct mRNA variants. And the third condition fails for virtually every gene whose expression is regulated — which is to say, nearly all of them. The proposed definition mistakes a caricature for a characterisation.

definition: A force exists between two bodies if and only if (i) there is a material medium through which the interaction is transmitted, (ii) the force is conservative, meaning the work done by the force around any closed path is zero, and (iii) the force can be derived from a scalar potential function.
conditions: there is a material medium through which the interaction is transmitted
the force is conservative
the force can be derived from a scalar potential function
counterexample: Consider the force of kinetic friction between a block sliding across a rough surface. This force unquestionably exists — it decelerates the block and converts kinetic energy into heat. There is direct contact between the surfaces, so one might argue a medium exists. Yet kinetic friction is a non-conservative force: the work done by friction around a closed path is not zero but strictly negative, as energy is irreversibly dissipated as heat. It cannot be derived from a scalar potential function. The second and third conditions fail, yet friction is a real, measurable force of great practical importance.
missing_condition: The definition restricts forces to conservative interactions derivable from scalar potentials, but dissipative forces such as friction, drag, and viscous resistance are genuine forces that do not satisfy these criteria. A proper characterisation of force must include both conservative and non-conservative interactions.
passage: Suppose one defines a force as existing between two bodies if and only if there is a material medium transmitting the interaction, the force is conservative, and it can be derived from a scalar potential function. This definition captures gravitational and electrostatic forces in certain formulations: the gravitational force between two masses can be derived from a gravitational potential, the work done around any closed path is zero, and in Newton's time one might have postulated an aether as the transmitting medium. The electrostatic force can likewise be derived from the Coulomb potential. The definition has a certain mathematical elegance. Yet one must consider friction. When a wooden block slides across a rough table, the surface of the table exerts a force on the block opposing its motion. This frictional force is real and consequential — it brings the block to rest, and the kinetic energy of the block is converted into thermal energy of the surfaces. Every physicist recognises friction as a force. But friction is not conservative. If one pushes the block around a closed path, returning it to its starting position, the work done by friction is not zero; it is negative, equal in magnitude to the frictional force multiplied by the total path length. Energy has been irreversibly dissipated as heat, and the block's kinetic energy at the end of the circuit is less than at the beginning (or the pushing agent has done positive work throughout). Friction cannot be derived from any scalar potential function; there is no function whose gradient yields the frictional force. The second and third conditions fail. The proposed definition captures only the mathematically well-behaved, conservative forces and excludes the dissipative forces that pervade everyday mechanics. Drag, viscous resistance, and air resistance all present the same difficulty. A complete characterisation of force must encompass non-conservative interactions, for they are no less real than the tidy conservative forces from which potentials may be derived.

definition: A condition is a disease if and only if (i) it arises from a genetic mutation, (ii) it is present from birth, and (iii) it produces progressive deterioration of bodily function over the lifetime of the affected individual.
conditions: it arises from a genetic mutation
it is present from birth
it produces progressive deterioration of bodily function over the lifetime of the affected individual
counterexample: Consider influenza. It is universally classified as a disease — indeed, it is among the most common infectious diseases in the world. It produces fever, myalgia, respiratory distress, and in severe cases death. Yet influenza does not arise from any genetic mutation in the patient; it is caused by infection with an RNA virus. It is not present from birth but is acquired at any age. And it does not produce progressive lifelong deterioration; in most cases it resolves within one to two weeks. All three conditions fail, yet influenza is indisputably a disease.
missing_condition: The definition restricts disease to congenital genetic conditions with progressive courses, but the majority of diseases — infections, injuries, nutritional deficiencies, autoimmune disorders — are acquired, non-genetic in origin, and often episodic or self-limiting. A sound characterisation must encompass any pathological deviation from normal function, regardless of origin, onset, or temporal course.
passage: One might propose that a condition is a disease if and only if it arises from a genetic mutation, it is present from birth, and it produces progressive deterioration of bodily function over the lifetime of the affected individual. This definition fits certain hereditary conditions well. Cystic fibrosis arises from mutations in the CFTR gene, is present from birth, and produces progressive pulmonary and digestive deterioration. Huntington's disease arises from a trinucleotide repeat expansion, is genetically present from conception, and causes progressive neurological decline. The conditions seem to capture the essence of disease. Yet one must consider influenza. This ailment afflicts millions of people every year and is classified as a disease by every medical authority in the world. Its symptoms — high fever, headache, muscle pain, cough, and prostration — are familiar to virtually every adult. In severe cases, particularly among the elderly or immunocompromised, it causes pneumonia and death. No physician would hesitate to call influenza a disease. But influenza does not arise from any genetic mutation in the patient. It is caused by infection with the influenza virus, an RNA virus transmitted by respiratory droplets. The first condition fails. It is not present from birth; a person acquires influenza at any point during life, sometimes repeatedly. The second condition fails. And it does not produce progressive lifelong deterioration; in the vast majority of cases, the illness resolves within seven to fourteen days, leaving no permanent damage. The third condition fails. All three conditions of the proposed definition fail for one of the most common diseases known to medicine. The definition has been constructed from a narrow class of genetic disorders and generalised without justification, excluding the entire domain of infectious, environmental, and acquired diseases. A proper characterisation of disease must be broad enough to encompass any condition that disrupts normal biological function, irrespective of its cause, timing of onset, or temporal trajectory.

definition: A population P constitutes a distinct biological species if and only if (i) the members of P share a unique and stable set of morphological characters that distinguish them from all other populations, (ii) the members of P occupy a single, continuous geographical range, and (iii) the members of P do not hybridise with members of any other population under natural conditions.
conditions: the members of P share a unique and stable set of morphological characters that distinguish them from all other populations
the members of P occupy a single, continuous geographical range
the members of P do not hybridise with members of any other population under natural conditions
counterexample: Consider the case of the so-called sibling species of the genus Drosophila — fruit flies that are morphologically indistinguishable even to experienced entomologists yet are reproductively isolated from one another and possess distinct chromosome arrangements. They share no unique morphological characters that set them apart; the distinguishing features are chromosomal and behavioural, not visible. The first condition fails, yet each population is a distinct species.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that species must be distinguishable by stable morphological characters, but sibling species demonstrate that reproductive isolation and genetic divergence may define species boundaries even where external morphology offers no diagnostic differences. A proper characterisation must allow for cryptic species whose distinctness resides in their genetics and reproductive behaviour rather than in their outward form.
passage: One might propose that a population constitutes a distinct biological species if and only if its members share a unique and stable set of morphological characters distinguishing them from all other populations, they occupy a single continuous geographical range, and they do not hybridise with members of any other population under natural conditions. The definition has considerable appeal, for it appears to capture what the working naturalist looks for when identifying species in the field: a characteristic form, a definite range, and reproductive separation from neighbouring populations. The common house sparrow, for instance, is distinguished from the tree sparrow by well-known plumage differences, occupies a broad but identifiable range, and does not ordinarily interbreed with its congener. Yet one must attend to the phenomenon of sibling species, or cryptic species, as they have been termed. In the genus Drosophila, there exist populations of fruit flies that are, to all outward appearances, identical. The most skilled entomologist, armed with the finest microscope, cannot reliably distinguish members of one population from members of another on the basis of morphological characters alone. The differences between them are chromosomal, behavioural, and genetic — differences that become apparent only when one examines their karyotypes or attempts to cross them in the laboratory. The first condition of the definition fails outright: these populations do not share a unique set of morphological characters that distinguish them, for they share the same morphological characters with one another. Yet they are distinct species by every meaningful biological criterion, for they are reproductively isolated and follow independent evolutionary trajectories. The definition has mistaken the most conspicuous mark of species distinctness — visible morphological difference — for an essential condition, when in truth species may diverge in ways that leave no trace upon their external form.

definition: An entity E is alive if and only if (i) E grows by the internal addition of material to its structure, (ii) E responds to external stimuli in a manner that promotes its continued existence, and (iii) E possesses a determinate lifespan terminating in death.
conditions: E grows by the internal addition of material to its structure
E responds to external stimuli in a manner that promotes its continued existence
E possesses a determinate lifespan terminating in death
counterexample: Consider the bacterium Escherichia coli. It is alive by every accepted criterion: it metabolises, it reproduces, it evolves. Yet individual bacterial cells do not possess a determinate lifespan terminating in death in the ordinary sense. A bacterium divides by binary fission, producing two daughter cells; it does not age and die as a metazoan does. In favourable conditions, the lineage persists indefinitely through successive divisions. The third condition fails, for there is no natural death of senescence, yet the organism is indisputably alive.
missing_condition: The definition imposes a metazoan model of mortality upon all life, but many organisms — bacteria, certain protists, vegetatively propagating plants — do not undergo senescence or programmed death. What is wanted is a characterisation that does not presuppose the life-cycle structure of complex multicellular organisms.
passage: Suppose one holds that an entity is alive if and only if it grows by the internal addition of material to its structure, it responds to external stimuli in a manner that promotes its continued existence, and it possesses a determinate lifespan terminating in death. The definition captures much of what we associate with living things. A tree grows by adding wood to its trunk, responds to the direction of light by bending its branches, and eventually dies after some span of years. A horse grows from foal to adult, flees from predators, and dies of old age. One might think these three features — growth, responsiveness, and mortality — jointly characterise the living. Yet one must consider the common intestinal bacterium Escherichia coli. This organism is alive in the fullest biological sense: it takes in nutrients, synthesises proteins, replicates its chromosome, and divides to produce two daughter cells. It responds to chemical gradients, swimming towards nutrients and away from noxious substances. But it does not possess a determinate lifespan terminating in death. When a bacterium divides, neither daughter cell is the corpse of the parent; each is a living continuation. There is no senescence, no ageing, no natural death in the manner of a mammal or a bird. In the absence of external insult, the lineage continues indefinitely through successive divisions. The third condition of the definition fails entirely. The definition has projected the life-cycle of complex animals and plants onto all living things, yet the microbial world — which encompasses the vast majority of living organisms on the Earth — does not conform to this pattern. A satisfactory characterisation of life must accommodate organisms for which death, in the familiar sense, simply does not occur.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a single star in a nearly circular path, (ii) B possesses a substantial gaseous atmosphere, and (iii) B has a mass sufficient to generate a magnetic field.
conditions: B orbits a single star in a nearly circular path
B possesses a substantial gaseous atmosphere
B has a mass sufficient to generate a magnetic field
counterexample: Consider the planet Mercury. It orbits the Sun and is classified as a planet by every astronomical authority. Yet its orbit is markedly eccentric — more so than that of any other planet in the solar system — so that the description 'nearly circular' fits it poorly. Mercury possesses only the thinnest of exospheres, not a substantial gaseous atmosphere in any meaningful sense. And Mercury does possess a weak magnetic field, but its atmosphere is essentially absent. The second condition fails decisively, yet Mercury is unquestionably a planet.
missing_condition: The definition requires a substantial gaseous atmosphere, but planetary status does not depend upon atmospheric retention. Mercury and, in a different fashion, the Moon-sized bodies that are not planets, show that atmosphere is a contingent property determined by mass, solar proximity, and magnetic shielding rather than a defining feature of planethood.
passage: One might propose that a celestial body is a planet if and only if it orbits a single star in a nearly circular path, it possesses a substantial gaseous atmosphere, and it has a mass sufficient to generate a magnetic field. The definition fits the most prominent members of the solar system tolerably well. The Earth orbits the Sun in a nearly circular path, possesses a substantial nitrogen-oxygen atmosphere, and generates a dipolar magnetic field by means of convective motions in its liquid iron core. Jupiter's orbit is approximately circular, its atmosphere is enormously thick, and its magnetic field is the strongest of any planet in the solar system. One might be tempted to generalise from these cases. Yet one must consider Mercury. This small, dense body orbits the Sun at a mean distance of roughly fifty-eight million kilometres. Its orbit is the most eccentric of any planet, with an eccentricity of approximately 0.21 — appreciably less circular than that of the Earth or Jupiter. More importantly, Mercury possesses no substantial gaseous atmosphere. Its surface is exposed to the vacuum of space, and what little gas surrounds it — a tenuous exosphere of sodium, potassium, and other elements — is continually stripped away by the solar wind and replenished by outgassing. The second condition fails entirely. No one would deny that Mercury is a planet, yet it lacks the atmospheric envelope that the definition demands. The definition has taken a feature common to many planets — the possession of a thick atmosphere — and elevated it to a necessary condition, when in truth atmosphere is a consequence of size, composition, temperature, and proximity to the parent star, not a defining characteristic of planetary status.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, (ii) S possesses a single, fixed set of physical properties — melting point, boiling point, density, and colour — that uniquely identify it, and (iii) S occurs naturally in the Earth's crust.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means
S possesses a single, fixed set of physical properties — melting point, boiling point, density, and colour — that uniquely identify it
S occurs naturally in the Earth's crust
counterexample: Consider the element technetium, with atomic number 43. It is a chemical element in every sense: it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, and it occupies a definite place in the periodic table. Yet technetium does not occur naturally in the Earth's crust in any appreciable quantity; it is produced artificially in nuclear reactors and particle accelerators. The third condition fails, yet technetium is indisputably a chemical element.
missing_condition: The definition requires natural occurrence in the Earth's crust, but many elements — technetium, promethium, and all the transuranic elements — are synthetic, produced only by human artifice. Elemental status is determined by atomic number and the indivisibility of the atom by chemical means, not by whether nature happens to have produced the substance in terrestrial conditions.
passage: One might define a chemical element as a substance that cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, that possesses a single fixed set of physical properties — melting point, boiling point, density, and colour — uniquely identifying it, and that occurs naturally in the Earth's crust. This definition appears to capture the classical understanding of an element rather well. Gold cannot be decomposed by chemical reactions, possesses a characteristic melting point, density, and colour, and is found in nature. Iron, copper, and sulphur likewise satisfy all three conditions. One might suppose that natural occurrence is part of what it is to be a genuine element, distinguishing real elements from hypothetical or fictitious ones. Yet one must consider technetium. This element, bearing atomic number 43, was the first element to be produced artificially, synthesised in 1937 by the bombardment of molybdenum with deuterons. It cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means; it is a true element, occupying its proper place in the periodic table between molybdenum and ruthenium. But technetium does not occur naturally in the Earth's crust in any appreciable quantity. Its isotopes are all radioactive, with half-lives too short for primordial technetium to have survived to the present day. Every sample of technetium that exists on Earth has been produced in a nuclear reactor or a cyclotron. The third condition of the definition fails. The same difficulty arises for promethium, for neptunium, and for all the transuranic elements. The definition has confused a contingent fact about certain elements — that they happen to be found in nature — with a necessary condition of elemental status, when in truth what makes a substance an element is its atomic constitution, not the circumstances of its provenance.

definition: A condition C constitutes a disease if and only if (i) C is caused by an identifiable pathogenic agent such as a bacterium, virus, or fungus, (ii) C produces observable symptoms in the affected organism, and (iii) C is transmissible from one organism to another.
conditions: C is caused by an identifiable pathogenic agent such as a bacterium, virus, or fungus
C produces observable symptoms in the affected organism
C is transmissible from one organism to another
counterexample: Consider scurvy. It is a disease in every ordinary and medical sense of the term: it produces severe symptoms — bleeding gums, loosening teeth, haemorrhages beneath the skin, extreme fatigue, and eventually death if untreated. Yet scurvy is not caused by any pathogenic agent; it is caused by a deficiency of ascorbic acid in the diet. And it is not transmissible from one person to another; one cannot catch scurvy from a sufferer. The first and third conditions fail, yet scurvy is unquestionably a disease.
missing_condition: The definition restricts disease to infectious conditions caused by pathogens, but many diseases — nutritional deficiencies, genetic disorders, degenerative conditions — have no pathogenic cause and are not communicable. A proper characterisation must encompass any condition that impairs the normal functioning of the organism, regardless of its aetiology.
passage: One might propose that a condition constitutes a disease if and only if it is caused by an identifiable pathogenic agent such as a bacterium, virus, or fungus, it produces observable symptoms in the affected organism, and it is transmissible from one organism to another. The definition captures the paradigm of infectious disease admirably. Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, produces cough, fever, and wasting, and is transmitted through the air. Malaria is caused by Plasmodium parasites, produces periodic fevers and anaemia, and is transmitted by mosquitoes. Smallpox, cholera, and typhus all satisfy the three conditions. One might suppose that the germ theory of disease has revealed the essential nature of disease as such: a condition caused by a living pathogen and spread from host to host. Yet one must consider scurvy. This condition was the scourge of long sea voyages from the sixteenth to the eighteenth century, killing more sailors than storms and battle combined. Its symptoms are unmistakable and devastating: the gums swell and bleed, the teeth loosen and fall out, old wounds reopen, haemorrhages appear beneath the skin, and the sufferer descends into a lethargy from which, without treatment, he does not recover. Scurvy is a disease by every medical criterion. Yet it is caused by no pathogenic agent. Its cause is purely nutritional — a prolonged deficiency of ascorbic acid, or vitamin C, in the diet. No bacterium, no virus, no fungus is responsible. And scurvy is not transmissible; one cannot contract it from contact with a sufferer, only from an inadequate diet. The first and third conditions fail entirely. The definition has generalised from the infectious diseases and mistaken the features of that important but limited class for the features of disease as such. Rickets, pellagra, diabetes, and the hereditary anaemias present analogous difficulties.

definition: A natural system S constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) S contains a diversity of species drawn from multiple trophic levels, (ii) S occupies a fixed and clearly bounded geographical area, and (iii) S has persisted in a state of equilibrium for a prolonged period.
conditions: S contains a diversity of species drawn from multiple trophic levels
S occupies a fixed and clearly bounded geographical area
S has persisted in a state of equilibrium for a prolonged period
counterexample: Consider the ecosystem that develops upon a newly cooled lava flow in a volcanic archipelago. In its earliest stages, this system may contain only a handful of colonising species — lichens, mosses, a few wind-dispersed insects — drawn from one or two trophic levels at most. It occupies an area whose boundaries shift as the lava field expands or contracts. And it is not in equilibrium; it is in a state of rapid ecological succession, changing in composition from year to year. All three conditions fail or are only marginally satisfied, yet the system is an ecosystem — a functioning assemblage of organisms interacting with their physical environment.
missing_condition: The definition requires species diversity, fixed boundaries, and equilibrium, but ecosystems may be species-poor, spatially dynamic, and in a state of continuous flux. What characterises an ecosystem is the interaction of organisms with one another and with their abiotic environment, not the attainment of any particular level of diversity or stability.
passage: One might propose that a natural system constitutes an ecosystem if and only if it contains a diversity of species drawn from multiple trophic levels, it occupies a fixed and clearly bounded geographical area, and it has persisted in a state of equilibrium for a prolonged period. The definition appears to describe many familiar ecosystems rather well. A mature temperate forest contains hundreds of species — trees, shrubs, herbivores, predators, decomposers — arranged across several trophic levels. It occupies a recognisable area whose boundaries can be mapped. And it appears to persist in a roughly stable state over centuries, with the same general composition maintained through processes of regeneration and replacement. One might take these features to be constitutive of an ecosystem. Yet one must consider the case of a newly cooled lava flow upon a volcanic island. When a volcanic eruption deposits fresh basalt upon the shore, the surface is initially barren — devoid of soil, of vegetation, of animal life. Within months, however, colonisation begins. Wind-borne spores of lichens and mosses settle upon the rock. Tiny arthropods arrive, carried by air currents. Seeds of hardy pioneer plants germinate in crevices. A system begins to form — organisms interacting with one another and with the physical substrate, exchanging energy and materials, competing for space and light. This is an ecosystem, albeit a young and simple one. Yet it fails all three conditions of the definition. Its diversity is minimal; it may contain fewer than a dozen species, drawn from one or two trophic levels. Its boundaries are not fixed but shift as new lava is deposited and as the pioneer community expands. And it is not in equilibrium; it is in rapid flux, its composition changing markedly from one year to the next as succession proceeds. The definition has taken the features of mature, stable, species-rich ecosystems and treated them as necessary conditions, when in truth an ecosystem is any system in which organisms interact with their environment, regardless of its age, diversity, or stability.

definition: A segment of hereditary material G is a gene if and only if (i) G encodes the information for the synthesis of exactly one protein, (ii) G occupies a fixed position on a specific chromosome, and (iii) G is inherited as a discrete, indivisible unit from parent to offspring.
conditions: G encodes the information for the synthesis of exactly one protein
G occupies a fixed position on a specific chromosome
G is inherited as a discrete, indivisible unit from parent to offspring
counterexample: Consider certain genes in higher organisms that, through a process known as alternative splicing, give rise not to one protein but to several distinct proteins from a single stretch of hereditary material. The gene encoding the Drosophila protein Dscam, for instance, can generate thousands of distinct protein variants through combinatorial splicing of its constituent exons. The first condition — that a gene encode exactly one protein — fails decisively, yet the segment in question is a gene by every standard criterion.
missing_condition: The definition assumes a one-gene-one-protein correspondence, but alternative splicing, overlapping reading frames, and polycistronic transcripts demonstrate that the relationship between genes and proteins is far more complex. A gene is better characterised as a functional unit of heredity rather than as a unit defined by a simple correspondence with a single protein product.
passage: One might define a gene as a segment of hereditary material that encodes the information for the synthesis of exactly one protein, occupies a fixed position on a specific chromosome, and is inherited as a discrete, indivisible unit from parent to offspring. This definition reflects a view of the gene that prevailed in the early decades of molecular biology and that retains a certain pedagogical clarity. Each gene, on this account, is a tidy unit: one segment of the chromosome, one protein, one heritable particle. The definition fits many of the simple cases admirably. The gene for haemoglobin beta occupies a known position on chromosome 11, encodes a single polypeptide chain, and is inherited in Mendelian fashion. Yet one must attend to the phenomenon of alternative splicing, which has been found to be pervasive in the genomes of higher organisms. The Dscam gene in the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster occupies a single locus on the chromosome and is inherited as a unit. But it does not encode one protein. Through the combinatorial selection of alternative exons during the processing of its messenger RNA, this single gene can give rise to many thousands of distinct protein variants — each with a different combination of structural domains, each with potentially different binding properties. The first condition of the definition fails spectacularly. The relationship between gene and protein is not one-to-one but one-to-many, and in some cases, through overlapping reading frames, many-to-one. The definition has imposed a simplicity upon the gene concept that the molecular facts do not support. What is wanted is a characterisation of the gene as a functional unit of heredity, one that accommodates the remarkable complexity of gene expression in living organisms.

definition: A physical interaction F constitutes a fundamental force if and only if (i) F acts between two material bodies possessing mass, (ii) F diminishes in strength with increasing distance between the bodies, and (iii) F is always attractive — that is, it draws the interacting bodies towards one another.
conditions: F acts between two material bodies possessing mass
F diminishes in strength with increasing distance between the bodies
F is always attractive — that is, it draws the interacting bodies towards one another
counterexample: Consider the electromagnetic force. It is one of the four fundamental forces recognised by physics, responsible for the interactions between electrically charged particles. Yet it does not act exclusively between bodies possessing mass; it acts between charges, and a massless photon mediates the interaction. Moreover, the electromagnetic force is not always attractive: like charges repel one another, whilst unlike charges attract. The third condition fails, yet the electromagnetic force is indisputably a fundamental force.
missing_condition: The definition models all fundamental forces upon gravitation — always attractive, acting between massive bodies, diminishing with distance — but the electromagnetic force demonstrates that a fundamental force may be either attractive or repulsive, may act between charges rather than masses, and may depend upon properties other than mass. The concept of a fundamental force must be broad enough to encompass the full diversity of physical interactions.
passage: One might propose that a physical interaction constitutes a fundamental force if and only if it acts between two material bodies possessing mass, it diminishes in strength with increasing distance between the bodies, and it is always attractive. The definition appears to describe gravitation with precision. Two masses attract one another with a force that varies inversely as the square of the distance between them; the force is always attractive and never repulsive; and it acts between all bodies that possess mass. One might be tempted to take these features as the hallmarks of any fundamental force. Yet one must consider the electromagnetic force. This force — recognised since the work of Coulomb, Faraday, and Maxwell as one of the fundamental interactions of nature — acts between electrically charged particles. Its strength, like that of gravitation, diminishes with distance. But the electromagnetic force is not always attractive. When two particles bear charges of the same sign, the force between them is repulsive; they are driven apart. Only when the charges are opposite is the force attractive. The third condition of the definition fails. Furthermore, the electromagnetic force acts between charges, not between masses as such; a body may be massive yet electrically neutral and thus subject to no electromagnetic interaction. The first condition, strictly construed, fails as well. The definition has taken the gravitational force as the model of a fundamental force and generalised from its particular features — universal attraction between masses — to a characterisation of fundamental forces as such. But the electromagnetic force, the strong nuclear force, and the weak nuclear force each differ from gravitation in significant respects, and a proper characterisation must accommodate this diversity.

definition: An ecosystem is healthy if and only if (i) its constituent populations are at or near their carrying capacities, (ii) no single species dominates the community to the exclusion of others, and (iii) the system exhibits no net loss of biomass over time.
conditions: its constituent populations are at or near their carrying capacities
no single species dominates the community to the exclusion of others
the system exhibits no net loss of biomass over time
counterexample: Consider a recently burned grassland. In the season following a fire, populations are far below their carrying capacities, as many individuals have perished and recolonisation is under way. A single species of fire-adapted grass may dominate the landscape almost to the exclusion of all others. And the system has suffered a dramatic net loss of biomass. All three conditions fail, yet fire ecologists recognise that the post-fire grassland is not an unhealthy ecosystem but rather an ecosystem in the midst of a natural disturbance cycle essential to its long-term vitality.
missing_condition: The definition equates ecosystem health with static equilibrium, but many healthy ecosystems depend upon periodic disturbance — fire, flood, windthrow — that temporarily reduces populations, allows dominance by pioneer species, and causes biomass loss. Ecosystem health is a dynamic property that must be assessed over the full cycle of disturbance and recovery, not at a single point in time.
passage: One might propose that an ecosystem is healthy if and only if its constituent populations are at or near their carrying capacities, no single species dominates the community to the exclusion of others, and the system exhibits no net loss of biomass over time. The definition has a certain intuitive plausibility. One thinks of a mature oak woodland, its canopy fully developed, its understorey rich with diverse shrubs and herbs, its animal populations stable from year to year — a system in which every niche is occupied and every trophic level is represented in abundance. Such a system seems the picture of ecological health. Yet one must consider the case of a recently burned grassland. In many grassland ecosystems, fire is not a catastrophe but a necessity. Periodic burning clears accumulated dead material, releases nutrients into the soil, and creates the conditions for vigorous regrowth. In the weeks and months following a fire, however, the system presents an appearance that the definition would classify as unhealthy. Populations of most species are far below their carrying capacities; many individuals have been killed outright, and recolonisation from surrounding areas is only beginning. A single species of fire-adapted grass may dominate the burned area almost completely, covering the blackened soil in a dense monoculture before slower-growing competitors have had time to establish themselves. And the system has suffered a dramatic net loss of biomass: the fire has consumed standing vegetation, litter, and organic matter. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the post-fire grassland is not an unhealthy ecosystem. It is an ecosystem in the midst of a natural disturbance cycle upon which its long-term diversity and productivity depend. The definition has confused a momentary snapshot of equilibrium with the dynamic process that sustains ecological health over decades and centuries.

definition: A population P constitutes a distinct biological species if and only if (i) the members of P share a characteristic set of morphological features that distinguish them from all other populations, (ii) members of P do not produce fertile offspring with members of any other population, and (iii) the members of P occupy a single, continuous geographical range.
conditions: the members of P share a characteristic set of morphological features that distinguish them from all other populations
members of P do not produce fertile offspring with members of any other population
the members of P occupy a single, continuous geographical range
counterexample: Consider the case of the monarch butterfly in North America. The eastern and western populations are separated by the Rocky Mountains and occupy discontinuous ranges — one breeding east of the Rockies and migrating to central Mexico, the other breeding west of the Rockies and overwintering along the Californian coast. The third condition is thus violated: the species does not occupy a single, continuous geographical range. Yet the monarch butterfly is universally classified as a single species, Danaus plexippus. Its morphological uniformity and the potential for interbreeding where stray individuals cross the divide leave no doubt of its specific unity.
missing_condition: The definition requires a single continuous geographical range, but many perfectly good species are distributed across disjunct areas separated by oceans, mountain chains, or other barriers. What is essential to species membership is reproductive and genealogical coherence, not spatial contiguity.
passage: One might propose that a population constitutes a distinct biological species if and only if its members share a characteristic set of morphological features distinguishing them from all other populations, members of the population do not produce fertile offspring with members of any other population, and the members occupy a single, continuous geographical range. The definition appears to capture the ordinary understanding of a species tolerably well. The European robin, for instance, exhibits a distinctive plumage shared by all its members, does not interbreed with other passerine species, and occupies a continuous range across much of Europe and western Asia. Yet one must consider the monarch butterfly of North America. The eastern population breeds across the central and eastern portions of the continent and migrates to the oyamel fir forests of central Mexico; the western population breeds along the Pacific coast and overwinters in groves of Monterey pine and eucalyptus in California. These two populations are separated by the Rocky Mountains, and their ranges are not continuous in any straightforward sense. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, no lepidopterist regards the eastern and western monarchs as distinct species. They are morphologically all but indistinguishable, they are capable of interbreeding where individuals from the two populations meet, and genetic analyses confirm their conspecificity. The proposed definition has mistaken a frequent accompaniment of species unity — continuous range — for a necessary condition, when in truth a species may be distributed across disjunct territories and remain a single species provided its populations are connected by gene flow or recent common ancestry.

definition: An entity X is alive if and only if (i) X is composed of one or more cells, (ii) X is capable of reproducing itself, and (iii) X undergoes metabolism — that is, it takes in energy from its environment and converts it into forms usable for growth and maintenance.
conditions: X is composed of one or more cells
X is capable of reproducing itself
X undergoes metabolism
counterexample: Consider a mule — the offspring of a male donkey and a female horse. The mule is composed of cells, and it takes in food, respires, and converts energy for growth and maintenance. It is, by any ordinary reckoning, alive. Yet the mule is sterile and cannot reproduce itself. The second condition fails. The definition would thus exclude the mule from the class of living things, which is plainly absurd.
missing_condition: The definition requires that an entity be capable of reproducing itself, but many organisms that are unambiguously alive — sterile hybrids, post-reproductive individuals, and worker insects in certain social species — are incapable of reproduction. Reproductive capacity may characterise living kinds in general without being a necessary condition for each individual organism's being alive.
passage: One might propose that an entity is alive if and only if it is composed of one or more cells, it is capable of reproducing itself, and it undergoes metabolism. The definition has considerable appeal: it captures the cellular basis of life emphasised by modern biology, it includes the capacity for self-replication that distinguishes living matter from mere chemical reaction, and it insists upon the energetic processes that sustain the organism. A bacterium, for example, satisfies all three conditions without difficulty. It is a single cell, it reproduces by binary fission, and it metabolises nutrients from its surroundings. Yet one must attend to a difficulty. Consider the mule, the well-known hybrid offspring of a male donkey and a female horse. The mule is composed of cells — it is a large, multicellular organism with tissues and organs of considerable complexity. It takes in food, digests it, and converts the chemical energy therein into the mechanical work of locomotion and the synthetic work of tissue repair. It is, by every common-sense criterion, alive. But the mule is almost invariably sterile; it cannot reproduce itself. The second condition of the definition is not satisfied. One is therefore compelled to deny that the mule is alive, which is a conclusion no biologist would accept. The difficulty extends beyond mules: worker honeybees are typically incapable of reproduction, and elderly organisms that have passed beyond the age of fertility are not thereby rendered non-living. The definition has confused a property of living lineages with a property of individual living things.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid-body forces so that it assumes a nearly round shape, and (iii) B is the largest body in its orbital region.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid-body forces so that it assumes a nearly round shape
B is the largest body in its orbital region
counterexample: Consider a very large body — considerably more massive than Jupiter — that has been ejected from its parent stellar system by gravitational interactions with other massive bodies and now wanders through interstellar space, bound to no star. Such free-floating planetary-mass objects have been detected by infrared surveys. The body is massive enough to be nearly spherical and, when it was in its original system, it was the dominant body in its orbital region. Yet it no longer orbits any star. The first condition fails. One would, however, be reluctant to deny that such a body is a planet in any physically meaningful sense; it formed by the same accretionary processes as the planets of our own system and possesses all their physical characteristics.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a planet orbit a star, but planetary-mass objects may exist in isolation, having been ejected from their birth systems. A satisfactory characterisation of planethood ought to rely upon the intrinsic physical properties and formation history of the body rather than upon its present dynamical situation.
passage: One might propose that a celestial body is a planet if and only if it orbits a star, it has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid-body forces so that it assumes a nearly round shape, and it is the largest body in its orbital region. The definition captures much of what is ordinarily meant by the term. The Earth, for instance, orbits the Sun, is massive enough to be spherical, and is the dominant body in its orbital neighbourhood. Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn satisfy the conditions equally well. Yet a difficulty arises when one considers the class of objects sometimes called rogue planets or free-floating planetary-mass objects. Observations in young stellar clusters and in the galactic field have revealed bodies with masses comparable to those of Jupiter or Saturn that are not gravitationally bound to any star. They drift through interstellar space, illuminated by no sun. Such a body may well have formed in a protoplanetary disc around a young star, accreted material in the same manner as the familiar planets, and been subsequently ejected by a close gravitational encounter with another massive body. It is round, it is massive, and its composition and internal structure are indistinguishable from those of a giant planet in orbit about a star. But it does not orbit a star. The first condition fails. The proposed definition would compel one to deny that this body is a planet, notwithstanding the fact that it possesses every physical attribute one associates with planethood. The error consists in treating a relational property — orbiting a star — as though it were an intrinsic feature of the kind.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons in their nuclei, and (iii) S exists as a stable entity at standard temperature and pressure.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means
all atoms of S have the same number of protons in their nuclei
S exists as a stable entity at standard temperature and pressure
counterexample: Consider the element francium, the heaviest of the alkali metals. Its atoms all possess eighty-seven protons, and it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means. Yet francium is intensely radioactive; its most stable isotope has a half-life of approximately twenty-two minutes. No macroscopic quantity of francium exists as a stable entity at standard temperature and pressure. The third condition is not satisfied. But francium is universally regarded as a chemical element, occupying its proper place in the periodic table.
missing_condition: The definition requires stability at standard conditions, but several elements — including technetium, promethium, and all elements beyond bismuth — have no stable isotopes and exist only fleetingly. Element-hood is determined by atomic number, not by macroscopic stability, and the definition has imported an extraneous requirement.
