passage: One might propose that a substance is a chemical element if and only if it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, all its atoms have the same number of protons in their nuclei, and it exists as a stable entity at standard temperature and pressure. The definition has much to commend it. Iron satisfies all three conditions: it resists chemical decomposition into anything simpler, every iron atom has twenty-six protons, and a bar of iron rests quietly on the laboratory bench at room temperature and atmospheric pressure. Gold, carbon, and oxygen likewise present no difficulty. Yet one must attend to the case of francium. Francium has atomic number eighty-seven; all its atoms share this proton count, and no chemical reaction decomposes francium into a simpler substance. But francium has no stable isotopes whatsoever. Its longest-lived isotope, francium-223, decays with a half-life of roughly twenty-two minutes. At standard temperature and pressure, any sample of francium would have largely disintegrated before one could weigh it. The third condition is flatly violated. Nevertheless, francium appears in every periodic table and is classified as a chemical element without the slightest hesitation. The same difficulty afflicts technetium, promethium, astatine, and all the transuranium elements. The proposed definition has confused a contingent feature of many familiar elements — their macroscopic stability — with a necessary condition for elementhood, when in truth what makes a substance an element is simply that all its atoms share a common nuclear charge.

definition: A condition C is a disease if and only if (i) C causes suffering or impairment in the organism that bears it, (ii) C has an identifiable biological cause — an infectious agent, a genetic mutation, or a physiological malfunction, and (iii) C is amenable to treatment or cure by medical intervention.
conditions: C causes suffering or impairment in the organism that bears it
C has an identifiable biological cause
C is amenable to treatment or cure by medical intervention
counterexample: Consider Huntington's chorea. It causes severe neurological impairment and great suffering in those afflicted; it has a clearly identifiable biological cause in the expansion of CAG repeats in the huntingtin gene. Yet there exists no treatment that halts or reverses the disease's progression; it is not amenable to cure by any presently available medical intervention. The third condition fails. But no physician would hesitate to classify Huntington's chorea as a disease.
missing_condition: The definition requires amenability to treatment, but many conditions universally recognised as diseases — including numerous degenerative neurological disorders and certain cancers — remain beyond the reach of effective medical intervention. Whether a condition is a disease depends upon its pathological character, not upon the present state of medical technology.
passage: One might propose that a condition is a disease if and only if it causes suffering or impairment in the organism that bears it, it has an identifiable biological cause, and it is amenable to treatment or cure by medical intervention. The definition captures a good deal of what is ordinarily meant by disease. Tuberculosis, for instance, causes suffering and impairment, has a clearly identifiable biological cause in the tubercle bacillus, and is amenable to treatment with antibiotics. Diabetes mellitus causes impairment, arises from a failure of insulin production or resistance to its action, and is treatable by the administration of insulin or related agents. Yet one must consider conditions such as Huntington's chorea. This hereditary disorder manifests in progressive neurological deterioration — involuntary movements, cognitive decline, and psychiatric disturbance of a most distressing character. Its biological cause is well understood: an abnormal expansion of trinucleotide repeats in a specific gene on the fourth chromosome. It satisfies the first two conditions beyond any doubt. But Huntington's chorea is, at present, without effective treatment. No drug arrests its progression; no surgical procedure reverses its effects. The third condition is not met. The definition would therefore compel one to deny that Huntington's chorea is a disease, a conclusion that no competent medical authority would endorse. The difficulty is that the definition has made the concept of disease depend upon the contingent state of medical knowledge and therapeutic capacity, when in truth a condition may be perfectly well characterised as a disease even though medicine has not yet found the means to combat it.

definition: A region R constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) R contains a community of living organisms, (ii) R possesses a set of abiotic conditions — climate, soil, and water — that sustain those organisms, and (iii) the organisms in R form a stable, self-regulating community that tends towards equilibrium.
conditions: R contains a community of living organisms
R possesses a set of abiotic conditions that sustain those organisms
the organisms in R form a stable, self-regulating community that tends towards equilibrium
counterexample: Consider the region surrounding a hydrothermal vent on the deep ocean floor shortly after the vent has opened. A community of organisms — tubeworms, chemosynthetic bacteria, specialised crustaceans — rapidly colonises the site, sustained by the chemical energy issuing from the vent. But the community is far from stable or self-regulating. The vent's output fluctuates unpredictably, species composition shifts rapidly as conditions change, and when the vent ceases activity the entire community perishes. The third condition is not satisfied, for there is no tendency towards equilibrium. Yet ecologists routinely describe such sites as ecosystems.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the community tend towards equilibrium, but many recognised ecosystems are inherently dynamic, disturbance-driven, or transient. What is wanted is not stability but the presence of functional ecological interactions — energy flow, nutrient cycling, and trophic relationships — regardless of whether the system exhibits equilibrium.
passage: One might propose that a region constitutes an ecosystem if and only if it contains a community of living organisms, it possesses a set of abiotic conditions that sustain those organisms, and the organisms form a stable, self-regulating community that tends towards equilibrium. The definition has a certain intuitive rightness. A temperate forest, for example, contains a rich community of plants, animals, fungi, and microorganisms; it is sustained by a particular range of temperature, rainfall, and soil composition; and it appears to maintain itself in a roughly steady state over long periods. But one must attend to certain cases that resist this characterisation. Consider the community of organisms surrounding a newly opened hydrothermal vent on the mid-ocean ridge. Chemosynthetic bacteria colonise the vent within weeks, and tubeworms, mussels, and blind shrimp follow in rapid succession, all dependent upon the chemical energy issuing from the Earth's interior. This is a community of living organisms sustained by a definite set of abiotic conditions. Yet the community is anything but stable or self-regulating. The vent's chemical output varies unpredictably; species composition shifts as flows wax and wane; and when the vent eventually goes extinct — as it will, perhaps within decades — the entire community collapses and dies. There is no tendency towards equilibrium; the system is transient by its very nature. Yet no ecologist would deny that it constitutes an ecosystem. The definition has mistaken a feature of certain long-lived, conspicuous ecosystems — apparent equilibrium — for a universal requirement, when what truly defines an ecosystem is the presence of functional ecological relationships between organisms and their abiotic environment.

definition: A segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if (i) it encodes the information necessary to produce a single polypeptide chain, (ii) it occupies a fixed locus on a specific chromosome, and (iii) it is inherited as a discrete, indivisible unit from parent to offspring.
conditions: it encodes the information necessary to produce a single polypeptide chain
it occupies a fixed locus on a specific chromosome
it is inherited as a discrete, indivisible unit from parent to offspring
counterexample: Consider a segment of DNA whose transcript undergoes alternative splicing, so that the same stretch of nucleotides gives rise to several distinct polypeptide chains depending upon which exons are included in the mature messenger RNA. In the fruit fly Drosophila, the Dscam gene can produce over thirty-eight thousand distinct protein isoforms through combinatorial alternative splicing. The segment occupies a fixed chromosomal locus and is inherited as a unit. But it does not encode a single polypeptide chain; it encodes thousands. The first condition fails, yet the Dscam locus is universally regarded as a gene.
missing_condition: The definition requires a one-to-one correspondence between gene and polypeptide, but the phenomenon of alternative splicing means that a single gene may encode multiple distinct polypeptides. A satisfactory definition of the gene must accommodate this multiplicity, perhaps by characterising the gene as a unit of transcription rather than as a unit encoding a single protein.
passage: One might propose that a segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if it encodes the information necessary to produce a single polypeptide chain, it occupies a fixed locus on a specific chromosome, and it is inherited as a discrete, indivisible unit from parent to offspring. The definition captures the classical picture of the gene admirably. The gene for haemoglobin beta, for instance, occupies a known position on chromosome eleven, is inherited in Mendelian fashion, and directs the synthesis of a single, well-characterised polypeptide chain. But a difficulty arises when one considers the phenomenon of alternative splicing. In many organisms, a single stretch of DNA is transcribed into a precursor RNA molecule from which different combinations of segments — exons — may be selected for inclusion in the mature messenger RNA. Each combination gives rise to a different polypeptide. The Dscam gene of the fruit fly Drosophila provides a particularly striking illustration: by combining different exons in different arrangements, this single locus can produce over thirty-eight thousand distinct protein isoforms. The segment occupies a fixed chromosomal position and is inherited as a unit. But it does not encode a single polypeptide; it encodes a vast repertoire of them. The first condition of the definition fails. Yet no molecular biologist would deny that Dscam is a gene. The definition has been framed upon the assumption that gene and polypeptide stand in a simple one-to-one correspondence, an assumption that molecular biology has shown to be false.

definition: A physical interaction F constitutes a force if and only if (i) F causes a change in the velocity of a material body, (ii) F can be characterised by a definite magnitude and direction — that is, F is a vector quantity, and (iii) F arises from contact between two material bodies.
conditions: F causes a change in the velocity of a material body
F can be characterised by a definite magnitude and direction
F arises from contact between two material bodies
counterexample: Consider the gravitational attraction between the Earth and the Moon. This interaction causes a continuous change in the Moon's velocity — bending its path into an orbit around the Earth — and it has a definite magnitude and direction. The first two conditions are satisfied. But there is no contact between the Earth and the Moon; they are separated by roughly two hundred and forty thousand miles of empty space. The third condition fails. Yet gravitational attraction is universally regarded as a force — indeed, it is one of the fundamental forces of nature.
missing_condition: The definition requires contact between material bodies, but several of the most important forces in physics — gravitation, electrostatic attraction and repulsion, and magnetic interaction — act at a distance without any contact. The definition has been abstracted from a limited class of mechanical interactions and does not accommodate action at a distance.
passage: One might propose that a physical interaction constitutes a force if and only if it causes a change in the velocity of a material body, it can be characterised by a definite magnitude and direction, and it arises from contact between two material bodies. The definition captures the most familiar cases with tolerable accuracy. When one pushes a ball across a table, the hand makes contact with the ball, the push has a definite magnitude and direction, and the ball accelerates. When a hammer strikes a nail, the contact between hammer and nail imparts a force that drives the nail into the wood. Friction, too, arises from the contact of surfaces and satisfies all three conditions. But one must consider gravitational attraction. The Earth attracts the Moon with a force that bends the Moon's trajectory into a closed orbit. This interaction has a perfectly definite magnitude — one may calculate it with great precision — and a definite direction, namely along the line joining the centres of the two bodies. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet there is no contact between the Earth and the Moon. They are separated by a vast expanse of space. No material medium connects them, and no chain of touching bodies transmits the interaction. The third condition fails entirely. Nevertheless, gravitation is the paradigmatic example of a force in physics, and no natural philosopher would hesitate to classify it as such. The definition has been constructed from everyday examples of mechanical interaction and has mistaken a feature peculiar to contact forces — the touching of surfaces — for a necessary condition of force in general.

definition: An organism O belongs to species S if and only if (i) O shares with other members of S a suite of anatomical characters that are unique to S, (ii) O is descended from the ancestral population that founded S, and (iii) O is capable of recognising other members of S as potential mates through species-specific courtship signals.
conditions: O shares with other members of S a suite of anatomical characters that are unique to S
O is descended from the ancestral population that founded S
O is capable of recognising other members of S as potential mates through species-specific courtship signals
counterexample: Consider a worker honeybee. She shares the anatomical characters of Apis mellifera, she is descended from the ancestral population of that species, yet she is altogether incapable of recognising drones as potential mates — she is sterile and takes no part in mating. The third condition is not satisfied. But no entomologist would deny that the worker bee belongs to the species Apis mellifera.
missing_condition: The definition requires individual mate-recognition capacity, but many organisms that unambiguously belong to a species — sterile castes in social insects, pre-reproductive juveniles, post-reproductive individuals — do not recognise or respond to conspecific mating signals. Species membership cannot depend upon each individual's reproductive behaviour.
passage: One might propose that an organism belongs to a given species if and only if it shares with other members of the species a suite of unique anatomical characters, it is descended from the ancestral population that founded the species, and it is capable of recognising other members as potential mates through species-specific courtship signals. The third condition appeals to the mate-recognition concept of species, which holds that species cohesion is maintained by shared systems of mating signals. A male firefly, for instance, produces a flash pattern unique to its species, and females respond only to the correct pattern. Here the definition works admirably. Yet one must consider the case of social insects, and in particular the worker caste of the honeybee, Apis mellifera. A worker bee shares the anatomical characters of her species — the distinctive wing venation, the corbiculae on the hind legs, the barbed sting. She is descended from the ancestral lineage of Apis mellifera beyond any doubt. But the worker is sterile. She does not engage in mating flights, she does not respond to the courtship signals of drones, and she is altogether incapable of recognising any organism as a potential mate. The third condition fails. Yet no one would propose that worker bees belong to a different species from their queen and the drones of the same colony. The definition has imported a condition — individual mate-recognition capacity — that is inapplicable to a large and conspicuous fraction of many species.

definition: An entity X is alive if and only if (i) X maintains a boundary separating its internal milieu from the external environment, (ii) X grows by assimilating matter from its environment, and (iii) X responds to external stimuli by altering its behaviour or internal state.
conditions: X maintains a boundary separating its internal milieu from the external environment
X grows by assimilating matter from its environment
X responds to external stimuli by altering its behaviour or internal state
counterexample: Consider a crystal of copper sulphate placed in a supersaturated solution. The crystal maintains a definite boundary between its solid lattice and the surrounding liquid. It grows by assimilating copper sulphate molecules from the solution, adding them to its surface in an orderly fashion. And it responds to changes in the external environment: if the temperature rises, the crystal dissolves; if more solute is added, it grows faster. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet a crystal of copper sulphate is not alive by any biological reckoning.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the entity's boundary-maintenance, growth, and responsiveness be sustained by an internal programme of hereditary information — a genome or its functional equivalent — that directs the processes of self-maintenance and reproduction. Crystals grow and respond to stimuli, but they do so by simple physico-chemical mechanisms rather than by the execution of an encoded developmental programme.
passage: One might propose that an entity is alive if and only if it maintains a boundary separating its interior from the external environment, it grows by assimilating matter from its surroundings, and it responds to external stimuli by altering its behaviour or internal state. The definition appears to mark off living things from the inorganic world in a principled manner. An amoeba, for instance, maintains a cell membrane, grows by incorporating nutrients, and responds to chemical gradients by moving towards or away from them. A plant maintains its cell walls, grows by photosynthesis and the uptake of water and minerals, and responds to light by bending towards it. But consider a crystal of copper sulphate placed in a supersaturated solution of the same salt. The crystal possesses a perfectly definite boundary — the planar faces of its lattice structure — separating its solid interior from the surrounding liquid. It grows by assimilating copper sulphate molecules from the solution, adding them to its surface in a geometrically regular fashion, and it may grow to considerable size. It responds to changes in the external environment with great sensitivity: a rise in temperature causes it to dissolve, an increase in the concentration of the surrounding solution causes it to grow more rapidly, and a mechanical disturbance may cause it to fracture and then heal as new material is deposited. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the crystal is plainly not alive. The definition has failed to distinguish the self-organised, informationally directed processes of living things from the purely physico-chemical processes of crystallisation.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B is spherical or nearly so, and (iii) B possesses an atmosphere.
conditions: B orbits a star
B is spherical or nearly so
B possesses an atmosphere
counterexample: Consider the planet Mercury. It orbits the Sun and is nearly spherical. Yet Mercury possesses only the most tenuous exosphere — a scattering of atoms blasted from its surface by the solar wind — which no astronomer would regard as an atmosphere in any ordinary sense. The third condition fails. But Mercury is without question a planet; it has been recognised as such since antiquity.
missing_condition: The definition requires the possession of an atmosphere, but several bodies universally classified as planets — Mercury being the clearest case — lack any substantial atmosphere. Atmospheres are contingent features that depend upon a planet's mass, temperature, and distance from its star, not upon its planethood as such.
passage: One might propose that a celestial body is a planet if and only if it orbits a star, it is spherical or nearly so, and it possesses an atmosphere. The definition appears to capture the principal members of our own solar system: the Earth has its nitrogen-oxygen atmosphere, Venus its dense shroud of carbon dioxide, Jupiter and Saturn their immense envelopes of hydrogen and helium. Mars, though its atmosphere is thin, possesses one nonetheless. The three conditions seem jointly sufficient. Yet the case of Mercury presents a decisive objection. Mercury orbits the Sun in a well-defined elliptical path and is sufficiently massive to be nearly spherical. It satisfies the first two conditions without difficulty. But Mercury's atmosphere, if it may be called such, consists of an exceedingly sparse collection of atoms — sodium, potassium, oxygen, and traces of other elements — liberated from the surface by the impact of solar-wind particles and micrometeorites. This exosphere is so tenuous that its total mass is negligible, and it bears no resemblance to the atmospheres of Venus, Earth, or the giant planets. By any reasonable standard, Mercury does not possess an atmosphere. The third condition is violated. Nevertheless, Mercury has been classified as a planet for as long as human beings have observed the heavens. The definition has taken a feature common to most planets — the possession of a gaseous envelope — and elevated it to the status of a necessary condition, when in fact atmospheres are contingent upon local circumstances and not constitutive of planethood.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed entirely of atoms having the same atomic number, (ii) S occurs naturally in the Earth's crust or atmosphere, and (iii) S can be isolated in pure form by chemical or physical means.
conditions: S is composed entirely of atoms having the same atomic number
S occurs naturally in the Earth's crust or atmosphere
S can be isolated in pure form by chemical or physical means
counterexample: Consider the element einsteinium, atomic number ninety-nine. It is composed entirely of atoms with ninety-nine protons in their nuclei, and it can be isolated — albeit with great difficulty — from the debris of nuclear explosions or from specially irradiated targets in nuclear reactors. But einsteinium does not occur naturally in the Earth's crust or atmosphere; it is produced solely by artificial nuclear reactions. The second condition fails. Yet einsteinium is universally classified as a chemical element and holds its place in the periodic table.
missing_condition: The definition requires natural occurrence, but numerous elements — the transuranic elements and several lighter ones such as technetium and promethium — do not occur naturally on Earth and are produced only by human artifice or extreme astrophysical processes. Element-hood depends upon atomic number, not upon terrestrial abundance.
passage: One might propose that a substance is a chemical element if and only if it is composed entirely of atoms having the same atomic number, it occurs naturally in the Earth's crust or atmosphere, and it can be isolated in pure form by chemical or physical means. The definition works well for the familiar elements. Oxygen, composed of atoms with eight protons, is found in abundance in the atmosphere and may be isolated by fractional distillation of liquid air. Iron, with atomic number twenty-six, occurs naturally in the crust and is extracted by smelting its ores. Gold, with seventy-nine protons, is found native in river gravels and may be separated by panning. But consider einsteinium, the element with atomic number ninety-nine. Every atom of einsteinium has ninety-nine protons, and the element has been isolated in weighable quantities from the products of intense neutron irradiation in nuclear reactors. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet einsteinium does not occur naturally in the Earth's crust or atmosphere. It is entirely a product of human artifice — it was first identified in the fallout of the first thermonuclear explosion in 1952, and all subsequent samples have been produced in reactors. The second condition fails. The same objection applies to all the transuranium elements, and indeed to technetium and promethium, which have no stable isotopes and are not found in nature in any appreciable quantity. The proposed definition has confused a contingent feature of most elements — natural terrestrial occurrence — with an essential characteristic of elementhood.

definition: A condition C is a disease if and only if (i) C is caused by the invasion of the body by a foreign pathogen — a bacterium, virus, fungus, or parasite, (ii) C produces a characteristic set of symptoms recognisable by clinical examination, and (iii) C can be transmitted from one individual to another.
conditions: C is caused by the invasion of the body by a foreign pathogen
C produces a characteristic set of symptoms recognisable by clinical examination
C can be transmitted from one individual to another
counterexample: Consider diabetes mellitus of the type that arises from the body's failure to produce sufficient insulin. The condition produces a recognisable set of symptoms — excessive thirst, frequent urination, and elevated levels of sugar in the blood and urine. It is universally regarded as a disease. Yet it is not caused by the invasion of any foreign pathogen; it arises from a malfunction of the body's own pancreatic cells. And it cannot be transmitted from one person to another by any means. The first and third conditions both fail.
missing_condition: The definition restricts disease to infectious conditions, but a vast number of diseases — diabetes, cancer, heart disease, genetic disorders — arise from internal physiological failures, environmental exposures, or hereditary defects rather than from pathogenic invasion, and are not transmissible. A satisfactory account of disease must encompass non-infectious as well as infectious conditions.
passage: One might propose that a condition is a disease if and only if it is caused by the invasion of the body by a foreign pathogen, it produces a characteristic set of symptoms recognisable by clinical examination, and it can be transmitted from one individual to another. The definition captures a large and familiar class of diseases. Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, it produces cough, fever, and the wasting of the body, and it spreads from person to person through the air. Smallpox is caused by the variola virus, it produces a distinctive pustular rash, and it is highly contagious. Malaria is caused by parasites of the genus Plasmodium, it produces periodic fevers and chills, and it is transmitted by the bite of the Anopheles mosquito. Yet one must consider conditions such as diabetes mellitus. In its most common forms, diabetes arises not from the invasion of any pathogen but from the body's own failure — either the pancreas ceases to produce insulin in sufficient quantity, or the body's tissues become resistant to insulin's action. The condition produces a well-recognised constellation of symptoms: excessive thirst, copious urination, and dangerously elevated blood sugar. But no bacterium, virus, fungus, or parasite is responsible. Nor can the condition be transmitted from one person to another; it is not contagious in any sense. Both the first and the third conditions of the definition fail. Yet diabetes is as much a disease as tuberculosis or malaria. The definition has been abstracted from the class of infectious diseases and mistakenly applied as though it captured the concept of disease in its entirety.

definition: A group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if (i) its members are capable of interbreeding with one another under natural conditions, (ii) its members produce fertile offspring when they do so interbreed, and (iii) its members are reproductively isolated from all organisms outside the group.
conditions: Members are capable of interbreeding with one another under natural conditions
Members produce fertile offspring when they interbreed
Members are reproductively isolated from all organisms outside the group
counterexample: Consider the case of certain willow species — Salix alba and Salix fragilis, let us say — which are recognised by botanists as distinct species on the basis of their differing morphology, growth habits, and ecological preferences. Yet these species interbreed freely where their ranges overlap, producing fertile hybrid offspring that are themselves capable of reproduction. The third condition — reproductive isolation — fails entirely, for the two groups are not isolated from each other. Nevertheless, botanists continue to classify them as distinct species, and with good reason, for away from the zones of hybridisation each maintains its characteristic form.
missing_condition: The definition requires complete reproductive isolation, but many recognised species hybridise freely where their ranges overlap, and species identity may be maintained by ecological, geographical, or behavioural factors rather than by absolute reproductive barriers.
passage: One might define a species as a group of organisms whose members are capable of interbreeding with one another under natural conditions, whose members produce fertile offspring when they do interbreed, and whose members are reproductively isolated from all organisms outside the group. This is, in essence, the biological species concept, and it has the considerable virtue of providing a clear and testable criterion for species membership. A population of robins in an English wood satisfies all three conditions: the birds interbreed with one another, produce fertile young, and do not breed with the thrushes, blackbirds, or wrens that share their habitat. Yet the definition encounters a serious difficulty when one considers the case of hybridising plants. Certain willow species — Salix alba and Salix fragilis — are recognised by competent botanists as distinct species. They differ in the shape of their leaves, in their growth habit, in their preferred habitats, and in numerous other morphological and ecological characters. But where their ranges overlap — along riverbanks in temperate Europe, for instance — they interbreed with ease, producing fertile hybrids that are themselves capable of reproduction. The offspring are not sterile mules; they are vigorous, fertile willows that may breed with either parent species or with other hybrids. The third condition of the definition — reproductive isolation — fails entirely. The two groups are not reproductively isolated from each other; they exchange genes wherever they come into contact. Yet botanists do not conclude that they are a single species, for away from the zones of hybridisation each maintains its distinctive form and occupies its characteristic ecological niche. The definition has elevated reproductive isolation to a necessary condition of species identity, when in truth many species — particularly among the plants — are held distinct by ecological, geographical, and developmental factors that fall short of absolute reproductive barriers.

definition: An entity E is alive if and only if (i) E is capable of metabolism — that is, E takes in matter and energy from its environment and transforms them to maintain its internal organisation, (ii) E is capable of reproduction — that is, E can produce copies of itself, and (iii) E responds to stimuli from its environment in ways that tend to preserve its continued existence.
conditions: E is capable of metabolism — taking in matter and energy and transforming them
E is capable of reproduction — producing copies of itself
E responds to stimuli from its environment in ways that tend to preserve its existence
counterexample: Consider a mule — the offspring of a horse and a donkey. The mule takes in food and water, metabolises them to maintain its bodily organisation, and responds to environmental stimuli with great vigour and intelligence. It is, by any ordinary reckoning, alive. Yet it is sterile; it cannot reproduce. The second condition fails. One might also consider a worker bee or a post-menopausal woman, neither of whom can reproduce, yet both of whom are indisputably alive.
missing_condition: The definition requires the capacity for reproduction, but many individual organisms — mules, sterile hybrids, post-reproductive animals — are alive without being capable of producing copies of themselves, suggesting that reproduction is a feature of lineages rather than a necessary condition of individual life.
passage: A natural and attractive definition of life might run as follows: an entity is alive if and only if it is capable of metabolism, capable of reproduction, and responsive to environmental stimuli in ways that tend to preserve its continued existence. Each condition seems to capture something important. Metabolism distinguishes living things from stones and rivers — even a river transforms matter, but not so as to maintain any internal organisation. Reproduction distinguishes living things from flames, which consume fuel and grow but do not copy themselves in any biologically relevant sense. Responsiveness to stimuli distinguishes the living organism from the crystal, which grows by accretion but does not adjust its behaviour to changing conditions. A bacterium satisfies all three conditions: it metabolises nutrients, it divides to produce copies of itself, and it moves toward food sources and away from toxins. Yet consider the mule — the offspring of a horse and a donkey, bred by man for thousands of years for its strength and endurance. The mule takes in food and water, metabolises them with the efficiency characteristic of equine physiology, and maintains the internal organisation of a large, warm-blooded mammal. It responds to stimuli with considerable vigour and intelligence — a good mule will navigate a narrow mountain path with more sure-footedness than either of its parents. But the mule is sterile. The incompatibility between the horse's and the donkey's chromosomes renders it incapable of producing offspring. It cannot reproduce; the second condition fails. Yet one would not for a moment entertain the suggestion that a mule is not alive. The same difficulty arises with the worker bee, which is alive but sterile, and with any animal that has passed its reproductive years. The definition has made reproduction a condition of individual life, when it is more properly a condition of biological lineages; an individual organism may be entirely alive without ever producing a copy of itself.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to have pulled it into a roughly spherical shape, and (iii) B is the dominant gravitational body in its orbital zone — that is, it has cleared the neighbourhood of its orbit of other comparably sized objects.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass for self-gravity to produce a roughly spherical shape
B is the dominant gravitational body in its orbital zone — it has cleared the neighbourhood of its orbit
counterexample: Consider a body of planetary mass — larger than the Earth, let us say — that has been ejected from its stellar system by a gravitational interaction with another massive body. It drifts through interstellar space, gravitationally unbound to any star. It is spherical, massive, and in every physical respect indistinguishable from a planet, save that it does not orbit a star. The first condition fails. Yet to say that this body is not a planet seems to confuse an accidental circumstance — its current lack of a stellar companion — with an essential characteristic.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a planet orbit a star, but rogue planetary bodies ejected into interstellar space possess all the intrinsic physical characteristics of planets, suggesting that the orbital requirement is an extrinsic and contingent feature rather than a necessary condition.
passage: It is commonly proposed that a celestial body is a planet if and only if it orbits a star, has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to have drawn it into a roughly spherical shape, and is the dominant gravitational body in its orbital zone, having cleared the neighbourhood of its orbit of other comparably sized objects. This definition has the merit of precision and has been employed in recent astronomical practice to distinguish planets from the many smaller bodies that populate a stellar system. Jupiter satisfies all three conditions admirably: it orbits the Sun, it is emphatically spherical, and it dominates its orbital zone, having swept up or expelled the debris of the early solar system. Yet one must consider a case that the definition does not accommodate. Suppose that a body of planetary mass — larger, even, than the Earth — is ejected from its stellar system by a close gravitational encounter with another massive body. Such events are believed to occur in the early history of stellar systems, when the orbits of massive planets are not yet stable. The ejected body now drifts through interstellar space, gravitationally unbound to any star. It is spherical, for its mass is more than sufficient for self-gravity to have shaped it so. It has no orbital zone to clear, for it has no orbit; but in every intrinsic physical respect it is indistinguishable from a planet of the same mass and composition that happens to remain bound to a star. The first condition of the definition fails: the body does not orbit a star. Yet to classify this body as a non-planet — to say that it ceased to be a planet at the moment it was ejected from its system — seems to confuse an extrinsic, accidental feature with an intrinsic and essential one. The body did not change its mass, its composition, its shape, or its internal structure when it was ejected; only its gravitational relationship to a particular star was altered. The definition has made an orbital circumstance a necessary condition of planethood, when what seems to matter is the intrinsic physical character of the body itself.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, (ii) S is composed of atoms that are all of the same type — that is, all having the same atomic number, and (iii) S occurs naturally — it is found in nature without requiring artificial synthesis.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means
S is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number
S occurs naturally — it is found in nature without requiring artificial synthesis
counterexample: Consider technetium, element forty-three. It is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number and cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means. Yet technetium has no stable isotopes and does not occur naturally on Earth in any detectable quantity; it was first produced by the artificial bombardment of molybdenum with deuterons. The third condition fails — technetium is not found in nature. Yet no chemist would deny that technetium is a chemical element. It occupies its proper place in the periodic table, it has a characteristic chemistry, and it behaves in all respects as elements are expected to behave.
missing_condition: The definition requires natural occurrence, but several elements — technetium, promethium, and all the transuranic elements — are known only through artificial synthesis, and their status as elements depends upon their atomic structure rather than upon the accident of their presence or absence in nature.
passage: One might define a chemical element as a substance that cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number, and occurs naturally — that is, it is found in nature without the need for artificial synthesis. This definition captures the classical notion of an element as one of the fundamental building blocks of matter, and it correctly identifies such familiar substances as iron, gold, oxygen, and carbon. These elements satisfy all three conditions: they resist chemical decomposition, they are composed of atoms of a single type, and they are found abundantly in the natural world. Yet one must consider the case of technetium, element forty-three. Technetium is composed of atoms all having atomic number forty-three, and it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical procedure. In these respects it is as much an element as iron or gold. But technetium has no stable isotopes. Every isotope of technetium is radioactive, and all decay on time scales that are negligibly short compared with the age of the Earth. As a consequence, technetium does not occur naturally on our planet in any detectable quantity; it was first produced in 1937 by the artificial bombardment of molybdenum with deuterons in a cyclotron. The third condition of the definition fails. Technetium is not found in nature. Yet no chemist would hesitate to call it an element. It occupies its proper place in the periodic table, between molybdenum and ruthenium; it has a characteristic chemistry; it forms compounds in accordance with the same principles that govern the chemistry of every other element. The same difficulty arises with promethium and with all the transuranic elements — neptunium, plutonium, americium, and the rest — which have been produced by artificial means and are not found in nature. The definition has confused an accidental feature — natural occurrence — with an essential one, when what makes a substance an element is its atomic structure, not the circumstances of its discovery.

definition: A condition C is a disease if and only if (i) C causes suffering or impairment of normal functioning in the organism that has it, (ii) C has an identifiable aetiology — a specific cause or set of causes that can in principle be determined, and (iii) C is an abnormality — that is, it deviates from the statistical norm for the organism's species and age.
conditions: C causes suffering or impairment of normal functioning
C has an identifiable aetiology
C is an abnormality — it deviates from the statistical norm for the species and age
counterexample: Consider malaria in certain tropical regions where the disease is endemic. In some communities, a very large proportion of the population — in some cases more than half — carries the malaria parasite and experiences periodic febrile episodes. The condition causes suffering and impairment; it has an identifiable aetiology in the Plasmodium parasite transmitted by the Anopheles mosquito. But in a community where the majority of the population is infected, the condition does not deviate from the statistical norm. It is, statistically speaking, normal. The third condition fails, yet malaria is a disease wherever it is found.
missing_condition: The definition requires deviation from the statistical norm, but a disease may be so prevalent in a population as to be statistically normal without ceasing to be a disease, suggesting that the concept of disease involves a notion of proper functioning that is not reducible to statistical frequency.
passage: It appears reasonable to define a disease as a condition that causes suffering or impairment of normal functioning, has an identifiable aetiology, and constitutes an abnormality — a deviation from the statistical norm for the organism's species and age. The definition captures a great deal of what physicians and biologists mean when they speak of disease. Tuberculosis causes suffering, has an identifiable cause in the tubercle bacillus, and is statistically abnormal in most populations. Diabetes mellitus causes impairment of metabolic function, has identifiable aetiological factors, and deviates from the norm. A broken leg causes suffering, has an identifiable cause in the fracture of the bone, and is abnormal in the statistical sense that most people do not have broken legs at any given time. Yet one must consider the case of malaria in those tropical regions where the disease is endemic. In certain communities in sub-Saharan Africa or South-East Asia, a very large proportion of the population — in some cases well over half — carries the Plasmodium parasite and experiences periodic febrile episodes. The condition causes suffering: the fevers are debilitating, the anaemia is severe, and in many cases the disease is fatal. It has an identifiable aetiology in the Plasmodium parasite, transmitted by the bite of the Anopheles mosquito. But in a community where the majority of the population is infected, the condition does not deviate from the statistical norm. It is, statistically speaking, normal — as normal as having two arms or breathing air. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet no physician would say that malaria ceases to be a disease merely because it is prevalent. A village in which every inhabitant has malaria is a village ravaged by disease, not a village in which a certain statistical norm has been established. The definition has confused two senses of the word normal — the statistical sense and the evaluative sense — and has made the wrong one a necessary condition.

definition: A system S constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) S comprises a community of living organisms interacting with one another, (ii) S includes the non-living physical environment — soil, water, atmosphere — with which those organisms interact, and (iii) S tends toward a state of equilibrium — that is, S maintains a roughly stable composition and structure over time through self-regulating processes.
conditions: S comprises a community of living organisms interacting with one another
S includes the non-living physical environment with which those organisms interact
S tends toward a state of equilibrium — maintaining roughly stable composition and structure over time
counterexample: Consider a recently devastated area — a forest destroyed by a volcanic eruption, or a coral reef bleached by a sudden rise in water temperature. In the months following the disturbance, pioneer organisms begin to colonise the area: lichens on bare rock, algae on bleached coral. These organisms interact with one another and with the physical environment. The first two conditions are satisfied. But the system does not tend toward equilibrium; it is in a state of rapid, directional change, passing through successive stages of colonisation. Its composition and structure alter dramatically from season to season. The third condition fails, yet ecologists would not hesitate to call the colonising community an ecosystem — one in the early stages of succession.
missing_condition: The definition requires a tendency toward equilibrium, but ecosystems in early succession, in the aftermath of disturbance, or in the process of collapse are undergoing directional change rather than maintaining stability, and they are ecosystems nonetheless.
passage: One might define an ecosystem as a system that comprises a community of living organisms interacting with one another, includes the non-living physical environment with which those organisms interact, and tends toward a state of equilibrium — maintaining a roughly stable composition and structure over time through self-regulating processes. This definition captures something important about mature, undisturbed ecosystems. A temperate forest, for instance, comprises trees, fungi, insects, birds, and mammals, all interacting with one another and with the soil, water, and atmosphere. Over decades, the forest maintains a broadly stable structure: the canopy trees shade the understory, the leaf litter enriches the soil, the fungi decompose organic matter and recycle nutrients. Perturbations — a fallen tree, a dry summer, an outbreak of caterpillars — are absorbed, and the system returns to something approximating its former state. Yet one must consider a different case. A forest has been destroyed by a volcanic eruption. The lava has cooled, and the ash has settled. In the months that follow, pioneer organisms begin to colonise the devastated area: lichens appear on bare rock, mosses grow in the crevices, windborne seeds germinate in thin layers of mineral soil. These organisms interact with one another and with the physical environment — the first two conditions are satisfied. But the system does not tend toward equilibrium. It is in a state of rapid, directional change, passing through successive stages of colonisation that will continue for decades or centuries. The composition of the community changes dramatically from year to year as new species arrive, compete, and replace the pioneers. The structure of the system is unstable, shifting, and far from any equilibrium. The third condition fails. Yet ecologists would not hesitate to call this colonising community an ecosystem — it is an ecosystem in the early stages of what is called primary succession. The definition has taken a feature of mature ecosystems — the tendency toward equilibrium — and elevated it to a necessary condition of ecosystem identity, when in truth an ecosystem may be in any stage of development, from the first lichen on bare rock to the climax forest.

definition: A segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if (i) it encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a protein, (ii) it is heritable — that is, it is transmitted from parent to offspring through reproduction, and (iii) it occupies a specific, fixed locus on a chromosome.
conditions: It encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a protein
It is heritable — transmitted from parent to offspring
It occupies a specific, fixed locus on a chromosome
counterexample: Consider the genes that encode ribosomal RNA. These DNA segments are transcribed into RNA molecules that form essential structural components of the ribosome — the cellular machinery responsible for protein synthesis — but they are not themselves translated into protein. They encode RNA, not protein. The first condition fails: these segments do not encode the information necessary for the synthesis of a protein. Yet they are among the most important and best-characterised genes in any organism, and no geneticist would deny them that title.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a gene encode a protein, but many genes encode functional RNA molecules — ribosomal RNA, transfer RNA, and various regulatory RNAs — that never become proteins, revealing that the concept of the gene is broader than protein-coding.
passage: A straightforward definition of the gene might run as follows: a segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if it encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a protein, it is heritable, and it occupies a specific, fixed locus on a chromosome. This definition has a pleasing clarity and reflects the central dogma of molecular biology — that DNA encodes protein through the intermediary of messenger RNA. A gene for haemoglobin, for instance, is a segment of DNA that encodes the amino acid sequence of the haemoglobin protein, is transmitted from parent to child, and sits at a definite position on a particular chromosome. The definition correctly identifies this segment as a gene and excludes non-functional stretches of DNA that encode no protein and have no heritable effect. Yet one must consider the genes that encode ribosomal RNA. The ribosome, which is the cellular machinery responsible for translating messenger RNA into protein, is itself composed in large part of RNA molecules. These RNA molecules are encoded by specific DNA segments that are transcribed — that is, copied into RNA — but never translated into protein. They are the final product; the RNA itself, not any protein, is what the cell requires. The segments of DNA that encode ribosomal RNA are heritable, for they are passed from parent to offspring with high fidelity. They occupy specific loci on the chromosomes. But they do not encode the information necessary for the synthesis of a protein. They encode RNA. The first condition of the definition fails. Yet these segments are among the most important genes in any organism — indeed, they are among the most conserved sequences across the whole of life, testimony to their indispensable function. No geneticist would deny that they are genes. The definition has taken one important function of genes — protein encoding — and mistaken it for the only function, overlooking the large and biologically crucial category of genes whose product is RNA rather than protein.

definition: An interaction F constitutes a force if and only if (i) F acts between two distinct physical bodies, (ii) F tends to change the state of motion of at least one of those bodies — that is, F tends to accelerate it, and (iii) F can be described by a vector quantity having both magnitude and direction.
conditions: F acts between two distinct physical bodies
F tends to change the state of motion of at least one body — it tends to accelerate it
F can be described by a vector quantity having both magnitude and direction
counterexample: Consider the centrifugal effect experienced by a body moving in a circular path — as, for example, a stone whirled on a string. The stone experiences what appears to be an outward pull, and in many practical calculations one treats this as a force. It can be described by a vector quantity with magnitude and direction, and it tends to change the state of motion of the stone if the string breaks. But the centrifugal effect does not act between two distinct physical bodies. There is no second body exerting the outward pull; the effect arises from the stone's own inertia in the rotating frame of reference. The first condition fails, yet the centrifugal effect is treated as a force in many branches of physics and engineering.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a force act between two distinct bodies, but fictitious or inertial forces — centrifugal, Coriolis — arise from the choice of reference frame rather than from the interaction of bodies, and they are treated as forces in practice because they produce observable accelerations.
passage: One might define a force as an interaction that acts between two distinct physical bodies, tends to change the state of motion of at least one of those bodies — that is, tends to accelerate it — and can be described by a vector quantity having both magnitude and direction. This analysis reflects Newton's conception of force as an interaction between bodies — every action having an equal and opposite reaction — and it captures the familiar forces of mechanics: gravitational attraction acts between the Earth and the Moon, the tension in a rope acts between the hands that pull at either end, and the normal force acts between a table and the book resting upon it. Each of these forces satisfies all three conditions. Yet one must consider the centrifugal effect experienced by a body moving in a circular path. Take the familiar case of a stone whirled on a string. From the perspective of the rotating stone, there appears to be an outward pull — a tendency to fly away from the centre of rotation. This apparent force has a magnitude that depends upon the stone's mass and the rate of rotation, and it has a definite direction — radially outward. It can be described by a vector quantity. And if the string breaks, the stone will fly off along a tangent, as though the outward force had been released. In many branches of physics and engineering — in the analysis of rotating machinery, in meteorology, in the study of planetary atmospheres — the centrifugal effect is treated as a force and enters into calculations on equal footing with gravitational and electromagnetic forces. But it does not act between two distinct physical bodies. There is no second body exerting the outward pull. The effect arises from the stone's own inertia when the situation is described from a rotating frame of reference. The first condition of the definition fails. The definition has restricted the concept of force to interactions between bodies, when in practice the concept is applied more broadly to include the apparent forces that arise from the choice of reference frame.

definition: A condition C is infectious if and only if (i) C is caused by a micro-organism — a bacterium, virus, fungus, or protozoan — that enters the body from outside, (ii) C can be transmitted from one individual to another through some medium of contagion, and (iii) C produces characteristic clinical signs and symptoms in the host.
conditions: C is caused by a micro-organism that enters the body from outside
C can be transmitted from one individual to another
C produces characteristic clinical signs and symptoms in the host
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who carries the typhoid bacillus in his gallbladder and sheds the organism in his faeces, thereby transmitting the infection to others through contaminated water or food. The micro-organism entered his body from outside and can be transmitted to others. Yet the carrier himself exhibits no clinical signs or symptoms whatever; he is in robust health, feels perfectly well, and would have no reason to suspect that he harbours the bacillus were it not for laboratory testing. The third condition fails — there are no characteristic signs or symptoms — yet the condition is infectious by any reasonable standard.
missing_condition: The definition requires the production of clinical signs and symptoms, but asymptomatic carriers harbour and transmit infectious agents without manifesting any illness, revealing that infectiousness is a property of the micro-organism's transmissibility rather than of its clinical expression.
passage: It appears natural to say that a condition is infectious if and only if it is caused by a micro-organism that enters the body from outside, it can be transmitted from one individual to another, and it produces characteristic clinical signs and symptoms in the host. The definition accommodates the paradigm cases well. Tuberculosis is caused by the tubercle bacillus, transmitted through the air, and produces a characteristic cough, fever, and wasting. Influenza is caused by a virus, transmitted by respiratory droplets, and produces fever, aching, and malaise. In each case, all three conditions are satisfied, and no one would hesitate to call the condition infectious. Yet one must consider the phenomenon of the asymptomatic carrier — a case brought vividly to public attention by the celebrated instance of Mary Mallon, known as Typhoid Mary, in the early years of this century. Mallon carried the typhoid bacillus in her gallbladder and shed the organism in her faeces, thereby infecting numerous persons through the food she prepared as a cook. The micro-organism had entered her body from outside, and she transmitted it to others with devastating effect. But Mallon herself exhibited no clinical signs or symptoms of typhoid fever. She was in excellent health, felt perfectly well, and denied vehemently that she could be the source of the outbreaks that followed her from household to household. The third condition of the definition fails: there were no characteristic signs or symptoms in the host. Yet the condition was infectious — indeed, it was infectious in the most practically important sense, for it was Mallon's very absence of symptoms that made her so dangerous, allowing her to move from kitchen to kitchen without suspicion. The definition has confused two things — being infected and being clinically ill — when in truth one may harbour and transmit an infectious agent without experiencing any illness at all.

definition: An ecological community constitutes a food web if and only if (i) it contains organisms at multiple trophic levels — producers, primary consumers, secondary consumers, and so forth, (ii) each organism at a higher trophic level obtains its energy by consuming organisms at a lower level, and (iii) the removal of any one species from the web has measurable effects on the populations of other species within it.
conditions: The community contains organisms at multiple trophic levels
Each higher-level organism obtains energy by consuming lower-level organisms
The removal of any one species has measurable effects on other species populations
counterexample: Consider a simplified community in a temporary pond that contains green algae, two species of herbivorous crustaceans that feed upon the algae, and a predatory insect larva that feeds upon the crustaceans. This community has multiple trophic levels and the appropriate feeding relationships. However, one of the two crustacean species is present in such small numbers, and contributes so little to the diet of the predatory larva, that its removal from the system has no measurable effect upon either the algal population or the predator's population. The third condition fails for this species, yet it is part of the food web — it feeds upon the algae and is occasionally eaten by the larva.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the removal of any single species have measurable effects, but many species in real food webs are functionally redundant or present in such small numbers that their removal produces no detectable impact, yet they are properly regarded as members of the web.
passage: One might propose that an ecological community constitutes a food web if and only if it contains organisms at multiple trophic levels, each organism at a higher level obtains its energy by consuming organisms at a lower level, and the removal of any one species from the web has measurable effects on the populations of other species within it. The third condition is intended to capture the idea that a food web is an interconnected system in which the fortunes of each species are bound up with those of the others. In a well-studied example — the intertidal communities of the Pacific coast — the removal of a single predatory starfish can lead to dramatic changes in the composition of the entire community, as mussels proliferate in the absence of predation and exclude other species from the rock surface. Here all three conditions are satisfied: there are multiple trophic levels, the appropriate feeding relationships obtain, and the removal of a species produces measurable effects. Yet consider a simplified community in a temporary pond. The pond contains green algae, two species of herbivorous crustaceans that feed upon the algae, and a predatory insect larva that feeds upon the crustaceans. The community has multiple trophic levels, and each higher-level organism obtains its energy from the level below. But one of the two crustacean species is present in very small numbers. It feeds upon the algae, certainly, and it is occasionally eaten by the predatory larva, but its contribution to the system is negligible. If this species were removed, neither the algal population nor the predator's population would change by any measurable amount; the other crustacean species is sufficiently abundant to sustain the predator on its own. The third condition fails for the minor crustacean. Yet it is part of the food web — it has feeding relationships with other members of the community, it occupies a position in the trophic structure, and under different conditions it might increase in number and become functionally significant. The definition has required that every species be functionally important, when in reality many food webs contain species whose removal would pass unnoticed.

definition: A group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if (i) its members are capable of interbreeding under natural conditions, (ii) such interbreeding produces fertile offspring, and (iii) the group is reproductively isolated from other such groups.
conditions: Members are capable of interbreeding under natural conditions
Interbreeding produces fertile offspring
The group is reproductively isolated from other such groups
counterexample: Consider two populations of birds geographically separated by a mountain range. In the laboratory, individuals from the two populations interbreed readily and produce fertile offspring, satisfying conditions (i) and (ii) if we set aside the phrase 'under natural conditions.' In nature, however, the populations never encounter one another, and so they are reproductively isolated in the geographical sense, satisfying condition (iii). Yet occasional stragglers crossing the range interbreed successfully, undermining the claim of strict reproductive isolation. It becomes unclear whether these populations constitute one species or two, since the conditions are met only equivocally.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the degree or kind of reproductive isolation required — whether it must be absolute, predominant, or merely typical — and thus cannot adjudicate borderline cases involving partial geographic or ecological barriers.
passage: The most widely invoked definition of a species holds that a group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if its members are capable of interbreeding under natural conditions, such interbreeding produces fertile offspring, and the group is reproductively isolated from other such groups. This definition has the virtue of grounding the species concept in a biological process rather than in mere morphological similarity, and it has served taxonomists well in a great many cases. Yet the definition encounters a difficulty that reveals its insufficiency when applied to populations separated by partial geographic barriers. Consider two populations of birds divided by a mountain range. In captivity, individuals from the two populations interbreed with ease and produce fertile young. In nature, the populations almost never meet, and so they are reproductively isolated in a straightforward geographical sense. But the isolation is not absolute: occasional individuals cross the barrier and breed successfully with members of the other population. The definition provides no guidance in such a case. Are the populations reproductively isolated or not? The answer depends upon the degree of isolation one requires, and the definition is silent on this point. It demands reproductive isolation without specifying whether this must be total, predominant, or merely typical, and it therefore leaves the boundaries of species indeterminate precisely where clarity is most needed.

definition: An entity X is alive if and only if (i) X is capable of metabolism — that is, of taking in energy from the environment and using it to maintain its internal organisation, (ii) X is capable of reproduction, and (iii) X responds to stimuli from its environment.
conditions: X is capable of metabolism
X is capable of reproduction
X responds to stimuli from its environment
counterexample: Consider a mule, which is the offspring of a horse and a donkey. A mule metabolises food, maintains its internal organisation with great efficiency, and responds to environmental stimuli with the same sensitivity as any mammal. Yet mules are sterile and cannot reproduce. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, and yet it would be absurd to deny that a mule is alive. The mule is as paradigmatically a living organism as any creature one might name.
missing_condition: The definition errs in treating reproductive capacity as a necessary condition of life; many organisms and individual living beings are incapable of reproduction without thereby ceasing to be alive.
passage: A familiar definition of life holds that an entity X is alive if and only if X is capable of metabolism, X is capable of reproduction, and X responds to stimuli from its environment. The metabolic condition ensures that living things are thermodynamically open systems that actively maintain their internal order. The reproductive condition captures the observation that life perpetuates itself across generations. And the responsiveness condition distinguishes the living from the merely chemical, for a candle flame metabolises after a fashion but does not respond to its environment in the relevant sense. This tripartite definition covers a wide range of organisms admirably. Yet it is undone by a case so commonplace as to be embarrassing. Consider the mule — the hybrid offspring of a horse and a donkey. A mule metabolises food and maintains its bodily organisation with impressive efficiency. It responds to environmental stimuli with all the sensitivity and subtlety of any large mammal. But it is sterile. It cannot reproduce. If we insist upon the definition as stated, we must conclude that the mule is not alive, a verdict that conflicts so sharply with ordinary judgement and with biological practice that it constitutes a decisive objection. The definition has made reproductive capacity a necessary condition when it is at most a common feature of living things.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid body forces so that it assumes a nearly round shape, and (iii) B has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit of other debris.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass to assume a nearly round shape
B has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit
counterexample: Consider a large body orbiting a distant star in a region densely populated by asteroids and cometary fragments. The body is massive enough to be spherical, and it orbits its host star in a stable and well-defined path. Yet it has not cleared its orbital neighbourhood; it shares its region of space with a substantial population of smaller bodies whose orbits overlap with its own. All authorities examining the body would recognise it as a planet-like world, yet condition (iii) is not satisfied. The difficulty becomes acute when we note that the concept of 'clearing' admits of no precise threshold.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify a quantitative threshold for what constitutes clearing one's orbital neighbourhood, rendering condition (iii) vague and inapplicable in borderline cases where a body dominates its region gravitationally without having wholly eliminated all co-orbital debris.
passage: The definition most widely adopted for a planet holds that a celestial body B is a planet if and only if B orbits a star, B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid body forces so that it assumes a nearly round shape, and B has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit of other debris. This definition has the appeal of combining dynamical and physical criteria: the orbital condition ties planets to stellar systems, the shape condition distinguishes them from irregular rocks and ice fragments, and the clearing condition ensures that a planet is the gravitationally dominant body in its orbital zone. Yet the third condition introduces a difficulty that no amount of reformulation seems wholly to resolve. Consider a large spherical body orbiting a distant star in a region thickly populated by asteroids and cometary fragments. The body is far more massive than any of its neighbours and exerts a commanding gravitational influence upon them, yet it has not swept its orbital zone entirely clear. Some co-orbital debris remains, drifting in resonant orbits or slowly being perturbed. Is the neighbourhood cleared or not? The definition provides no quantitative threshold, no measure of what fraction of the original debris must have been removed or what ratio of the body's mass to the total mass of co-orbital material suffices. In the absence of such a threshold, condition (iii) is irreducibly vague, and the definition cannot adjudicate the very cases in which a definition is most needed.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, (ii) S is composed of atoms all of which have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs in a stable form under standard conditions of temperature and pressure.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means
S is composed of atoms all having the same number of protons
S occurs in a stable form under standard conditions
counterexample: Consider the element francium, whose atoms all possess eighty-seven protons and which cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means. Yet francium is so radioactive that no macroscopic sample has ever been observed to persist under standard conditions; its most stable isotope has a half-life of approximately twenty-two minutes. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is not. Nevertheless, francium is universally classified as a chemical element.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement that the substance occur in a stable form under standard conditions is too restrictive; it should be replaced by a condition referring to atomic number alone, without regard to the stability of bulk samples.
passage: A traditional characterisation of a chemical element holds that a substance S is a chemical element if and only if S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, S is composed of atoms all of which have the same number of protons, and S occurs in a stable form under standard conditions of temperature and pressure. The first condition captures the classic understanding of elementhood as chemical irreducibility. The second modernises this understanding by appeal to atomic structure. And the third ensures that the element is a genuine substance, something one might in principle isolate and store. This definition works well for the familiar elements — hydrogen, oxygen, iron, gold — which can be isolated and maintained in stable form. Yet it fails conspicuously for the highly radioactive elements that occupy the lower reaches of the periodic table. Consider francium, element eighty-seven. Its atoms all share the same number of protons, and it cannot be broken down by chemical means into anything simpler. But no one has ever held a stable sample of francium under standard conditions; its most stable isotope decays with a half-life of roughly twenty-two minutes. Condition (iii) is plainly unsatisfied. Yet no chemist would deny that francium is a chemical element. The difficulty is that stability under standard conditions is at most a common feature of elements, not a necessary one, and the definition errs in treating it as such.

definition: A condition C in an organism O constitutes a disease if and only if (i) C involves a departure from the normal physiological functioning of O, (ii) C causes suffering or impairment of capacity in O, and (iii) C is caused by a specifiable pathological agent or process.
conditions: C involves a departure from normal physiological functioning
C causes suffering or impairment of capacity
C is caused by a specifiable pathological agent or process
counterexample: Consider a person who carries a genetic variant causing sickle-cell trait in heterozygous form. Under normal conditions, this person experiences no suffering, no impairment of capacity, and functions physiologically within the normal range. She does not satisfy conditions (i) or (ii). Yet the same genetic variant, in homozygous form, causes severe disease. Moreover, her heterozygous condition, while asymptomatic, is a departure from the statistically typical genotype. The definition cannot cleanly distinguish between the carrier state and the disease state, because the pathological agent — the genetic variant — is present in both cases, but suffering and functional departure are absent in the carrier.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for conditions in which a pathological agent is present but produces no departure from normal functioning or suffering; it needs a condition specifying that the pathological process must be actively manifesting its deleterious effects, not merely latently present.
passage: One might propose that a condition C in an organism O constitutes a disease if and only if C involves a departure from the normal physiological functioning of O, C causes suffering or impairment of capacity in O, and C is caused by a specifiable pathological agent or process. The first condition grounds the concept of disease in a norm of biological function. The second connects disease to its characteristic impact upon the organism's welfare. The third ensures that the disease has an identifiable cause, distinguishing it from mere statistical anomaly. This definition has considerable intuitive force and covers paradigm cases of disease admirably — infections, cancers, metabolic disorders, and the like. Yet it encounters a difficulty when we consider carrier states and subclinical conditions. Consider an individual who carries the sickle-cell trait in heterozygous form. A specifiable genetic variant is present — the pathological agent, as it were, is identifiable. Yet under ordinary conditions, this person suffers not at all. Her physiological functioning falls within the normal range by any reasonable measure. She is healthy, active, and unimpaired. Conditions (i) and (ii) are not met, and so the definition correctly refrains from classifying her as diseased. But the case reveals a deeper problem: the very same genetic agent, present in double dose, causes severe disease. The definition draws the line between health and disease at the point of manifest dysfunction, yet the pathological agent is already present, already causally operative in a subtler way. The definition cannot accommodate the medically important category of latent or subclinical pathology.

definition: A system S is an ecosystem if and only if (i) S comprises a community of living organisms together with their non-living environment, (ii) the organisms in S interact with one another and with their environment through flows of energy and cycling of materials, and (iii) S is spatially bounded and distinguishable from adjacent systems.
conditions: S comprises a community of living organisms and their non-living environment
Organisms in S interact through energy flows and material cycling
S is spatially bounded and distinguishable from adjacent systems
