counterexample: Consider the transitional zone between a forest and a grassland — an ecotone. This zone contains living organisms and non-living components, and these interact through flows of energy and cycling of materials just as they do in any recognised ecosystem. Yet the ecotone is not spatially bounded in any clear sense; it grades imperceptibly into the forest on one side and the grassland on the other, and its limits cannot be drawn without arbitrary stipulation. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, yet ecologists routinely study ecotones as ecosystems in their own right.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement of clear spatial boundedness is too demanding; ecosystems frequently grade into one another along continuous environmental gradients, and the definition should allow for functional rather than strictly spatial delineation.
passage: A common definition holds that a system S is an ecosystem if and only if S comprises a community of living organisms together with their non-living environment, the organisms in S interact with one another and with their environment through flows of energy and cycling of materials, and S is spatially bounded and distinguishable from adjacent systems. The first condition ensures that an ecosystem is not merely a collection of organisms but includes the abiotic matrix in which they are embedded. The second captures the dynamic character of ecosystems, their ceaseless traffic in energy and matter. The third provides the spatial individuality without which the concept would apply to the entire biosphere indiscriminately. Yet the third condition is precisely where the definition falters. Consider the transitional zone between a temperate forest and an open grassland — an ecotone, as ecologists term it. This zone contains living organisms, both those characteristic of the forest and those of the grassland, intermingled with species found in neither. The organisms interact through energy flows and material cycling in all the usual ways. But the zone lacks clear spatial boundaries. It shades into the forest by imperceptible gradations on one side and into the grassland on the other. No principled line can be drawn separating it from its neighbours. If we insist on condition (iii), we must deny that the ecotone is an ecosystem, a conclusion that sits poorly with ecological practice, where ecotones are studied as systems in their own right.

definition: A segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if (i) it encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a single polypeptide chain, (ii) it is inherited as a discrete unit from parent to offspring, and (iii) it occupies a fixed position — a locus — on a chromosome.
conditions: It encodes the information for synthesis of a single polypeptide chain
It is inherited as a discrete unit
It occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome
counterexample: Consider a segment of DNA that undergoes alternative splicing, such that the same stretch of genetic material gives rise to multiple distinct polypeptide chains depending upon which exons are included in the mature messenger RNA. The segment occupies a fixed chromosomal locus and is inherited as a discrete unit, satisfying conditions (ii) and (iii). Yet it encodes not one but several polypeptide chains. If we adhere strictly to condition (i), we must say either that the segment is not a gene or that it is several genes occupying the same locus, neither of which conforms to standard biological usage.
missing_condition: The definition's insistence on a one-to-one correspondence between gene and polypeptide is too restrictive; it should allow for a single gene to encode multiple polypeptides through mechanisms such as alternative splicing.
passage: The classical definition of a gene holds that a segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if it encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a single polypeptide chain, it is inherited as a discrete unit from parent to offspring, and it occupies a fixed position on a chromosome. This definition served molecular biology well in its early decades, when the relationship between genes and their protein products appeared to be straightforward. Each gene, it was supposed, specified one polypeptide, and this one-to-one correspondence was taken to be a fundamental principle. Yet advances in molecular biology have revealed this picture to be considerably oversimplified. Consider a stretch of DNA that undergoes alternative splicing: the same genetic material, occupying a fixed chromosomal locus and inherited as a unit, gives rise to several distinct polypeptide chains depending upon which portions of the initial transcript are retained in the mature message. This segment satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) without difficulty, but it violates condition (i), for it encodes not one polypeptide but many. If we insist upon the definition, we are forced into the uncomfortable position of saying either that this segment is not a gene at all — contrary to universal scientific usage — or that it is several genes sharing a single locus, which undermines the very notion of discrete genetic units. The definition, it appears, has mistaken a common case for a necessary condition.

definition: An interaction F constitutes a force if and only if (i) F is exerted by one body upon another, (ii) F is capable of producing a change in the state of motion of the body upon which it acts, and (iii) F can be quantified as a vector possessing both magnitude and direction.
conditions: F is exerted by one body upon another
F is capable of producing a change in the state of motion of the body acted upon
F can be quantified as a vector with magnitude and direction
counterexample: Consider two forces of equal magnitude acting upon the same body in precisely opposite directions. Each force individually is capable of producing a change in the body's state of motion, and each is exerted by a distinct body upon the object in question. Each can be quantified as a vector. All three conditions are satisfied for each force individually. Yet the body does not move; the forces cancel. One might observe that the definition, while adequate for each force taken separately, fails to address the crucial question of how forces combine, and in particular fails to distinguish a genuinely operative force from one that is wholly balanced by its counterpart.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the distinction between an applied force and a net force; it should include a condition addressing the composition of forces or specify that a force produces actual acceleration only when unbalanced.
passage: A straightforward definition of force holds that an interaction F constitutes a force if and only if F is exerted by one body upon another, F is capable of producing a change in the state of motion of the body upon which it acts, and F can be quantified as a vector possessing both magnitude and direction. This definition captures the Newtonian conception in broad outline. The first condition identifies force as relational — something that obtains between two bodies. The second connects force to its characteristic effect: acceleration. And the third ensures that forces are the sort of thing amenable to mathematical treatment. Yet the definition, while serviceable, leaves an important matter unaddressed. Consider a body upon which two forces act simultaneously, each exerted by a different body, each capable of producing motion, each quantifiable as a vector, but directed in precisely opposite directions and equal in magnitude. All three conditions are satisfied for each force individually. Yet the body remains stationary; the forces balance one another perfectly. The definition provides no vocabulary for distinguishing a force that actually produces motion from one that is wholly cancelled by an opposing force. It speaks only of what a force is capable of producing, not of what it actually produces when composed with other forces. What is missing is an account of the composition of forces — the principle that it is the net or resultant force, not each individual force, that determines the body's actual change of motion.

definition: A process P constitutes biological growth if and only if (i) P involves an increase in the size or mass of an organism, (ii) P is driven by the organism's own metabolic activity, and (iii) P results in the formation of new cellular material.
conditions: P involves an increase in size or mass of an organism
P is driven by the organism's own metabolic activity
P results in the formation of new cellular material
counterexample: Consider a plant that absorbs a large quantity of water after a rainfall. The plant increases in size and mass, this increase is driven by the plant's metabolic processes of water uptake and turgor regulation, and it might be argued that the water is incorporated into cells. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet mere water absorption is not what biologists mean by growth; the plant has swollen but has not grown in the relevant sense. It has added water, not structure.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the increase in size or mass be the result of the synthesis of new organic macromolecules — proteins, nucleic acids, structural carbohydrates — rather than the mere uptake of inorganic materials such as water.
passage: One might define biological growth as a process P that involves an increase in the size or mass of an organism, is driven by the organism's own metabolic activity, and results in the formation of new cellular material. This definition aims to capture the intuitive idea that growth is an active, internally driven process of material accretion. The first condition ensures that growth is measurable. The second distinguishes growth from passive swelling caused by external forces. And the third connects growth to the cellular level, where the fundamental biological work occurs. Yet the definition is not so precise as it first appears. Consider a plant that absorbs a great deal of water after a heavy rainfall. The plant increases considerably in size and mass. This increase is driven by the plant's own metabolic processes — the active uptake of water through root cells, the maintenance of turgor pressure. And the water is incorporated into cellular compartments, so one might say that new cellular material, in the sense of material within cells, has been formed. All three conditions seem to be met. But no botanist would describe mere water absorption as growth. The plant has swollen, but it has not grown. What the definition lacks is a requirement that the increase involve the synthesis of new organic macromolecules — proteins, nucleic acids, structural carbohydrates — rather than the simple uptake of water or mineral salts.

definition: An organism O belongs to species S if and only if (i) O shares with the other members of S a set of morphological features that are diagnostic for S, (ii) O can be placed in a lineage that descends from the ancestral population of S, and (iii) O is recognisably distinct from organisms belonging to any other species.
conditions: O shares diagnostic morphological features with other members of S
O descends from the ancestral population of S
O is recognisably distinct from organisms of any other species
counterexample: Consider two species of butterflies that are virtually indistinguishable in morphology — so-called cryptic species. An individual butterfly shares all the diagnostic morphological features of both species, since these features are identical across the two groups. It descends from the ancestral population of one of them. Yet it is not recognisably distinct from organisms belonging to the other species, because the two species are morphologically identical. The definition cannot determine to which species the butterfly belongs, for condition (iii) is violated no matter which assignment is made.
missing_condition: The definition relies too heavily upon morphological distinctness and lacks a condition based on genetic, reproductive, or ecological criteria that would permit the identification of cryptic species whose morphological features are indistinguishable.
passage: A seemingly adequate definition of species membership holds that an organism O belongs to species S if and only if O shares with the other members of S a set of morphological features that are diagnostic for S, O can be placed in a lineage descending from the ancestral population of S, and O is recognisably distinct from organisms belonging to any other species. The first condition appeals to the taxonomist's traditional tool — the identification of shared morphological characters. The second adds an evolutionary dimension, linking membership to ancestry. And the third provides the negative criterion of distinctness from other groups. This tripartite account appears robust. Yet it encounters an instructive difficulty when applied to cryptic species — groups of organisms that are reproductively isolated and genetically divergent but morphologically identical or nearly so. Consider two species of butterflies that an expert lepidopterist cannot tell apart by any external or internal morphological feature. An individual butterfly shares the diagnostic features of both species equally. It descends from the ancestral population of one of them. But it is not recognisably distinct from organisms of the other species, for the simple reason that the two species look exactly alike. The definition cannot resolve the question of species membership, because the morphological criterion upon which it relies has ceased to be informative. What is needed is a condition that appeals to something other than morphology — genetic divergence, reproductive isolation, or ecological differentiation.

definition: A group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if (i) the members of the group are capable of interbreeding under natural conditions, (ii) interbreeding produces fertile offspring, and (iii) the group is reproductively isolated from all other such groups — members do not naturally interbreed with members of other groups.
conditions: Members are capable of interbreeding under natural conditions
Interbreeding produces fertile offspring
The group is reproductively isolated from all other groups
counterexample: Consider the case of ring species, such as the Ensatina salamanders of the Pacific coast of North America. Adjacent populations along the ring can interbreed and produce fertile offspring — conditions (i) and (ii) are met for each pair of neighbouring populations. And each population is, to a considerable extent, reproductively isolated from distant populations. But the terminal populations at the southern end of the ring, where the chain closes upon itself, cannot interbreed, even though they are connected by a continuous chain of interbreeding populations. The species concept yields contradictory verdicts: neighbouring populations belong to the same species, yet the terminal populations belong to different species, even though there is no point along the chain at which the species boundary can be drawn.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that reproductive compatibility is a transitive relation — that if A can breed with B, and B with C, then A can breed with C — but ring species demonstrate that this assumption fails. A condition addressing the transitivity of reproductive compatibility, or permitting species boundaries to be drawn along gradients rather than at sharp boundaries, is needed.
passage: One might define a species as a group of organisms whose members are capable of interbreeding under natural conditions, produce fertile offspring when they do so, and are reproductively isolated from all other such groups. This definition — the biological species concept — has the considerable merit of providing a clear and empirically testable criterion for species membership. The interbreeding condition ensures that species are genuinely integrated reproductive communities. The fertility condition excludes cases of hybridisation that produce sterile offspring, such as the mule. And the isolation condition ensures that species are discrete units, not merging into one another. Yet one might observe that the definition encounters a difficulty with ring species. Consider the Ensatina salamanders distributed along the Pacific coast of North America. The populations form a roughly ring-shaped chain around the Central Valley. Each population can interbreed with its neighbours and produce fertile offspring; conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied for every pair of adjacent populations. And each population is substantially isolated from distant populations. But the two terminal populations at the southern end of the ring, where the chain meets itself, cannot interbreed. The definition yields a paradox: by the criterion of interbreeding, each population belongs to the same species as its neighbours, yet the terminal populations belong to different species. There is no point along the chain at which one can draw a sharp boundary between species. The definition presupposes that reproductive compatibility is transitive — that if A breeds with B, and B with C, then A breeds with C — but the ring species demonstrates that this presupposition is false.

definition: An entity E is alive if and only if (i) E is composed of one or more cells, (ii) E maintains its internal organisation through metabolic processes — the chemical reactions by which it transforms matter and energy, and (iii) E is capable of reproduction — of producing new entities substantially similar to itself.
conditions: E is composed of one or more cells
E maintains internal organisation through metabolism
E is capable of reproduction
counterexample: Consider a mule — the offspring of a horse and a donkey. A mule is composed of cells and maintains its internal organisation through metabolic processes. But mules are, with very rare exceptions, sterile: they cannot reproduce. Condition (iii) is not met. Yet no one would say that a mule is not alive. It eats, breathes, moves, responds to stimuli, grows, and eventually dies. It possesses every hallmark of life save the capacity for reproduction. The definition, by requiring reproduction, excludes an organism that is unambiguously alive.
missing_condition: The definition places reproduction among the necessary conditions for life, but sterile organisms — mules, worker bees, post-reproductive individuals — are manifestly alive. Reproduction may be a characteristic of living species but is not a necessary property of every living individual.
passage: One might propose that an entity is alive if and only if it is composed of one or more cells, maintains its internal organisation through metabolic processes, and is capable of reproduction. The cellular condition reflects the foundational insight of cell theory: all known living things are composed of cells. The metabolic condition captures the dynamic character of life — the ceaseless chemical activity by which an organism maintains its structure against the tendency toward disorder. The reproductive condition ensures that the entity participates in the continuity of life, producing offspring that carry its characteristics into the future. These three conditions jointly describe the paradigmatic features of living organisms. Yet one might observe that the reproductive condition, though it characterises life in general, does not hold for every living individual. Consider the mule. A mule is composed of cells; it maintains its internal organisation through vigorous metabolic activity — it eats, breathes, and converts food into energy and structure. But the mule is sterile: it cannot produce offspring. Condition (iii) fails. The definition therefore implies that the mule is not alive, a conclusion that is plainly absurd. The mule is alive by every reasonable standard. It is born, it grows, it is active and responsive, and it eventually dies. What the definition has done is to confuse a property that is characteristic of living species — the capacity for reproduction — with a necessary condition for the life of every individual organism. A more adequate definition would need to accommodate the fact that many individual organisms are alive without being capable of reproduction.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid-body forces so that it assumes a nearly round shape, and (iii) B is the gravitationally dominant body in its orbital region — no other body of comparable size shares its orbital zone.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass to assume a nearly round shape
B is gravitationally dominant in its orbital region
counterexample: Consider a rogue planet — a body that has been ejected from its stellar system and wanders through interstellar space. Such a body may have sufficient mass to be round; it may have cleared its orbital zone before ejection. But it no longer orbits a star. Condition (i) is not met. Yet in every intrinsic respect — mass, composition, geological activity — the rogue planet is indistinguishable from a planet that remains in orbit. The definition, by requiring that the body orbit a star, denies planetary status to an object that possesses all the intrinsic characteristics of a planet, merely because of an accident of dynamical history.
missing_condition: The definition makes orbital context a necessary condition, but the intrinsic nature of a celestial body — its mass, composition, and shape — does not change when it is ejected from its stellar system. A condition that classifies bodies by their intrinsic physical characteristics rather than by their current dynamical circumstances is needed.
passage: One might define a planet as a celestial body that orbits a star, has sufficient mass for self-gravity to produce a nearly round shape, and is the gravitationally dominant body in its orbital region. This definition has the merit of clarity: it draws a sharp distinction between planets, which dominate their orbits, and smaller bodies — asteroids and the like — which share their orbital regions with many comparable objects. The roundness condition ensures that the body is massive enough to have undergone gravitational differentiation, and the stellar orbit condition situates the body in the kind of system in which planets are typically found. Yet one might observe that the requirement of a stellar orbit introduces a difficulty. Consider a body of planetary mass and composition that has been ejected from its stellar system by a gravitational encounter — a rogue planet, as it is called, wandering through interstellar space. Such a body may be as massive as Jupiter, may be round, and may once have dominated its orbital zone. But it no longer orbits a star. The definition denies it the status of a planet. Yet in every intrinsic respect — its mass, its composition, its geological character — the rogue planet is identical to the body it was before ejection. Its nature has not changed; only its dynamical circumstances have. The definition, it appears, has confused the context in which a planet is typically found with the features that make it a planet. A more satisfactory definition would classify bodies by their intrinsic physical properties rather than by the accident of their current orbital situation.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, (ii) S is composed of atoms all of which have the same number of protons in their nuclei, and (iii) S has a fixed set of characteristic physical properties — melting point, boiling point, density, and so forth — that uniquely identify it.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means
S is composed of atoms with the same number of protons
S has a fixed set of characteristic physical properties that uniquely identify it
counterexample: Consider the element carbon. Carbon exists in several allotropes — diamond, graphite, fullerene, and amorphous carbon — each with dramatically different physical properties. Diamond is the hardest natural substance; graphite is soft and slippery. Diamond is transparent; graphite is opaque. Their melting points, densities, and electrical conductivities differ markedly. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met — neither diamond nor graphite can be decomposed chemically, and both are composed of atoms with six protons. But condition (iii) is violated: carbon does not have a single fixed set of physical properties. Its properties depend upon the arrangement of its atoms. The definition implies that carbon is not an element, or that diamond and graphite are different elements, both of which are absurd conclusions.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that each element possesses a unique, fixed set of physical properties, but allotropy — the existence of an element in multiple structural forms with different physical properties — refutes this assumption. A condition acknowledging that the identity of an element is determined by atomic number alone, independent of the physical properties that vary with structural arrangement, is needed.
passage: One might propose that a substance is a chemical element if and only if it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, is composed of atoms with the same number of protons, and has a fixed set of characteristic physical properties that uniquely identify it. The first condition is historical: it reflects Lavoisier's criterion for distinguishing elements from compounds. The second condition is modern: it grounds the identity of an element in its atomic number. The third condition adds a practical criterion of identification, reflecting the fact that chemists have long recognised elements by their distinctive physical properties — the lustre of gold, the density of lead, the melting point of tungsten. Yet one might observe that the third condition is at odds with the phenomenon of allotropy. Consider carbon. Carbon satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) admirably: it cannot be decomposed by chemical means, and every carbon atom has exactly six protons. But carbon exists in several radically different forms. Diamond is transparent, extraordinarily hard, and a poor conductor of electricity. Graphite is opaque, soft, and an excellent conductor. Their densities differ; their melting points differ; their appearances could scarcely be more dissimilar. Carbon does not possess a fixed set of physical properties; its properties depend upon the spatial arrangement of its atoms. The definition, if taken strictly, implies either that carbon is not an element or that diamond and graphite are different elements. Both conclusions are absurd. What the definition lacks is the recognition that the identity of an element is determined by its atomic number alone, and that the physical properties of an element may vary dramatically with the structural arrangement of its atoms.

definition: A condition D is a disease if and only if (i) D involves a deviation from the normal physiological functioning of the organism, (ii) D causes suffering, discomfort, or impairment to the affected individual, and (iii) D has an identifiable cause — a pathogen, a genetic defect, or an environmental insult.
conditions: D involves a deviation from normal physiological functioning
D causes suffering, discomfort, or impairment
D has an identifiable cause
counterexample: Consider an individual who carries sickle cell trait — one copy of the sickle cell allele and one normal allele. In regions where malaria is endemic, this individual has a physiological deviation from the norm: her red blood cells sometimes assume a sickle shape under low-oxygen conditions. The condition has an identifiable genetic cause. Yet the carrier experiences no suffering, discomfort, or impairment under ordinary conditions and, indeed, enjoys a significant advantage — increased resistance to malaria. Condition (ii) is not met. The definition denies that the condition is a disease, which seems correct. But now consider a carrier who travels to high altitude and experiences a painful sickling crisis. The same genetic condition that was not a disease at sea level becomes one at altitude. The definition implies that the same condition is a disease in one environment and not in another, raising the question of whether the disease resides in the organism or in the relation between the organism and its environment.
missing_condition: The definition treats disease as a property of the organism alone, but the case reveals that whether a condition constitutes a disease may depend on the environment in which the organism finds itself. A condition addressing the relational character of disease — the interaction between organism and environment — is needed.
passage: One might define a disease as a condition involving a deviation from normal physiological functioning, causing suffering or impairment to the affected individual, and having an identifiable cause. The first condition captures the biomedical intuition that disease is a departure from how the body is supposed to work. The second condition adds a humanistic element, ensuring that mere statistical abnormality does not suffice — the deviation must matter to the individual. The third condition reflects the aspiration of scientific medicine to understand diseases in terms of their causes. Yet one might observe that these conditions, taken together, encounter a difficulty with conditions whose status as diseases depends upon the environment. Consider sickle cell trait — the possession of one sickle cell allele and one normal allele. This is a physiological deviation, and its cause is identifiable: a specific genetic mutation. But under ordinary conditions the carrier experiences no suffering or impairment. Indeed, in malaria-endemic regions the trait confers a substantial survival advantage. Condition (ii) is not met, and the definition correctly denies that sickle cell trait is a disease. But suppose the carrier ascends to high altitude, where the reduced oxygen pressure triggers a painful sickling crisis. Now condition (ii) is met, and the definition classifies the condition as a disease. The same genetic condition is a disease in one environment and not in another. The definition, it appears, treats disease as a property of the organism alone, when in truth the boundary between disease and health may depend upon the relation between the organism and its environment.

definition: A system S constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) S comprises a community of living organisms together with the non-living components of their environment, (ii) the organisms within S interact through flows of energy and cycling of nutrients, and (iii) S has definite spatial boundaries that distinguish it from adjacent systems.
conditions: S comprises living organisms together with non-living environmental components
The organisms interact through energy flows and nutrient cycling
S has definite spatial boundaries
counterexample: Consider a river. A river comprises a community of organisms — fish, invertebrates, algae, bacteria — together with non-living components such as water, sediment, and dissolved minerals. These organisms interact through energy flows and nutrient cycling. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met. But a river does not have definite spatial boundaries in the way the definition requires. Its floodplain merges gradually into the surrounding terrestrial landscape. Nutrients and organisms flow freely between the river and its banks, between the main channel and its backwaters, between the surface water and the groundwater. Condition (iii) is not met. Yet a river is among the most studied and best-understood of all ecosystems. The definition, by requiring definite boundaries, excludes systems whose ecological interest lies precisely in the gradients and exchanges that blur their edges.
missing_condition: The definition requires definite spatial boundaries, but many of the most important ecosystems — rivers, estuaries, ecotones — are characterised by gradual transitions rather than sharp boundaries. A condition permitting ecosystems to be delineated by functional criteria (energy flow, nutrient cycling) rather than by fixed spatial limits is needed.
passage: One might define an ecosystem as a system comprising living organisms together with the non-living components of their environment, in which the organisms interact through flows of energy and cycling of nutrients, and which has definite spatial boundaries distinguishing it from adjacent systems. The first condition captures the inclusive character of the ecosystem concept: it encompasses both the biotic and the abiotic. The second condition identifies the functional relationships that bind the components together into a system — the flows of energy from producers to consumers and the cycling of nutrients through the biotic and abiotic compartments. The third condition provides the criterion of individuation: an ecosystem is a spatially bounded unit, distinguishable from its neighbours. Yet one might observe that the boundary condition is far more problematic than it initially appears. Consider a river. It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) without difficulty: it supports a community of organisms that interact through energy flows and nutrient cycling. But where are its boundaries? The river merges into its floodplain, which merges into the surrounding terrestrial landscape. Organisms move freely between the water and the land; nutrients wash in from the catchment and flow out to the sea; groundwater connects the river to aquifers that extend far beyond its visible course. There is no sharp spatial boundary that sets the river ecosystem apart from its surroundings. The definition, by requiring definite boundaries, either excludes the river from the category of ecosystems or forces us to impose arbitrary boundaries that have no ecological significance. What is needed is a conception of ecosystem identity based on functional criteria — patterns of energy flow and nutrient cycling — rather than on fixed spatial boundaries.

definition: A segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if (i) it encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a single polypeptide chain, (ii) it occupies a fixed position (locus) on a chromosome, and (iii) it is transmitted as a unit from parent to offspring during reproduction.
conditions: It encodes information for synthesising a single polypeptide
It occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome
It is transmitted as a unit from parent to offspring
counterexample: Consider the phenomenon of alternative splicing, in which a single segment of DNA gives rise to multiple different polypeptide chains depending on how its RNA transcript is processed. The segment occupies a fixed locus and is transmitted as a unit. But it does not encode a single polypeptide; it encodes several, depending on which exons are included in the mature messenger RNA. Condition (i) is violated. Yet molecular biologists regard the segment as a single gene. The definition, by insisting on a one-to-one correspondence between gene and polypeptide, fails to accommodate a fundamental feature of eukaryotic gene expression.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that each gene encodes exactly one polypeptide, but alternative splicing allows a single gene to encode multiple polypeptides. A condition permitting a gene to encode one or more polypeptides through differential processing of its transcript is needed.
passage: One might define a gene as a segment of DNA that encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a single polypeptide chain, occupies a fixed position on a chromosome, and is transmitted as a unit from parent to offspring. This definition — sometimes called the one gene, one polypeptide hypothesis — has the considerable virtue of simplicity. It identifies the gene with a specific, localised stretch of DNA whose function is to specify a particular protein. The locus condition ensures that the gene has a definite physical address, and the transmission condition connects the molecular concept of the gene to the classical genetic concept of the heritable unit. Yet one might observe that the definition encounters serious difficulties with the phenomenon of alternative splicing. In many eukaryotic organisms, a single stretch of DNA can give rise to several different polypeptide chains, depending on how the RNA transcript is processed. The pre-messenger RNA may be spliced in different ways, including different combinations of exons, so that the mature messenger RNA — and hence the polypeptide it encodes — varies from one cell type to another or from one developmental stage to another. The DNA segment occupies a single locus and is transmitted as a unit. But it does not encode a single polypeptide; it encodes many. The definition, by insisting on a one-to-one correspondence between gene and polypeptide, implies either that the segment is not a gene or that it is several genes occupying the same locus. Neither conclusion is satisfactory. What the definition lacks is the recognition that the relationship between a gene and its products may be one-to-many rather than one-to-one.

definition: An interaction F is a force if and only if (i) F acts upon a body by contact or across a distance, (ii) F produces, or tends to produce, a change in the motion of the body upon which it acts, and (iii) F can be measured in terms of the acceleration it imparts to a body of known mass.
conditions: F acts upon a body by contact or across a distance
F produces or tends to produce a change in motion
F can be measured by the acceleration imparted to a known mass
counterexample: Consider the centrifugal force experienced by a passenger in a car rounding a curve. The passenger feels herself pressed outward against the door. If she releases an object, it appears to accelerate outward. In the rotating reference frame, one can measure the centrifugal force by the acceleration it imparts to a body of known mass. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the centrifugal force is not a genuine force in the Newtonian sense; it is a fictitious or pseudo-force, an artefact of describing motion from a non-inertial reference frame. No physical interaction — neither contact nor action at a distance — pushes the passenger outward. The definition, by admitting any interaction that produces a measurable change in motion, fails to distinguish genuine forces from pseudo-forces that arise only in non-inertial frames.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the force be frame-independent — that it be present in all inertial reference frames rather than arising as an artefact of a particular non-inertial frame. A condition restricting forces to those that are invariant under transformation to any inertial frame is needed.
passage: One might propose that an interaction is a force if and only if it acts upon a body by contact or across a distance, produces or tends to produce a change in the motion of the body, and can be measured by the acceleration it imparts to a body of known mass. The first condition captures the two traditional modes of causal influence in physics: contact forces, such as the push of a hand, and forces acting at a distance, such as gravitation. The second condition embodies Newton's insight that force is that which changes motion. The third condition provides an operational criterion: a force is real if and only if it can be measured through its effects on a standard mass. These three conditions together constitute a tolerably clear and empirically grounded conception of force. Yet one might observe that the definition admits a class of entities that physicists have long recognised as not genuine forces. Consider the centrifugal force experienced by a passenger in a car rounding a curve. In the rotating reference frame of the car, the passenger experiences an apparent outward push. An object released inside the car appears to accelerate outward, and this acceleration can be measured against a known mass. All three conditions seem to be met. But the centrifugal force is not a genuine interaction; it is a pseudo-force, an artefact of describing the motion from a non-inertial frame of reference. In an inertial frame, the passenger is simply continuing in a straight line while the car curves beneath her. No physical interaction — neither contact nor action at a distance — produces the outward acceleration. The definition, by failing to distinguish frame-dependent from frame-independent effects, admits pseudo-forces alongside genuine ones. What is needed is a condition requiring that the force be present in all inertial reference frames, not merely in the particular frame from which the motion happens to be described.

definition: A process P constitutes a chemical reaction if and only if (i) P transforms one or more substances into one or more different substances, (ii) the transformation involves the breaking and forming of chemical bonds between atoms, and (iii) the products of P have different chemical properties from the reactants.
conditions: P transforms one or more substances into different substances
The transformation involves breaking and forming chemical bonds
Products have different chemical properties from reactants
counterexample: Consider the dissolution of sodium chloride in water. When salt is added to water, the ionic bonds holding the crystal lattice together are broken, and new interactions — ion-dipole interactions between the sodium and chloride ions and the water molecules — are formed. The resulting solution has chemical properties different from those of solid salt: it conducts electricity, for example, whereas the dry crystal does not conduct under ordinary conditions. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet most chemists would classify the dissolution of salt in water as a physical change rather than a chemical reaction, since no new chemical substances are formed — the sodium and chloride ions remain unchanged.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the breaking of bonds in a chemical reaction proper and the disruption of intermolecular or interionic forces in a physical process such as dissolution. A condition requiring the formation of new chemical species — substances with different molecular or atomic composition — rather than merely the rearrangement of existing species in a new medium, is needed.
passage: One might define a chemical reaction as a process that transforms one or more substances into different substances, involves the breaking and forming of chemical bonds, and produces products with different chemical properties from the reactants. The first condition captures the transformative character of chemical reactions: something new comes into being. The second condition identifies the mechanism: the rearrangement of atoms through the breaking of old bonds and the forming of new ones. The third condition provides an empirical test: the products must be chemically distinguishable from the reactants. Together, these conditions describe what is common to the paradigmatic chemical reactions — combustion, synthesis, decomposition, and the rest. Yet one might observe that the conditions admit processes that chemists would not classify as chemical reactions. Consider the dissolution of sodium chloride in water. The ionic bonds of the crystal lattice are broken. New interactions — ion-dipole forces between the ions and the water molecules — are established. And the resulting saline solution differs in its chemical properties from the dry crystal: it conducts electricity, depresses the freezing point of water, and participates in reactions that the dry crystal does not. All three conditions are apparently met. But chemists regard dissolution as a physical change, not a chemical reaction, because no new chemical species are formed. The sodium and chloride ions persist unchanged; they have merely been dispersed into a new medium. The definition, it appears, does not adequately distinguish the breaking and forming of bonds in a genuine chemical reaction from the disruption and establishment of intermolecular forces in a physical process.

definition: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species without prior learning or experience, (ii) B appears in a complete and functional form the first time it is performed, and (iii) B is invariant — it is performed in the same way by every individual of the species under the same conditions.
conditions: B is exhibited by all normal members without prior learning
B appears in complete functional form the first time
B is invariant across individuals under the same conditions
counterexample: Consider the song of certain bird species. The song is exhibited by all normal males of the species, and the basic template of the song appears without formal instruction — a bird raised in isolation will produce a rudimentary version of the species-typical song. To that extent, conditions (i) and (ii) seem to be met. But the song is not invariant: individual birds produce recognisably different renditions, with local dialects developing through imitation of neighbouring birds. Furthermore, the song does not appear in a fully functional form on the first occasion; young birds produce a crude subsong that is gradually refined through practice and, in many species, through exposure to the songs of adult conspecifics. The behaviour is partly instinctive and partly learnt, and the definition, by requiring complete invariance and immediate functionality, cannot accommodate this mixture.
missing_condition: The definition assumes a sharp dichotomy between instinct and learning, but many behaviours — birdsong being a paradigm case — have both an innate foundation and a learnt component. A condition permitting instinctive behaviour to include a developmental and experiential component, rather than requiring it to appear fully formed and invariant, is needed.
passage: One might propose that a behaviour is instinctive if and only if it is exhibited by all normal members of the species without prior learning, appears in complete functional form the first time it is performed, and is invariant across individuals under the same conditions. The first condition captures the idea that instinct is innate: it does not require teaching or practice. The second condition ensures that the behaviour is fully formed from the outset, distinguishing instinct from skills that are gradually acquired. The third condition ensures stereotypy — the hallmark rigidity that distinguishes instinctive behaviour from flexible, learnt behaviour. These conditions jointly express a conception of instinct as rigid, innate, and universal. Yet one might observe that this conception, though it fits some behaviours admirably — the web-spinning of spiders, the egg-rolling of geese — fails to accommodate others that are equally regarded as instinctive. Consider birdsong. The basic song template is innate: a bird raised in complete isolation will produce a recognisable version of the species-typical song. In this respect, condition (i) is satisfied. But the song does not appear in fully functional form the first time. Young birds produce a crude, formless subsong that is gradually refined over weeks or months, often through exposure to the songs of adult conspecifics. And the song is not invariant: individual birds develop recognisably different versions, and local populations develop dialects. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are violated. Yet birdsong is widely regarded as one of the best examples of instinctive behaviour in the animal kingdom. The definition, it appears, has insisted on too sharp a dichotomy between instinct and learning. Many of the most characteristic instinctive behaviours have a developmental dimension — a period of maturation and refinement — that the definition excludes.

definition: A group of organisms G constitutes a species if and only if (i) the members of G are capable of interbreeding with one another, (ii) the members of G produce fertile offspring when they interbreed, and (iii) the members of G are reproductively isolated from all organisms not in G.
conditions: Members of G are capable of interbreeding
Members of G produce fertile offspring
Members of G are reproductively isolated from non-members
counterexample: Consider the case of ring species, such as the Larus gulls distributed around the Arctic. Adjacent populations interbreed freely and produce fertile offspring. But the terminal populations at the ends of the ring, though connected by a chain of interbreeding populations, cannot themselves interbreed. The chain satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) between neighbours, yet violates condition (iii) since some members of the putative species can breed with organisms outside it. The definition cannot accommodate this continuous gradation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the phenomenon of ring species, where the interbreeding relation is not transitive — adjacent populations interbreed, but distant populations do not — revealing the need for a criterion that accommodates gradual reproductive divergence.
passage: The biological species concept holds that a species is a group of organisms capable of interbreeding, producing fertile offspring, and reproductively isolated from all other organisms. This definition has proved enormously useful in zoology and botany, providing a principled criterion for drawing boundaries between species. Yet it admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider the well-known example of the Larus gulls, distributed in a chain of populations around the Arctic. Each population interbreeds freely with its immediate neighbours, and the offspring are fully fertile. But the chain forms a ring, and the populations at the two ends of the ring — which are geographically adjacent — cannot interbreed at all. If we apply the definition to any two neighbouring populations, conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But the third condition — reproductive isolation from non-members — is violated, since each population can breed with its neighbours, who can in turn breed with theirs, and so on around the ring to populations that are reproductively incompatible with the first. The interbreeding relation is not transitive, and the definition, which presupposes that it is, cannot accommodate this gradual and continuous divergence. What is needed is a criterion that recognises species boundaries as potentially vague, admitting of degrees rather than sharp demarcations.

definition: An entity E is alive if and only if (i) E is capable of metabolism — the conversion of external materials into energy and bodily constituents, (ii) E is capable of reproduction, and (iii) E responds to stimuli from its environment.
conditions: E is capable of metabolism
E is capable of reproduction
E responds to stimuli from its environment
counterexample: Consider a mule — the offspring of a horse and a donkey. A mule metabolises food, responds to stimuli, and exhibits all the hallmarks of a living organism. Yet mules are almost invariably sterile; they cannot reproduce. The second condition is not met. Yet no biologist would deny that a mule is alive. The definition, by requiring reproduction, excludes organisms that are manifestly living.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that reproductive capacity, while characteristic of living things as a class, is not necessary for each individual member of that class, since sterile organisms are plainly alive.
passage: One might define life by requiring metabolism, reproductive capacity, and responsiveness to stimuli. This definition captures a great deal of what we mean when we distinguish the living from the non-living: a bacterium metabolises, reproduces, and responds to chemical gradients; a plant metabolises, reproduces by seed, and responds to light. Yet the definition admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider the mule, an animal produced by crossing a horse with a donkey. The mule is a vigorous and hardy creature. It metabolises food into energy and tissue with admirable efficiency. It responds to stimuli — to the voice of its handler, to changes in terrain, to the approach of predators. But the mule is, with exceedingly rare exceptions, sterile. It cannot reproduce. The second condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet it would be absurd to conclude that a mule is not alive. The animal eats, breathes, moves, grows, heals its wounds, and eventually dies — all activities that we associate exclusively with living things. What the definition overlooks is that reproductive capacity, though characteristic of living organisms as a class, is not a necessary condition for each individual organism. A more adequate analysis must allow that an entity may be fully alive while lacking the capacity to reproduce.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to give it a roughly spherical shape, and (iii) B is the dominant gravitational body in its orbital region.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass for a roughly spherical shape
B is the dominant gravitational body in its orbital region
counterexample: Consider a body of planetary mass — larger than Earth — ejected from its stellar system by gravitational interactions early in its formation. It drifts through interstellar space, spherical, massive, geologically active, possessing an atmosphere retained by its own gravity. It satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii) trivially — it is the only body in its vicinity. But it does not orbit a star. The first condition is not met, yet the object possesses every intrinsic property we associate with planethood.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate rogue planets — bodies of planetary mass and composition that have been ejected from their stellar systems and no longer orbit any star, yet retain every intrinsic characteristic of a planet.
passage: A widely accepted analysis holds that a planet is a celestial body that orbits a star, has sufficient mass for a roughly spherical shape, and dominates its orbital neighbourhood. This analysis has the virtue of providing clear criteria that distinguish planets from asteroids, comets, and other small bodies. Yet there is a case which reveals its insufficiency. Astrophysical simulations and, increasingly, observational evidence suggest the existence of free-floating bodies of planetary mass — objects ejected from their natal stellar systems by gravitational perturbations during formation. Such an object may be larger than Earth, roughly spherical under its own gravity, geologically active, and possessed of a substantial atmosphere. It satisfies the second and third conditions: it is spherical, and it is trivially dominant in its orbital region, there being no other bodies nearby. But it does not orbit a star, and so the first condition is not met. The definition compels us to deny planethood to an object that possesses every intrinsic property we associate with planets — mass, shape, composition, geological activity, atmosphere. What is wanted is an analysis that distinguishes planets by their intrinsic physical characteristics rather than by the contingent circumstance of their orbital arrangement.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) S consists of atoms all having the same number of protons, and (iii) S is stable under standard conditions of temperature and pressure.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means
S consists of atoms with the same number of protons
S is stable under standard conditions
counterexample: Consider francium, which consists of atoms all having eighty-seven protons. It cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means. Yet francium is profoundly unstable — its most long-lived isotope has a half-life of approximately twenty-two minutes. Under standard conditions, any sample of francium will have largely decayed before it can be examined at leisure. The third condition is not met, yet francium is undeniably a chemical element; it occupies a place in the periodic table and exhibits the chemical behaviour characteristic of the alkali metals.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously includes a stability requirement that excludes highly radioactive elements such as francium, which are chemical elements despite their fleeting existence under standard conditions.
passage: One might define a chemical element as a substance that cannot be decomposed by chemical means, consists of atoms sharing a common proton number, and is stable under standard conditions. This definition captures the paradigmatic elements — iron, oxygen, gold — which are indeed chemically indecomposable, defined by their atomic number, and stable under ordinary conditions. Yet the definition admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider francium, the heaviest of the alkali metals. Francium consists of atoms all possessing eighty-seven protons, and it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical reaction. But francium is spectacularly unstable: its longest-lived isotope, francium-223, has a half-life of roughly twenty-two minutes. A macroscopic sample of francium under standard conditions would be in rapid decay, transmuting itself into other elements through radioactive processes almost as quickly as it could be assembled. The third condition of the definition is plainly not satisfied. Yet francium is, beyond any doubt, a chemical element — it occupies its proper place in the periodic table and exhibits the chemical properties predicted by its position among the alkali metals. What the definition overlooks is that elementhood is determined by atomic number, not by macroscopic stability, and that an element may be perfectly genuine while being profoundly short-lived.

definition: A condition D is a disease if and only if (i) D involves a departure from the normal functioning of an organism, (ii) D causes suffering or impairment to the organism, and (iii) D has a specific aetiology — an identifiable causal agent or process.
conditions: D involves departure from normal functioning
D causes suffering or impairment
D has a specific identifiable aetiology
counterexample: Consider essential hypertension — chronically elevated blood pressure with no identifiable underlying cause. It departs from normal physiological functioning and, if left untreated, causes significant impairment: damage to the heart, kidneys, and blood vessels. The first two conditions are met. But essential hypertension is, by definition, hypertension without an identifiable specific cause. The third condition is not satisfied. Yet no physician would hesitate to call it a disease.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for diseases whose aetiology is unknown or multifactorial, thereby excluding conditions that are manifestly pathological yet lack a single identifiable causal agent.
passage: One might analyse the concept of disease by requiring a departure from normal functioning, the causation of suffering or impairment, and a specific identifiable aetiology. This analysis captures many paradigmatic diseases: tuberculosis involves departure from normal pulmonary function, causes suffering, and has a specific aetiology in the tubercle bacillus; diabetes involves departure from normal metabolic function, causes impairment, and has identifiable aetiological mechanisms. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by essential hypertension. Essential hypertension is chronically elevated blood pressure for which no specific underlying cause can be identified — the word 'essential' in this context means precisely 'without known cause.' The condition departs from normal cardiovascular functioning. If untreated, it causes severe impairment: damage to the heart, kidneys, vasculature, and brain. The first two conditions are met. But the third is not — essential hypertension has, by definition, no identifiable specific aetiology. It appears to arise from a complex interplay of genetic, environmental, and behavioural factors, none of which can be singled out as the cause. Yet no one in medicine doubts that essential hypertension is a disease. The definition, by requiring a specific aetiology, excludes a condition that is manifestly pathological. What is needed is an analysis that accommodates diseases of unknown or multifactorial causation.

definition: A system S constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) S comprises a community of living organisms and their physical environment, (ii) the organisms in S interact through nutrient cycling and energy transfer, and (iii) S is spatially bounded — that is, S occupies a determinate region.
conditions: S comprises living organisms and their physical environment
Organisms interact through nutrient cycling and energy transfer
S is spatially bounded
counterexample: Consider the open ocean — the pelagic zone far from any coast or seafloor feature. It contains living organisms — plankton, fish, marine mammals — interacting through nutrient cycling and energy transfer. Yet the open ocean has no determinate spatial boundaries; it shades continuously into adjacent waters with no natural demarcation. The third condition is not met. Yet marine ecologists study the pelagic zone as an ecosystem without apology.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate ecosystems whose boundaries are diffuse, gradient-like, or conventionally imposed rather than naturally determinate, thereby excluding open systems that ecologists routinely study.
passage: A common analysis of ecosystem requires a community of organisms and their physical environment, interactions through nutrient cycling and energy transfer, and determinate spatial boundaries. This analysis captures many familiar examples: a pond, a forest, a coral reef — each comprises organisms and their physical setting, features nutrient cycling and energy flow, and occupies a recognisable bounded region. Yet the analysis admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider the pelagic zone of the open ocean — the vast expanse of water far from any coast, island, or seafloor feature. It contains a rich community of organisms, from photosynthetic phytoplankton at the surface to predatory fish and whales in the depths. These organisms interact through nutrient cycling and energy transfer in ways that ecologists study with great care. The first two conditions are met. But the open ocean has no natural spatial boundaries. It shades continuously into adjacent bodies of water; one cannot draw a line on the map and say 'here the pelagic ecosystem ends.' The third condition is not satisfied. Yet marine ecologists do not hesitate to speak of the pelagic ecosystem and to study it as a functioning ecological unit. The definition's insistence upon determinate spatial boundaries reflects a bias toward terrestrial systems with visible edges — the pond, the forest — and cannot accommodate the diffuse, boundary-less systems that are ubiquitous in marine ecology.

definition: A segment of DNA D constitutes a gene if and only if (i) D encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a single polypeptide chain, (ii) D occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome, and (iii) D is transmitted from parent to offspring as a discrete unit.
conditions: D encodes information for synthesis of a single polypeptide
D occupies a fixed chromosomal locus
D is transmitted as a discrete unit
counterexample: Consider a segment of DNA that, through alternative splicing, gives rise to multiple distinct polypeptide chains depending on which exons are included in the messenger RNA. The segment occupies a fixed locus and is transmitted as a unit. But it does not encode a single polypeptide — it encodes several. The first condition is not met. Yet molecular biologists would not deny that it is a gene; indeed, alternative splicing is now understood to be the rule rather than the exception in complex organisms.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the phenomenon of alternative splicing, whereby a single segment of DNA may encode multiple polypeptides, revealing that the one-gene-one-polypeptide correspondence is not a necessary feature of genes.
passage: One might define a gene as a segment of DNA that encodes a single polypeptide, occupies a fixed chromosomal locus, and is transmitted as a discrete unit from parent to offspring. This definition captures the classical picture of the gene that prevailed for much of the twentieth century: one gene, one polypeptide; a gene as a bead on the chromosomal string, passed down through the generations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis has become increasingly prominent in molecular biology. Consider a segment of DNA in a complex organism — a metazoan, say — that undergoes alternative splicing. The primary transcript produced from this segment may be processed in several different ways, with different combinations of exons included in the mature messenger RNA. Each combination directs the synthesis of a different polypeptide chain. The segment occupies a fixed chromosomal locus and is transmitted as a discrete unit. But it does not encode a single polypeptide; it encodes several, sometimes with quite different functions. The first condition of the definition is not met. Yet no molecular biologist would deny that this segment is a gene. Indeed, alternative splicing is now recognised as pervasive in complex organisms — the human genome encodes far more proteins than it has genes, precisely because many genes produce multiple polypeptides. The one-gene-one-polypeptide picture, though historically important, is no longer adequate.

definition: A physical quantity F acting upon a body B constitutes a force if and only if (i) F causes a change in B's state of motion — an acceleration, (ii) F is measurable in magnitude and direction, and (iii) F is exerted by one body upon another through physical contact or a field.
conditions: F causes a change in state of motion
F is measurable in magnitude and direction
F is exerted through contact or a field
counterexample: Consider a book resting on a table. The table exerts a normal force upon the book, directed upward. This force is measurable in magnitude and direction and is exerted through physical contact. Yet the book does not accelerate — it remains at rest. The force causes no change in the book's state of motion, because it is exactly balanced by gravity. The first condition is not met, yet the normal force is genuinely a force.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate forces that are balanced by other forces and therefore produce no acceleration, conflating the presence of a force with the production of a net change in motion.
passage: A natural analysis of force requires that a force cause a change in a body's state of motion, be measurable in magnitude and direction, and be exerted through contact or a field. This analysis captures the paradigmatic cases — the push that sets a ball rolling, the gravitational pull that curves a planet's path. Yet it admits of a case which reveals its insufficiency. Consider a book resting upon a table. The table exerts a normal force upon the book, directed vertically upward. This force is perfectly measurable: its magnitude equals the weight of the book, and its direction is perpendicular to the table's surface. It is exerted through physical contact between the book and the table. The second and third conditions are satisfied. But the book does not accelerate. It remains perfectly at rest, because the upward normal force is exactly balanced by the downward gravitational force. The first condition — that the force cause a change in the body's state of motion — is not met. Yet no physicist would deny that the normal force is a genuine force. What the definition overlooks is that forces may exist in equilibrium, balancing one another so that no net change in motion occurs. The presence of a force is not to be identified with the production of acceleration; acceleration results only when the net force is non-zero.

definition: A trait T of an organism O is an adaptation if and only if (i) T enhances O's fitness in its current environment, (ii) T was produced by natural selection, and (iii) T is heritable.
conditions: T enhances O's fitness in its current environment
T was produced by natural selection
T is heritable
counterexample: Consider the vestigial hind limb bones found in some species of whale. These bones are heritable and were produced by natural selection — they are remnants of the functional hind limbs of the whales' terrestrial ancestors. Yet they do not enhance the whale's fitness in its current aquatic environment; they serve no present function. The first condition is not met, yet biologists regard vestigial hind limbs as adaptations — adaptations of an ancestral organism to a terrestrial environment that persist as relics in the descendant.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow that a trait may be an adaptation with respect to a past environment, even though it confers no fitness advantage in the organism's current environment, thereby conflating present utility with adaptive origin.
passage: One might define an adaptation as a heritable trait that enhances an organism's fitness and was produced by natural selection. This definition captures the standard cases: the hawk's keen vision enhances its fitness, was shaped by selection, and is heritable; the cactus's water-storing tissues enhance its fitness in arid environments, were selected for, and are inherited. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider vestigial structures. Certain species of whale possess small, internally embedded bones that are the remnants of the hind limbs of their terrestrial ancestors. These bones are heritable — they are genetically determined and passed from parent to offspring. They were produced by natural selection, in the sense that they are the residue of limbs that were adaptations in ancestral organisms. But they do not enhance the whale's fitness in its current environment. They serve no locomotor function; the whale swims by means of its flukes and flippers. The first condition of the definition is not met. Yet biologists do not deny that these structures are adaptations — they are adaptations of the ancestral organism to a terrestrial environment, persisting as vestiges in the descendant. The definition, by requiring present fitness enhancement, cannot accommodate the temporal dimension of adaptation. What is needed is an analysis that recognises a trait may be an adaptation by virtue of its selective history, regardless of its current utility.

definition: A species S has undergone extinction if and only if (i) every individual member of S has died, (ii) no individual capable of producing offspring of the species S remains, and (iii) the disappearance of S is permanent and irreversible.
conditions: Every individual member of S has died
No individual capable of producing S offspring remains
The disappearance is permanent and irreversible
counterexample: Consider the coelacanth, a fish believed to have been extinct for approximately sixty-five million years on the basis of a fossil record that showed no specimens more recent than the Cretaceous. In 1938, a living coelacanth was caught off the coast of South Africa. For decades, the scientific community had treated the coelacanth as extinct — conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) were believed to be met. The species had not, in fact, undergone extinction, though every available criterion said that it had. The definition provides no criterion for distinguishing genuine extinction from what might be called apparent extinction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify how the absence of living members is to be established with certainty, given that a species may persist undetected in remote or unexplored habitats, and thus provides no criterion for distinguishing confirmed from apparent extinction.
passage: One might propose that a species has undergone extinction when every individual member has died, no individual capable of producing offspring remains, and the disappearance is permanent. This proposal appears straightforward and captures the paradigmatic cases — the dodo, the passenger pigeon — where the last known individual died and no recovery is possible. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the celebrated example of the coelacanth. The coelacanth was known exclusively from the fossil record, the most recent specimens dating to the late Cretaceous, some sixty-five million years ago. The scientific community accepted the species as extinct with the same confidence it accepted the extinction of the ammonites or the non-avian dinosaurs. Every condition of the definition appeared satisfied: all individuals were believed to have died, no breeding population was thought to exist, and the disappearance was taken to be permanent. Then, in 1938, a living coelacanth was caught off the coast of South Africa. The species had persisted, undetected, in the deep waters of the Indian Ocean. The definition provides no criterion for establishing with certainty that every individual has died and that no breeding population remains. In the absence of exhaustive surveillance of every habitat on Earth, the conditions can be believed to hold when they do not, and the definition cannot distinguish genuine extinction from apparent extinction.

definition: A group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if (i) its members are capable of interbreeding with one another, (ii) they produce fertile offspring, and (iii) they are reproductively isolated from members of other such groups.
conditions: Members are capable of interbreeding
They produce fertile offspring
They are reproductively isolated from other groups
counterexample: Consider the case of ring species, such as the herring gull and lesser black-backed gull complex around the Arctic. Adjacent populations interbreed freely and produce fertile offspring, forming a chain that encircles the Arctic. Yet the populations at the two ends of the chain — where they meet in Northern Europe — do not interbreed, despite being connected by a continuous series of interbreeding populations. The chain satisfies the definition pairwise at each link, but the endpoints fail condition (iii) with respect to their neighbours elsewhere in the chain. The definition cannot assign these organisms to species in a consistent manner.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that reproductive isolation is a transitive relation — that if A breeds with B and B breeds with C, then A and C belong to the same species — but ring species demonstrate that interbreeding capacity can be non-transitive.
passage: One might define a species as a group of organisms whose members can interbreed, produce fertile offspring, and are reproductively isolated from other such groups. This definition — often called the biological species concept — has considerable authority and captures the way in which species boundaries are delineated in the paradigmatic cases: horses and donkeys produce mules, which are sterile, and this sterility marks the boundary between the two species. Dogs interbreed freely with other dogs but not with cats, and this reproductive isolation confirms their specific distinctness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the phenomenon of ring species. The herring gull and lesser black-backed gull complex forms a chain of populations encircling the Arctic. Each population interbreeds with its immediate neighbour, producing fertile offspring, so that the chain appears to belong to a single species. But the populations at the two ends of the chain — where they meet in Northern Europe — do not interbreed; they behave as distinct species. The definition cannot accommodate this situation. If we follow the chain of interbreeding populations, all members belong to the same species. If we attend to the reproductive isolation of the endpoints, they belong to different species. The definition presupposes that reproductive compatibility is transitive — that if population A breeds with B, and B with C, then A and C belong to the same species. Ring species show that this presupposition is false. One might observe that the biological species concept, for all its utility, cannot accommodate the continuous gradations of reproductive isolation that arise when speciation is in progress rather than complete.

definition: A trait T in a population has evolved by natural selection if and only if (i) there is heritable variation in T among members of the population, (ii) individuals with certain variants of T survive and reproduce at higher rates than individuals with other variants, and (iii) the frequency of the favoured variant increases over successive generations.
conditions: There is heritable variation in T
Certain variants of T confer higher survival and reproductive rates
The frequency of the favoured variant increases over generations
counterexample: Consider a population of beetles in which darker individuals are better camouflaged against predators and thus survive at higher rates. There is heritable variation in colour, and the darker variant confers a survival advantage. But the population is very small, and by chance the darker individuals happen to be struck by a falling tree, so that it is the lighter variant whose frequency increases over the next generations. All three conditions are met — there was variation, differential survival, and a frequency change — but the frequency change was produced by the accident rather than by the selective advantage. The trait did not evolve by natural selection; it evolved by drift, despite satisfying the definition.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the increase in frequency be caused by the differential survival and reproduction rather than by chance events, and thus cannot distinguish selection from drift when both produce the same directional change.
passage: One might propose that a trait has evolved by natural selection if there is heritable variation, individuals with certain variants survive and reproduce at higher rates, and the favoured variant increases in frequency over generations. This definition captures the logic of natural selection as it operates in textbook cases: the darkening of peppered moths during industrialisation, the spread of antibiotic resistance in bacteria, the elongation of the giraffe's neck. In each case, heritable variation exists, one variant is favoured by the environment, and its frequency increases. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition may be constructed by attending to the role of chance. Consider a small population of beetles in which darker individuals are better camouflaged against predators. There is heritable variation in pigmentation, and the darker variant confers a genuine survival advantage.
