But the population is small — a few hundred individuals — and a chance event, such as a flood that disproportionately affects the area where the darker beetles happen to reside, eliminates many of them. The lighter beetles, sheltered by the accident of geography, increase in frequency. Alternatively, suppose the darker beetles do increase in frequency, but this increase is caused by a chance fluctuation in birth rates rather than by the camouflage advantage. In both cases, the three conditions may be formally satisfied — variation exists, one variant has higher fitness, and a frequency change occurs — but the frequency change is not caused by the fitness difference. It is produced by drift. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that variation, differential fitness, and frequency change coexist, fails to require the causal connection between differential fitness and frequency change that is the essence of natural selection.

definition: A process P is a chemical reaction if and only if (i) P involves the breaking and forming of chemical bonds between atoms, (ii) the products of P differ in composition from the reactants, and (iii) P is accompanied by a measurable change in energy — either the release or absorption of heat.
conditions: P involves breaking and forming of chemical bonds
Products differ in composition from reactants
P is accompanied by a measurable energy change
counterexample: Consider the dissolution of sodium chloride in water. The ionic bonds between sodium and chloride ions are broken as the crystal lattice dissolves, and new interactions — ion-dipole bonds — form between the ions and the surrounding water molecules. The composition of the solution differs from that of the dry salt and the pure water considered separately. There is a measurable energy change — the process is slightly endothermic. All three conditions are met. Yet chemists do not classify dissolution as a chemical reaction; they classify it as a physical process. The sodium and chloride ions are not transformed into different chemical species; they are merely dispersed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the atoms be rearranged into new molecular species — that is, that the chemical identity of at least one substance be changed — and thus cannot distinguish genuine chemical reactions from physical processes such as dissolution that involve bond changes without species transformation.
passage: It appears natural to define a chemical reaction as a process that involves the breaking and forming of bonds, that produces substances differing in composition from the reactants, and that is accompanied by a measurable change in energy. This definition accommodates the familiar reactions of the chemistry laboratory: the combustion of hydrogen in oxygen produces water, with the breaking of H-H and O=O bonds and the forming of O-H bonds, a new substance, and a considerable release of heat. The neutralisation of an acid by a base produces a salt and water, with analogous bond changes and energy release. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the simple dissolution of table salt in water. When sodium chloride is added to water, the ionic bonds holding the crystal lattice together are broken. New interactions form between the liberated ions and the polar water molecules — ion-dipole interactions that are properly described as a form of chemical bonding. The resulting solution differs in composition from the separate reactants: it contains hydrated sodium and chloride ions rather than a crystal and pure water. The process is accompanied by a measurable energy change — it is slightly endothermic. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet no chemist would classify the dissolution of salt in water as a chemical reaction. It is a physical process. The sodium and chloride ions have not been transformed into different species; they have merely been dispersed from a lattice into a solution. One might observe that what distinguishes a chemical reaction from a mere physical change is not the breaking and forming of bonds, nor the change in composition, nor the energy change, but the transformation of at least one substance into a chemically distinct species — and this the definition does not require.

definition: A collection of organisms and their physical environment constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) the organisms interact with one another through feeding relationships, (ii) the organisms interact with the physical environment through exchanges of energy and matter, and (iii) the collection is self-sustaining over ecologically relevant timescales.
conditions: Organisms interact through feeding relationships
Organisms interact with the physical environment through energy and matter exchanges
The collection is self-sustaining over relevant timescales
counterexample: Consider the community of organisms that inhabits a deep-sea hydrothermal vent. The organisms — tube worms, chemosynthetic bacteria, crabs, and various other species — interact through feeding relationships and exchange energy and matter with the physical environment. But the vent itself has a lifespan of only decades to centuries, after which the flow of mineral-rich water ceases and the community perishes. The collection is not self-sustaining over ecologically relevant timescales; it is dependent upon a geological process that is inherently temporary. Yet ecologists universally regard hydrothermal vent communities as ecosystems — indeed, as among the most remarkable ecosystems on Earth.
missing_condition: The definition requires self-sustainability, but many recognised ecosystems — vents, ephemeral pools, volcanic island communities — are inherently transient, sustained only so long as an external geological or hydrological process continues to supply their energy source.
passage: One might define an ecosystem as a collection of organisms and their physical environment in which the organisms interact through feeding relationships, exchange energy and matter with the environment, and the whole is self-sustaining over ecologically relevant timescales. This definition captures the features of the paradigmatic ecosystems — a temperate forest, a coral reef, a freshwater lake — in which a stable community of producers, consumers, and decomposers cycles nutrients and energy through a persistent physical setting. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by hydrothermal vent communities. At the mid-ocean ridges, superheated water laden with minerals issues from fissures in the sea floor. Around these vents, dense communities of organisms flourish: chemosynthetic bacteria that derive energy from hydrogen sulphide, tube worms that harbour these bacteria, crabs and shrimp that graze upon microbial mats, and various predators. The organisms interact through feeding relationships and exchange energy and matter with the vent environment. But the vents themselves are temporary geological features. A given vent may be active for decades or a few centuries before the flow of mineral-rich water ceases, at which point the community collapses. The collection is not self-sustaining over ecologically relevant timescales; it is entirely dependent upon a transient geological process. The definition excludes it from the category of ecosystem. Yet ecologists regard vent communities as ecosystems without hesitation, and they are studied as such in every major textbook of marine ecology. One might observe that the definition, by requiring self-sustainability, has been constructed from the model of stable, persistent systems and cannot accommodate the transient but ecologically genuine communities that depend upon impermanent energy sources.

definition: A segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if (i) it encodes a polypeptide chain — that is, a protein or a subunit of a protein, (ii) it is a contiguous stretch of nucleotides on a chromosome, and (iii) it is transmitted as a unit from parent to offspring.
conditions: It encodes a polypeptide chain
It is a contiguous stretch of nucleotides
It is transmitted as a unit from parent to offspring
counterexample: Consider the genes in eukaryotic organisms that contain introns — stretches of non-coding DNA interspersed within the coding sequence. The gene is not, strictly speaking, a contiguous stretch of coding nucleotides; it is interrupted by introns that are transcribed but then excised before the messenger RNA is translated into a polypeptide. Condition (ii) is violated if we understand contiguity as applying to the coding sequence, or satisfied only trivially if we include the non-coding introns. Moreover, some genes encode functional RNA molecules — ribosomal RNA, transfer RNA — rather than polypeptide chains, violating condition (i) while being universally recognised as genes.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that genes are contiguous coding sequences for proteins, but the discovery of introns and of genes encoding functional RNA molecules shows that neither contiguity nor protein-coding is a necessary feature of genes.
passage: It appears reasonable to define a gene as a contiguous stretch of DNA that encodes a polypeptide chain and is transmitted as a unit from parent to offspring. This definition captures what was long regarded as the fundamental unit of heredity: a segment of DNA whose sequence specifies a protein, passed intact from one generation to the next. In the simplest organisms — bacteria, for instance — this definition works admirably: bacterial genes are contiguous, protein-coding, and transmitted as units. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the structure of genes in eukaryotic organisms. When the fine structure of eukaryotic genes was elucidated, it was discovered that the coding sequences are typically interrupted by stretches of non-coding DNA called introns. The gene, considered as a stretch of chromosome, includes both the coding exons and the non-coding introns. The introns are transcribed into RNA but are then excised before the messenger RNA is translated into a polypeptide. The gene is not a contiguous coding sequence in the sense the definition requires; it is a mosaic of coding and non-coding segments. Furthermore, some genes do not encode polypeptide chains at all. The genes for ribosomal RNA and transfer RNA encode functional RNA molecules that are never translated into protein, yet they are universally recognised as genes. Condition (i) excludes them. One might observe that the definition was constructed upon the model of the bacterial gene and that the complexities of eukaryotic molecular biology — introns, RNA genes, alternative splicing — have revealed it to be an oversimplification that mistakes one form of genetic organisation for the universal case.

definition: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
conditions: B is exhibited by all normal members of the species
B appears without prior learning
B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern
counterexample: Consider birdsong in many passerine species. The basic song template is innate — a young bird raised in isolation will produce a rudimentary version of the species-typical song. It appears without explicit training, and it is universal among normal males of the species. But the song is not performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern. Young birds must hear adult song during a critical period in order to develop the full, species-typical version, and individual birds develop recognisable variations — local dialects and personal flourishes. Condition (iii) fails. Yet birdsong is universally regarded as an instinctive behaviour, albeit one that requires environmental input for its full expression.
missing_condition: The definition requires fixed, stereotyped performance, but many instinctive behaviours — including birdsong, nest-building, and foraging — exhibit individual variation and require environmental input for their full development, without thereby ceasing to be instinctive.
passage: One might propose that a behaviour is instinctive if it is exhibited by all normal members of the species, appears without learning, and is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern. This definition captures the behaviours most readily identified as instinctive: the web-spinning of spiders, the egg-rolling of greylag geese, the suckling reflex of mammalian neonates. In each case, the behaviour is universal, unlearned, and performed in a characteristic, invariant manner. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by birdsong in passerine species. The basic song template is innate: a young bird raised in acoustic isolation will produce a simplified but recognisable version of the species-typical song. The song appears without explicit training — no bird teaches another to sing in the way a human teaches a child to read. It is exhibited by all normal males of the species. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But the song is not performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern. Young birds must hear adult song during a sensitive period to develop the full, elaborated version of the song, and individual birds develop personal variations — local dialects that differ from one valley to the next, and individual flourishes that allow experienced listeners to identify particular birds. Condition (iii) fails. Yet birdsong is universally classified as instinctive by ethologists. One might observe that the definition has been built from the model of the simple, invariant reflex — the knee-jerk, the pupillary response — and that many of the most important instinctive behaviours in nature involve an innate template that is refined by experience, admitting of considerable individual variation while remaining unmistakably species-typical.

definition: A trait T in an organism O is an adaptation if and only if (i) T enhances O's fitness in O's current environment, (ii) T was produced by natural selection operating on heritable variation, and (iii) T is currently maintained in the population because of the fitness advantage it confers.
conditions: T enhances fitness in the current environment
T was produced by natural selection
T is currently maintained because of its fitness advantage
counterexample: Consider the human appendix, which is a vestigial remnant of the caecum found in herbivorous ancestors. The appendix was produced by natural selection in ancestral species, where it served a digestive function. It may confer a modest fitness advantage in modern humans — some evidence suggests it serves as a reservoir for beneficial gut bacteria. It is maintained in the population, at least in part, because removing it confers no significant advantage and it causes harm only occasionally. Yet biologists do not classify the appendix as an adaptation to the current environment; they classify it as a vestigial structure that has been co-opted or tolerated. The definition, however, would count it as an adaptation if even a slight current fitness benefit can be demonstrated.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the current fitness benefit be the reason for which the trait originally evolved, and thus conflates traits that are genuine adaptations to present conditions with vestigial structures that happen to confer incidental benefits.
passage: It appears natural to define an adaptation as a trait that enhances fitness in the organism's current environment, was produced by natural selection, and is currently maintained because of the fitness advantage it confers. This definition captures the paradigmatic adaptations: the cheetah's speed, the chameleon's colour change, the bat's echolocation. Each enhances fitness in the current environment, was shaped by selection, and is maintained by its ongoing advantage. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider vestigial structures that have acquired incidental secondary functions. The human appendix is a vestigial remnant of the large caecum found in herbivorous ancestors. It was produced by natural selection in those ancestors, where it served a digestive function that it no longer performs in humans. Recent research suggests that it may confer a modest fitness benefit in modern humans by serving as a reservoir for beneficial intestinal bacteria, aiding in the repopulation of the gut after illness. If this is correct, then the appendix enhances fitness in the current environment, was produced by natural selection (albeit for a different function), and may be maintained in part because of the current benefit. All three conditions are met. Yet no biologist would classify the human appendix as an adaptation in the same sense as echolocation or colour change. It is a vestigial organ that has been tolerated by selection and may have acquired a secondary benefit. One might observe that the definition fails to distinguish between traits that were selected for their current function and traits that were selected for a different function in an ancestral environment and merely happen to be useful now.

definition: An entity E is a cell if and only if (i) E is enclosed by a membrane that separates its internal contents from the external environment, (ii) E contains genetic material in the form of DNA, and (iii) E is capable of independent metabolic activity — that is, it can carry out chemical reactions that sustain its own existence.
conditions: E is enclosed by a membrane
E contains DNA
E is capable of independent metabolic activity
counterexample: Consider the mature mammalian red blood cell (erythrocyte). It is enclosed by a membrane and was produced from a precursor cell that contained DNA. But in the course of its maturation, the erythrocyte expels its nucleus and all its DNA. It retains the membrane and carries out metabolic activity — glycolysis, for instance — but it contains no genetic material. Condition (ii) is violated. Yet the erythrocyte is universally classified as a cell by biologists and medical scientists. It is counted in blood cell counts, studied in cell biology, and treated as a cell in every relevant context.
missing_condition: The definition requires that every cell contain DNA, but certain specialised cells — most notably mature mammalian erythrocytes — lose their nuclear DNA during maturation while remaining genuine cells in every other respect.
passage: One might propose that an entity is a cell if and only if it is enclosed by a membrane, contains DNA, and is capable of independent metabolic activity. This definition captures the essential features of the paradigmatic cells: a bacterium has a membrane, contains a circular chromosome of DNA, and carries out the metabolic reactions necessary for its survival. A human liver cell has a membrane, contains DNA in its nucleus, and performs the complex metabolic functions characteristic of hepatic tissue. The three conditions work together to distinguish cells from other biological structures — viruses lack independent metabolism, lipid vesicles lack DNA, and free DNA lacks a membrane. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the mature mammalian red blood cell. During the process of erythropoiesis, the developing red blood cell expels its nucleus and, with it, all of its DNA. The mature erythrocyte that enters the circulation is a membrane-bounded entity capable of metabolic activity — it carries out glycolysis to generate the energy needed to maintain its shape and function. But it contains no DNA. Condition (ii) is violated. Yet no biologist would deny that the erythrocyte is a cell. It is counted as such in clinical blood tests, discussed as such in textbooks of cell biology, and treated as such in haematological research. One might observe that the definition has been constructed from the model of the nucleated cell and does not accommodate the specialised cells that have sacrificed their genetic material in the course of differentiation, achieving a streamlined form adapted to a single function.

definition: An object F is a fossil if and only if (i) F is the preserved remains or trace of a once-living organism, (ii) F has undergone mineralisation — the replacement of organic material by minerals, and (iii) F is found in sedimentary rock.
conditions: F is preserved remains or trace of a once-living organism
F has undergone mineralisation
F is found in sedimentary rock
counterexample: Consider the insects preserved in amber — fossilised tree resin — found in deposits across the Baltic region. These specimens are the preserved remains of once-living organisms and are millions of years old. They are universally classified as fossils by palaeontologists. But they have not undergone mineralisation; the organic material of the insect's body has been preserved by the resin, which has acted as a sealant rather than a mineralising agent. Nor are they found in sedimentary rock; they are found in amber, which is an organic substance. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are violated, yet the specimens are paradigmatic fossils.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that all fossils are mineralised and found in sedimentary rock, but preservation in amber, tar pits, ice, and peat bogs produces genuine fossils by different mechanisms and in different geological contexts.
passage: It appears reasonable to define a fossil as the preserved remains or trace of a once-living organism that has undergone mineralisation and is found in sedimentary rock. This definition captures the standard cases encountered in palaeontology: a trilobite preserved in limestone, a fern leaf impression in shale, a dinosaur bone in sandstone. In each case, the original organic material has been replaced by minerals over geological time, and the specimen is recovered from sedimentary rock. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is provided by the celebrated amber fossils of the Baltic region. Insects trapped in tree resin millions of years ago have been preserved in extraordinary detail — their wings, legs, and even internal structures are visible. These specimens are universally classified as fossils; they appear in palaeontological journals, museum collections, and textbooks of evolutionary biology. But they have not undergone mineralisation. The resin has sealed the insect from the environment, preventing decay, but the organic material has not been replaced by minerals. Furthermore, the specimens are not found in sedimentary rock but in amber, which is itself a fossilised organic substance. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are both violated. Similar difficulties arise with organisms preserved in tar pits, in permafrost, and in peat bogs — all of which are recognised as fossils but do not fit the definition. One might observe that the definition has been built upon the most common mode of fossilisation and has mistaken the usual mechanism for a necessary one, when in fact any natural process that preserves the remains or traces of an organism over geological timescales produces a fossil.

definition: A region R has a particular climate C if and only if (i) the average temperature, precipitation, and wind patterns in R over a period of at least thirty years correspond to the parameters of C, (ii) these averages are stable — that is, they do not exhibit a systematic trend over the period, and (iii) the parameters of C are determined by R's latitude, altitude, and proximity to large bodies of water.
conditions: Average temperature, precipitation, and wind over thirty years correspond to C
These averages are stable over the period
The parameters are determined by latitude, altitude, and proximity to water
counterexample: Consider the climate of a region downwind of a large industrial complex that has been in operation for several decades. The particulate emissions from the complex have altered local cloud formation, increasing rainfall substantially over the surrounding area. The thirty-year averages of precipitation in this region are stable — the increase occurred before the measurement period began — and they correspond to the parameters of a wet maritime climate. But the climate is not determined by latitude, altitude, and proximity to water; it is determined in significant part by the industrial emissions. Condition (iii) fails. Yet meteorologists would not deny that the region has a climate; they would describe it as a climate modified by anthropogenic factors.
missing_condition: The definition restricts climatic determinants to natural geographical factors, but human activity can durably alter the climatic parameters of a region, producing a genuine climate that the definition cannot accommodate.
passage: One might propose that a region has a particular climate if its thirty-year averages of temperature, precipitation, and wind correspond to the parameters of that climate, these averages are stable, and the parameters are determined by latitude, altitude, and proximity to large bodies of water. This definition captures what is standardly meant by climate in the paradigmatic cases: the Mediterranean climate is warm and dry in summer and mild and wet in winter, these averages are stable, and they are determined by the region's latitude and its proximity to the sea. The definition distinguishes climate from weather by insisting on thirty-year averages and stability, and it grounds climate in geography. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by regions whose climatic parameters have been durably altered by human activity. Consider a region situated downwind of a large industrial complex that has operated for half a century. The particulate emissions from the complex have persistently altered local cloud formation and increased precipitation over the surrounding countryside. The thirty-year averages are stable — the industrial influence predates the measurement period — and the averages correspond to the parameters of a wet climate. But the climate is not determined by latitude, altitude, and proximity to water alone; it is determined in significant part by the industrial emissions. The definition cannot accommodate this case because condition (iii) restricts the determinants of climate to natural geographical factors. One might observe that the definition was formulated for a world in which climate was shaped solely by geography, and that it cannot accommodate the durable anthropogenic modifications that characterise the contemporary era.

definition: A trait T in an organism O is an adaptation if and only if (i) T enhances O's fitness in its current environment, (ii) T arose through a process of natural selection, and (iii) T is heritable.
conditions: T enhances O's fitness in its current environment
T arose through a process of natural selection
T is heritable
counterexample: Consider the capacity of certain bacteria to metabolise a novel synthetic antibiotic introduced into the environment within the past decade. This capacity enhances the bacteria's fitness in hospital environments, it is heritable, and it was selected for by the selective pressure of the antibiotic. All three conditions are met. Yet biologists would hesitate to call this an adaptation in the classical sense; it is better described as a recently acquired resistance, for the term 'adaptation' typically implies a trait shaped over many generations by sustained selective pressure rather than a rapid response to a novel challenge.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the trait has been shaped by sustained selective pressure over a significant number of generations, thereby conflating rapid evolutionary responses with the long-term adaptations that the concept is ordinarily intended to capture.
passage: One might define an adaptation as a heritable trait that enhances an organism's fitness in its current environment and that arose through natural selection. This definition captures the canonical examples: the long neck of the giraffe, the camouflage of the peppered moth, the streamlined form of the dolphin. Each enhances fitness, each is heritable, and each is the product of natural selection. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the capacity of certain bacteria to metabolise novel synthetic antibiotics. Within a decade of an antibiotic's introduction, bacterial populations may evolve the capacity to break it down. This capacity enhances fitness in the hospital environment; it is heritable; and it was produced by the selective pressure of the antibiotic. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that biologists would hesitate to describe this as an adaptation in the full sense of the term. It is better characterised as a recently acquired resistance — a rapid evolutionary response to a novel challenge. The concept of adaptation, as ordinarily employed, carries the implication that the trait has been shaped by sustained selective pressure over many generations, and it is this temporal dimension that the definition has omitted.

definition: An entity E is a cell if and only if (i) E is bounded by a membrane that separates its interior from its external environment, (ii) E contains genetic material that directs its activities, and (iii) E is capable of independent metabolism.
conditions: E is bounded by a membrane that separates its interior from its external environment
E contains genetic material that directs its activities
E is capable of independent metabolism
counterexample: Consider a mature mammalian red blood cell. It is bounded by a membrane, and it carries out a limited metabolic activity — principally the transport of oxygen. Yet it contains no genetic material whatever, having expelled its nucleus during maturation. The second condition fails. We should not, however, deny that a red blood cell is a cell; it is the paradigmatic example from which the very name 'cell' in blood biology derives.
passage: A natural definition of a cell holds that an entity is a cell if and only if it is bounded by a membrane separating its interior from its external environment, contains genetic material that directs its activities, and is capable of independent metabolism. This definition captures the standard examples: a bacterium is membrane-bound, contains DNA, and metabolises independently; a plant cell is membrane-bound, contains DNA in its nucleus, and carries out photosynthesis and respiration. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the mature mammalian red blood cell. The red blood cell is bounded by a membrane and carries out a limited metabolic activity, principally the transport of oxygen via haemoglobin. But it contains no genetic material whatever, having expelled its nucleus during the process of maturation. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet one might observe that we should not deny that the red blood cell is a cell. It is the paradigmatic example of a cell in haematology, and the very term 'blood cell' presupposes its cellular status. The definition, by requiring the presence of genetic material, has excluded a well-recognised type of cell and thereby revealed that it has mistaken a feature typical of cells for a necessary condition of cellularity.

definition: A process P is a chemical reaction if and only if (i) P involves the rearrangement of atoms among molecules, (ii) P results in the formation of one or more new substances with properties distinct from those of the initial substances, and (iii) P is accompanied by a change in energy — either the absorption or release of heat.
conditions: P involves the rearrangement of atoms among molecules
P results in the formation of one or more new substances with properties distinct from those of the initial substances
P is accompanied by a change in energy — either the absorption or release of heat
counterexample: Consider certain thermoneutral reactions — processes in which the enthalpy change is zero or negligibly small. The dissolution of certain salts, for instance, may involve the rearrangement of ionic bonds and the formation of new species in solution without any measurable absorption or release of heat. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third fails. Yet chemists would not hesitate to classify these as chemical reactions.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires an accompanying change in energy, failing to accommodate thermoneutral reactions in which the enthalpy change is zero.
passage: One might define a chemical reaction as a process involving the rearrangement of atoms among molecules, resulting in the formation of new substances with properties distinct from those of the starting materials, and accompanied by a change in energy — either the absorption or release of heat. This definition captures the familiar cases well: the combustion of wood involves atomic rearrangement, produces new substances such as carbon dioxide and water, and releases heat. The photosynthesis of glucose involves rearrangement, produces new substances, and absorbs energy from sunlight. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when one considers thermoneutral reactions — processes in which the enthalpy change is zero or negligibly small. The dissolution of certain salts involves the breaking and forming of bonds and the production of new species in solution, yet it may proceed without any measurable absorption or release of heat. The first two conditions are satisfied, but the third fails. One might observe that chemists would not hesitate to classify such a process as a chemical reaction. The definition, by requiring an energy change, has taken what is commonly present in chemical reactions — a thermal signature — and elevated it to a necessary condition, thereby excluding a class of genuine reactions.

definition: A region R has a particular climate C if and only if (i) R exhibits a characteristic pattern of temperature variation over the course of the year, (ii) R exhibits a characteristic pattern of precipitation, and (iii) these patterns have remained stable over a period of at least thirty years.
conditions: R exhibits a characteristic pattern of temperature variation over the course of the year
R exhibits a characteristic pattern of precipitation
These patterns have remained stable over a period of at least thirty years
counterexample: Consider a volcanic island that emerged from the sea fifteen years ago. Within a few years it developed a characteristic pattern of temperature and precipitation — warm tropical temperatures and heavy seasonal rains — that is perfectly stable and closely matches the climate of neighbouring islands. Meteorologists would describe the island as having a tropical maritime climate. Yet the patterns have not been stable for thirty years, since the island has not existed for thirty years. The definition, by requiring a thirty-year record, would deny that the island has a climate at all.
passage: It is natural to define the climate of a region as the characteristic pattern of temperature and precipitation that the region exhibits over the course of the year, provided these patterns have remained stable over a period of at least thirty years. The thirty-year requirement is motivated by the desire to distinguish climate from weather: weather is variable and transient, whilst climate is the stable background against which weather varies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when one considers a volcanic island that emerged from the sea only fifteen years ago. Within a few years of its emergence, the island developed a characteristic pattern of temperature and precipitation — warm tropical temperatures and heavy seasonal rains — that matches closely the climate of neighbouring long-established islands and shows every sign of stability. Meteorologists would not hesitate to describe this island as having a tropical maritime climate. Yet the patterns have not been stable for thirty years, since the island has not existed for thirty years. One might observe that the thirty-year criterion, whilst useful as a practical convention, should not be mistaken for a necessary condition. The definition has confused a methodological rule of thumb with a conceptual requirement.

definition: A system S is an ecosystem if and only if (i) S comprises a community of living organisms together with their physical environment, (ii) the organisms in S are connected by flows of energy and cycling of nutrients, and (iii) S is self-sustaining — it does not depend upon external inputs to maintain its characteristic processes.
conditions: S comprises a community of living organisms together with their physical environment
The organisms in S are connected by flows of energy and cycling of nutrients
S is self-sustaining — it does not depend upon external inputs to maintain its characteristic processes
counterexample: Consider a deep-sea hydrothermal vent community. It comprises a community of organisms — tube worms, chemosynthetic bacteria, shrimp — together with their physical environment. The organisms are connected by flows of energy derived from chemical reactions and by nutrient cycling. Ecologists universally regard it as an ecosystem. Yet it is not self-sustaining in the sense that the definition requires, for it depends crucially upon the continuous input of geothermally heated, mineral-rich water from the Earth's interior. If the vent ceases, the community dies.
missing_condition: The definition's requirement of self-sustainability is too strong; it should instead require only that the system exhibit characteristic ecological processes — energy flow and nutrient cycling — without demanding that these processes be independent of external inputs.
passage: One might propose that an ecosystem is a system comprising a community of living organisms together with their physical environment, connected by flows of energy and nutrient cycling, and self-sustaining in the sense that it does not depend upon external inputs. This definition captures certain cases well: a mature forest, for instance, recycles its nutrients through decomposition and maintains itself through photosynthesis and internal energy flows. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the proposal arises when one considers a deep-sea hydrothermal vent community. Such a community comprises chemosynthetic bacteria, tube worms, shrimp, and other organisms, together with their physical environment at the ocean floor. The organisms are connected by flows of energy — the bacteria derive energy from chemical reactions with minerals in the vent fluid, and the other organisms depend upon the bacteria. Nutrient cycling occurs within the community. Ecologists universally regard this as an ecosystem. Yet it is not self-sustaining: it depends crucially upon the continuous input of geothermally heated, mineral-rich water from the Earth's interior. Were the vent to cease, the entire community would perish. One might observe that the definition has set the bar for self-sustainability too high, excluding systems that are unquestionably ecosystems but that depend upon external geological inputs for their continued existence.

definition: A population P has undergone evolution if and only if (i) the frequency of at least one heritable trait in P has changed over successive generations, (ii) the change is the result of differential reproductive success among individuals, and (iii) the change is adaptive — it increases the average fitness of individuals in P.
conditions: The frequency of at least one heritable trait in P has changed over successive generations
The change is the result of differential reproductive success among individuals
The change is adaptive — it increases the average fitness of individuals in P
counterexample: Consider a small island population of birds in which a rare allele for unusually large beak size becomes prevalent over several generations, not because large-beaked birds are better adapted, but because a storm happened to kill most of the small-beaked birds in the founding population. The frequency of the heritable trait has changed; the change is the result of differential survival and thus differential reproductive success. But the change is not adaptive — it was produced by a random catastrophe unrelated to the trait's fitness value. Biologists would nonetheless describe this as evolution by genetic drift.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires that evolutionary change be adaptive, thereby excluding non-adaptive evolutionary processes such as genetic drift, in which random events alter trait frequencies without any fitness advantage.
passage: A natural definition of evolution holds that a population has undergone evolution if and only if the frequency of at least one heritable trait has changed over successive generations, the change results from differential reproductive success among individuals, and the change is adaptive. This definition captures the Darwinian paradigm well: the evolution of darker colouration in peppered moths during industrialisation involved a heritable change in frequency, resulted from differential predation, and was adaptive in the polluted environment. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when one considers genetic drift. On a small island, a storm kills a disproportionate number of small-beaked birds, leaving the survivors — who happen to have large beaks — to found the next generation. The frequency of the heritable trait for large beak size has changed over generations; the change results from differential survival and thus differential reproductive success. But the change is not adaptive: it was produced by a random catastrophe, and large beaks confer no fitness advantage in the island's environment. Biologists would not hesitate to describe this as evolution — specifically, evolution by genetic drift. One might observe that the definition, by requiring adaptiveness, has excluded a well-recognised mechanism of evolutionary change.

definition: An object F is a fossil if and only if (i) F is the preserved remains or impression of an organism that lived in a past geological age, (ii) F has undergone mineralisation — the replacement of organic material by mineral deposits, and (iii) F is found within a sedimentary rock formation.
conditions: F is the preserved remains or impression of an organism that lived in a past geological age
F has undergone mineralisation — the replacement of organic material by mineral deposits
F is found within a sedimentary rock formation
counterexample: Consider a woolly mammoth discovered frozen in the Siberian permafrost, its flesh, hair, and internal organs preserved by the cold. This is unquestionably the preserved remains of an organism from a past geological age. Yet it has not undergone mineralisation — the organic material remains organic — and it is not found within a sedimentary rock formation but within frozen soil. The definition would exclude it, yet palaeontologists regard it as a fossil of the highest scientific interest.
passage: One might define a fossil as the preserved remains or impression of an organism from a past geological age that has undergone mineralisation and is found within a sedimentary rock formation. This definition captures the canonical examples: the petrified bones of dinosaurs, in which organic material has been replaced by mineral deposits, found within layers of sandstone or limestone. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the woolly mammoths discovered frozen in the Siberian permafrost. Such specimens are unquestionably the preserved remains of organisms from a past geological age — they died thousands of years ago and belong to a species that is now extinct. Yet their organic material has not been mineralised; the flesh, hair, and even the stomach contents remain organic. Nor are they found within sedimentary rock formations; they are entombed in frozen soil. Both the second and third conditions fail. One might observe, however, that palaeontologists regard these specimens as fossils of the highest scientific value. The definition, by requiring mineralisation and sedimentary context, has taken the most common mode of preservation and treated it as the only mode, thereby excluding frozen, desiccated, and amber-preserved specimens that are equally deserving of the name.

definition: A segment of DNA D is a gene if and only if (i) D encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a single polypeptide chain, (ii) D occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome, and (iii) D is transmitted from parent to offspring according to the laws of Mendelian inheritance.
conditions: D encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a single polypeptide chain
D occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome
D is transmitted from parent to offspring according to the laws of Mendelian inheritance
counterexample: Consider the genes of mitochondria. Mitochondrial DNA contains segments that encode polypeptide chains essential for cellular respiration. These segments occupy fixed positions on the mitochondrial genome. Yet they are not transmitted according to Mendelian laws — mitochondrial genes are inherited maternally, without the segregation and independent assortment characteristic of Mendelian inheritance. Biologists nonetheless regard these segments as genes.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires Mendelian transmission, thereby excluding genes carried in organellar genomes — mitochondrial and chloroplast DNA — which are transmitted through non-Mendelian, typically maternal, inheritance.
passage: It is tempting to define a gene as a segment of DNA that encodes the information for a single polypeptide chain, occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome, and is transmitted from parent to offspring according to the laws of Mendelian inheritance. This definition captures the classical cases: the gene for eye colour in Drosophila occupies a fixed locus, encodes a polypeptide, and segregates in Mendelian ratios. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation concerns mitochondrial genes. Mitochondrial DNA contains segments that encode polypeptide chains essential for cellular respiration. These segments occupy fixed positions within the mitochondrial genome. But they are not transmitted according to Mendelian laws. Mitochondrial genes are inherited maternally — the offspring receives its mitochondrial DNA from the mother alone, without the segregation and independent assortment that characterise Mendelian inheritance. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that biologists do not hesitate to call these segments genes. They are genes in every functional sense: they encode proteins, they are subject to mutation, and they play essential roles in cellular metabolism. The definition, by insisting upon Mendelian transmission, has confined the concept of the gene to nuclear inheritance and excluded a well-recognised class of extranuclear genes.

definition: A behaviour B in an organism O is instinctive if and only if (i) B is unlearned — it appears in the organism without prior training or experience, (ii) B is species-universal — it is exhibited by all normal members of O's species, and (iii) B is stereotyped — it follows a fixed pattern whenever it is performed.
conditions: B is unlearned — it appears in the organism without prior training or experience
B is species-universal — it is exhibited by all normal members of O's species
B is stereotyped — it follows a fixed pattern whenever it is performed
counterexample: Consider birdsong in species such as the white-crowned sparrow. The basic impulse to sing is unlearned and species-universal — all normal males produce vocalisations. Yet the song is not stereotyped in the required sense: young males must hear the song of adult conspecifics during a critical period in order to develop the correct species-typical song, and the precise pattern of the song varies between individuals and regional populations. The behaviour is instinctive in its origins yet shaped by learning in its mature form, and it is not stereotyped in the manner the definition requires.
passage: One might define instinctive behaviour as behaviour that is unlearned, species-universal, and stereotyped — following a fixed pattern whenever it is performed. This definition captures certain paradigmatic cases: the web-spinning of spiders, which appears without training, is exhibited by all normal members of the species, and follows a remarkably fixed geometric pattern. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition arises when one considers birdsong in species such as the white-crowned sparrow. The basic impulse to sing is unlearned: young males will produce vocalisations even in isolation. It is species-universal: all normal males sing. Yet the song is not stereotyped in the manner the definition requires. Young males must hear the song of adult conspecifics during a sensitive period in order to develop the correct species-typical pattern, and the precise form of the song varies between individuals and between regional populations. One might observe that we should not deny that birdsong is instinctive merely because its mature form is shaped by learning. The definition has taken the rigidity of spider web-spinning and treated it as a necessary condition of instinct, thereby excluding behaviours that are instinctive in their origins and motivation but flexible in their final expression.

definition: A group of organisms G constitutes a species if and only if (i) the members of G are capable of interbreeding with one another, (ii) the members of G produce fertile offspring when they interbreed, and (iii) the members of G are reproductively isolated from organisms outside G.
conditions: The members of G are capable of interbreeding with one another
The members of G produce fertile offspring when they interbreed
The members of G are reproductively isolated from organisms outside G
counterexample: Consider two species of oak that grow in overlapping ranges and regularly hybridise, producing fertile offspring. Botanists classify them as distinct species on the basis of their distinct morphologies, ecological preferences, and genetic lineages, even though they are not reproductively isolated. The definition, which requires reproductive isolation, would force us to classify them as a single species, contrary to standard botanical practice.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the widespread phenomenon of interspecific hybridisation with fertile offspring, which occurs frequently in plants and certain animal groups; it should allow that species may be distinguished by morphological, ecological, or phylogenetic criteria even in the absence of complete reproductive isolation.
passage: A natural definition of species holds that a group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if its members are capable of interbreeding, produce fertile offspring, and are reproductively isolated from organisms outside the group. This biological species concept has great intuitive appeal and works well for many animal groups. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the oaks. Numerous species of oak that grow in overlapping geographical ranges hybridise regularly and produce fertile offspring. The red oak and the scarlet oak, for instance, interbreed freely where their ranges overlap. Yet botanists classify them as distinct species, citing their distinct morphologies, their different ecological preferences, and their separate genetic lineages. The third condition of the definition — reproductive isolation — fails. One might observe that the definition, by making reproductive isolation a necessary condition, has adopted a criterion that works well for sexually reproducing animals but that fails to capture the complexity of species boundaries in plants and other groups where hybridisation is common. The difficulty suggests that no single criterion can serve as the universal mark of species membership.

definition: A group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if (i) the members are capable of interbreeding with one another under natural conditions, (ii) the members produce fertile offspring when they do interbreed, and (iii) the members are reproductively isolated from all other such groups.
conditions: The members are capable of interbreeding with one another under natural conditions
The members produce fertile offspring when they do interbreed
The members are reproductively isolated from all other such groups
counterexample: Consider two populations of oak trees, traditionally classified as distinct species, which occupy adjacent but ecologically different habitats. Where the habitats meet, the two populations interbreed freely and produce fully fertile hybrid offspring. Yet the parent populations maintain their distinctive morphological and ecological characters because hybrids are less fit in either parental habitat. All three conditions fail to classify these as distinct species, since interbreeding occurs and fertile offspring result. Yet botanists, ecologists, and systematists recognise them as distinct species on the basis of their stable morphological and ecological differences.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to recognise that reproductive isolation need not be absolute; species may be maintained as distinct by ecological or selective barriers even when some degree of interbreeding and fertile hybridisation occurs.
passage: The most widely invoked characterisation of species holds that a species is a group of organisms whose members can interbreed, produce fertile offspring, and are reproductively isolated from all other such groups. This biological species concept has proven enormously useful in zoology and has provided a principled basis for taxonomic practice. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is readily found in the plant kingdom. Consider two populations of oak trees — one adapted to dry, sandy soils and the other to moist, loamy soils — that have been classified by botanists as distinct species for over a century. Where their habitats abut, the two populations interbreed freely and produce fully fertile hybrid offspring. Reproductive isolation, in the strict sense demanded by the definition, does not obtain. Yet the two populations maintain their distinctive morphologies and ecological preferences over vast geographical ranges, because the hybrid offspring are poorly adapted to either parental habitat and are therefore selected against. Botanists and ecologists recognise the populations as distinct species, and there are compelling practical and theoretical reasons for doing so. It appears that the definition, by demanding absolute reproductive isolation and universal interfertility, cannot accommodate the well-documented phenomenon of species that are maintained as distinct by ecological and selective pressures rather than by the impossibility of hybridisation.

definition: An entity X is alive if and only if (i) X is capable of metabolism — that is, of taking in energy and materials from its environment and converting them to maintain its internal organisation, (ii) X is capable of reproduction, and (iii) X responds to stimuli from its environment.
conditions: X is capable of metabolism
X is capable of reproduction
X responds to stimuli from its environment
counterexample: Consider a mule — the offspring of a horse and a donkey. A mule metabolises food, maintains its internal organisation, and responds to environmental stimuli. Yet mules are almost universally sterile and cannot reproduce. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Nevertheless, we should find it absurd to deny that a mule is alive. It eats, breathes, moves, and behaves in every way as a living organism.
passage: A natural proposal for defining life holds that an entity is alive if and only if it metabolises, reproduces, and responds to stimuli. This tripartite definition captures the features most commonly associated with living organisms in biology textbooks. Yet one might observe that a familiar case puts pressure on the requirement of reproduction. Consider a mule — the offspring of a cross between a horse and a donkey. A mule metabolises food and converts it to energy; it maintains the internal organisation of its cells and tissues with remarkable efficiency. It responds to environmental stimuli — it reacts to changes in temperature, to the presence of predators, and to the commands of its handler. Yet mules are, with vanishingly rare exceptions, sterile. They cannot reproduce. The second condition of the definition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, it would be extraordinary to deny that a mule is alive. It is as plainly and robustly alive as any horse or donkey. The same difficulty arises for worker bees, for individual organisms past reproductive age, and for any number of living beings that are, for one reason or another, incapable of reproduction. It appears that the definition, by including reproduction as a necessary condition, excludes entities that are paradigmatically alive.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid body forces so that it assumes a roughly spherical shape, and (iii) B has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit of other debris.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to overcome rigid body forces so that it assumes a roughly spherical shape
B has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit of other debris
counterexample: Consider a body of planetary mass that has been ejected from its stellar system by gravitational interactions with other bodies. It drifts through interstellar space, possessing sufficient mass to be spherical and having long ago cleared its original orbital neighbourhood. It satisfies conditions (ii) and (iii), but fails condition (i), since it no longer orbits any star. Yet its internal structure, composition, and geological activity are indistinguishable from those of a body we should unhesitatingly call a planet.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for rogue planets — bodies of planetary mass and character that are not gravitationally bound to any star — by making stellar orbit a necessary condition.
passage: The definition of a planet adopted by the International Astronomical Union holds that a planet must orbit a star, must be massive enough to assume a spherical shape under its own gravity, and must have cleared the neighbourhood of its orbit. This definition has the practical virtue of being applicable to the bodies of our own solar system. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be described as follows. Suppose a body of planetary mass — comparable, let us say, to Jupiter — is ejected from its stellar system in the early stages of the system's formation by gravitational interactions with other massive bodies. It drifts through interstellar space, bound to no star. It possesses sufficient mass to be spherical. And at the time of its ejection, it had cleared the neighbourhood of its original orbit. Its internal structure, its atmosphere, its geological and perhaps even meteorological activity are indistinguishable from those of the gas giants we recognise as planets. Yet it fails the first condition: it does not orbit a star. One might observe that it seems arbitrary to deny it the status of a planet solely on the ground that gravitational contingencies have cast it into the void. It appears that the definition, by requiring stellar orbit as a necessary condition, is parochial — tied to the circumstances of our own solar system — rather than capturing what is essential to planethood.

definition: A substance X is a chemical element if and only if (i) X cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, (ii) all atoms of X have the same number of protons in their nuclei, and (iii) X is stable under standard conditions of temperature and pressure.
conditions: X cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means
All atoms of X have the same number of protons in their nuclei
X is stable under standard conditions of temperature and pressure
counterexample: Consider the element francium. It cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, and all its atoms possess eighty-seven protons. Yet francium is extraordinarily unstable under standard conditions: its most stable isotope has a half-life of approximately twenty-two minutes. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. Nevertheless, francium appears in every periodic table and is universally recognised as a chemical element.
passage: One might attempt to define a chemical element as a substance that cannot be decomposed by ordinary chemical means, whose atoms all share the same number of protons, and that is stable under standard conditions of temperature and pressure. The first two conditions capture the essential chemical and nuclear character of elements, and the third is intended to exclude ephemeral or artificial entities from the category. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the stability condition arises with the naturally occurring element francium. Francium cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, and every atom of francium possesses eighty-seven protons in its nucleus. It is listed on every periodic table and is studied by chemists and physicists as a matter of course. Yet francium is profoundly unstable: its most long-lived isotope, francium-223, has a half-life of only about twenty-two minutes. Under standard conditions, any macroscopic sample would decay almost instantly. The third condition is plainly not met. Yet no chemist would deny that francium is a chemical element. It appears that the stability requirement, however intuitive, excludes substances that are paradigmatic elements. The definition, by imposing stability as a necessary condition, is too restrictive.

definition: A condition C afflicting an organism O constitutes a disease if and only if (i) C causes a departure from the normal physiological functioning of O, (ii) C produces symptoms that are detectable by the organism or by a clinician, and (iii) C reduces the organism's capacity to survive or reproduce.
conditions: C causes a departure from the normal physiological functioning of O
C produces symptoms that are detectable by the organism or by a clinician
C reduces the organism's capacity to survive or reproduce
counterexample: Consider sickle-cell trait — the heterozygous condition in which an individual carries one normal and one sickle-cell allele. It causes a measurable departure from normal haemoglobin function. It produces detectable clinical signs under certain conditions, such as extreme altitude or vigorous exercise. Yet in regions where malaria is endemic, carriers of sickle-cell trait have a substantial survival advantage over individuals with two normal alleles. Condition (iii) is not merely unsatisfied — it is reversed: the condition enhances rather than reduces the organism's capacity to survive.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the same physiological departure may constitute a disease in one environment and a beneficial adaptation in another, by fixing the survival-reducing requirement without reference to the organism's ecological context.
passage: A natural characterisation of disease holds that a condition is a disease if it departs from normal physiological functioning, produces detectable symptoms, and reduces the organism's capacity to survive or reproduce. This definition has obvious appeal in clinical medicine, where diseases are precisely those conditions that impair health and require treatment. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the genetics of haemoglobin. Consider sickle-cell trait — the heterozygous condition in which an individual possesses one normal haemoglobin allele and one sickle-cell allele. The condition causes a measurable departure from normal haemoglobin function: a proportion of the individual's red blood cells may assume the characteristic sickle shape under conditions of low oxygen. It produces detectable clinical signs, particularly under extreme altitude or during vigorous physical exertion. Yet in regions of sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia where malaria is endemic, carriers of sickle-cell trait enjoy a substantial survival advantage over individuals homozygous for the normal allele, because the sickle-cell trait confers resistance to the malaria parasite. The third condition of the definition is not merely unsatisfied — it is inverted. One might observe that the same physiological departure that would count as a disease in one environment counts as a beneficial adaptation in another. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the condition reduce survival capacity in absolute terms, cannot accommodate the context-dependence of disease.

definition: A region R constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) R contains a community of living organisms interacting with one another, (ii) R contains abiotic components — such as water, minerals, and sunlight — that the organisms utilise, and (iii) there is a flow of energy through R from producers to consumers.
conditions: R contains a community of living organisms interacting with one another
R contains abiotic components that the organisms utilise
There is a flow of energy through R from producers to consumers
counterexample: Consider a deep-sea hydrothermal vent community. It contains organisms interacting with one another and utilising abiotic components. Yet the flow of energy does not run from photosynthetic producers to consumers, as the definition implicitly presupposes. The primary producers are chemosynthetic bacteria that derive energy from the oxidation of hydrogen sulphide. All three conditions are nominally met if we read 'producers' broadly, but if we read the definition as tacitly requiring photosynthetic energy flow — as its reference to sunlight among the abiotic components suggests — then the vent community fails the definition despite being a paradigmatic ecosystem.
missing_condition: The definition's implicit reliance on solar energy and photosynthesis as the basis of the producer-consumer relationship fails to accommodate ecosystems sustained by chemosynthesis, in which the primary energy source is geochemical rather than solar.
passage: An intuitive characterisation of an ecosystem holds that it is a region containing interacting organisms, abiotic components such as water and sunlight, and a flow of energy from producers to consumers. This definition captures the essential structure of the terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems most familiar to ecologists. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the deep ocean. Consider a hydrothermal vent community on the mid-ocean ridge, thousands of metres below the surface, in perpetual darkness. The community contains a rich array of organisms — tube worms, shrimp, crabs, and diverse microbial populations — that interact with one another in complex trophic relationships. The organisms utilise abiotic components: dissolved minerals, heated water, and hydrogen sulphide. And there is a flow of energy from producers to consumers. Yet the producers are not photosynthetic organisms; they are chemosynthetic bacteria that derive their energy from the oxidation of hydrogen sulphide emitted by the vent. Sunlight plays no role whatever. If the definition's enumeration of abiotic components — water, minerals, and sunlight — is taken as characterising the type of ecosystem intended, then the vent community falls outside the definition. One might observe that hydrothermal vent communities are among the most remarkable ecosystems on Earth. It appears that the definition, by implicitly grounding its account of energy flow in the solar-driven food chains of surface ecosystems, is too narrow to encompass the full range of ecological organisation.

definition: A segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if (i) it encodes a protein via transcription and translation, (ii) it is inherited from parent to offspring according to Mendelian principles, and (iii) it occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome.
conditions: It encodes a protein via transcription and translation
It is inherited from parent to offspring according to Mendelian principles
It occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome
counterexample: Consider a segment of DNA that encodes a functional RNA molecule — a transfer RNA or a ribosomal RNA — that is never translated into a protein. It is inherited in Mendelian fashion and occupies a fixed chromosomal locus. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not, since the segment does not encode a protein. Yet molecular biologists universally regard such segments as genes. The genes encoding ribosomal RNA, for instance, are among the most conserved and functionally important in the genome.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring that a gene encode a protein, thereby excluding the many well-established genes whose functional product is an RNA molecule that is never translated.
passage: One might define a gene as a segment of DNA that encodes a protein via transcription and translation, is inherited according to Mendelian principles, and occupies a fixed chromosomal locus. This definition captures the classical conception of the gene that dominated genetics from the early twentieth century through the elucidation of the genetic code. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency has become increasingly prominent in molecular biology. Consider the segments of DNA that encode transfer RNA and ribosomal RNA. These segments are transcribed into RNA molecules that perform essential functions in the cell — they are indispensable to the machinery of translation itself — but they are never translated into protein. They are inherited from parent to offspring in Mendelian fashion. They occupy fixed loci on specific chromosomes. Two of the three conditions are met, but the first is not: no protein is encoded. Nevertheless, molecular biologists universally regard these segments as genes. The genes encoding ribosomal RNA are among the most conserved and functionally important sequences in the genomes of all living organisms. It appears that the definition, by requiring that a gene encode a protein, reflects an outdated understanding of gene function and cannot accommodate the diverse repertoire of functional RNA genes.

definition: An interaction F constitutes a fundamental force if and only if (i) F acts between particles of matter, (ii) F obeys an inverse-square law with respect to distance, and (iii) F cannot be reduced to or derived from any other interaction.
conditions: F acts between particles of matter
F obeys an inverse-square law with respect to distance
F cannot be reduced to or derived from any other interaction
counterexample: Consider the strong nuclear force that binds protons and neutrons within an atomic nucleus. It acts between particles of matter and cannot be reduced to any other interaction. Yet at the scale of the nucleus, it does not obey an inverse-square law; rather, it is extremely short-ranged, increasing with distance at very short separations before rapidly falling to zero. Conditions (i) and (iii) are met, but condition (ii) is not. Yet the strong force is universally recognised as one of the four fundamental forces.
passage: One might attempt to characterise fundamental forces by requiring that they act between particles of matter, obey an inverse-square law, and be irreducible to other interactions. This proposal captures two of the four traditionally recognised fundamental forces — gravity and electromagnetism — both of which obey the inverse-square law in their classical formulations. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with the strong nuclear force. The strong force acts between quarks and, at a larger scale, between nucleons — protons and neutrons — within the atomic nucleus. It is irreducible to any other interaction; it is, in the standard model of particle physics, mediated by its own class of gauge bosons, the gluons. Yet it emphatically does not obey an inverse-square law. At very short distances, the strong force increases with separation — a phenomenon known as confinement — and at distances beyond approximately one femtometre, it drops rapidly to zero. The definition would exclude the strong force from the category of fundamental forces, a result that no physicist would accept. It appears that the inverse-square requirement, while applicable to gravity and electromagnetism, is parochial and cannot accommodate the full range of fundamental interactions recognised by modern physics.

definition: A substance M is a mineral if and only if (i) M is naturally occurring, (ii) M is inorganic, and (iii) M has a definite and uniform chemical composition throughout.
conditions: M is naturally occurring
M is inorganic
M has a definite and uniform chemical composition throughout
counterexample: Consider the mineral olivine, which is universally recognised by geologists. Olivine is naturally occurring and inorganic. Yet its chemical composition is not uniform: it forms a solid solution series between the magnesium end-member forsterite and the iron end-member fayalite, with the ratio of magnesium to iron varying continuously. Any given specimen of olivine may have a different proportion of these two components. Condition (iii) is not satisfied in the strict sense, yet olivine is one of the most common and well-studied minerals in geology.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate minerals that form solid solution series, in which the chemical composition varies continuously within a defined range rather than being fixed at a single stoichiometric formula.
passage: A standard definition holds that a mineral is a naturally occurring, inorganic substance with a definite and uniform chemical composition. This characterisation captures the essential features of many paradigmatic minerals: quartz is always SiO2, halite is always NaCl, and calcite is always CaCO3. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement arises with the extremely common mineral olivine. Olivine is naturally occurring — it is one of the most abundant minerals in the Earth's upper mantle. It is inorganic. Yet its chemical composition is not definite and uniform in the manner the definition requires. Olivine forms a continuous solid solution between forsterite (Mg2SiO4) and fayalite (Fe2SiO4), with the magnesium-to-iron ratio varying from specimen to specimen and even within a single crystal. One specimen may be nearly pure forsterite; another may be rich in iron. The chemical composition varies continuously across the series. Nevertheless, geologists and mineralogists unhesitatingly classify olivine as a mineral. It appears that the definition, by demanding a definite and uniform composition, cannot accommodate the phenomenon of solid solution — a feature common to many of the most important mineral groups, including the feldspars, the pyroxenes, and the amphiboles.

definition: A landmass L constitutes a continent if and only if (i) L is a large, continuous expanse of land, (ii) L is entirely surrounded by water or nearly so, and (iii) L is geologically distinct from adjacent landmasses by virtue of resting on its own tectonic plate.
conditions: L is a large, continuous expanse of land
L is entirely surrounded by water or nearly so
L is geologically distinct from adjacent landmasses by virtue of resting on its own tectonic plate
counterexample: Consider Europe, which is universally counted among the continents. It is a large, continuous expanse of land, and it is nearly surrounded by water. Yet it does not rest on its own tectonic plate: Europe and Asia share the Eurasian Plate, and the boundary between them is conventionally drawn along the Ural Mountains and the Caspian Sea — a geographical convention rather than a geological fact. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, yet no geographer would deny that Europe is a continent.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the distinction between continents may rest on historical, cultural, and geographical conventions rather than on geological plate boundaries alone.
passage: A natural characterisation of a continent holds that it is a large, continuous landmass, entirely or nearly surrounded by water, and geologically distinct from adjacent landmasses by virtue of resting on its own tectonic plate. This definition captures the paradigmatic cases: Africa rests on the African Plate, Australia on the Australian Plate, and Antarctica on the Antarctic Plate. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with Europe. Europe is a large, continuous expanse of land. It is nearly surrounded by water — the Atlantic to the west, the Arctic to the north, the Mediterranean to the south. Yet it does not rest on its own tectonic plate. Europe and Asia share the Eurasian Plate, and the conventional boundary between them — the Ural Mountains, the Ural River, the Caspian Sea, the Caucasus — is a geographical and cultural convention, not a geological reality. There is no plate boundary, no subduction zone, no transform fault along this line. Condition (iii) is plainly not met. Yet Europe is universally counted among the continents. One might observe that the continental status of Europe depends not on geology but on millennia of cultural, historical, and cartographic tradition. It appears that the definition, by grounding the concept of a continent in tectonic plate theory alone, cannot accommodate the conventional and historical character of at least some continental distinctions.

definition: A population of organisms constitutes a distinct species if and only if (i) its members share a cluster of morphological characteristics that distinguish them from all other populations, (ii) these characteristics are heritable, and (iii) the population occupies a distinct ecological niche.
conditions: Its members share a cluster of morphological characteristics that distinguish them from all other populations
These characteristics are heritable
The population occupies a distinct ecological niche
counterexample: Consider two populations of butterflies that are morphologically indistinguishable — identical in wing pattern, body form, and coloration — and that occupy the same ecological niche. Yet the two populations do not interbreed because they rely on different chemical pheromones for mate recognition. Biologists classify them as distinct species on the basis of their reproductive isolation. None of the three conditions in the definition distinguishes them, since they share the same morphological characters and the same niche. The definition would classify them as a single species.
