missing_condition: The definition neglects to require that the members of the population share a reproductive compatibility that is absent with respect to other populations, so that morphologically identical but reproductively isolated populations may be recognised as distinct species.
passage: One might propose that a species is a population whose members share a distinctive cluster of heritable morphological characteristics and occupy a distinct ecological niche. This morphological-ecological conception captures the criteria by which naturalists have traditionally recognised species in the field. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be found among the Lepidoptera. Consider two populations of butterflies inhabiting the same tropical forest. They are morphologically indistinguishable: their wing patterns, body proportions, and coloration are identical even under close examination. They occupy the same ecological niche, feeding on the same host plants and being preyed upon by the same predators. Their morphological characteristics are heritable. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied by neither population as distinct from the other — indeed, the definition would classify them as a single species. Yet the two populations do not interbreed. They rely upon different chemical pheromones for mate recognition, and individuals from one population are entirely unresponsive to the mating signals of the other. Biologists who study these populations classify them as distinct species, and there are compelling reasons for doing so: they are evolving independently and will, over time, accumulate genetic and perhaps morphological differences. It appears that the definition, by attending only to morphology and ecology, cannot capture the reproductive dimension of species identity that is essential for recognising species that are morphologically cryptic.

definition: An entity X is a living organism if and only if (i) X maintains its internal organisation by means of metabolic processes, (ii) X grows by the assimilation of materials from its environment, and (iii) X is composed of one or more cells.
conditions: X maintains its internal organisation by means of metabolic processes
X grows by the assimilation of materials from its environment
X is composed of one or more cells
counterexample: Consider a virus particle outside a host cell. It is not composed of cells, it does not metabolise, and it does not grow. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet when the virus enters a host cell, it commandeers the cellular machinery to replicate itself, producing hundreds of copies that go on to infect other cells. The virus stands in an uneasy position: it is not alive by the definition, yet it possesses a genome, undergoes natural selection, evolves, and exhibits many of the characteristics we associate with living things.
passage: A familiar characterisation of living organisms holds that they maintain their internal organisation through metabolism, grow by assimilating environmental materials, and are composed of one or more cells. This cellular-metabolic definition captures the features that are most obvious in the organisms one encounters in everyday life — plants, animals, fungi, bacteria. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the viruses. Consider a virus particle — a bacteriophage, say — outside its host cell. It is not composed of cells. It does not metabolise. It does not grow. By the definition, it is not a living organism. Yet this verdict sits uneasily with the remarkable properties that viruses display. The bacteriophage possesses a genome of nucleic acid. It undergoes mutation and natural selection. It evolves resistance to the defences of its hosts. When it enters a bacterial cell, it commandeers the cellular machinery to produce hundreds of copies of itself, which burst from the cell and go on to infect others. One might observe that an entity capable of evolving, of adapting to its environment, and of reproducing — albeit only by parasitising the cellular apparatus of another organism — possesses many of the hallmarks of life. It appears that the definition, by requiring cellular composition and independent metabolism as necessary conditions, draws a sharp boundary where nature presents a continuum, and excludes entities whose claim to be called living is, at the very least, not easily dismissed.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical process, (ii) S is characterised by a unique atomic number, and (iii) all atoms of S are identical in their chemical properties.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical process
S is characterised by a unique atomic number
All atoms of S are identical in their chemical properties
counterexample: Consider the element chlorine, which has atomic number 17. Chlorine occurs naturally as a mixture of two stable isotopes: chlorine-35 and chlorine-37. These isotopes differ in atomic mass and in certain physical properties — their rates of diffusion, for instance, are not identical. Yet they share the same atomic number and cannot be decomposed by chemical means. Condition (iii) requires that all atoms be identical in their chemical properties, and while the isotopes are very nearly identical chemically, they do exhibit slight differences in reaction rates owing to the kinetic isotope effect. The definition must either acknowledge that the atoms of an element are not strictly identical or abandon the requirement.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to recognise that the atoms of a single element may differ in mass number — that is, in neutron count — and may exhibit slight differences in physical and even chemical behaviour, so that the requirement of strict identity among all atoms is too strong.
passage: One might define a chemical element as a substance that cannot be decomposed by chemical means, is characterised by a unique atomic number, and consists of atoms that are identical in their chemical properties. This definition captures the central idea that elements are the fundamental building blocks of chemistry, distinguished by their atomic number and unified by the uniformity of their atoms. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the phenomenon of isotopy. Take the element chlorine, atomic number 17. Natural chlorine is a mixture of two stable isotopes: chlorine-35, with eighteen neutrons, and chlorine-37, with twenty. These isotopes share the same number of protons and electrons and are therefore very nearly identical in their chemical behaviour. Yet they are not strictly identical. The heavier isotope diffuses more slowly, and in certain reactions the rate of chemical transformation differs measurably between the two — a phenomenon known as the kinetic isotope effect. Condition (iii) of the definition requires that all atoms of the element be identical in their chemical properties, and this requirement is not, in strictness, met. One might observe that the definition therefore faces a dilemma: either it must relax the requirement of strict identity and speak instead of approximate or predominant similarity, or it must conclude that no naturally occurring element fully satisfies its conditions. It appears that the definition, by insisting on the strict identity of all atoms of an element, cannot accommodate the isotopic variation that is a pervasive feature of the natural world.

definition: A condition D afflicting an organism O constitutes a disease if and only if (i) D involves a departure from the normal physiological functioning of O's body, (ii) D causes suffering or incapacity in O, and (iii) D has a specific identifiable aetiology — that is, a determinate cause.
conditions: D involves a departure from the normal physiological functioning of O's body
D causes suffering or incapacity in O
D has a specific identifiable aetiology
counterexample: Consider an individual who carries a single copy of the gene for sickle-cell anaemia. Under normal conditions, she exhibits no departure from normal physiological functioning and experiences no suffering or incapacity. Yet under conditions of low oxygen — at high altitude or during strenuous exercise — her red blood cells may sickle, producing mild symptoms. The condition has a specific identifiable aetiology: the genetic mutation. But under ordinary conditions, conditions (i) and (ii) are not met, and the carrier is clinically healthy. Indeed, in regions where malaria is endemic, the carrier state confers a survival advantage. The definition struggles to classify a condition that is pathological only in specific environments and advantageous in others.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that whether a condition constitutes a disease may depend upon the environment in which the organism finds itself, so that the same genetic or physiological state may be pathological in one context and neutral or advantageous in another.
passage: One might characterise disease as a condition involving departure from normal physiological function, causing suffering or incapacity, and possessing a specific identifiable aetiology. This definition captures the clinical intuition that diseases are harmful deviations from the healthy norm with determinate causes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the sickle-cell trait. An individual who carries a single copy of the sickle-cell gene — that is, who is heterozygous for the mutation — typically exhibits no departure from normal physiological function under ordinary conditions. She experiences no suffering and no incapacity. Conditions (i) and (ii) of the definition are not satisfied, and she is clinically healthy. Yet the same individual, when exposed to conditions of low oxygen at high altitude or during extreme exertion, may experience episodes of red blood cell sickling that produce genuine symptoms. The condition has a specific identifiable aetiology — the point mutation in the haemoglobin gene. Moreover, in regions where falciparum malaria is endemic, the heterozygous sickle-cell state confers a significant survival advantage, for sickled red blood cells resist malarial parasites. One might observe that the definition, by treating disease as an intrinsic property of the organism, cannot accommodate conditions whose pathological character depends upon the environment. The same genetic state is pathological at altitude, neutral at sea level, and advantageous in a malarial zone. It appears that the definition requires supplementation by a recognition that the boundary between health and disease is, in many cases, relative to the organism's ecological circumstances.

definition: A trait T of an organism O is an adaptation if and only if (i) T enhances the fitness of O in its current environment, (ii) T is heritable, and (iii) T is the product of natural selection acting on variation in previous generations.
conditions: T enhances the fitness of O in its current environment
T is heritable
T is the product of natural selection acting on variation in previous generations
counterexample: Consider a population of moths that, prior to industrialisation, were predominantly light-coloured and camouflaged against lichen-covered tree bark. After industrial pollution darkened the bark, a pre-existing dark variant increased in frequency because it was now better camouflaged. The dark coloration enhances fitness in the current sooty environment, it is heritable, and its increase in frequency is the product of natural selection. All three conditions are met. Yet the dark coloration was not originally produced by natural selection as a response to industrial pollution; it was a pre-existing variant that happened to be advantageous in the new environment. One might question whether a trait that was not originally selected for its current function should be called an adaptation to the current environment.
missing_condition: The definition neglects to distinguish between traits that were selected for their current function and traits that were selected for a different function, or arose for non-selective reasons, and only incidentally serve their present role — a distinction that requires attending to the historical function for which the trait was originally selected.
passage: A natural proposal for characterising adaptation holds that a trait is an adaptation if it enhances the organism's fitness in its current environment, is heritable, and is the product of natural selection. This definition captures the functional and historical dimensions that biologists typically associate with the concept. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the well-known phenomenon of industrial melanism in moths. Before the onset of heavy industrialisation in nineteenth-century England, populations of the peppered moth were predominantly light-coloured, their pale wings providing camouflage against the lichen-covered bark of trees. A dark variant existed at low frequency. When industrial soot darkened the tree bark, the dark variant enjoyed a marked advantage in concealment from predators, and its frequency increased rapidly under natural selection. The dark coloration enhances fitness in the current sooty environment. It is heritable. And its increase in frequency is the product of natural selection acting upon pre-existing variation. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the dark coloration was not originally produced by natural selection as a response to industrial pollution. It was a pre-existing variant — perhaps originally neutral or even slightly disadvantageous — that happened to be advantageous when the environment changed. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the trait be a product of natural selection without specifying the function for which it was selected, cannot distinguish genuine adaptations from traits that are merely fortuitously useful in a new environment.

definition: A process P constitutes a chemical reaction if and only if (i) P involves the rearrangement of atoms by the breaking and forming of chemical bonds, (ii) the substances present at the conclusion of P differ in composition from those present at its commencement, and (iii) P is accompanied by a measurable change in energy — either the absorption or release of heat.
conditions: P involves the rearrangement of atoms by the breaking and forming of chemical bonds
The substances present at the conclusion differ in composition from those at the commencement
P is accompanied by a measurable change in energy
counterexample: Consider the dissolution of sodium chloride in water. The ionic bonds holding the crystal lattice together are broken as sodium and chloride ions are solvated by water molecules, which form new ion-dipole interactions. The substances present at the conclusion — hydrated sodium ions and hydrated chloride ions in solution — differ from the solid salt and liquid water present at the start. The process is accompanied by a small but measurable change in energy. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet chemists typically classify dissolution as a physical process rather than a chemical reaction, for no covalent bonds are formed and no new chemical compounds are produced.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the rearrangement of atoms produce new chemical compounds with different molecular identities, rather than merely separating the components of an existing compound into its constituent ions or molecules.
passage: One might define a chemical reaction as a process involving the rearrangement of atoms through the breaking and forming of bonds, yielding substances of different composition, and accompanied by a measurable energy change. This proposal captures the essential features that distinguish chemical transformations from mere physical mixing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the familiar process of dissolving common salt in water. When sodium chloride is added to water, the ionic bonds of the crystal lattice are disrupted as individual sodium and chloride ions are surrounded by water molecules that form ion-dipole interactions. The substances present at the end of the process — hydrated ions dispersed throughout the liquid — differ from those present at the start — a solid crystalline salt and pure liquid water. The process is accompanied by a small but measurable enthalpy change. All three conditions of the definition appear to be satisfied. Nevertheless, chemists standardly classify dissolution as a physical process rather than a chemical reaction. No new chemical compounds are formed. The sodium and chloride ions retain their chemical identity; they are merely dispersed rather than transformed. One might observe that the definition, by speaking broadly of bond-breaking and bond-forming without distinguishing the types of bond involved, and by requiring only a change in the composition of the mixture rather than the production of genuinely new compounds, cannot distinguish chemical reactions from physical processes that involve the disruption of intermolecular or ionic forces without the creation of new molecular species.

definition: A procedure M yields an accurate measurement of a quantity Q if and only if (i) M produces a numerical value that approximates the true value of Q within a stated margin of error, (ii) M is reproducible — that is, repeated applications of M under the same conditions yield values within the stated margin, and (iii) M employs an instrument that has been calibrated against a recognised standard.
conditions: M produces a numerical value that approximates the true value of Q within a stated margin of error
M is reproducible under the same conditions
M employs an instrument that has been calibrated against a recognised standard
counterexample: Consider a thermometer that has been calibrated against a recognised standard and that, when used to measure the temperature of a liquid, produces reproducible readings that fall within the stated margin of error of the liquid's true temperature. All three conditions are met. Now suppose that the liquid is a chemical mixture undergoing a slow exothermic reaction, so that its temperature is rising gradually. The thermometer, owing to thermal lag, consistently reports a temperature slightly below the liquid's actual current temperature — but within the stated margin. The measurement is accurate by the definition. Yet the investigator, unaware of the reaction, interprets the stable readings as evidence that the liquid is in thermal equilibrium, which it is not.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the conditions under which M is applied be appropriate for the quantity being measured — specifically, that the system under measurement be in a state compatible with the assumptions upon which the measurement procedure rests.
passage: One might characterise an accurate measurement as one in which a calibrated instrument produces reproducible numerical values that approximate the true value within a stated margin of error. This definition captures the metrological requirements of calibration, precision, and accuracy that laboratory practice demands. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when the conditions of measurement are subtly inappropriate. Consider a mercury thermometer that has been calibrated against a recognised standard and that produces highly reproducible readings. It is used to measure the temperature of a beaker of liquid, and the readings fall within the stated margin of error of the liquid's true temperature. All three conditions are satisfied. Now suppose that the liquid is a chemical mixture undergoing a slow exothermic reaction. Its temperature is rising gradually — by a fraction of a degree per minute. The thermometer, owing to the finite time required for thermal equilibration between the mercury and the liquid, consistently reports a temperature that lags slightly behind the liquid's actual current temperature. The lag falls within the stated margin of error, so the measurement counts as accurate by the definition. Yet the investigator, unaware of the ongoing reaction, interprets the stable and reproducible readings as evidence that the liquid is in thermal equilibrium. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the instrument's calibration and the reproducibility and accuracy of its readings, cannot detect cases in which the measurement procedure rests upon an assumption — here, thermal equilibrium — that is not satisfied.

definition: A report R constitutes a scientific observation if and only if (i) R describes a state of affairs that was directly perceived by the observer through one or more sense modalities, (ii) R is expressed in terms that are intersubjectively accessible — that is, other competent observers could verify the same state of affairs under the same conditions, and (iii) R is free from theoretical interpretation.
conditions: R describes a state of affairs directly perceived through one or more sense modalities
R is expressed in intersubjectively accessible terms
R is free from theoretical interpretation
counterexample: Consider an astronomer who reports observing a stellar parallax — a slight shift in the apparent position of a nearby star against the background of more distant stars, as measured from two positions in the earth's orbit. The report describes what is directly perceived — the shift in apparent position — and is expressed in intersubjectively accessible terms. Yet the very identification of the phenomenon as parallax involves the theoretical assumption that the shift is caused by the earth's orbital motion rather than by some other factor. The observation is not free from theoretical interpretation; indeed, it presupposes a heliocentric framework. All the same, it would be peculiar to deny that the astronomer has made a genuine scientific observation.
passage: One might propose that a scientific observation is a report describing a directly perceived state of affairs, expressed in intersubjectively accessible terms, and free from theoretical interpretation. This proposal captures the empiricist ideal of observations as the neutral foundation upon which scientific theories are built. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers the theory-laden character of scientific observation in practice. Suppose an astronomer reports observing a stellar parallax — a minute displacement in the apparent position of a nearby star when observed from opposite points in the earth's annual orbit. The report describes what the astronomer directly perceives: a shift in the star's position against the background field. It is expressed in terms that any competent astronomer could verify. Yet the very act of identifying the phenomenon as parallax involves a considerable burden of theoretical interpretation. The astronomer assumes that the shift is caused by the earth's motion around the sun rather than by proper motion of the star, atmospheric refraction, or instrumental error. She assumes a heliocentric model of the solar system and a particular account of the geometry of stellar distances. Condition (iii) of the definition is not satisfied, for the observation is thoroughly permeated by theoretical commitments. Nevertheless, it would be peculiar to deny that the astronomer has made a genuine scientific observation. It appears that the definition, by requiring freedom from theoretical interpretation, imposes a condition that no interesting scientific observation can satisfy, for the identification and description of scientifically significant phenomena invariably involves theoretical presuppositions.

definition: A procedure E constitutes a well-designed scientific experiment if and only if (i) E tests a specific hypothesis by comparing an experimental group with a control group, (ii) E holds all variables constant except the one under investigation, and (iii) E is described in sufficient detail to permit replication by other investigators.
conditions: E tests a specific hypothesis by comparing an experimental group with a control group
E holds all variables constant except the one under investigation
E is described in sufficient detail to permit replication
counterexample: Consider Darwin's voyage on HMS Beagle, during which he observed the variation of finch species across the Galapagos archipelago. His observations were systematic, carefully recorded, and described in detail sufficient for others to verify them. Yet there was no experimental group, no control group, and no manipulation of variables. Darwin's investigation was observational rather than experimental. Nevertheless, his observations constituted some of the most important scientific work of the nineteenth century and were essential to the formulation of the theory of natural selection.
passage: One might characterise a well-designed scientific experiment as a procedure that tests a hypothesis by comparing experimental and control groups, holds all variables constant except the one under investigation, and is described in sufficient detail for replication. This definition captures the controlled-experiment paradigm that has become the gold standard in many branches of science. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation as a general account of good scientific procedure arises when one considers the great observational investigations of natural history. Consider Darwin's study of the finches of the Galapagos archipelago during the voyage of HMS Beagle. Darwin did not compare an experimental group with a control group. He did not manipulate any variable. He observed the natural variation of finch species across different islands, recording beak morphology, feeding habits, and habitat with meticulous care. His descriptions were detailed enough for any competent naturalist to verify them. Yet no condition of the definition is satisfied except the third. There was no hypothesis-driven comparison of groups and no controlled manipulation of variables. Nevertheless, Darwin's Galapagos observations rank among the most consequential pieces of scientific work ever undertaken. They provided the empirical foundation for the theory of natural selection. It appears that the definition, by requiring the controlled-experiment format as a necessary condition for good scientific procedure, excludes observational investigations that have been indispensable to the advancement of science. Not all rigorous scientific inquiry takes the form of a controlled experiment.

definition: A species S has undergone extinction if and only if (i) no living individual of S exists anywhere on earth, (ii) the last individual of S has died, and (iii) S cannot be revived or reconstituted by any means.
conditions: No living individual of S exists anywhere on earth
The last individual of S has died
S cannot be revived or reconstituted by any means
counterexample: Consider the case of a plant species believed to be extinct for over a century, whose seeds are later discovered in a sealed herbarium specimen. The seeds prove to be viable: when planted under appropriate conditions, they germinate and produce healthy individuals of the species. Conditions (i) and (ii) were satisfied at the time the species was declared extinct — no living individual existed and the last individual had long since died. Yet condition (iii) was not satisfied, for the species could be reconstituted from its dormant seeds. One might observe that the species was genuinely extinct in one sense — no living individual existed — and yet not extinct in another — its revival was possible. The definition, by combining these requirements, conflates two distinct concepts.
missing_condition: The definition conflates the absence of all living individuals with the impossibility of reconstitution, failing to recognise that a species may have no living members and yet retain the potential for revival through dormant propagules, preserved genetic material, or other means.
passage: One might define extinction as the state in which no living individual of a species exists, the last individual has died, and the species cannot be revived by any means. This definition aims to capture the finality that the concept of extinction is ordinarily taken to imply. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the botanical sciences, where the boundary between existence and extinction is less sharp than it might appear. Consider a species of flowering plant native to a remote oceanic island. The last known individual died over a century ago, and the species was declared extinct. No living individual exists anywhere on earth. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet decades later, a botanist examining a sealed herbarium collection discovers seeds of the species, carefully preserved in a dry envelope. She plants them under appropriate conditions. They germinate. Healthy individuals of the species grow, flower, and set seed. The species has been reconstituted from dormant material. Condition (iii) of the definition was not satisfied at the time the species was declared extinct, though this was not known. One might observe that the definition, by requiring that revival be impossible, imposes a condition that can never be verified at the time an extinction is declared, for one cannot know what dormant propagules or preserved materials may exist in undiscovered collections. It appears that the definition conflates two distinct questions — whether any individual is currently alive and whether revival is possible — and that the concept of extinction would be better served by attending to the former without insisting upon the latter.

definition: Two organisms belong to the same species if and only if (i) they can interbreed to produce viable offspring, (ii) their offspring are themselves fertile, and (iii) the interbreeding occurs under natural conditions without human intervention.
conditions: They can interbreed to produce viable offspring
Their offspring are themselves fertile
The interbreeding occurs under natural conditions without human intervention
counterexample: Consider the case of ring species in certain warblers distributed around a mountain range. Population A interbreeds with population B, B with C, C with D, and D with E, each pair producing fertile offspring under natural conditions. Yet populations A and E, though connected by this chain of interfertile populations, cannot interbreed at all where their ranges overlap. The definition assigns A and B to the same species, B and C to the same species, and so on, yet it must deny that A and E belong to the same species. Species membership, which one might expect to be transitive, is not.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the possibility that interfertility may be a non-transitive relation, so that a chain of interfertile populations may connect organisms that are themselves reproductively isolated from one another.
passage: One might define species membership by the capacity for interbreeding under natural conditions to produce fertile offspring. This biological species concept captures the reproductive cohesion that binds populations into evolutionary units. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the phenomenon of ring species. Consider a series of warbler populations distributed around a mountain range. Population A, at the western end, interbreeds with population B to its east, producing fertile offspring under natural conditions. Population B interbreeds with population C, C with D, and D with population E at the eastern end. Each adjacent pair satisfies all three conditions of the definition. Yet where the ranges of populations A and E overlap at the southern end of the ring, they do not interbreed at all. They behave, in that zone of contact, as distinct species. The definition assigns each adjacent pair to the same species, yet the terminal populations are reproductively isolated. Species membership, which the definition treats as a relation that can be extended transitively through chains of interfertile populations, proves not to be transitive. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only pairwise interfertility, cannot accommodate the gradual divergence that transforms a single species into two through a connected chain of intermediates. It appears that the reproductive criterion, however sound in the paradigmatic cases, breaks down where speciation is a continuous rather than a discrete process.

definition: A substance S qualifies as a pure chemical element if and only if (i) every atom of S has the same atomic number, (ii) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, and (iii) S exists in a single stable allotropic form under standard conditions.
conditions: Every atom of S has the same atomic number
S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means
S exists in a single stable allotropic form under standard conditions
counterexample: Consider carbon. Every atom of carbon has atomic number 6. Carbon cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means. All three conditions require, however, that carbon exist in a single stable allotropic form under standard conditions. Yet carbon exists in at least two well-known stable allotropes at standard temperature and pressure: diamond and graphite. These differ dramatically in their physical properties — hardness, electrical conductivity, optical behaviour — despite being composed of identical atoms. Condition (iii) is not satisfied, yet no one would deny that carbon is a pure chemical element.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that a single element may exist in multiple stable allotropic forms under the same conditions, so that the requirement of a single allotrope incorrectly excludes elements whose atoms arrange themselves into structurally distinct stable configurations.
passage: One might characterise a pure chemical element as a substance all of whose atoms share the same atomic number, which cannot be decomposed by chemical means, and which exists in a single stable allotropic form under standard conditions. This proposal adds to the familiar criteria of atomic identity and chemical irreducibility a requirement of structural uniqueness that aims to capture the idea that a pure element presents a single, determinate character. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is furnished by one of the most familiar elements: carbon. Every atom of carbon possesses atomic number six. Carbon cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical process. But carbon does not exist in a single stable allotropic form under standard conditions. At standard temperature and pressure, carbon is stable both as diamond — a transparent, extraordinarily hard crystalline solid — and as graphite — a soft, opaque, electrically conductive layered material. The two forms differ so dramatically in their physical properties that they might, to an observer unfamiliar with chemistry, appear to be entirely different substances. Condition (iii) of the definition is not satisfied, and yet no chemist would deny that carbon is a pure element. One might observe that the definition, by requiring a single allotropic form, confuses the identity of a substance at the atomic level with its macroscopic structural organisation. It appears that the concept of a chemical element is more fundamental than the concept of an allotrope, and that an element may present itself in multiple stable guises without ceasing to be a single element.

definition: An entity X is alive if and only if (i) X is capable of self-reproduction, (ii) X undergoes metabolism — that is, it converts energy from its environment to sustain its internal organisation, and (iii) X responds to stimuli from its environment in ways that promote its continued existence.
conditions: X is capable of self-reproduction
X undergoes metabolism
X responds to stimuli from its environment in ways that promote its continued existence
counterexample: Consider a sterile mule — the offspring of a horse and a donkey. The mule metabolises food, maintains its internal organisation, and responds to environmental stimuli in ways that promote its survival: it seeks shelter, avoids predators, and regulates its body temperature. Yet the mule is incapable of self-reproduction; it is sterile. Condition (i) is not satisfied. Nevertheless, it would be absurd to classify the mule as non-living.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate organisms that are alive in every recognisable sense yet are incapable of reproduction, whether by virtue of hybrid sterility, age, injury, or developmental circumstance.
passage: One might define a living entity as one that self-reproduces, metabolises, and responds to environmental stimuli in self-preserving ways. This tripartite analysis captures the features that are most conspicuous in the organisms we encounter: bacteria divide, plants photosynthesise, animals flee predators. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the common mule. A mule is the offspring of a male donkey and a female horse. It metabolises food with impressive efficiency — mules are prized for their stamina and endurance. It responds to environmental stimuli in sophisticated and self-preserving ways: it avoids precipices, seeks water, and retreats from threats. But it is sterile. The chromosomal incompatibility inherited from its two parent species renders it incapable of reproduction. Condition (i) of the definition is not satisfied. Yet it would be extraordinary to conclude that a mule is not alive. It breathes, eats, moves, perceives, and will eventually die — all hallmarks of a living creature. One might observe that the definition, by including self-reproduction as a necessary condition, draws the boundary of life in a way that excludes organisms whose claim to be living is beyond any serious doubt. It appears that reproduction, while a characteristic feature of living things considered as populations, is not a necessary property of each individual organism.

definition: A condition C afflicting an organism O constitutes a disease if and only if (i) C produces signs or symptoms that are detectable by clinical examination, (ii) C diminishes the well-being or functional capacity of O, and (iii) C has a natural history — that is, it progresses through characteristic stages over time.
conditions: C produces signs or symptoms detectable by clinical examination
C diminishes the well-being or functional capacity of O
C has a natural history that progresses through characteristic stages
counterexample: Consider an individual found to carry a genetic mutation that confers a substantially elevated risk of developing a serious malignancy. The mutation is detectable by laboratory examination but produces no signs or symptoms on clinical examination. The individual is entirely well — she has no diminished well-being or functional capacity. The condition does not progress through stages; it is a fixed feature of her genome. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet physicians treat the mutation as a medically significant condition, schedule regular surveillance, and may recommend prophylactic intervention. One might observe that the definition cannot accommodate conditions that are genuinely pathological in their potential yet clinically silent in their present state.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that a condition may constitute a disease, or at least a medically significant pathological state, by virtue of its disposition to produce future harm, even when it presently produces no symptoms, causes no functional impairment, and follows no characteristic temporal course.
passage: One might characterise disease as a condition that produces detectable signs or symptoms, diminishes well-being or functional capacity, and progresses through characteristic stages. This analysis captures the clinical picture that physicians associate with recognised diseases: the patient presents with complaints, the examination reveals abnormalities, and the condition follows a known course. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the domain of genetic medicine. Consider a woman who, in the course of a routine screening, is found to carry a germline mutation that confers a substantially elevated lifetime risk of developing a serious malignancy. At the time of discovery, she is entirely asymptomatic. Clinical examination reveals no abnormalities. She experiences no diminution of well-being or functional capacity. The mutation does not progress through stages — it is a fixed feature of her genome, present from conception. None of the three conditions of the definition is satisfied. Yet her physicians treat the finding with the gravity they would accord to a diagnosis. They schedule regular surveillance imaging. They discuss prophylactic surgical options. They enter the mutation into her medical record as a significant pathological finding. One might observe that the definition, by requiring present symptoms, present functional impairment, and a progressive natural history, cannot accommodate conditions that are genuinely and seriously pathological in their potential while being entirely silent in their present manifestation. It appears that the concept of disease extends beyond the presently symptomatic to encompass dispositional states that threaten future harm.

definition: A biological trait T is an adaptation for function F if and only if (i) T currently performs F in the organism's natural environment, (ii) the performance of F by T enhances the organism's reproductive success, and (iii) T was shaped by natural selection to perform F.
conditions: T currently performs F in the organism's natural environment
The performance of F by T enhances the organism's reproductive success
T was shaped by natural selection to perform F
counterexample: Consider the feathers of birds. Feathers are currently used for flight and clearly enhance reproductive success by permitting access to food sources and escape from predators. Yet palaeontological evidence suggests that feathers first appeared in small theropod dinosaurs for the purpose of thermal insulation, not flight. Feathers were shaped by natural selection for insulation, not for the aerial function they now serve. Condition (iii) is not satisfied with respect to flight. Yet it would be strange to deny that feathers are an adaptation for flight in modern birds.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the original selective function for which a trait was shaped and the current function it serves, so that traits which have been co-opted from one function to another cannot be classified as adaptations for their present role.
passage: One might propose that a trait is an adaptation for a particular function if it currently performs that function, if doing so enhances reproductive success, and if natural selection shaped the trait for that purpose. This analysis captures the historical and functional dimensions that biologists associate with the concept of adaptation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when one considers traits that have been co-opted from one function to another over evolutionary time. Consider the feathers of modern birds. Feathers currently serve the function of flight, and their contribution to reproductive success through aerial mobility is beyond dispute. Conditions (i) and (ii) are plainly satisfied with respect to flight. Yet the palaeontological record reveals that feathers first appeared in small non-avian theropod dinosaurs that did not fly. In these organisms, feathers appear to have functioned as thermal insulation, and it was for insulation — not flight — that natural selection originally shaped them. The co-option of feathers for aerial locomotion came later, in a lineage that exploited an existing structure for a novel purpose. Condition (iii) is not satisfied with respect to flight, for feathers were not shaped by selection to serve that function. Yet one might observe that it would be perverse to deny that feathers are an adaptation for flight in modern birds. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the current function coincide with the historical function for which the trait was selected, cannot accommodate the widespread phenomenon of evolutionary co-option.

definition: A chemical reaction has reached equilibrium if and only if (i) the concentrations of all reactants and products remain constant over time, (ii) no further macroscopic change is observable in the system, and (iii) the system has been left undisturbed for a sufficient period.
conditions: The concentrations of all reactants and products remain constant over time
No further macroscopic change is observable in the system
The system has been left undisturbed for a sufficient period
counterexample: Consider a supersaturated sugar solution that has been prepared by dissolving sugar in hot water and then cooling the solution slowly without disturbance. The concentrations remain constant. No macroscopic change is observable. The system has been undisturbed for an extended period. All three conditions are met. Yet the system is not at equilibrium — it is in a metastable state. The introduction of a single seed crystal precipitates a rapid crystallisation, demonstrating that the constant concentrations were maintained not by a balance of forward and reverse processes but by a kinetic barrier to the transition to the true equilibrium state.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the constancy of concentrations arise from a genuine dynamic balance between forward and reverse processes, rather than from a kinetic barrier that merely prevents the system from reaching its true equilibrium state.
passage: One might characterise chemical equilibrium as a state in which concentrations remain constant, no macroscopic change is observable, and the system has been left undisturbed. This characterisation captures the appearance of equilibrium as encountered in the laboratory: a system that has settled into apparent stability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation is provided by the phenomenon of supersaturation. Consider a sugar solution prepared by dissolving a large quantity of sugar in hot water and then allowing the solution to cool slowly and without disturbance. At room temperature, the concentration of dissolved sugar exceeds the solubility limit — the solution is supersaturated. Yet the concentrations remain constant over time. No macroscopic change is observable; the solution appears perfectly stable. The system has been undisturbed. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the system is not at equilibrium. It is in a metastable state, held there by a kinetic barrier to nucleation. The introduction of a single seed crystal, or even a slight mechanical disturbance, precipitates a rapid and dramatic crystallisation, as the dissolved sugar transitions to the thermodynamically favoured solid phase. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the constancy of concentrations and the absence of observable change, cannot distinguish true dynamic equilibrium from metastable states in which constancy is maintained not by a balance of opposing processes but by a barrier that prevents the system from reaching its thermodynamic minimum.

definition: A measurement of a physical quantity Q is objective if and only if (i) the measurement procedure is specified in terms that any competent observer could follow, (ii) different observers following the procedure obtain the same result within the stated margin of error, and (iii) the result is independent of the theoretical commitments of the observer.
conditions: The measurement procedure is specified in terms any competent observer could follow
Different observers following the procedure obtain the same result within the stated margin of error
The result is independent of the theoretical commitments of the observer
counterexample: Consider the measurement of stellar brightness by photographic plates in the early twentieth century. The procedure was specified precisely enough for any trained astronomer to follow. Different observers obtained closely agreeing results. Yet the results depended upon the spectral sensitivity of the photographic emulsion, which weighted certain wavelengths of light more heavily than others. Two observers using plates with different emulsions — both following the procedure correctly — would obtain systematically different brightness values for the same star. The discrepancy arises not from the observers' theoretical commitments but from a feature of the instrument that the procedure takes for granted.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the measurement procedure specify or control for the instrumental characteristics that mediate between the quantity measured and the result obtained, so that results may vary systematically with the properties of the instrument even when all stated procedural requirements are satisfied.
passage: One might characterise the objectivity of a measurement by the public specifiability of the procedure, the agreement of results among observers, and the independence of the result from the observer's theoretical commitments. This proposal captures the intersubjectivity and theory-neutrality that are commonly regarded as hallmarks of objective measurement. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the history of astronomical photometry. In the early decades of the twentieth century, the brightness of stars was measured by exposing photographic plates to starlight and comparing the resulting images. The procedure was specified in precise terms that any competent astronomer could follow. Different observers using the same type of plate obtained closely agreeing results. The results did not depend upon the observers' theoretical views about stellar physics. All three conditions of the definition were satisfied. Yet the results depended critically upon the spectral sensitivity of the photographic emulsion employed. An emulsion sensitive to blue light would assign a different brightness to a red star than an emulsion sensitive to red light, even though both measurements were performed correctly according to the stated procedure. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only inter-observer agreement and procedural specifiability, cannot detect systematic dependencies on instrumental properties that the procedure does not control for. It appears that the objectivity of a measurement requires not merely agreement among observers but the identification and specification of all features of the measuring apparatus that could influence the result.

definition: An event A is the cause of an event B if and only if (i) A precedes B in time, (ii) A and B are spatiotemporally contiguous or connected by a chain of spatiotemporally contiguous events, and (iii) events of type A are regularly followed by events of type B.
conditions: A precedes B in time
A and B are spatiotemporally contiguous or connected by a chain of contiguous events
Events of type A are regularly followed by events of type B
counterexample: Consider the sounding of a factory whistle at noon and the departure of workers from a neighbouring factory at the same hour. The whistle precedes the departure by a few moments. The two events are spatiotemporally proximate. And events of the first type — the sounding of the whistle — are regularly followed by events of the second type — the workers' departure. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the whistle does not cause the workers to leave; they leave because their shift has ended. The regular conjunction of whistle and departure is explained by a common cause — the clock striking noon — rather than by any causal relation between them.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish genuine causal connections from mere regular conjunctions produced by a common cause, so that any pair of events that are regularly conjoined and appropriately ordered in space and time qualifies as causally related.
passage: One might analyse causation as a relation between events in which the cause precedes the effect, the two are spatiotemporally connected, and events of the relevant types are regularly conjoined. This Humean analysis has the virtue of resting upon observable features — temporal order, spatial contiguity, and regularity — without appeal to any mysterious necessary connection. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed from the familiar phenomenon of common-cause correlations. Consider a factory whistle that sounds at noon each day. A few moments later, workers emerge from a neighbouring factory and make their way home. The whistle precedes the departure. The two events are spatiotemporally proximate — the factories stand across the road from one another. And the conjunction is perfectly regular: the whistle sounds and the workers depart, day after day, without exception. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the whistle does not cause the workers to leave. They leave because their shift has ended, and the shift ends at noon, and the whistle sounds at noon. The regular conjunction of whistle and departure is produced by a common cause — the clock striking twelve — rather than by any causal relation between the whistle and the departure. One might observe that the definition, by resting causation upon regularity, temporal precedence, and contiguity alone, cannot distinguish genuine causal relations from the spurious regularities that arise whenever two effects of a common cause are regularly conjoined.

definition: A regularity R qualifies as a law of nature if and only if (i) R holds without exception across all observed instances, (ii) R is expressible as a universal generalisation of the form 'For all x, if Fx then Gx,' and (iii) R supports predictions about unobserved instances.
conditions: R holds without exception across all observed instances
R is expressible as a universal generalisation
R supports predictions about unobserved instances
counterexample: Consider the regularity that all the screws in the drawer of a particular workbench are brass. This regularity holds without exception across all observed instances — every screw examined has been brass. It is expressible as a universal generalisation: for all x, if x is a screw in this drawer, then x is brass. And it supports a prediction: the next screw drawn from the drawer will be brass. All three conditions are met. Yet the regularity is plainly accidental; it reflects the purchasing habits of the workbench's owner and not any feature of natural necessity.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that R hold in virtue of the natural kinds or properties it invokes, so that merely accidental regularities whose universality is a product of contingent circumstances cannot be distinguished from regularities that express genuine natural necessities.
passage: One might characterise a law of nature as an exceptionless regularity that is expressible as a universal generalisation and supports predictions about unobserved cases. This empiricist analysis captures the evidential basis upon which scientists typically rely when identifying laws and the predictive power that laws are expected to possess. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the mundane circumstances of a workshop. Consider the regularity that all the screws in the drawer of a particular workbench are brass. Every screw that has been examined has indeed been brass. The regularity is expressible as a universal generalisation: for all x, if x is a screw in this drawer, then x is brass. And the regularity supports a prediction: if one draws another screw from the drawer, it will be brass, and this prediction will prove correct. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet no one would regard this as a law of nature. The regularity reflects nothing more than the fact that the owner of the workbench purchased a box of brass screws and placed them in the drawer. It is an accident of purchasing, not a feature of the natural order. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only exceptionlessness, universality, and predictive power, cannot distinguish laws that hold in virtue of the natures of the properties they invoke from regularities that hold merely because of contingent arrangements of particular objects.

definition: An observation O is scientifically reliable if and only if (i) O is made under controlled conditions designed to minimise the influence of confounding factors, (ii) O is reproducible by independent investigators, and (iii) O is recorded by means of calibrated instruments whose operation is well understood.
conditions: O is made under controlled conditions designed to minimise confounding factors
O is reproducible by independent investigators
O is recorded by calibrated instruments whose operation is well understood
counterexample: Consider a series of astronomical observations of a supernova made by independent investigators using well-calibrated telescopes under excellent viewing conditions. The observations are reproducible and the instruments are well understood. All three conditions are met. Yet the supernova occurred in a galaxy whose light has been gravitationally lensed by an intervening mass, producing multiple images of the same event arriving at different times. The astronomers, unaware of the lensing, record what they take to be multiple distinct supernovae. Their observations are reliable by the definition, yet the phenomenon they observe is not what they take it to be.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the observers possess an adequate understanding of the conditions intervening between the phenomenon and the instrument, so that distortions introduced by the medium of observation — in this case, gravitational lensing — may produce systematically misleading results even when all stated conditions of reliability are satisfied.
passage: One might characterise scientifically reliable observation as observation made under controlled conditions, reproducible by independent investigators, and recorded with calibrated instruments. This analysis captures the methodological safeguards that the experimental sciences employ to ensure the trustworthiness of their data. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in observational astronomy. Suppose a supernova is observed in a distant galaxy by several independent teams of astronomers, each using well-calibrated telescopes under excellent atmospheric conditions. The observations are reproducible: each team records the same event with closely agreeing measurements of brightness, spectral characteristics, and position. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the galaxy in which the supernova occurred lies behind a massive intervening cluster of galaxies whose gravitational field bends the light from the supernova, producing multiple images that arrive at the observer at different times. The astronomers, unaware of the gravitational lensing, record what they take to be several distinct supernovae occurring at slightly different times and positions. Their individual observations are reliable by the definition — controlled, reproducible, and instrumentally sound — yet their interpretation of the data is systematically mistaken. One might observe that the definition, by confining reliability to the conditions of observation and the characteristics of the instruments, cannot detect distortions introduced by the medium through which the observation passes. It appears that the reliability of an observation requires not only instrumental and procedural adequacy but an understanding of the physical conditions that intervene between the phenomenon and the observer.

definition: A group of organisms G constitutes a species if and only if (i) the members of G can interbreed and produce fertile offspring, (ii) the members of G are reproductively isolated from members of other groups, and (iii) the members of G share a characteristic morphology.
conditions: The members of G can interbreed and produce fertile offspring
The members of G are reproductively isolated from other groups
The members of G share a characteristic morphology
counterexample: Consider the case of ring species, such as the herring gull and lesser black-backed gull complex encircling the Arctic. Adjacent populations can interbreed and produce fertile offspring, but the terminal populations of the ring — those that meet after the chain has encircled the pole — cannot interbreed. The first and second conditions are satisfied between some pairs of populations and violated between others. Yet biologists treat the entire complex as involving distinct species at the terminal ends. The definition cannot accommodate this continuous gradient from interfertility to reproductive isolation.
missing_condition: The definition treats species boundaries as sharply defined by interbreeding capacity, failing to accommodate the continuous and gradual character of speciation, in which reproductive isolation develops incrementally and species boundaries may be genuinely indeterminate.
passage: One might define a species as a group of organisms whose members can interbreed to produce fertile offspring, are reproductively isolated from other groups, and share a characteristic morphology. This biological species concept captures many familiar cases — lions, tigers, horses — with apparent success. Yet a case which tells against its sharp boundaries may be drawn from the phenomenon of ring species. Consider the gull populations that form a chain around the Arctic. Adjacent populations interbreed freely and produce fertile offspring; the gulls at each point along the chain are morphologically similar to their neighbours. But at the point where the chain closes — where the terminal populations meet — the gulls can no longer interbreed. They are reproductively isolated. The first condition is satisfied between adjacent populations and violated between terminal populations; the second condition is violated between adjacent populations and satisfied between terminal ones. The definition requires a sharp boundary between species, but the ring species presents a continuous gradient from interfertility to reproductive isolation. It appears that the definition cannot accommodate the gradual and continuous character of speciation, in which reproductive isolation develops incrementally and species boundaries may be genuinely indeterminate rather than sharply drawn.

definition: An entity E is alive if and only if (i) E is capable of metabolism — the chemical transformation of energy and materials, (ii) E is capable of reproduction, and (iii) E responds to stimuli from its environment.
conditions: E is capable of metabolism
E is capable of reproduction
E responds to stimuli from its environment
counterexample: Consider a mule — the offspring of a horse and a donkey. A mule is capable of metabolism; it eats, breathes, and transforms food into energy. It responds to stimuli — it reacts to light, sound, touch, and temperature. But it is incapable of reproduction; mules are almost invariably sterile. The second condition is violated. Yet a mule is unquestionably alive. To deny life to a mule because it cannot reproduce would be absurd.
missing_condition: The definition makes reproductive capacity a necessary condition of life, failing to recognise that individual organisms may be alive without being able to reproduce — sterility does not entail death, and life at the level of the individual does not require the capacity to produce offspring.
passage: One might define life as the conjunction of metabolism, reproductive capacity, and responsiveness to environmental stimuli. This analysis captures the characteristic activities of paradigmatic living things — bacteria, plants, animals — with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the reproductive condition may be drawn from the world of domestic animals. Consider a mule — the sterile offspring of a horse and a donkey. The mule metabolises food, converting it into energy through the same biochemical pathways as any mammal. It responds to stimuli — to light, sound, touch, the presence of predators, and the availability of food. But it cannot reproduce; mules are, with vanishingly rare exceptions, sterile. The second condition is violated. Yet no biologist, no veterinarian, and no farmer would deny that a mule is alive. To withhold the predicate 'alive' from a vigorous, healthy animal because it cannot produce offspring would be to mistake a common feature of living things for a necessary condition of life. It appears that the definition, by including reproductive capacity among its conditions, confuses a property typical of living species with a property essential to living individuals.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its own gravity to overcome rigid body forces so that it assumes a nearly round shape, and (iii) B has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit of other debris.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass to assume a nearly round shape
B has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit
counterexample: Consider a body of planetary mass ejected from its stellar system by gravitational interactions — a so-called rogue planet, wandering through interstellar space. It has sufficient mass to be round, and it has no orbital neighbourhood to clear. But it does not orbit a star. The first condition is violated. Yet such a body is, in its intrinsic physical character, indistinguishable from a planet — it has the mass, the composition, and the geological complexity of a planet. Its status seems to depend upon an accident of dynamical history rather than upon any intrinsic property.
missing_condition: The definition makes orbital context — circling a star and clearing a neighbourhood — essential to planethood, but these are extrinsic relational properties that can change over time, and the definition thereby fails to capture the intrinsic physical character that makes a body a planet regardless of its current dynamical situation.
passage: One might define a planet as a celestial body that orbits a star, has sufficient mass for a nearly round shape, and has cleared its orbital neighbourhood. This definition, adopted by the International Astronomical Union, captures the eight recognised planets of our solar system with precision. Yet a case which tells against the orbital requirement may be drawn from the phenomenon of rogue planets. Consider a body of planetary mass — comparable to Jupiter or Earth — that has been ejected from its original stellar system by gravitational interactions with other bodies. It now wanders through interstellar space, orbiting no star. It has sufficient mass to have assumed a round shape. It has no orbital neighbourhood to clear, for it is not in orbit. The first condition is violated. Yet the body is, in its intrinsic physical character, indistinguishable from a planet. It has the mass, the composition, the internal differentiation, and the geological complexity of a planet. Its failure to orbit a star is an accident of dynamical history, not a reflection of its intrinsic nature. It appears that the definition, by making orbital context essential, ties planethood to extrinsic relational properties that can change over time and thereby fails to capture the intrinsic physical character that makes a body a planet regardless of where it happens to be.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means
All atoms of S have the same number of protons
S occurs naturally on Earth
counterexample: Consider the element technetium, atomic number 43. It cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, and all of its atoms have 43 protons. But technetium does not occur naturally on Earth in any appreciable quantity — it was first produced artificially in a cyclotron. The third condition is violated. Yet technetium is unquestionably a chemical element; it occupies its proper place in the periodic table and exhibits all the chemical properties one would expect of element 43.
missing_condition: The definition restricts elements to naturally occurring substances on Earth and thereby fails to accommodate synthetic elements, which are no less genuinely elements for being produced in laboratories rather than found in nature.
passage: One might define a chemical element as a substance that cannot be chemically decomposed, whose atoms share a common number of protons, and that occurs naturally on Earth. This characterisation captures the classical elements — hydrogen, oxygen, gold, iron — with accuracy. Yet a case which tells against the third condition may be drawn from the history of nuclear physics. Consider technetium, the element with atomic number 43. It cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means. All of its atoms have 43 protons. Yet it does not occur naturally on Earth in any appreciable quantity. Technetium was first produced in 1937 by bombarding molybdenum with deuterons in a cyclotron. Its terrestrial existence is entirely artificial. The third condition is violated. Yet technetium is unquestionably a chemical element. It occupies its place in the periodic table between molybdenum and ruthenium, forms compounds with predictable chemical properties, and behaves in every respect as element 43 should behave. It appears that the definition, by requiring natural terrestrial occurrence, introduces a contingent geographical restriction into what is properly a concept of fundamental chemistry. An element is defined by its atomic number, not by where or how it happens to be found.

definition: A condition C is a disease if and only if (i) C involves a deviation from normal biological functioning, (ii) C causes suffering or impairment to the affected individual, and (iii) C has a specific identifiable cause — an infectious agent, a genetic mutation, or an environmental toxin.
conditions: C involves a deviation from normal biological functioning
C causes suffering or impairment to the affected individual
C has a specific identifiable cause
counterexample: Consider essential hypertension — persistently elevated blood pressure with no identifiable specific cause. It involves a deviation from normal biological functioning and causes significant impairment and risk of harm. But it has no single identifiable cause; it results from a complex interplay of genetic predisposition, diet, stress, and other factors, none of which can be singled out as the specific cause. The third condition is violated. Yet essential hypertension is classified as a disease by every medical authority.
missing_condition: The definition requires a specific identifiable cause but fails to accommodate diseases of multifactorial or unknown aetiology, in which no single causal agent can be identified and the condition results from a complex interaction of contributing factors.
passage: One might define a disease as a condition involving deviation from normal functioning, causing suffering or impairment, and having a specific identifiable cause. This analysis captures infectious diseases — tuberculosis caused by a bacillus, malaria caused by a parasite — and certain genetic conditions with considerable accuracy. Yet a case which tells against the requirement of a specific identifiable cause may be drawn from internal medicine. Consider essential hypertension — persistently elevated blood pressure that has no single identifiable cause. It involves a clear deviation from normal biological functioning: the sustained elevation of arterial pressure beyond the normal range. It causes suffering and impairment: it increases the risk of stroke, heart failure, kidney damage, and other serious complications. But its aetiology is multifactorial — it results from a complex interplay of genetic predisposition, dietary salt intake, physical inactivity, psychological stress, and other factors, no one of which can be singled out as the specific cause. The third condition is violated. Yet essential hypertension is classified as a disease by every medical authority and treated as such throughout the world. It appears that the definition, by requiring a specific identifiable cause, cannot accommodate the many diseases whose origins are multifactorial, complex, and incompletely understood.

definition: A system S constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) S comprises a community of interacting organisms, (ii) S includes the physical environment in which those organisms live, and (iii) S is self-sustaining — it maintains itself without external inputs of energy or matter.
conditions: S comprises a community of interacting organisms
S includes the physical environment in which those organisms live
S is self-sustaining without external inputs
counterexample: Consider a coral reef ecosystem. It comprises a rich community of interacting organisms — corals, fish, algae, invertebrates — living within a physical environment of warm, shallow, sunlit water. Yet it is not self-sustaining without external inputs: it depends upon solar energy from outside the system, upon ocean currents that bring nutrients from distant sources, and upon the input of larvae from other reef systems. The third condition is violated. Yet a coral reef is a paradigmatic ecosystem.
missing_condition: The definition imposes an impossible standard of self-sufficiency, failing to recognise that all ecosystems depend upon external inputs of energy — most fundamentally, sunlight — and that the concept of ecosystem denotes a system of ecological interactions within a physical environment, not a closed and self-sustaining unit.
passage: One might define an ecosystem as a community of interacting organisms together with their physical environment, which is self-sustaining without external inputs. This characterisation, in its aspiration to define ecosystems as closed systems, introduces a condition that no actual ecosystem satisfies. Consider a coral reef — one of the most complex and species-rich ecological systems on Earth. It comprises a community of interacting organisms of extraordinary diversity: corals, fish, algae, sponges, crustaceans, molluscs, and countless other invertebrates. These organisms live within a physical environment of warm, shallow, sunlit water. Yet the reef is not self-sustaining in the sense required by the third condition. It depends upon solar energy from outside the system. It depends upon ocean currents that deliver nutrients from distant sources. It depends upon the input of larvae from other reef populations. The third condition is violated. Yet a coral reef is a paradigmatic ecosystem by any biological standard. It appears that the definition, by requiring self-sufficiency, imposes a standard that no real ecosystem meets. All ecosystems are open systems that depend upon external inputs — most fundamentally, the energy of the sun. The concept of ecosystem denotes a system of interactions within a physical environment, not a closed and self-sustaining unit.

definition: A segment of DNA D is a gene if and only if (i) D encodes the information necessary for the production of a protein, (ii) D is transmitted from parent to offspring, and (iii) D occupies a fixed position (locus) on a chromosome.
conditions: D encodes the information necessary for the production of a protein
D is transmitted from parent to offspring
D occupies a fixed position on a chromosome
counterexample: Consider a segment of DNA that encodes a ribosomal RNA molecule rather than a protein. It is transmitted from parent to offspring and occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome. The first condition is violated — it does not encode a protein. Yet it is classified as a gene (an rRNA gene) by molecular biologists, for it encodes a functional product essential to the cell's machinery.
missing_condition: The definition restricts genes to protein-coding sequences, failing to accommodate the large class of genes that encode functional RNA molecules — ribosomal RNA, transfer RNA, and various regulatory RNAs — which perform essential biological functions without being translated into protein.
passage: One might define a gene as a segment of DNA that encodes a protein, is transmitted from parent to offspring, and occupies a fixed chromosomal locus. This characterisation, sometimes called the 'one gene, one protein' conception, captures many familiar examples: the gene for haemoglobin, the gene for insulin, the gene for eye colour. Yet a case which tells against the protein-coding requirement may be drawn from molecular biology. Consider a segment of DNA that encodes a ribosomal RNA (rRNA) molecule. This segment is transmitted faithfully from parent to offspring. It occupies a fixed position on a chromosome. But it does not encode a protein; the rRNA molecule it produces is never translated but functions directly as a structural and catalytic component of the ribosome. The first condition is violated. Yet this segment is classified as a gene — an rRNA gene — by molecular biologists. Its product is essential to every cell's protein-synthesis machinery. It appears that the definition, by restricting genes to protein-coding sequences, cannot accommodate the substantial class of genes whose functional products are RNA molecules rather than proteins. The concept of gene, one might observe, is defined by the encoding of a functional product, whether that product is a protein or an RNA molecule.

definition: An influence F is a force if and only if (i) F causes a change in the velocity of a body, (ii) F is quantifiable as a vector with magnitude and direction, and (iii) F results from the interaction of two distinct bodies.
conditions: F causes a change in the velocity of a body
F is quantifiable as a vector with magnitude and direction
F results from the interaction of two distinct bodies
counterexample: Consider a body moving in a circle at constant speed, subject to a centripetal force directed towards the centre of the circle. The force is quantifiable as a vector and results from an interaction — the tension in a string, or gravitational attraction. But the speed of the body does not change; only its direction changes. If 'velocity' is understood as including direction, then the first condition is satisfied.
