But now consider a body at rest on a table, subject to the gravitational force pulling it downward and the normal force of the table pushing it upward. The two forces balance; there is no change in velocity at all. Each force is real, quantifiable, and results from an interaction. Yet neither causes a change in velocity, because they cancel. The first condition is violated for each individual force.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a force cause a change in velocity, but forces in equilibrium produce no change in velocity yet remain genuine forces — the definition confuses the net effect of unbalanced forces with the existence of individual forces, which may be present and real without producing acceleration.
passage: One might define a force as an influence that causes a change in velocity, is quantifiable as a vector, and results from the interaction of two bodies. This analysis captures Newton's second law in definitional form: force as the cause of acceleration. Yet a case which tells against the first condition may be constructed from the simplest of physical situations. Consider a book resting upon a table. The book is subject to two forces: the gravitational force pulling it downward and the normal force of the table pushing it upward. The two forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction; they balance perfectly. There is no change in velocity — the book remains at rest. The first condition is violated for each individual force, since neither force, taken alone, causes a change in velocity in the actual situation. Yet both forces are real, quantifiable, and result from genuine physical interactions. The gravitational force exists regardless of whether it is balanced by another force. It appears that the definition, by requiring that a force cause a change in velocity, confuses the net effect of unbalanced forces — which does produce acceleration — with the existence of individual forces, which may be present and genuine even when their effects cancel.

definition: Two organisms A and B belong to the same species if and only if (i) A and B can interbreed, (ii) A and B share more than 97 per cent of their DNA sequences, and (iii) A and B occupy the same ecological niche.
conditions: A and B can interbreed
A and B share more than 97% of their DNA sequences
A and B occupy the same ecological niche
counterexample: Consider the polar bear and the grizzly bear. They can interbreed — hybrid 'grolar bears' or 'pizzly bears' have been documented both in captivity and in the wild. They share a high percentage of their DNA. But they occupy different ecological niches: the polar bear is adapted to Arctic sea ice and marine prey, while the grizzly inhabits forested and mountainous terrain and feeds on salmon, berries, and roots. The third condition is violated. Yet they are classified as distinct species — demonstrating both that interbreeding capacity does not suffice for conspecificity and that ecological niche is not a reliable criterion either.
missing_condition: The definition conflates several distinct criteria — interbreeding capacity, genetic similarity, and ecological role — without recognising that none of these, individually or in conjunction, provides a sufficient condition for species membership, and that the species concept may be irreducibly pluralistic.
passage: One might define conspecificity as requiring interbreeding capacity, high genetic similarity, and shared ecological niche. This analysis attempts to unify the biological, genetic, and ecological species concepts into a single definition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this conjunction may be drawn from the bears of North America. Consider the polar bear and the grizzly bear. They can interbreed: hybrid offspring — variously called 'grolar bears' or 'pizzly bears' — have been documented both in captivity and in the wild, and these hybrids are at least partially fertile. Their DNA sequences are highly similar — well above the 97 per cent threshold. Yet they occupy markedly different ecological niches: the polar bear is adapted to life on Arctic sea ice, hunting seals and navigating frozen coastal environments, while the grizzly inhabits forested and mountainous terrain, feeding on salmon, berries, and roots. The third condition is violated, yet the two are classified as distinct species. This case reveals a deeper problem: the criteria proposed by the definition pull in different directions, and no conjunction of interbreeding capacity, genetic similarity, and ecological role provides a reliable sufficient condition for species membership. The species concept, it appears, may resist reduction to any single set of necessary and sufficient conditions.

definition: A system S is alive if and only if (i) S maintains itself far from thermodynamic equilibrium, (ii) S is composed of organic molecules — carbon-based compounds, and (iii) S contains water as a solvent for its biochemical reactions.
conditions: S maintains itself far from thermodynamic equilibrium
S is composed of organic molecules
S contains water as a solvent
counterexample: Consider a hypothetical organism on a world where liquid methane serves as the biochemical solvent rather than water, and whose chemistry is based upon silicon rather than carbon. If such an organism maintained itself far from thermodynamic equilibrium, metabolised, reproduced, and responded to its environment, we should be reluctant to deny that it was alive merely because it violated the second and third conditions. The definition ties life to the particular chemistry of terrestrial biology rather than to the functional properties that make something alive.
missing_condition: The definition restricts life to carbon-and-water-based chemistry and thereby fails to accommodate the possibility of life with a fundamentally different biochemical basis — it mistakes the contingent material substrate of terrestrial life for a necessary condition of life as such.
passage: One might define life as a system that maintains itself far from thermodynamic equilibrium, is composed of organic molecules, and uses water as a biochemical solvent. This characterisation captures terrestrial life with considerable accuracy — every known living organism is carbon-based and water-dependent. Yet a case which tells against the material conditions may be constructed by considering the possibility of life with a different chemistry. Suppose that on a distant world, where temperatures are far lower than on Earth, organisms exist whose biochemistry is based upon silicon rather than carbon, and whose biochemical reactions are mediated by liquid methane rather than water. These organisms maintain themselves far from thermodynamic equilibrium; they metabolise, converting environmental energy into the maintenance of their internal order; they reproduce; they respond to environmental stimuli. They satisfy the first condition but violate the second and third. We should be reluctant to deny that such entities are alive merely because their chemistry differs from ours. It appears that the definition, by tying life to the particular chemistry of terrestrial biology, mistakes the contingent material substrate of life on Earth for a necessary condition of life as such. Life, one might observe, is characterised by its functional properties — self-maintenance, metabolism, reproduction — rather than by the particular chemical ingredients from which it happens to be constructed.

definition: A group of organisms G constitutes a species if and only if (i) the members of G are capable of interbreeding with one another, (ii) such interbreeding produces fertile offspring, and (iii) the members of G are reproductively isolated from organisms not belonging to G.
conditions: The members of G are capable of interbreeding with one another
Such interbreeding produces fertile offspring
The members of G are reproductively isolated from organisms not belonging to G
counterexample: Consider two populations of ducks — one resident in western Europe and one resident in eastern Asia — that have long been classified as distinct species. Suppose that along a continuous chain of intermediate populations stretching across central Asia, each neighbouring population can interbreed with its neighbours and produce fertile offspring. The westernmost population cannot interbreed with the easternmost, yet every adjacent pair satisfies the interbreeding condition. The members of the ring interbreed, produce fertile offspring, and appear reproductively isolated at the endpoints. All three conditions are simultaneously satisfied and violated depending on which segment of the ring one examines, and the definition gives no consistent answer as to whether we have one species or two.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify how the interbreeding criterion is to be applied when populations form a continuous ring or chain in which adjacent groups interbreed successfully but the terminal groups do not — it provides no principle for determining the boundaries of the interbreeding community when reproductive compatibility is a matter of degree rather than a sharp division.
passage: One might define a species as a group of organisms whose members can interbreed with one another, produce fertile offspring, and are reproductively isolated from all other organisms. This characterisation — which we may call the interbreeding criterion — has the virtue of drawing a clear biological line between species on the basis of reproductive compatibility. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the biogeography of certain birds. Suppose there exists a chain of duck populations distributed around a broad geographical arc from western Europe, through central Asia, to eastern Asia. Each population along the chain can interbreed with its immediate neighbours and produce fertile offspring. The populations grade into one another so smoothly that no sharp reproductive boundary can be drawn at any point along the chain. Yet the westernmost and easternmost populations, when brought into contact, prove incapable of interbreeding. The chain forms a ring: every adjacent pair satisfies the interbreeding condition, but the terminal populations do not. If we ask whether the entire chain constitutes one species, the definition yields an affirmative answer for any pair of neighbours but a negative answer for the endpoints. The conditions are simultaneously satisfied and violated depending upon which segment of the ring we examine. One might observe that the definition offers no principle for resolving this difficulty. It presupposes that reproductive compatibility divides the organic world into sharply bounded groups, when in fact the capacity for interbreeding may diminish gradually across geographical or genetic distance, producing cases in which the concept of a discrete species boundary simply has no determinate application.

definition: An entity X is alive if and only if (i) X maintains its internal organisation through metabolic processes, (ii) X is capable of reproduction, and (iii) X responds to stimuli from its environment.
conditions: X maintains its internal organisation through metabolic processes
X is capable of reproduction
X responds to stimuli from its environment
counterexample: Consider a mule — the offspring of a horse and a donkey. A mule maintains its internal organisation through the full range of metabolic processes: it digests food, circulates blood, regulates temperature, and repairs tissue. It responds vigorously to environmental stimuli: it reacts to light, sound, temperature, and touch. Yet the mule is almost invariably sterile and cannot reproduce. The second condition is not satisfied. By the proposed definition, the mule is not alive — a conclusion that no biologist would accept.
missing_condition: The definition errs in making the capacity for reproduction a necessary condition of life at the level of the individual organism, when in fact many individual organisms — sterile hybrids, post-reproductive individuals, worker insects — are unambiguously alive despite lacking the capacity to reproduce.
passage: One might propose that an entity is alive if and only if it maintains its internal organisation through metabolism, is capable of reproduction, and responds to environmental stimuli. This characterisation captures several of the most conspicuous features of living things and serves well in the central cases — a growing plant, a swimming fish, a dividing bacterium. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the familiar phenomenon of sterile hybrids. Consider the mule, the offspring of a horse and a donkey. The mule is a robust organism that maintains its internal organisation through the full complement of metabolic processes. It digests food, circulates blood, regulates its temperature, heals its wounds, and sustains itself over a span of decades. It responds to environmental stimuli with evident sensitivity: it reacts to light, sound, temperature, the presence of predators, and the signals of its handlers. Yet the mule is, in nearly every case, entirely sterile. It cannot reproduce. The second condition of the proposed definition is not met. We are thus compelled by the definition to conclude that the mule is not alive — a conclusion that no biologist, and indeed no ordinary person, would accept. The mule is as plainly alive as any horse or donkey. It appears that the definition, by including the capacity for reproduction as a necessary condition for life at the level of the individual organism, conflates a property that is characteristic of species and lineages with a property that must be possessed by each individual member. Many individual organisms — sterile hybrids, post-reproductive animals, worker insects — are unambiguously alive despite being unable to reproduce.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to produce a roughly spherical shape, and (iii) B is the dominant gravitational body in its orbital region.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass for its self-gravity to produce a roughly spherical shape
B is the dominant gravitational body in its orbital region
counterexample: Suppose astronomers discover a massive, roughly spherical body — significantly larger than the Earth — wandering through interstellar space, having been ejected from its parent star system billions of years ago by a close gravitational encounter. The body has sufficient mass for self-gravity to produce a spherical shape. It would be the dominant gravitational body in any orbital region, were it in one. Yet it does not orbit any star. By the proposed definition, it is not a planet. Yet its composition, structure, and formation history are identical to those of recognised planets; it differs from them only in having been gravitationally expelled from its original orbit.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate bodies that possess all the intrinsic physical characteristics of planets — mass, composition, spherical shape, formation history — but have been ejected from stellar orbits through gravitational interactions, thereby making the extrinsic property of currently orbiting a star an inappropriately necessary condition.
passage: One might define a planet as a celestial body that orbits a star, possesses sufficient mass for self-gravity to produce a roughly spherical shape, and is the dominant gravitational body in its orbital region. This characterisation succeeds in distinguishing planets from asteroids, comets, and smaller debris, and it captures the intuition that planets are substantial, round bodies associated with stellar systems. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from recent astronomical discoveries. Suppose that observers detect a massive body drifting through interstellar space — a body several times the mass of the Earth, roughly spherical in shape, with a composition and internal structure indistinguishable from those of the terrestrial planets in our own solar system. Detailed analysis of its chemical signature reveals that it formed in the ordinary way, through the accretion of material in a protoplanetary disc around a young star. Billions of years ago, a close gravitational encounter with another massive body ejected it from its original orbit, and it has been wandering through the void ever since. The body has sufficient mass for self-gravity to maintain a spherical shape. It would dominate any orbital region it occupied. Yet it does not orbit a star. The first condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied, and we must conclude, on this account, that the body is not a planet. One might observe that this conclusion sits uneasily with the fact that the body is, in every intrinsic respect, identical to a planet: its mass, shape, composition, and formation history are precisely those of the familiar planets. It was a planet before its ejection, and nothing about its physical nature has changed since. The definition, by making the extrinsic property of orbiting a star a necessary condition, excludes bodies that possess every intrinsic characteristic of planethood but have been displaced by gravitational accident.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occupies a unique position in the periodic table.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means
All atoms of S have the same number of protons
S occupies a unique position in the periodic table
counterexample: Consider a sample consisting entirely of carbon-12 atoms. It cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means. All its atoms have six protons. It occupies a unique position in the periodic table. All three conditions are met. But now consider a sample consisting entirely of carbon-14 atoms. It too satisfies all three conditions — same proton number, same periodic-table position, chemically indecomposable. Yet carbon-14 is radioactive and spontaneously transmutes into nitrogen through beta decay, a transformation that is not a chemical decomposition but that does convert the substance into a different element. The definition, by restricting itself to chemical means of decomposition, fails to capture the fact that certain isotopes of an element are inherently unstable and will transform into other elements through non-chemical processes.
missing_condition: The definition fails to address whether the identity of an element should be understood as encompassing all its isotopes, including those that are inherently unstable and undergo spontaneous nuclear transformation — it treats the element as chemically indecomposable while ignoring nuclear processes that decompose it into a different element entirely.
passage: One might define a chemical element as a substance that cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, whose atoms all share the same number of protons, and which occupies a unique position in the periodic table. This characterisation is serviceable in the central cases: oxygen, gold, and iron are readily identified as elements on these grounds. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by attending to the isotopes of a single element. Consider a sample of pure carbon-12. It cannot be decomposed by chemical means. Every atom contains six protons. It occupies the sixth position in the periodic table. All three conditions are satisfied, and we rightly call it an element. Now consider a sample of pure carbon-14. This substance also satisfies all three conditions: it is chemically indecomposable, every atom has six protons, and it occupies the same position in the periodic table. Yet carbon-14 is inherently unstable. Over time, each atom of carbon-14 will spontaneously emit a beta particle and transform into an atom of nitrogen — a different element entirely. This transformation is not a chemical decomposition; it is a nuclear process that the definition does not address. One might observe that the definition, by restricting the notion of decomposition to chemical means, overlooks the fact that certain isotopes of what we call the same element will, by their own nature, transform into atoms of a different element. The substance that the definition declares to be indecomposable is, in a perfectly real sense, decomposing itself. It appears that the characterisation of elements solely in terms of chemical indecomposability and proton number leaves unaddressed a significant class of transformations that alter elemental identity through processes the definition was not designed to capture.

definition: A condition D constitutes a disease if and only if (i) D involves a deviation from normal biological functioning, (ii) D causes suffering or impairment in the affected organism, and (iii) D has an identifiable aetiology — a specifiable cause or set of causes.
conditions: D involves a deviation from normal biological functioning
D causes suffering or impairment in the affected organism
D has an identifiable aetiology — a specifiable cause or set of causes
counterexample: Consider the condition of sickle-cell trait — the heterozygous carrier state for sickle-cell anaemia. In regions where malaria is endemic, individuals with sickle-cell trait enjoy a significant survival advantage over those with two normal copies of the haemoglobin gene. The trait involves a deviation from normal biological functioning: the haemoglobin molecule is altered. It has an identifiable aetiology: a single nucleotide substitution. Yet it causes no suffering or impairment — indeed, it confers a benefit. The second condition fails. We should not call sickle-cell trait a disease in these circumstances, yet the definition's insistence on suffering as a necessary condition, while correct here, exposes a deeper difficulty: the very same genetic alteration constitutes a debilitating disease in the homozygous state and a beneficial adaptation in the heterozygous state, suggesting that whether a condition counts as a disease cannot be determined solely from its biological characteristics but depends upon context.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that whether a biological deviation constitutes a disease may depend upon the ecological and genetic context in which it occurs — the same deviation may be pathological in one configuration and adaptive in another, and the definition provides no resources for handling this context-dependence.
passage: One might define a disease as a condition involving a deviation from normal biological functioning, causing suffering or impairment, and possessing an identifiable aetiology. This characterisation seems to capture what is common to infections, cancers, and metabolic disorders: they are departures from the norm, they harm the organism, and they have causes that can in principle be specified. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from human genetics. Consider sickle-cell trait — the condition of carrying one altered and one normal copy of the gene for haemoglobin. The trait involves a clear deviation from normal biological functioning: the haemoglobin molecule is structurally altered, and red blood cells assume an abnormal shape under certain conditions. The aetiology is precisely identifiable: a single nucleotide substitution in the gene for haemoglobin. Yet in regions where malaria is endemic, individuals carrying sickle-cell trait enjoy a substantial survival advantage. The altered haemoglobin provides significant protection against the malaria parasite, and carriers suffer no appreciable impairment from the trait itself. The second condition — suffering or impairment — is not satisfied. By the proposed definition, sickle-cell trait in these circumstances is not a disease. This is perhaps the correct conclusion in this case, but it exposes a deeper difficulty. The very same genetic alteration, when present in two copies rather than one, produces sickle-cell anaemia — a severely debilitating condition that unambiguously satisfies all three criteria. The definition provides no framework for understanding how the same biological deviation can constitute a disease in one genetic context and a beneficial adaptation in another. It treats disease as a function solely of the deviation, its effects, and its causes, without recognising that the relationship among these factors is mediated by the broader ecological and genetic context.

definition: A collection of organisms and their physical environment E constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) the organisms interact with one another through feeding relationships, (ii) energy flows through E from producers to consumers, and (iii) nutrients are cycled within E through biological and geological processes.
conditions: The organisms interact with one another through feeding relationships
Energy flows through E from producers to consumers
Nutrients are cycled within E through biological and geological processes
counterexample: Consider a deep-sea hydrothermal vent community. Chemosynthetic bacteria at the base of the food web derive energy not from sunlight but from the oxidation of hydrogen sulphide emitted by the vent. Other organisms — tube worms, crabs, shrimp — feed upon these bacteria and upon one another. Energy flows from chemosynthetic producers to consumers. Nutrients are cycled through biological and geological processes. Feeding relationships are present. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the community is entirely dependent upon a geological feature — the vent — that is transient; when the vent ceases, the entire community perishes. The definition, by requiring only internal cycling and energy flow, gives no account of the degree to which an ecosystem must be self-sustaining, and it classifies as an ecosystem a community whose continued existence depends wholly upon an external geological accident.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify whether the energy source and nutrient inputs that sustain an ecosystem must be stable and self-renewing, or whether a community wholly dependent upon a transient and externally supplied energy source — one that may cease at any moment — qualifies equally as an ecosystem.
passage: One might characterise an ecosystem as a collection of organisms and their physical environment in which organisms interact through feeding relationships, energy flows from producers to consumers, and nutrients are cycled through biological and geological processes. This formulation captures the dynamic and interconnected character of ecological communities. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the deep ocean. Consider a hydrothermal vent community on the ocean floor. At the base of the food web, chemosynthetic bacteria derive their energy not from sunlight but from the oxidation of hydrogen sulphide that escapes from the vent. Tube worms harbour these bacteria in symbiotic arrangements; crabs and shrimp feed upon bacterial mats and upon one another. Energy flows from chemosynthetic producers to a hierarchy of consumers. Nutrients are cycled through the biological activities of the organisms and through the geological processes of the vent itself. Feeding relationships are abundant and complex. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied, and we may be inclined to call this an ecosystem. Yet the entire community is dependent upon a geological feature — the hydrothermal vent — that is inherently transient. Vents open and close over timescales of decades or centuries. When the vent ceases, the energy source vanishes and the community perishes entirely. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only the presence of feeding relationships, energy flow, and nutrient cycling, provides no account of the degree to which an ecosystem must be self-sustaining. It draws no distinction between a community sustained by a stable, self-renewing energy source and one entirely dependent upon a transient geological accident that may cease without warning.

definition: A segment of DNA D constitutes a gene if and only if (i) D encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a protein, (ii) D is inherited from parent to offspring as a discrete unit, and (iii) D occupies a specific locus on a chromosome.
conditions: D encodes the information necessary for the synthesis of a protein
D is inherited from parent to offspring as a discrete unit
D occupies a specific locus on a chromosome
counterexample: Consider a segment of DNA that is transcribed into a ribosomal RNA molecule — an RNA that forms part of the ribosome's structure and is essential to the translation of all proteins. This segment is inherited as a discrete unit and occupies a specific locus on a chromosome. Yet it does not encode a protein; it encodes a functional RNA molecule. The first condition is not satisfied. By the proposed definition, this segment is not a gene. Yet biologists universally regard ribosomal RNA genes as genes, and their importance to the organism's functioning is beyond question.
missing_condition: The definition fails to encompass segments of DNA that encode functional RNA molecules rather than proteins — it restricts the concept of a gene to protein-coding sequences when in fact many genes produce RNA products that serve essential biological functions without ever being translated into protein.
passage: One might define a gene as a segment of DNA that encodes the information for protein synthesis, is inherited as a discrete unit, and occupies a specific chromosomal locus. This characterisation reflects the classical picture of the gene as a unit of heredity whose function is to specify a protein. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the molecular biology of the ribosome. Consider the segments of DNA that encode ribosomal RNA — the structural RNA molecules that form the core of the ribosome, the cellular machinery responsible for translating all messenger RNA into protein. These DNA segments are inherited as discrete units from parent to offspring. They occupy specific and well-characterised loci on specific chromosomes. They are indispensable to the functioning of the cell. Yet they do not encode proteins. The product of their transcription is an RNA molecule that is never translated; it folds into a complex three-dimensional structure and serves a structural and catalytic role within the ribosome itself. The first condition of the proposed definition — encoding the information for protein synthesis — is not met. We are thus compelled to conclude that ribosomal RNA genes are not genes, a conclusion that no molecular biologist would accept. One might observe that the definition, by restricting the concept of a gene to protein-coding sequences, overlooks a large and functionally crucial class of genetic elements — those that produce RNA molecules serving structural, catalytic, or regulatory roles. The assumption that the function of a gene is always to specify a protein reflects an incomplete picture of molecular biology.

definition: An interaction F between two bodies constitutes a force if and only if (i) F causes a change in the motion of at least one of the bodies, (ii) F can be quantified by a magnitude and a direction, and (iii) F acts along the line connecting the two bodies.
conditions: F causes a change in the motion of at least one of the bodies
F can be quantified by a magnitude and a direction
F acts along the line connecting the two bodies
counterexample: Consider the magnetic force experienced by a charged particle moving through a magnetic field. This force causes a change in the particle's motion — it deflects the particle's trajectory. It can be quantified by a magnitude and a direction. Yet the magnetic force does not act along the line connecting the particle to the source of the field; it acts perpendicular to both the particle's velocity and the field direction. The third condition is not satisfied. By the proposed definition, the magnetic force on a moving charge is not a force — an absurd conclusion.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that all forces act along the line connecting the interacting bodies, when in fact certain forces — most notably magnetic forces on moving charges — act in directions perpendicular to that line, and the definition must accommodate forces whose direction is determined by factors other than the spatial arrangement of the bodies.
passage: One might propose that a force is an interaction between two bodies that causes a change in motion, can be quantified by magnitude and direction, and acts along the line connecting the bodies. This characterisation is suggested by the paradigm cases of gravitational and electrostatic attraction, where the force is indeed directed along the line joining the interacting bodies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from electromagnetism. Consider a charged particle moving through a magnetic field generated by a magnet. The particle experiences a force that deflects its trajectory — its motion changes. The force has a definite magnitude and direction and can be precisely quantified. Yet the direction of this force is perpendicular to both the particle's velocity and the magnetic field. It does not act along the line connecting the particle to the magnet. The third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied. We should thus be compelled, on this account, to deny that the magnetic deflection of a charged particle is a force — a conclusion that no physicist would entertain. One might observe that the definition has been constructed by generalising from a restricted class of central forces — gravitation, electrostatic attraction — in which the direction of action happens to coincide with the line between the bodies. It fails to accommodate the magnetic force, whose direction is determined not by the spatial arrangement of the interacting bodies but by the velocity of the charge and the orientation of the field. The definition, by insisting that force must act along the connecting line, excludes a fundamental and well-understood class of physical interactions.

definition: A group of organisms G constitutes a single species if and only if (i) the members of G share a common ancestor, (ii) the members of G possess a set of morphological characteristics that distinguish them from all other groups, and (iii) these distinguishing characteristics are stable across generations.
conditions: The members of G share a common ancestor
The members of G possess a set of morphological characteristics that distinguish them from all other groups
These distinguishing characteristics are stable across generations
counterexample: Consider a population of butterflies in which some individuals bear bright orange wing patterns while others, in the same population and fully interbreeding, bear dark brown patterns that closely mimic a different, toxic species. Both morphs share a common ancestor, interbreed freely, and are regarded by biologists as a single species. Yet the two morphs possess strikingly different morphological characteristics, and neither set of characteristics uniquely distinguishes the species from all other groups — the mimic morph is morphologically more similar to the toxic species it resembles than to the non-mimetic members of its own species. The second condition fails, for no single set of distinguishing morphological features can be identified that encompasses all members of the species.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate species in which significant morphological polymorphism exists — where members of the same species display strikingly different physical forms, so that no single set of morphological characteristics reliably distinguishes the species from all others.
passage: One might define a species as a group of organisms sharing a common ancestor, possessing a distinctive set of morphological characteristics, with those characteristics being stable across generations. This characterisation emphasises genealogy and observable form, and it works well for many familiar groups. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of mimicry in butterflies. Consider a species of butterfly in which two distinct morphological forms coexist within the same population. One morph displays bright orange wing patterns; the other displays dark brown patterns that closely resemble those of a different, toxic species. The two morphs interbreed freely and share a recent common ancestor — they are, by every other criterion, members of a single species. Yet their morphological characteristics are strikingly different. The mimetic morph, with its dark brown wings, is more similar in appearance to the toxic species it imitates than to the orange morph of its own species. There is no single set of morphological features that encompasses both morphs and simultaneously distinguishes them from all other butterfly groups. The second condition of the proposed definition is not met: the species does not possess a unified set of distinguishing morphological characteristics. One might observe that the definition presupposes a degree of morphological uniformity within species that does not always obtain. Many species exhibit pronounced polymorphism — the coexistence of multiple distinct forms maintained by natural selection — and in such cases the attempt to identify the species by a stable set of distinguishing physical features is bound to fail. The definition, by tying species identity to morphological distinctiveness, cannot accommodate the biological reality of intraspecific variation.

definition: An entity X is a living organism if and only if (i) X is composed of one or more cells, (ii) X carries heritable genetic information encoded in nucleic acids, and (iii) X undergoes growth and development according to a programme specified by its genetic material.
conditions: X is composed of one or more cells
X carries heritable genetic information encoded in nucleic acids
X undergoes growth and development according to a programme specified by its genetic material
counterexample: Consider a virus — say, the tobacco mosaic virus. It carries heritable genetic information encoded in RNA, a nucleic acid. It undergoes a process of development: upon infecting a host cell, it commandeers the cell's machinery to produce new copies of itself according to its genetic programme. Yet the virus is not composed of cells; it consists of a protein coat enclosing a strand of RNA. The first condition is not satisfied. By the proposed definition, the virus is not a living organism. Yet the virus replicates, evolves, adapts to its host, and exhibits many of the hallmarks of life; the question of whether viruses are alive remains one of the most contested in biology.
missing_condition: The definition fails to address entities that possess genetic information and undergo replication and development but are not cellular — it treats cellular composition as a necessary condition for life when the boundary between living and non-living may not coincide with the boundary between cellular and acellular organisation.
passage: One might define a living organism as an entity composed of one or more cells, carrying heritable genetic information in nucleic acids, and undergoing growth and development according to a genetic programme. This characterisation succeeds for the vast majority of organisms: bacteria, plants, fungi, and animals are all cellular, all carry DNA or RNA, and all develop according to genetic instructions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found at the boundary of biology. Consider the tobacco mosaic virus, a well-studied pathogen of plants. It carries heritable genetic information encoded in RNA — a nucleic acid. Upon infecting a host cell, it directs the synthesis of new viral particles according to a programme specified by its own genetic material. New viruses are assembled, and the genetic information is faithfully transmitted to the next generation of viral particles. The virus satisfies the second and third conditions. Yet it is not composed of cells. It consists of nothing more than a strand of RNA enclosed within a protein coat. The first condition is not met, and by the proposed definition, the tobacco mosaic virus is not a living organism. One might observe that this conclusion is far from uncontroversial. The virus replicates, evolves through mutation and selection, adapts to its host, and competes with other viral strains for access to cellular machinery. It exhibits many of the functional hallmarks of life. The definition, by making cellular composition a necessary condition, draws the line between living and non-living at a point that excludes entities whose biological significance and life-like behaviour are beyond question. Whether viruses are truly alive remains deeply contested, and a definition that settles the matter by stipulation rather than by analysis of the relevant biological features seems to miss what is genuinely at issue.

definition: A group of organisms constitutes a species if and only if (i) the members are capable of interbreeding with one another, (ii) the members produce fertile offspring when they interbreed, and (iii) the members are reproductively isolated from organisms outside the group.
conditions: The members are capable of interbreeding with one another
The members produce fertile offspring when they interbreed
The members are reproductively isolated from organisms outside the group
counterexample: Consider the case of ring species, such as the Ensatina salamanders of the Pacific coast of North America. Populations distributed along the coast form a continuous chain in which each neighbouring pair can interbreed and produce fertile offspring. Yet the populations at the two ends of the chain, where they meet in southern California, cannot interbreed with one another. Each population satisfies the interbreeding and fertility conditions with its immediate neighbours, yet the terminal populations are not reproductively isolated from all organisms outside the group — for they interbreed freely with neighbouring populations — nor are they capable of interbreeding with one another. The definition cannot determine whether these populations constitute one species or several.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases in which the capacity for interbreeding is transitive along a chain of populations but not between the endpoints, revealing that reproductive isolation is not an all-or-nothing relation and that the definition requires a condition addressing how interbreeding capacity is to be evaluated when it varies continuously across a geographic range.
passage: One might define a species as a group of organisms whose members are capable of interbreeding, produce fertile offspring, and are reproductively isolated from organisms outside the group. This biological species concept captures the intuitive idea that species are reproductive communities, bound together by gene flow and separated from other such communities by barriers to reproduction. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be found in the phenomenon of ring species. Consider the Ensatina salamanders distributed along the Pacific coast of North America. Populations along the coast form a continuous chain, and each neighbouring pair of populations can interbreed and produce fertile offspring. The gene flow runs smoothly from one population to the next. Yet the populations at the two extremities of the chain, where they meet in southern California, cannot interbreed with one another at all. Each population in the chain satisfies the interbreeding and fertility conditions with respect to its immediate neighbours, and none is fully reproductively isolated from all organisms outside any particular grouping, for each merges reproductively with the populations adjacent to it. The definition cannot determine how to delimit the species: if we group all the populations together, the terminal populations violate the interbreeding condition; if we separate them, the intermediate populations violate the condition of reproductive isolation. It appears that the definition presupposes that reproductive capacity is an all-or-nothing relation, when in fact it may vary continuously across a geographic range. One is led to conclude that a further condition is needed, specifying how the criterion of interbreeding is to be applied when reproductive compatibility is a matter of degree.

definition: An entity E is alive if and only if (i) E is capable of metabolism — of taking in energy from its environment and using it to maintain its internal organisation, (ii) E is capable of reproduction, and (iii) E responds to stimuli from its environment.
conditions: E is capable of metabolism
E is capable of reproduction
E responds to stimuli from its environment
counterexample: Suppose one considers the case of a mule. A mule metabolises food, converting it to energy to sustain its bodily organisation. It responds to environmental stimuli — light, sound, the approach of predators — with appropriate behavioural reactions. Yet a mule is sterile; it cannot reproduce. The second condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, no biologist would deny that a mule is alive. The case tells against the proposed definition, for it shows that an entity can be unambiguously alive while lacking the capacity for reproduction.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the capacity for reproduction, while characteristic of living things as a class, is not a necessary condition for any individual organism to be alive. A condition is needed that distinguishes the properties required of living organisms individually from those that characterise life at the population or lineage level.
passage: One might propose that an entity is alive just when it is capable of metabolism, capable of reproduction, and responsive to environmental stimuli. This tripartite characterisation captures what many regard as the hallmarks of the living: the capacity to sustain oneself through the exchange of matter and energy, the capacity to produce new individuals of one's kind, and the capacity to detect and react to changes in one's surroundings. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from familiar biological practice. Consider the mule, the offspring of a male donkey and a female horse. A mule is indisputably alive by any ordinary reckoning. It metabolises food, converting it to the energy needed to maintain its complex internal organisation. It responds to environmental stimuli with the sensitivity and behavioural flexibility characteristic of mammals: it startles at loud sounds, orients toward food, and adjusts its gait to uneven terrain. Yet a mule is sterile; it cannot reproduce. The second condition of the proposed definition is not met. We are thus confronted with an entity that possesses two of the three stipulated marks of life and is unquestionably alive, yet which fails the definition. It appears that the definition, by requiring reproductive capacity of each individual organism, confuses a property that pertains to life as a general phenomenon — or to lineages and populations — with a property that must be exhibited by every living individual. One is led to conclude that the analysis is too restrictive and that a more careful account must distinguish individual-level from population-level characteristics of life.

definition: A condition C afflicting an organism constitutes a disease if and only if (i) C causes detectable deviation from the organism's normal physiological functioning, (ii) C produces suffering or impairment in the organism, and (iii) C has an identifiable pathological cause.
conditions: C causes detectable deviation from the organism's normal physiological functioning
C produces suffering or impairment in the organism
C has an identifiable pathological cause
counterexample: Suppose one considers the condition of a person who carries the gene for sickle cell trait in heterozygous form. This person's haemoglobin has a detectable deviation from the normal form — a measurable proportion of his red blood cells assume an abnormal sickle shape under low-oxygen conditions. The condition has an identifiable pathological cause, namely the mutation in the haemoglobin gene. Yet the carrier experiences no suffering or impairment; indeed, in regions where malaria is prevalent, the trait confers a survival advantage by providing resistance to the malarial parasite. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not. Yet this situation is more puzzling than a simple failure, for whether the condition constitutes a disease appears to depend not on any intrinsic property of the condition itself but on the environment in which the organism lives.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the environment-relative character of disease, neglecting the fact that whether a physiological deviation constitutes suffering or impairment may depend upon the ecological context in which the organism finds itself. A condition concerning the relationship between the deviation and the organism's environment is needed.
passage: One might define a disease as a condition that causes detectable deviation from normal physiological functioning, produces suffering or impairment, and has an identifiable pathological cause. This characterisation captures the intuitive idea that disease involves a departure from health that is both measurable and traceable to a specific pathological origin. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from human genetics. Consider the condition of a person who carries the sickle cell trait in heterozygous form. A measurable proportion of his red blood cells deviate from the normal discoid shape, assuming an abnormal sickle configuration under conditions of reduced oxygen. The deviation is detectable by standard haematological examination. The condition has an identifiable cause: a point mutation in the gene encoding the beta chain of haemoglobin. Two of the three conditions are satisfied. Yet the carrier experiences no suffering and no impairment of function; under ordinary conditions, his health is indistinguishable from that of a person with normal haemoglobin. More striking still, in regions where malaria is endemic, the sickle cell trait confers a marked survival advantage, providing resistance to the malarial parasite. It appears that whether this condition constitutes a disease depends not solely upon the intrinsic character of the physiological deviation but upon the environment in which the organism lives. The definition, by requiring suffering or impairment as a fixed condition, fails to accommodate the environment-relative character of disease. One is led to conclude that a further condition is required, specifying the relationship between the physiological deviation and the ecological circumstances of the organism.

definition: A collection of organisms and their physical environment constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) the organisms interact with one another through predation, competition, or symbiosis, (ii) energy flows through the system from producers to consumers, and (iii) the system maintains a stable equilibrium over time.
conditions: The organisms interact with one another through predation, competition, or symbiosis
Energy flows through the system from producers to consumers
The system maintains a stable equilibrium over time
counterexample: Consider the case of a recently devastated forest in which a volcanic eruption has destroyed all existing vegetation and animal life. Within months, pioneer species — lichens, mosses, certain insects — begin to colonise the barren surface. These organisms interact through competition for space and through rudimentary symbiotic relations. Energy flows from photosynthetic pioneers to the small consumers that feed upon them. The first two conditions are met. Yet the system does not maintain a stable equilibrium; it is undergoing rapid ecological succession, with its species composition and energy dynamics changing dramatically from year to year. Nevertheless, ecologists would not hesitate to call this an ecosystem. The definition excludes dynamic, successional systems that are undeniably ecosystems.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate ecosystems that are in a state of flux, transition, or succession, erroneously requiring stable equilibrium as a necessary feature. A condition is needed that allows for dynamic change and recognises that ecosystems may persist as functional systems even while their composition and energy flows are undergoing substantial transformation.
passage: One might propose that an ecosystem consists in a collection of organisms and their physical environment in which the organisms interact through predation, competition, or symbiosis, energy flows from producers to consumers, and the system maintains a stable equilibrium. This characterisation captures the interconnectedness, the trophic structure, and the apparent persistence that we associate with ecological systems. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of ecological succession. Suppose a volcanic eruption devastates a forest, destroying all existing vegetation and animal life. Within months, pioneer species begin to colonise the barren landscape: lichens encrust bare rock, mosses establish themselves in crevices, and certain hardy insects arrive to feed upon the pioneer vegetation. These organisms interact through competition for limited space and through symbiotic relationships. Energy flows from the photosynthetic pioneers to the small consumers that depend upon them. Two of the three conditions are clearly met. Yet the system does not maintain a stable equilibrium; it is undergoing rapid and dramatic change. The species composition shifts from year to year, the relative abundances of organisms fluctuate wildly, and the energy dynamics are in constant transformation as new species arrive and displace the pioneers. Nevertheless, no ecologist would deny that this is an ecosystem. It appears that the definition, by requiring stable equilibrium, excludes precisely those dynamic, successional systems that are among the most studied and theoretically important ecosystems in the science. One is led to conclude that equilibrium is not a necessary feature of an ecosystem and that the analysis requires a condition permitting functional persistence through change.

definition: A segment of DNA constitutes a gene if and only if (i) it encodes a specific protein, (ii) it is transmitted from parent to offspring as a discrete hereditary unit, and (iii) it occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome.
conditions: It encodes a specific protein
It is transmitted from parent to offspring as a discrete hereditary unit
It occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome
counterexample: Consider the segments of DNA that encode ribosomal RNA. These segments are transmitted from parent to offspring as discrete hereditary units and occupy fixed loci on specific chromosomes. They satisfy the second and third conditions. Yet they do not encode any protein; their product is a functional RNA molecule that forms a structural component of the ribosome. Biologists universally regard these segments as genes — ribosomal RNA genes. The definition excludes them by requiring that a gene encode a protein, yet they are paradigmatic examples of genes.
missing_condition: The definition is too narrow in requiring that a gene encode a protein, failing to accommodate genes whose products are functional RNA molecules rather than proteins. A broader condition specifying that the segment encodes a functional product — whether protein or RNA — is required.
passage: One might define a gene as a segment of DNA that encodes a specific protein, is transmitted from parent to offspring as a discrete hereditary unit, and occupies a fixed locus on a chromosome. This characterisation captures the classical picture of the gene as a unit of heredity with a specific molecular product and a definite chromosomal position. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from molecular biology. Consider the segments of DNA that encode ribosomal RNA. These segments are transmitted from parent to offspring as discrete hereditary units, occupying fixed loci on particular chromosomes. They satisfy the second and third conditions of the proposed definition without difficulty. Yet they do not encode a protein. Their product is a functional RNA molecule — an essential structural component of the ribosome, the molecular machinery responsible for protein synthesis. Biologists universally regard these segments as genes, referring to them as ribosomal RNA genes, and their importance to the functioning of the cell is beyond question. It appears that the definition, by requiring that a gene encode a specific protein, excludes an entire and fundamental class of genes whose products are functional RNA molecules. The same difficulty arises for transfer RNA genes, small nuclear RNA genes, and many other loci whose products never undergo translation into protein. One is led to conclude that the protein-encoding condition is too restrictive and that the analysis must be broadened to accommodate any segment whose product — whether protein or RNA — plays a functional role in the organism.

definition: An interaction F constitutes a physical force if and only if (i) F produces an observable change in the motion or shape of a body, (ii) F has a definite magnitude and direction, and (iii) F can be traced to a specific physical source.
conditions: F produces an observable change in the motion or shape of a body
F has a definite magnitude and direction
F can be traced to a specific physical source
counterexample: Consider two equal and opposite forces applied simultaneously to a rigid body, one pushing from the left and one pushing from the right, each of definite magnitude and direction, each traceable to a specific physical source. The net effect is that the body does not move or change shape; the forces are in perfect equilibrium. Each individual force has a definite magnitude and direction and can be traced to its source, yet neither produces an observable change in the motion or shape of the body. The first condition is not satisfied for either force considered individually. Yet each force is undeniably a physical force; the absence of observable change results from the cancellation of forces, not from the absence of force.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the presence of a force and the net observable effect of all forces acting upon a body, erroneously requiring that each individual force produce an observable change. A condition recognising that forces may exist in equilibrium without producing net motion or deformation is needed.
passage: One might propose that a physical force is an interaction that produces an observable change in the motion or shape of a body, has a definite magnitude and direction, and can be traced to a specific physical source. This formulation appears to capture the essential features of force as understood in the physical sciences: causal efficacy, measurability, and material origin. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from elementary mechanics. Suppose two persons stand on opposite sides of a heavy crate, each pushing with equal strength toward the centre. Each push has a definite magnitude and a definite direction; the left-hand push is directed rightward, and the right-hand push is directed leftward. Each push can be traced to a specific physical source — the muscular exertion of the person applying it. The second and third conditions are satisfied for each interaction individually. Yet the crate does not move, and its shape does not change. The two forces are in perfect equilibrium; they cancel one another exactly. The first condition — the production of an observable change in motion or shape — is not met by either force considered on its own. Nevertheless, each push is indisputably a physical force. The absence of observable change results not from the absence of force but from the precise cancellation of two forces. It appears that the definition, by requiring that a force produce an observable change, confuses the presence of a force with the net observable effect of all forces acting on the body. One is led to conclude that the analysis must accommodate cases in which real forces exist in equilibrium, producing no net change yet remaining genuine forces nonetheless.

definition: A trait T of an organism is an adaptation if and only if (i) T enhances the organism's fitness in its current environment, (ii) T is heritable, and (iii) T is widespread in the population.
conditions: T enhances the organism's fitness in its current environment
T is heritable
T is widespread in the population
counterexample: Consider the vestigial hip bones found in whales. These bones are heritable, being genetically determined and passed from parent to offspring. They are widespread in whale populations; virtually all whales possess them. Yet they do not enhance the organism's fitness in its current aquatic environment; they serve no locomotor function and are not employed in any activity that contributes to the whale's survival or reproduction in the sea. Nevertheless, biologists regard these bones as adaptations — they are remnants of the hindlimb structure that was an adaptation to terrestrial locomotion in the whale's distant ancestors. The bones are adaptations in virtue of their history, not their current utility.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a historical condition requiring that T was selected for in virtue of its contribution to the fitness of the organism's ancestors in their environment. An adaptation is a trait shaped by past selection, not merely one that happens to be beneficial, heritable, and widespread in the present.
passage: One might define an adaptation as a heritable trait that enhances the organism's fitness in its current environment and is widespread in the population. This characterisation captures the apparent functionality, genetic basis, and prevalence that we associate with adaptive traits. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from comparative anatomy. Consider the vestigial hip bones found in modern whales. These small, seemingly functionless bones are homologous with the pelvic structures of terrestrial mammals and are remnants of the hindlimb apparatus that supported locomotion on land. The bones are heritable — their development is genetically determined and they are reliably transmitted from parent to offspring. They are widespread in whale populations; virtually every whale possesses them. Yet they do not enhance the organism's fitness in its current aquatic environment. They serve no locomotor function; they are not employed in swimming, feeding, or any other activity contributing to the whale's survival or reproductive success in the ocean. The first condition of the proposed definition is not met. Nevertheless, biologists regard these structures as adaptations — or more precisely, as remnants of what were once adaptations to terrestrial life. Their status as adaptations derives from their history: they were shaped by natural selection in ancestral populations where they served a vital function. It appears that the definition, by attending only to current utility, heritability, and prevalence, fails to capture the essentially historical character of adaptation. One is led to conclude that a further condition is needed, requiring that the trait have been selected for in virtue of its contribution to the fitness of the organism's ancestors.

definition: A celestial body B is a planet if and only if (i) B orbits a star, (ii) B has sufficient mass for its own gravity to have pulled it into a roughly spherical shape, and (iii) B is the gravitationally dominant body in its orbital region.
conditions: B orbits a star
B has sufficient mass for its own gravity to have pulled it into a roughly spherical shape
B is the gravitationally dominant body in its orbital region
counterexample: Suppose astronomers discover a massive, spherical body, several times the mass of Jupiter, drifting through interstellar space. It does not orbit any star; it was ejected from its parent system by a gravitational interaction with another massive body billions of years ago. It has more than sufficient mass for gravity to have shaped it into a sphere, and in its current region of space it is overwhelmingly the dominant gravitational influence. The second and third conditions are satisfied. Yet the first is not, for the body does not orbit a star. Nevertheless, its composition, structure, and formation history are identical to those of a gas giant planet. It appears that the definition, by requiring that a planet orbit a star, excludes objects that are planetary in every intrinsic respect but happen to have been gravitationally ejected from their stellar systems.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate so-called rogue or free-floating planets — bodies that are planetary in composition, structure, and origin but no longer orbit a star. A condition distinguishing intrinsically planetary properties from the contingent circumstance of stellar orbit is required.
passage: One might propose that a planet is a celestial body that orbits a star, possesses sufficient mass for its own gravity to have shaped it into a roughly spherical form, and is the gravitationally dominant body in its orbital region. This characterisation captures the orbital, morphological, and dynamical features commonly associated with planethood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from recent astronomical discoveries. Suppose observers detect a massive body, several times the mass of Jupiter, drifting through interstellar space far from any star. Detailed spectroscopic analysis reveals that its composition and internal structure are identical to those of a gas giant planet: a hydrogen-helium atmosphere, a dense metallic hydrogen interior, and a rocky core. Dynamical modelling shows that the body was formed in a protoplanetary disc around a young star and was subsequently ejected by a close gravitational encounter with another massive body in the system. The body has more than sufficient mass for gravity to have rendered it spherical, and in its current region of interstellar space it is overwhelmingly the dominant gravitational presence. The second and third conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the body does not orbit any star; it drifts freely through the void. The first condition is not met. Nevertheless, in every intrinsic respect — composition, structure, formation history — the body is indistinguishable from a planet. It appears that the definition, by requiring stellar orbit as a necessary condition, confuses a contingent circumstance of a body's dynamical history with a constitutive feature of its nature. One is led to conclude that the analysis must accommodate bodies that are planetary in all intrinsic respects but have been separated from their parent stars.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same atomic number, and (iii) S has a unique set of physical properties — melting point, boiling point, density — by which it may be identified.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means
All atoms of S have the same atomic number
S has a unique set of physical properties by which it may be identified
counterexample: Consider the element carbon. Carbon cannot be decomposed by ordinary chemical means, and all atoms of carbon have atomic number six. Yet carbon exists in several allotropic forms — diamond, graphite, and amorphous carbon — each possessing dramatically different physical properties. Diamond is transparent and extremely hard, with a melting point above 3500 degrees Celsius; graphite is opaque, soft, and a good conductor of electricity; amorphous carbon has yet different properties. There is no unique set of physical properties — melting point, density, conductivity — by which carbon may be identified, for the properties vary with the allotrope. The third condition is not satisfied, yet carbon is indisputably a chemical element.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for allotropy — the phenomenon whereby a single element may exist in multiple structural forms with distinct physical properties. A condition recognising that the identity of an element is determined by its atomic number alone, independently of the physical properties of its various allotropic forms, is needed.
passage: One might define a chemical element as a substance that cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, whose atoms all share the same atomic number, and which possesses a unique set of physical properties — melting point, boiling point, density — by which it may be identified. This characterisation captures the chemical indivisibility, atomic uniformity, and physical distinctiveness that we associate with elementhood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the chemistry of carbon. Carbon cannot be decomposed by ordinary chemical reactions into anything simpler; all its atoms have atomic number six. The first two conditions of the proposed definition are clearly satisfied. Yet carbon exists in several allotropic forms — diamond, graphite, and amorphous carbon — each possessing dramatically different physical properties. Diamond is transparent, extraordinarily hard, and an electrical insulator; graphite is opaque, soft, and an electrical conductor; amorphous carbon differs from both. The melting points, densities, and other physical constants vary so markedly across these forms that there is no unique set of physical properties by which carbon may be identified. The third condition is not met. Nevertheless, carbon is indisputably a chemical element. It appears that the definition, by requiring a unique set of physical properties, fails to accommodate the phenomenon of allotropy, in which a single element assumes multiple structural forms with distinct physical characteristics. One is led to conclude that the identity of an element must be grounded in its atomic number alone and that the analysis must abandon the requirement of a unique physical profile.

definition: A substance S qualifies as a distinct chemical element if and only if (i) every atom of S has the same number of protons in its nucleus, (ii) S exhibits a characteristic spectrum of electromagnetic radiation when excited, and (iii) S cannot be converted into another substance by chemical reactions.
conditions: Every atom of S has the same number of protons in its nucleus
S exhibits a characteristic spectrum of electromagnetic radiation when excited
S cannot be converted into another substance by chemical reactions
counterexample: Consider the substance promethium, element sixty-one. Every atom of promethium has sixty-one protons; it exhibits a characteristic emission spectrum; and it cannot be converted into another substance by ordinary chemical reactions. All three conditions are satisfied, and promethium is indeed a chemical element. Yet now consider the isotopes of hydrogen: protium, deuterium, and tritium. All three have one proton and thus satisfy the first condition as a single element. But deuterium and protium exhibit measurably different spectra due to the isotope effect, and in certain contexts they behave as if they were spectroscopically distinct. More troublingly, the definition's third condition is satisfied by noble gases and other stable elements but fails to address the phenomenon of nuclear transmutation, in which one element is converted into another by bombardment with particles — a process that is not a chemical reaction. A substance such as nitrogen can be converted into oxygen by alpha-particle bombardment, yet the definition would still classify nitrogen as an element because the conversion is nuclear rather than chemical. The definition thus draws the line at chemical reactions in a way that becomes arbitrary when nuclear processes are considered.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify the level of physical process at which identity is to be assessed, drawing an arbitrary boundary at chemical reactions while ignoring nuclear transformations. A condition clarifying that elemental identity is constituted solely by atomic number, regardless of the means by which transformation might be achieved, is needed.
passage: One might propose that a substance qualifies as a distinct chemical element when every atom of the substance has the same number of protons, the substance exhibits a characteristic emission spectrum, and it cannot be converted into another substance by chemical reactions. This characterisation captures the nuclear identity, spectroscopic individuality, and chemical indivisibility that we associate with elementhood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed by attending to the boundary between chemical and nuclear processes. Consider nitrogen. Every atom of nitrogen has seven protons; nitrogen exhibits a well-defined characteristic spectrum; and nitrogen cannot be converted into another substance by any ordinary chemical reaction. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet Rutherford demonstrated in 1919 that nitrogen can be converted into oxygen by bombardment with alpha particles — a nuclear rather than a chemical process. The definition, by restricting the third condition to chemical reactions, draws a boundary that becomes arbitrary when nuclear transformations are brought into view. If the point of the condition is to identify substances that resist decomposition into simpler constituents, the limitation to chemical means leaves the definition vulnerable to any process that transcends that boundary. It appears that the definition, by combining nuclear identity with a restriction to chemical indecomposability, introduces an inconsistency in its own terms. One is led to conclude that elemental identity should be grounded solely in atomic number, without reference to the particular means by which transformation might or might not be effected.

definition: A trait T of an organism O constitutes an adaptation to an environmental challenge C if and only if (i) T increases O's probability of surviving or reproducing when C is present, (ii) T is genetically based and heritable, and (iii) organisms possessing T have higher reproductive success than those lacking T in environments where C is present.
conditions: T increases O's probability of surviving or reproducing when C is present
T is genetically based and heritable
Organisms possessing T have higher reproductive success than those lacking T in environments where C is present
counterexample: Suppose a mutation arises in a population of moths that causes their wings to be transparent rather than opaque. In an environment where predatory birds hunt by detecting the silhouettes of moths resting on tree bark, transparent wings happen to reduce predation, increasing the moth's probability of survival. The trait is genetically based and heritable. Moths with transparent wings have higher reproductive success than those without in this predator-rich environment. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the mutation arose by chance only one generation ago and has not been selected for by any evolutionary process; its fitness advantage is a coincidence of the present environment. Biologists would not regard this novel mutation as an adaptation to predation, for it has no history of selection — it is a fortuitous variation that happens to be advantageous.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that T have been shaped or maintained by natural selection over multiple generations in response to the environmental challenge C. A trait that happens to be beneficial due to a recent mutation is not yet an adaptation; it must have been selected for through an evolutionary process extending across generations.
passage: One might characterise an adaptation as a heritable trait that increases an organism's fitness in the face of an environmental challenge and that confers higher reproductive success upon those who possess it. This formulation captures the fitness-enhancing, genetic, and differential-success features associated with adaptive traits. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from population genetics. Suppose a mutation arises spontaneously in a population of moths, causing their wings to become transparent rather than bearing the usual opaque colouration. In the moths' environment, predatory birds locate their prey by detecting the dark silhouettes of moths resting on pale tree bark. The transparent-winged moths, presenting no silhouette, happen to escape predation at a much higher rate. The trait increases the probability of survival when predation pressure is present. It is genetically based and heritable: the mutation is passed from parent to offspring. Moths with transparent wings enjoy higher reproductive success than their opaque-winged counterparts. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet the mutation arose only a single generation ago, entirely by chance. It has not been subject to natural selection over time; there has been no process of differential reproduction extending across generations. Biologists would describe this as a fortuitous variation, not an adaptation. It appears that the definition, by requiring only present fitness advantage, heritability, and differential success, fails to demand that the trait have a history of selection. One is led to conclude that adaptation is an essentially historical concept, requiring that the trait have been shaped or maintained by selection over successive generations in response to the relevant environmental challenge.

definition: A group of organisms constitutes a biological species if and only if (i) its members are capable of interbreeding to produce fertile offspring, (ii) its members are reproductively isolated from organisms outside the group, and (iii) the group occupies a distinct ecological niche.
conditions: Members are capable of interbreeding to produce fertile offspring
Members are reproductively isolated from organisms outside the group
The group occupies a distinct ecological niche
counterexample: Consider a case in which two populations of fish inhabit the same lake but at different depths. Population A breeds near the surface and Population B near the bottom.
