Members of each population are capable of interbreeding with one another to produce fertile offspring, and each population is reproductively isolated from the other due to differing breeding depths. Each occupies a distinct ecological niche defined by depth, diet, and light exposure. All three conditions are satisfied for each population considered separately. Yet the two populations are not genuinely distinct species; they are merely depth-segregated subpopulations of the same species that will interbreed freely if placed at the same depth, and the reproductive isolation is entirely a contingent product of habitat preference rather than of any intrinsic biological barrier.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the reproductive isolation be intrinsic to the biology of the organisms, such as genetic incompatibility or morphological divergence, rather than merely a contingent consequence of habitat separation that would disappear if the external circumstances were altered.
passage: One might define a biological species as a group whose members are capable of interbreeding to produce fertile offspring, are reproductively isolated from organisms outside the group, and occupy a distinct ecological niche. This characterisation captures the reproductive cohesion and ecological distinctness that we ordinarily attribute to species. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of freshwater fish. Suppose two populations of fish inhabit the same lake but at different depths. Population A breeds near the surface in well-lit, warm waters, and Population B breeds near the bottom in cold, dark waters. Members of each population are capable of interbreeding to produce fertile offspring, and each population is reproductively isolated from the other, since their breeding grounds do not overlap. Each occupies a distinct ecological niche defined by depth, diet, and light exposure. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied for each population considered separately. Nevertheless, one might observe that the two populations are not genuinely distinct species. If members of the two populations are placed at the same depth under experimental conditions, they interbreed freely and produce fertile offspring indistinguishable from those of either parent population. The reproductive isolation is entirely a contingent product of habitat preference rather than of any intrinsic biological barrier. It appears that the definition, by requiring only interbreeding capacity, reproductive isolation, and ecological distinctness, fails to demand that the isolation be intrinsic to the biology of the organisms rather than a mere artefact of environmental circumstance.

definition: An entity E is alive if and only if (i) E maintains itself in a state far from thermodynamic equilibrium by means of metabolic processes, (ii) E is capable of reproducing itself or contributing to the reproduction of entities of its kind, and (iii) E responds to environmental stimuli by altering its behaviour or internal state.
conditions: E maintains itself in a state far from thermodynamic equilibrium by means of metabolic processes
E is capable of reproducing itself or contributing to reproduction of entities of its kind
E responds to environmental stimuli by altering its behaviour or internal state
counterexample: Suppose a chemist constructs an autocatalytic chemical system enclosed in a vessel. The system maintains itself far from thermodynamic equilibrium by continuously consuming a chemical fuel. It reproduces itself: when a portion of the solution is transferred to a fresh vessel of fuel, the autocatalytic cycle re-establishes itself. And it responds to environmental stimuli, for changes in temperature alter the reaction rates in ways that maintain the cycle's stability. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the system is nothing more than a set of self-sustaining chemical reactions without any organisation into functional parts, without hereditary information, and without the capacity for adaptation over successive generations. We should hesitate to call it alive.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that E possess hereditary information capable of variation and selection, so that its reproduction is not mere chemical replication but involves the transmission of a structured informational template subject to modification over generations.
passage: One might propose that an entity is alive when it maintains itself far from thermodynamic equilibrium through metabolic processes, is capable of reproduction, and responds to environmental stimuli. This characterisation captures the energetic, reproductive, and responsive qualities that we typically associate with living systems. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from synthetic chemistry. Suppose a chemist constructs an autocatalytic chemical system enclosed within a glass vessel. The system maintains itself far from thermodynamic equilibrium by continuously consuming a chemical fuel that is supplied from without. It reproduces itself in the sense that when a portion of the solution is transferred to a fresh vessel of fuel, the autocatalytic cycle re-establishes itself spontaneously. And it responds to environmental stimuli: changes in the ambient temperature alter the rates of the constituent reactions in ways that preserve the overall stability of the cycle. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear to be met. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to say that the system is alive. It is nothing more than a set of self-sustaining chemical reactions, devoid of any organisation into functional parts, lacking hereditary information, and incapable of adaptation over successive generations. It appears that the definition, by requiring only metabolism, reproduction, and stimulus-response, fails to demand that the entity possess hereditary information capable of variation and selection, so that its reproduction involves the transmission of a structured informational template rather than mere chemical replication.

definition: A celestial body C is a planet if and only if (i) C orbits a star, (ii) C has sufficient mass that its own gravity has shaped it into a roughly spherical form, and (iii) C does not produce energy by nuclear fusion.
conditions: C orbits a star
C has sufficient mass that its own gravity has shaped it into a roughly spherical form
C does not produce energy by nuclear fusion
counterexample: Suppose an astronomer discovers a celestial body that orbits a distant star, possesses sufficient mass for gravity to have compressed it into a roughly spherical shape, and does not produce energy by nuclear fusion. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the body shares its orbital region with thousands of other similarly sized objects, none of which it has gravitationally swept away or incorporated. It is one member of a vast swarm of comparable bodies, more akin to the largest fragment in a belt of debris than to what we intuitively regard as a planet. Despite satisfying the conditions, the object lacks the gravitational pre-eminence in its orbital neighbourhood that we ordinarily take to distinguish a planet from a mere large body.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that C has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit of other bodies of comparable size, so that it is gravitationally dominant in its orbital zone rather than merely one large body among many.
passage: One might define a planet as a celestial body that orbits a star, possesses sufficient mass for gravity to have shaped it into a roughly spherical form, and does not produce energy by nuclear fusion. This characterisation captures the orbital, morphological, and non-stellar character that we associate with the planetary concept. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the observation of distant stellar systems. Suppose an astronomer discovers a body orbiting a distant star, spherical in form owing to its considerable mass, and entirely devoid of nuclear fusion. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the body shares its orbital region with thousands of other similarly sized objects, none of which it has gravitationally swept away or incorporated into itself. It is one member of a vast swarm of comparable bodies, occupying its orbit not as a dominant gravitational presence but as the largest fragment in what amounts to a belt of debris. We should hesitate to classify this object as a planet, for it lacks the gravitational pre-eminence in its orbital neighbourhood that we intuitively take to distinguish planets from mere large bodies. It appears that the definition, by requiring only stellar orbit, spherical form, and the absence of fusion, neglects the condition that the body must have cleared its orbital neighbourhood of objects of comparable size, thereby establishing itself as the gravitationally dominant entity in its region of space.

definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means, (ii) S is composed entirely of atoms having the same number of protons, and (iii) S exhibits a characteristic set of chemical properties that distinguishes it from all other substances.
conditions: S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by any chemical means
S is composed entirely of atoms having the same number of protons
S exhibits a characteristic set of chemical properties distinguishing it from all other substances
counterexample: Consider a case in which a chemist isolates a pure sample of a particular isotope of an element, say carbon-12, and a separate pure sample of carbon-13. Each sample cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, and each is composed entirely of atoms having the same number of protons, namely six. Yet the two samples exhibit subtly different physical properties: different atomic masses, different rates of participation in certain reactions, and different spectroscopic signatures. If a characteristic set of distinguishing chemical properties is required, one might argue that carbon-12 and carbon-13 each satisfies all three conditions independently, and the definition would seem to count them as distinct elements rather than as isotopes of the same element.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify that the characteristic properties defining an element are determined solely by the number of protons and the electronic configuration, rather than by mass-dependent features that vary among isotopes, so that isotopic variants of the same element are not erroneously classified as distinct elements.
passage: One might propose that a chemical element is a substance that cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, is composed entirely of atoms sharing the same number of protons, and exhibits a characteristic set of chemical properties distinguishing it from all other substances. This characterisation captures the indivisibility, atomic uniformity, and distinctiveness that we associate with the concept of an element. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of isotopy. Suppose a chemist isolates a pure sample of carbon-12 and a separate pure sample of carbon-13. Each sample resists decomposition by any chemical means, and each is composed entirely of atoms having six protons. Yet the two samples differ subtly in their physical and chemical behaviour: they possess different atomic masses, participate at different rates in certain reactions, and yield distinct spectroscopic signatures. If the definition requires a characteristic set of distinguishing properties, one might argue that carbon-12 and carbon-13 each satisfies all three conditions independently, and the definition would seem to classify them as distinct elements rather than as isotopic variants of the same element. It appears that the definition, by requiring only chemical indecomposability, proton-number uniformity, and a distinguishing set of properties, neglects the requirement that the characteristic properties be determined solely by the number of protons and the electronic configuration. Without this specification, the analysis cannot prevent isotopic variants from being counted as separate elements.

definition: A condition D is a disease if and only if (i) D involves a departure from the normal physiological functioning of the organism, (ii) D causes suffering or impairment in the organism, and (iii) D has an identifiable cause, whether internal or external to the organism.
conditions: D involves a departure from the normal physiological functioning of the organism
D causes suffering or impairment in the organism
D has an identifiable cause whether internal or external to the organism
counterexample: Suppose a woman of advanced age experiences a gradual decline in bone density. This departure from the norm of youthful physiology causes her suffering in the form of pain and increased vulnerability to fracture, and it has an identifiable cause in the hormonal changes associated with ageing. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the decline in bone density is a normal part of the ageing process, expected in virtually all individuals of her age and sex. We should hesitate to classify normal ageing as a disease, for the concept of disease seems to require something beyond the mere statistical departure from youthful functioning that the passage of time inevitably brings.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the departure from normal functioning be pathological in the sense that it represents a malfunction of the organism's systems, as distinguished from a predictable and universal change that is part of the normal life course of the organism.
passage: One might define disease as a condition involving a departure from normal physiological functioning, causing suffering or impairment, and possessing an identifiable cause. This characterisation captures the abnormality, harmfulness, and causal intelligibility that we ordinarily attribute to disease. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the biology of ageing. Suppose a woman of advanced age experiences a gradual decline in bone density. This constitutes a departure from the norm of youthful physiology, and it causes her genuine suffering in the form of chronic pain and increased vulnerability to fracture. The condition has an identifiable cause in the hormonal changes associated with the ageing process. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to classify this decline as a disease, for it is a normal part of the ageing process, expected in virtually all individuals of her age and sex. The concept of disease seems to require something more than a mere departure from youthful functioning that the passage of time inevitably brings to every organism. It appears that the definition, by requiring only departure from normal function, suffering, and an identifiable cause, fails to distinguish between genuine pathological malfunction and the predictable, universal changes that constitute the normal life course of the organism. Without this distinction, every aspect of ageing would count as a disease, a conclusion that strains our ordinary understanding of the concept.

definition: A collection of organisms and their physical environment constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) the organisms interact with one another through relationships such as predation, competition, and symbiosis, (ii) energy flows through the collection via trophic levels from producers to consumers, and (iii) the collection occupies a geographically bounded region.
conditions: The organisms interact with one another through relationships such as predation competition and symbiosis
Energy flows through the collection via trophic levels from producers to consumers
The collection occupies a geographically bounded region
counterexample: Suppose one considers the organisms inhabiting a large rock in the middle of a stream. Lichens on the rock's surface produce energy from sunlight, small invertebrates graze on the lichens, and a predatory insect feeds upon the invertebrates. The organisms interact through predation and competition, energy flows from producers through trophic levels to consumers, and the collection occupies the geographically bounded region of the rock. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the rock community is not self-sustaining: the invertebrates depend upon organic debris washing over from the surrounding stream, and the predatory insect regularly leaves the rock to hunt elsewhere. The community is so thoroughly dependent upon exchanges with the surrounding stream that it cannot be meaningfully understood in isolation.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the collection possess a degree of functional self-sufficiency, such that the cycling of materials and energy within the system is substantial enough for the collection to be meaningfully analysed as a unit, rather than being merely a fragment entirely dependent upon external exchanges.
passage: One might propose that an ecosystem is a collection of organisms and their physical environment in which the organisms interact through ecological relationships, energy flows through trophic levels, and the whole occupies a geographically bounded region. This characterisation captures the biotic interactions, energy dynamics, and spatial delimitation that we associate with the ecosystem concept. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of micro-habitats. Suppose one considers the community of organisms inhabiting a large rock in the middle of a stream. Lichens on the rock's surface fix energy from sunlight, small invertebrates graze upon the lichens, and a predatory insect feeds upon the invertebrates. The organisms interact through predation and competition, energy flows from producers through consumers, and the community occupies the geographically bounded region of the rock. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the rock community is not self-sustaining in any meaningful sense. The invertebrates depend heavily upon organic debris that washes over from the surrounding stream, and the predatory insect regularly departs the rock to hunt in adjacent habitats. The community is so thoroughly dependent upon exchanges with the broader stream ecosystem that it cannot be meaningfully understood or analysed in isolation. It appears that the definition, by requiring only biotic interaction, energy flow through trophic levels, and geographic boundedness, fails to demand a degree of functional self-sufficiency that would make the collection a meaningful ecological unit rather than an arbitrary fragment of a larger system.

definition: A segment of nucleic acid G is a gene if and only if (i) G encodes information for the synthesis of a functional product, whether protein or RNA, (ii) G is transmitted from parent to offspring during reproduction, and (iii) G occupies a specific locus on a chromosome.
conditions: G encodes information for the synthesis of a functional product whether protein or RNA
G is transmitted from parent to offspring during reproduction
G occupies a specific locus on a chromosome
counterexample: Suppose a geneticist identifies a segment of DNA that encodes the information for a functional protein, is reliably transmitted from parent to offspring, and occupies a specific chromosomal locus. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the functional protein is produced only when information from this segment is combined, after transcription, with information from a second segment located on a different chromosome entirely. Neither segment alone encodes a complete functional product; the functional product arises from the splicing together of transcripts from two distant loci. The definition, which speaks of a single segment encoding information for a functional product, cannot accommodate the case in which the informational contribution of one locus is incomplete without the contribution of another.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the phenomenon of trans-splicing and split genes, in which the information required for a single functional product may be distributed across multiple non-contiguous chromosomal loci, so that no single segment at a single locus constitutes the complete informational unit.
passage: One might define a gene as a segment of nucleic acid that encodes information for the synthesis of a functional product, is transmitted from parent to offspring, and occupies a specific chromosomal locus. This characterisation captures the informational, hereditary, and positional features that we ordinarily attribute to genes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from molecular biology. Suppose a geneticist identifies a segment of DNA that encodes information contributing to the synthesis of a functional protein, is reliably transmitted during reproduction, and occupies a specific locus on a particular chromosome. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Nevertheless, one might observe that the functional protein is produced only when information from this segment is combined, after transcription, with information from a second segment located on a different chromosome. Neither segment alone encodes a complete functional product; the protein arises from the splicing together of transcripts originating at two distant loci. The definition, which speaks of a single segment at a single locus encoding information for a functional product, cannot accommodate this case, in which the informational contribution of one locus is incomplete without the contribution of another. It appears that the analysis, by requiring only that a segment encode functional information, be heritable, and occupy a fixed locus, neglects the possibility that the information required for a single functional product may be distributed across multiple non-contiguous chromosomal sites.

definition: An interaction F between two bodies constitutes a force if and only if (i) F produces or tends to produce a change in the state of motion of at least one of the bodies, (ii) F can be characterised by a magnitude and a direction, and (iii) F acts along the line connecting the two bodies.
conditions: F produces or tends to produce a change in the state of motion of at least one of the bodies
F can be characterised by a magnitude and a direction
F acts along the line connecting the two bodies
counterexample: Suppose a charged particle moves through a magnetic field. The magnetic interaction produces a change in the particle's state of motion, deflecting its trajectory. The interaction has a definite magnitude and direction at each point. Yet the force does not act along the line connecting the particle and the source of the magnetic field; it acts perpendicular to the particle's velocity and to the field direction. All three conditions appear to be satisfied in spirit, but condition (iii) is violated by the fundamental geometry of the magnetic force. If one weakens condition (iii) to require merely that the force have some definite spatial orientation, then the condition becomes trivially satisfied by any directed quantity and loses its discriminating power.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate forces that do not act along the line connecting the interacting bodies, such as magnetic forces, and thereby either excludes genuine forces or must weaken its directional requirement to the point of vacuity.
passage: One might define a force as an interaction between two bodies that produces or tends to produce a change in the state of motion of at least one body, can be characterised by a magnitude and direction, and acts along the line connecting the two bodies. This characterisation captures the dynamical efficacy, vector character, and geometric simplicity that we associate with the classical concept of force. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from electrodynamics. Suppose a charged particle moves through a magnetic field produced by a current-carrying wire. The magnetic interaction produces a change in the particle's state of motion, deflecting its trajectory. The interaction possesses a definite magnitude and direction at each point along the particle's path. Yet the force does not act along the line connecting the particle and the wire; it acts perpendicular both to the particle's velocity and to the magnetic field direction. Condition (iii) of the proposed definition is violated by the fundamental geometry of the magnetic force. If one attempts to salvage the definition by weakening the directional requirement to demand merely that the force have some definite spatial orientation, the condition becomes trivially satisfied by any directed quantity and loses its capacity to discriminate forces from other vector quantities. It appears that the definition, by requiring that forces act along the line connecting the interacting bodies, excludes genuine forces whose geometry is more complex, and thereby fails to capture the full range of physical interactions that we recognise as forces.

definition: A trait T of an organism O is an adaptation if and only if (i) T enhances the fitness of O in O's current environment, (ii) T was produced by natural selection operating on heritable variation in ancestral populations, and (iii) the function for which T was selected in ancestral populations is the same as the function T performs in O's current environment.
conditions: T enhances the fitness of O in O's current environment
T was produced by natural selection operating on heritable variation in ancestral populations
The function for which T was selected in ancestral populations is the same as the function T performs in the current environment
counterexample: Suppose a species of bird possesses feathers. Feathers enhance the bird's fitness in its current environment by enabling flight. Feathers were produced by natural selection operating on heritable variation in ancestral populations. Yet in the earliest feathered ancestors, feathers were selected not for flight but for thermal insulation. The function for which feathers were originally selected is thermoregulation, whereas the function they predominantly serve in the current species is aerodynamic. Condition (iii) is violated: the ancestral and current functions differ. The definition would require us to say that feathers are not adaptations for flight, which conflicts with our intuitive classification.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the phenomenon of co-option, whereby a trait that was originally selected for one function comes to serve a different function in descendant organisms, so that a trait may be a genuine adaptation for its current function even though that function differs from the one for which it was ancestrally selected.
passage: One might propose that a trait is an adaptation if it enhances fitness in the current environment, was produced by natural selection on heritable variation in ancestral populations, and serves the same function in the current environment as it did in ancestral populations. This characterisation captures the fitness-enhancing, selectional, and functional continuity features that we associate with adaptation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the evolution of feathers. Feathers enhance the fitness of modern birds by enabling flight; they were produced by natural selection on heritable variation in ancestral populations; yet in the earliest feathered ancestors, feathers were selected not for flight but for thermal insulation. The function for which feathers were originally selected differs from the function they predominantly serve today. The third condition of the definition is violated, and the analysis would compel us to deny that feathers are adaptations for flight. This conclusion conflicts sharply with our intuitive classification. It appears that the definition, by requiring functional continuity between ancestral selection and current performance, fails to accommodate the phenomenon of co-option, whereby a trait originally selected for one function comes to serve a different and equally important function in descendant organisms. Without provision for this possibility, the analysis excludes a large class of traits that we should very much wish to count as adaptations.

definition: A community of organisms C constitutes a stable ecosystem if and only if (i) C contains species occupying multiple trophic levels, (ii) C maintains its composition and structure over time without progressive change, and (iii) C is capable of returning to its prior state following a minor disturbance.
conditions: C contains species occupying multiple trophic levels
C maintains its composition and structure over time without progressive change
C is capable of returning to its prior state following a minor disturbance
counterexample: Suppose a pond community contains algae as primary producers, zooplankton as primary consumers, and small fish as secondary consumers. The community maintains its composition and structure over several years without progressive change. And when a minor disturbance, such as a brief drought, temporarily reduces the water level, the community returns to its prior state upon restoration of normal conditions. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the stability is entirely dependent upon a single species of algae. If that species is removed by a disease, the entire food web collapses, because no other producer can sustain the zooplankton population. The community is stable only in the narrow sense that it persists under the conditions that happen to prevail, but it is catastrophically fragile in the face of a perturbation that the definition does not address.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the stability of C be robust, in the sense that C's capacity to maintain itself and recover from disturbance does not depend upon any single species whose removal would cause the system to collapse entirely.
passage: One might define a stable ecosystem as a community containing species at multiple trophic levels, maintaining its composition over time, and capable of recovering from minor disturbances. This characterisation captures the structural complexity, temporal persistence, and resilience that we associate with ecological stability. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the study of simple aquatic communities. Suppose a pond contains algae as primary producers, zooplankton as primary consumers, and small fish as secondary consumers. The community maintains its composition and structure over several years without progressive change, and when a brief drought temporarily reduces the water level, the community returns to its prior state upon the restoration of normal conditions. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, the stability of the entire system depends upon a single species of alga. Should that species be eliminated by disease, no other producer can sustain the zooplankton population, and the entire food web collapses. The community is stable only in the narrow sense that it persists under the conditions that happen to obtain, but it is catastrophically fragile in the face of a perturbation that removes the single keystone producer. It appears that the definition, by requiring only trophic complexity, temporal constancy, and recovery from minor disturbance, fails to demand that the stability be robust, in the sense that it does not depend upon any single species whose loss would precipitate total collapse.

definition: A genetic variant V is the cause of a trait T in an organism O if and only if (i) V is present in O's genome, (ii) organisms with V reliably exhibit T whereas organisms without V do not, and (iii) there is a biochemical pathway by which V contributes to the production of T.
conditions: V is present in O's genome
Organisms with V reliably exhibit T whereas organisms without V do not
There is a biochemical pathway by which V contributes to the production of T
counterexample: Suppose a genetic variant is present in an organism's genome. Organisms with this variant reliably exhibit a particular plumage colour, whereas organisms without it do not. And a biochemical pathway has been identified by which the variant contributes to pigment production. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the plumage colour appears only in organisms that are also exposed to a specific dietary compound. In the absence of the dietary compound, organisms carrying the variant exhibit no distinctive plumage whatsoever. The variant is a necessary cause, but it is not a sufficient cause; the trait emerges only from the interaction of gene and environment, yet the definition attributes causation to the gene alone.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the causal attribution to V account for the environmental conditions necessary for V to produce T, so that the gene is not credited as the sole cause of a trait that in fact depends upon a gene-environment interaction.
passage: One might define a genetic variant as the cause of a trait when the variant is present in the organism's genome, organisms with the variant reliably exhibit the trait whilst those without it do not, and a biochemical pathway links the variant to the trait's production. This characterisation captures the genomic presence, phenotypic correlation, and mechanistic connection that we associate with genetic causation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of gene-environment interaction. Suppose a genetic variant is present in a bird's genome. Birds carrying the variant reliably exhibit a distinctive plumage colour, whereas birds lacking it do not. A biochemical pathway has been identified by which the variant contributes to the production of the relevant pigment. All three conditions are satisfied. Nevertheless, the distinctive plumage appears only in birds that are also exposed to a specific dietary compound found in certain seeds. In the absence of this compound, birds carrying the variant exhibit no distinctive colouration whatsoever. The variant is a necessary but not sufficient cause of the trait; the plumage emerges only from the interaction of gene and diet. It appears that the definition, by attributing causation to the genetic variant alone, neglects the environmental conditions without which the variant cannot produce the trait. Without requiring that the causal account incorporate these conditions, the analysis credits the gene with a causal power it exercises only in partnership with the environment.

definition: An organism belongs to a given species if and only if (i) it can interbreed with other members of the group to produce fertile offspring, (ii) it shares a common gene pool with those members, and (iii) it is reproductively isolated from members of other groups.
conditions: it can interbreed with other members of the group to produce fertile offspring
it shares a common gene pool with those members
it is reproductively isolated from members of other groups
counterexample: Suppose that two populations of plants, classified as distinct species, occasionally hybridise along a narrow contact zone, producing offspring that are fully fertile and capable of backcrossing with either parent population. Each population shares a common gene pool with its own members and is generally isolated from the other, yet the existence of fertile hybrids means neither population satisfies the third condition absolutely. One would hesitate to say that neither group constitutes a species, for they maintain their distinctness across the vast majority of their range.
missing_condition: The definition treats reproductive isolation as an absolute requirement, failing to accommodate cases of partial or incomplete isolation in which species boundaries remain biologically meaningful despite occasional gene flow.
passage: The biological characterisation of a species has often been framed in terms of three jointly sufficient conditions: the capacity for interbreeding to produce fertile offspring, participation in a shared gene pool, and reproductive isolation from other groups. This formulation has the merit of precision and appears to accord with our intuitive discrimination of natural kinds. Yet one may observe that nature does not always respect such tidy boundaries. Let us imagine two populations of flowering plants, long classified as separate species on morphological and ecological grounds, which meet along a narrow strip of territory where their ranges adjoin. In this contact zone, occasional hybridisation occurs, and the resulting offspring are fully fertile, capable of crossing back with either parent stock. Each population maintains a common gene pool and is, across the greater part of its range, wholly isolated from the other. Nevertheless, the fact of fertile hybridisation means that the third condition — reproductive isolation — is not absolutely satisfied. It appears unreasonable to conclude that neither population constitutes a genuine species, for their distinctness is manifest in morphology, ecology, and behaviour throughout most of their distribution. We are thus brought to recognise that the demand for absolute reproductive isolation is too stringent and that a satisfactory analysis of species membership must accommodate cases of partial gene flow.

definition: An entity is alive if and only if (i) it maintains homeostasis, (ii) it undergoes metabolism by converting energy from its environment, and (iii) it is capable of reproduction.
conditions: it maintains homeostasis
it undergoes metabolism by converting energy from its environment
it is capable of reproduction
counterexample: Consider a case in which a sterile hybrid animal — say, a mule — maintains homeostasis with great efficiency and metabolises nutrients drawn from its surroundings, yet is wholly incapable of reproduction. It satisfies the first two conditions but fails the third. One would be hard pressed to deny that the mule is alive; indeed, it exhibits every outward sign of vitality save the power to propagate its kind.
missing_condition: The definition makes reproductive capacity a necessary condition of life, thereby excluding sterile organisms that are manifestly alive by every other criterion.
passage: A venerable tradition in the philosophy of biology holds that life may be characterised by three conditions taken jointly: the maintenance of homeostasis, the metabolic conversion of environmental energy, and the capacity for reproduction. On this view, an entity that satisfies all three is alive, and one that fails any is not. The analysis has a pleasing economy and captures a wide range of paradigmatic cases. Yet we should hesitate to accept it without reservation. Let us imagine the familiar case of the mule, an animal produced by crossing a horse with a donkey. The mule maintains its internal equilibrium with admirable efficiency, metabolises the grasses and grains of its diet, and performs feats of labour that attest to its robust vitality. Nevertheless, it is sterile; it cannot reproduce. On the proposed analysis, the mule would fail to qualify as alive, a conclusion so at variance with common observation that it casts doubt upon the analysis itself. We are led to recognise that reproductive capacity, though characteristic of living things in general, is not a necessary condition of life in the individual case. The definition must therefore be amended to allow that an entity may be fully alive even when the power of reproduction is absent.

definition: A celestial body is a planet if and only if (i) it orbits a star, (ii) it has sufficient mass for self-gravity to mould it into a roughly spherical shape, and (iii) it has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit of other debris.
conditions: it orbits a star
it has sufficient mass for self-gravity to mould it into a roughly spherical shape
it has cleared the neighbourhood around its orbit of other debris
counterexample: Suppose that a large, spherical body of planetary mass is ejected from its stellar system by a gravitational encounter and wanders through interstellar space. It possesses sufficient mass for self-gravity to have shaped it into a sphere, and it has, in its former orbit, cleared its neighbourhood of debris. Yet it no longer orbits any star. By the proposed definition it is not a planet, though it is in every intrinsic respect indistinguishable from one.
missing_condition: The definition makes orbiting a star a necessary condition, failing to account for rogue bodies of planetary mass that possess every intrinsic characteristic of a planet but have been gravitationally ejected from their stellar systems.
passage: The classification of celestial bodies as planets has commonly been made to rest upon three conditions: the body must orbit a star, it must possess sufficient mass that self-gravity imposes a roughly spherical figure, and it must have cleared the region about its orbit of competing debris. This tripartite analysis has the virtue of discriminating neatly between planets and lesser bodies such as asteroids and cometary fragments. Yet one might observe that it is not without difficulty. Let us imagine a body of considerable mass — comparable, say, to that of Jupiter — which was once in stable orbit about a star and which, through a close gravitational encounter with a passing stellar companion, was flung from its system into the void between the stars. This body retains its spherical shape; it carries with it the same composition and internal structure it possessed while in orbit; and in its former career it had cleared its orbital neighbourhood with thoroughness. The sole change is that it no longer orbits any star. By the terms of the proposed definition, it has ceased to be a planet at the moment of ejection. Yet nothing intrinsic to the body has altered; only its dynamical relationship to a star has changed. We are thus brought to recognise that the requirement of stellar orbit may be too restrictive, and that a satisfactory account of planethood ought perhaps to attend more closely to the intrinsic properties of the body than to its current gravitational associations.

definition: A substance is a chemical element if and only if (i) it consists of atoms all having the same number of protons, (ii) it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means, and (iii) it occupies a unique position in the periodic table.
conditions: it consists of atoms all having the same number of protons
it cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical means
it occupies a unique position in the periodic table
counterexample: Consider a case in which a sample of an element contains atoms with differing numbers of neutrons — that is, it is a mixture of isotopes. Each isotope has the same number of protons and cannot be chemically decomposed, and they share the same position in the periodic table. Yet one isotope may be stable and another radioactive, decaying spontaneously into a lighter element. The radioactive isotope, while satisfying all three conditions at the moment of observation, will shortly fail the second, for it decomposes — not by ordinary chemical means, but by nuclear processes. The definition fails to distinguish between chemical indecomposability and nuclear stability.
missing_condition: The definition specifies indecomposability by ordinary chemical means but does not address nuclear transformations, leaving ambiguous the status of radioactive isotopes that spontaneously transmute into other elements.
passage: A standard characterisation of a chemical element holds that it is a substance whose atoms share the same number of protons, which cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by ordinary chemical processes, and which occupies a unique place in the periodic table. This analysis has served chemists well for the better part of two centuries and captures a great many intuitions about the nature of elementary matter. Yet one may observe a difficulty. Let us imagine a sample of a substance — say, uranium — which contains atoms all possessing the same proton number. The sample cannot be broken down by chemical reactions; it sits unambiguously in its assigned position in the table. Yet certain atoms in the sample are radioactive: they will, in due course, emit particles and transmute into atoms of a different element entirely. At the instant of observation, all three conditions are met. Nevertheless, the substance is, in a perfectly real sense, in the process of becoming something else — a process that the definition, restricted as it is to ordinary chemical means of decomposition, does not acknowledge. We are led to conclude that the analysis is incomplete, for it fails to reckon with the nuclear transformations that may alter the very identity of the atoms in question. A more adequate account would need to specify the sense in which indecomposability is to be understood and to address the status of substances whose atoms are inherently unstable.

definition: An organism has a disease if and only if (i) it exhibits abnormal physiological functioning, (ii) the abnormal functioning is caused by a specific pathological agent or process, and (iii) the condition causes suffering or impairment to the organism.
conditions: it exhibits abnormal physiological functioning
the abnormal functioning is caused by a specific pathological agent or process
the condition causes suffering or impairment to the organism
counterexample: Consider a case in which a person carries a bacterial infection — a specific pathological agent — that produces measurable abnormalities in blood chemistry. The person satisfies the first two conditions: her physiology deviates from the norm, and the deviation is caused by a known pathogen. Yet she experiences no suffering or impairment whatever; she feels entirely well, performs her daily activities without difficulty, and would never suspect herself to be ill were it not for a routine laboratory test. By the proposed definition she does not have a disease, yet medical practice would classify her condition as one — an asymptomatic infection — and would recommend treatment to prevent future harm.
missing_condition: The definition requires present suffering or impairment, overlooking asymptomatic conditions that constitute genuine diseases despite causing no current distress, often because they pose a risk of future harm or are transmissible to others.
passage: An attractive proposal defines disease in terms of three conditions: abnormal physiological functioning, a specific pathological cause, and resultant suffering or impairment. The analysis seems to capture our ordinary understanding — we think of the sick person as one whose body functions abnormally, owing to some identifiable cause, and who suffers in consequence. Yet one may observe that this characterisation is not without difficulty. Let us imagine a woman who, upon undergoing a routine examination, is found to harbour a bacterial infection that has produced measurable deviations in her blood chemistry. A definite pathological agent has been identified, and her physiology is demonstrably abnormal. Yet she feels no discomfort, experiences no impairment of her capacities, and would, in the absence of the laboratory findings, have had no reason to suppose herself ill. The third condition — suffering or impairment — is plainly unsatisfied. On the proposed definition, she does not have a disease. Yet the medical profession would unhesitatingly classify her condition as a disease, would prescribe treatment, and would regard neglect of the condition as irresponsible, both because of the risk of future deterioration and because the infection may be communicable. We are thus led to conclude that suffering or impairment, though commonly attendant upon disease, is not a necessary condition of it, and that a more adequate analysis must accommodate the phenomenon of asymptomatic illness.

definition: A region constitutes an ecosystem if and only if (i) it contains a community of interacting organisms, (ii) those organisms exchange energy and materials with their abiotic environment, and (iii) the system maintains a stable equilibrium over time.
conditions: it contains a community of interacting organisms
those organisms exchange energy and materials with their abiotic environment
the system maintains a stable equilibrium over time
counterexample: Consider a case in which a volcanic eruption devastates a forested region. In the months following, pioneer species — lichens, mosses, certain insects — colonise the barren rock. These organisms interact, exchange energy and materials with the abiotic substrate, and yet the system is in a state of rapid flux, far from any stable equilibrium. It appears to be an ecosystem in the making, and ecologists would not hesitate to study it as such, yet it fails the third condition.
missing_condition: The definition demands stable equilibrium, thereby excluding successional, disturbed, or transitional communities that possess all other characteristics of ecosystems but are undergoing directional change.
passage: Ecologists have often characterised an ecosystem by three jointly sufficient conditions: the presence of a community of interacting organisms, the exchange of energy and materials between those organisms and their abiotic surroundings, and the maintenance of a stable equilibrium over time. This analysis captures the paradigmatic cases — a mature forest, a coral reef, a long-established grassland — in which a recognisable community persists in a roughly steady state. Yet one may observe that the third condition is more restrictive than it first appears. Let us imagine a volcanic island whose forests have been obliterated by a recent eruption. Within months, pioneer organisms — lichens colonising bare rock, mosses establishing in crevices, insects arriving on the wind — begin to form a community. These organisms interact with one another and exchange energy and materials with the ash and rock of their environment. Yet the system is in a state of rapid directional change; new species arrive continually, competitive relationships shift, and the community bears little resemblance from one year to the next. The third condition — stable equilibrium — is plainly unsatisfied. Nevertheless, ecologists would regard this successional community as an ecosystem, albeit a young and dynamic one. We are thus led to conclude that the demand for stability is too stringent and that a satisfactory analysis of the concept of an ecosystem must accommodate systems in transition as well as those at equilibrium.

definition: A segment of DNA is a gene if and only if (i) it encodes a functional product — either a protein or a functional RNA molecule, (ii) it is heritable, passed from parent to offspring, and (iii) it has a determinate location on a chromosome.
conditions: it encodes a functional product — either a protein or a functional RNA molecule
it is heritable, passed from parent to offspring
it has a determinate location on a chromosome
counterexample: Consider a case in which a segment of DNA known as a transposable element — or transposon — encodes a protein that enables it to move from one chromosomal location to another. It produces a functional product, it is heritable, and yet it does not have a determinate, fixed location on a chromosome, for it migrates through the genome. The third condition is not satisfied, yet molecular biologists routinely speak of transposons as containing genes.
missing_condition: The definition requires a fixed chromosomal location, failing to accommodate mobile genetic elements whose defining characteristic is that they change position within the genome while retaining their capacity to encode functional products.
passage: A familiar definition holds that a gene is a segment of DNA that encodes a functional product, is heritable, and occupies a determinate location upon a chromosome. The analysis accords well with the classical picture in which genes are arranged along chromosomes like beads upon a string, each occupying a fixed and identifiable locus. Yet the discoveries of molecular biology have complicated this picture in ways that the traditional definition does not easily accommodate. Let us imagine a segment of DNA known as a transposable element, which encodes a protein — a transposase — whose function is to excise the segment from its current chromosomal position and insert it elsewhere in the genome. This segment produces a functional product and is faithfully transmitted from parent to offspring; it satisfies the first two conditions without difficulty. Yet it does not have a determinate location on a chromosome, for its very nature is to move. In one generation it may reside on chromosome three; in the next, on chromosome seven. The third condition is thus violated. Nevertheless, it appears that the segment in question contains a gene in the fullest sense — it encodes a product, it is heritable, and it plays a significant role in the biology of the organism. We are thus led to recognise that the requirement of a fixed chromosomal locus is too restrictive and that the concept of a gene must be broad enough to encompass elements whose location is inherently variable.

definition: A physical interaction constitutes a force if and only if (i) it produces or tends to produce a change in the state of motion of a body, (ii) it acts upon a body through direct or mediated physical contact, and (iii) it can be quantified as a vector having both magnitude and direction.
conditions: it produces or tends to produce a change in the state of motion of a body
it acts upon a body through direct or mediated physical contact
it can be quantified as a vector having both magnitude and direction
counterexample: Suppose that a magnet attracts a small iron nail across an empty gap. The interaction produces a change in the nail's state of motion, and it can be quantified as a vector with magnitude and direction. Yet there is no direct or mediated physical contact between the magnet and the nail — the magnetic force acts at a distance through a field. The second condition is unsatisfied, and yet the magnetic interaction is paradigmatically a force.
missing_condition: The definition restricts force to interactions involving physical contact, failing to accommodate action-at-a-distance forces and field-mediated interactions that operate without any contact between the interacting bodies.
passage: A natural analysis of force might hold that a physical interaction is a force when it produces or tends to produce a change in a body's state of motion, acts upon the body through direct or mediated contact, and admits of quantification as a vector possessing both magnitude and direction. This characterisation accords with our everyday experience of pushes and pulls, in which one body acts upon another through some form of tangible contact. Yet we should consider whether the second condition is truly necessary. Let us imagine a magnet and a small iron nail separated by a gap of several centimetres. The magnet exerts an attractive influence upon the nail; the nail accelerates toward the magnet; and the interaction is readily described as a vector with a definite magnitude and a definite direction. The first and third conditions are met without difficulty. Yet there is no physical contact, direct or mediated, between the magnet and the nail. The interaction is conveyed through a magnetic field — an entity that pervades the intervening space but does not constitute a material medium of the sort that the second condition appears to require. It would be extravagant to deny that the magnetic interaction is a force; it is, indeed, one of the paradigmatic cases. We are thus led to conclude that the requirement of physical contact is too restrictive, and that a satisfactory definition of force must accommodate interactions that operate across spatial separations without any material intermediary.

definition: A trait is an adaptation if and only if (i) it enhances the organism's fitness in its current environment, (ii) it arose through natural selection, and (iii) it performs the function for which it was selected.
conditions: it enhances the organism's fitness in its current environment
it arose through natural selection
it performs the function for which it was selected
counterexample: Consider a case in which the feathers of a certain bird species originally arose through natural selection for thermal insulation. In the species' present environment, the feathers no longer serve an insulating function — the climate has grown warm — but they now play a crucial role in courtship display, significantly enhancing reproductive fitness. The feathers satisfy the first condition, since they enhance fitness. They satisfy the second condition, since they arose through natural selection. But they do not satisfy the third, for they no longer perform the function for which they were originally selected. Yet it seems clear that the feathers are an adaptation — they are simply an adaptation to a different selective pressure than the one that first produced them.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the trait perform its original selected function, failing to accommodate traits that have been co-opted for a new function and are currently maintained by selection for that new role.
passage: Evolutionary biologists commonly define an adaptation as a trait that enhances fitness, arose through natural selection, and continues to perform the function for which selection originally favoured it. This analysis serves well for many paradigmatic cases — the eye for seeing, the wing for flying — in which a trait's current function coincides with its selective history. Yet one may observe that not all adaptive traits fit this mould. Let us imagine a species of bird whose ancestors inhabited a cold climate and whose plumage was selected for its capacity to retain body heat. Over many generations, the climate warms and the insulating function of the feathers becomes superfluous. In the new environment, however, the bright colouration and elaborate patterning of the plumage prove highly attractive to potential mates, and sexual selection now maintains and elaborates the trait. The feathers enhance fitness in the current environment and arose originally through natural selection, satisfying the first two conditions. But they no longer perform the function — thermal insulation — for which they were first selected. The third condition fails. Nevertheless, it appears that the feathers are an adaptation in the fullest sense; they have merely been recruited into the service of a new function. We are thus brought to recognise that the definition, by tying adaptation to the original selected function, is too rigid, and that a satisfactory analysis must allow for the evolutionary co-option of traits to serve ends different from those for which they first arose.

definition: An entity is a living organism if and only if (i) it is composed of one or more cells, (ii) it responds to stimuli from its environment, and (iii) it grows and develops according to a genetically determined programme.
conditions: it is composed of one or more cells
it responds to stimuli from its environment
it grows and develops according to a genetically determined programme
counterexample: Consider a case in which a virus — say, a bacteriophage — responds to chemical stimuli on the surface of a host cell, triggering its injection apparatus, and replicates according to the instructions encoded in its nucleic acid. It responds to stimuli and develops according to a genetic programme, satisfying the second and third conditions. Yet it is not composed of cells and therefore fails the first. Nevertheless, it possesses genetic material, evolves by natural selection, and in many respects behaves as a living thing, prompting one to question whether the cellular condition is truly necessary.
missing_condition: The definition insists upon cellular composition as a necessary condition of life, thereby excluding entities such as viruses that possess genetic programmes, respond to environmental stimuli, and participate in evolutionary processes despite lacking cellular structure.
passage: A widely accepted characterisation of a living organism specifies three conditions: it must be composed of one or more cells, it must respond to environmental stimuli, and it must grow and develop in accordance with a genetically determined programme. This analysis reflects the foundational insight of cell theory and has proved serviceable across a wide range of biological inquiry. Yet we should ask whether the first condition is indispensable. Let us imagine a bacteriophage — a virus that preys upon bacteria. When such an entity encounters a suitable host, chemical signals on the host's surface trigger a precise mechanical response: the phage attaches, contracts its tail sheath, and injects its nucleic acid into the cell. This behaviour constitutes a response to an environmental stimulus. Once inside, the phage's genetic programme commandeers the host machinery to produce new copies of itself — a process that, while dependent on the host, is directed by the phage's own nucleic acid. The second and third conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the phage is not composed of cells; it is, in the conventional description, a particle of protein and nucleic acid. On the proposed definition, it is not alive. And yet it evolves, it adapts, and it exhibits purposive behaviour of a sort. We are thus brought to wonder whether the cellular requirement is too restrictive and whether the boundary of life might profitably be drawn somewhat more broadly.

definition: Two organisms belong to the same species if and only if (i) they share a recent common ancestor, (ii) they are morphologically similar, and (iii) they occupy the same ecological niche.
conditions: they share a recent common ancestor
they are morphologically similar
they occupy the same ecological niche
counterexample: Consider a case in which two populations of a single bird species inhabit different islands. Over several generations, one population adapts to feed on seeds, developing a stout beak, while the other feeds on insects and develops a slender beak. They share a very recent common ancestor and remain interfertile. Yet they are no longer morphologically similar, and they occupy different ecological niches — one a granivore, the other an insectivore. The second and third conditions fail, and yet by any reasonable biological standard they remain members of the same species.
missing_condition: The definition requires morphological similarity and ecological overlap, failing to account for the well-documented phenomenon of intraspecific variation in which members of the same species diverge in form and ecological role while remaining a single interbreeding population.
passage: One might propose that two organisms are conspecific when they share a recent common ancestor, are morphologically similar, and occupy the same ecological niche. These conditions appear to capture a cluster of features commonly associated with species membership: shared descent, physical resemblance, and ecological equivalence. Yet the analysis encounters difficulty when confronted with the natural variability of populations. Let us imagine two island populations of a single bird species, descended from a common ancestral flock that colonised both islands only a few generations ago. On one island, the abundance of hard-shelled seeds has favoured the evolution of a stout, powerful beak; on the other, a diet of insects has selected for a slender, elongated bill. The two populations share a very recent ancestor and remain fully capable of interbreeding when brought together, yet they are no longer morphologically similar, and they exploit quite different ecological niches. The second and third conditions are unmet, and yet taxonomists would not hesitate to classify the two populations as belonging to the same species. We are thus led to recognise that morphological similarity and ecological overlap, though often present among conspecifics, are not necessary conditions of species membership, and that the definition must be revised to accommodate the considerable variation that may obtain within a single species.
